translation: Alif, Lam, Meem
commentary:
**الٓمٓ**

The Quran begins Surah Al-Baqarah with these mysterious letters: Alif Lam Meem. These are known as the *Huruf Muqatta'at* (the separated letters). The word *muqatta'at* means separated, and these letters appear scattered across the Quran—sometimes one letter, sometimes two, sometimes three, and occasionally four or five.

The overwhelming position among Muslim scholars is that only Allah ﷻ knows their true meaning. While there has been scholarly discussion about their benefits and why they're placed at the beginning of certain surahs, most of that remains speculative. However, one benefit stands out as particularly brilliant and obvious, especially for Surah Al-Baqarah.

In Surah Al-Fatihah, we ask Allah ﷻ for guidance: *Ihdinas siratal mustaqeem* (Guide us to the straight path). Surah Al-Baqarah serves as the first response to that request in the divinely ordained sequence of the Quran. But notice how Allah ﷻ begins this response—with words we cannot even understand. This is profoundly important because it immediately puts our attitude in check.

Do not come to this Book expecting that you know everything. Come to this Book understanding and acknowledging that you know nothing, that only Allah ﷻ knows. Until you have that attitude—what I call intellectual humility—you won't be able to receive the guidance this Book offers. The letters Alif Lam Meem put the human being in their proper place.

But there's another fascinating dimension to these letters. Our Messenger ﷺ did not know how to read or write. Allah ﷻ made sure he remained among the *ummiyyin*—the unlettered. The Quran itself declares: "You never read any book before this, nor did you write with your hand, lest those who follow falsehood should doubt" (29:48). Yet here, this unlettered prophet is reciting the names of alphabetical letters: Alif, Lam, Meem.

These letter names are only known to people who can read and write. For someone who cannot read, these words are meaningless—they don't represent anything except to someone learning literacy. This becomes especially significant when we remember that Al-Baqarah is a Medinan surah, revealed after the Prophet's ﷺ migration. In Medina, he came into contact with a scholarly community—particularly the Jewish community, who prided themselves on being people of knowledge, people who could read and write. Most Arabs at that time were illiterate, so this was a source of pride for them and a basis for questioning: "How can this be a prophet? He doesn't even know how to read and write."

The recitation of Alif Lam Meem baffles even the learned rabbi sitting there: "How does he know the names of letters? I thought he couldn't read?" That's because he's being taught by Someone else—Someone beyond human capacity.

Notice also that Allah ﷻ doesn't just call this a recitation (which could come from memory), but uses the word *Kitab*—a book. The word *Kitab* inherently refers to something written, something that requires literacy to access. Yet this unlettered prophet is being given knowledge of a Book. Later in the Quran, we find the word *iqra* used for the Prophet ﷺ—he reads like an educated person reads, but it's a different kind of education. It's divine education, instruction from the unseen.

translation: This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of Allah
commentary:
**ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ**

Now that you've been put in your proper place through those mysterious letters, you're told: *Dhalika al-Kitab* (That is the Book). The Arabic word *dhalika* is a demonstrative pronoun for distance—pointing to something far away. The closer pointer would be *hadha* (this). But the Quran was right there being recited, so why does Allah ﷻ refer to it as *dhalika* (that)?

The benefit is profound. The word *Kitab* refers to something etched, carved, written, or documented. But the actual documented Quran—the source—is not here. It's very far away. It's in the seventh heaven, in *al-Lawh al-Mahfudh* (the Preserved Tablet), mentioned in Surah Al-Buruj: "Nay, it is a Glorious Quran in a Preserved Tablet" (85:21-22). In other places, the Quran calls this source "the hidden book" or *Umm al-Kitab* (the Mother of the Book)—the essence of all revelation that exists with Allah ﷻ. So *dhalika* is appropriate because it refers to that sacred document that exists far away in the divine realm. That, in fact, is the Book.

The phrase continues: *La rayba feehi* (There is no doubt in it). This can be understood in multiple complementary ways, each adding depth to our understanding.

First: This is clearly answering your question. You asked for guidance in Al-Fatihah. No doubt, this is the Book that offers that guidance. There's absolutely no doubt about it. This is what you were asking for. The conversation continues from one surah to the next.

Second: This Book has a special quality—there can be absolutely no doubt whatsoever about this Book. Whether you understand everything in it or not, one thing is clear: you can never doubt its legitimacy, its divine origin. That doubt is impossible for someone who comes to it with the right attitude.

Now, some people have told me they read the Quran and fell into doubt. They weren't doubtful before, but something they read disturbed them, bothered them, caused them problems with faith. This seems to contradict what the ayah says—that there's no possibility of doubt in this Book. But notice the next phrase: *Hudan lil-muttaqeen* (Guidance for those who have taqwa). That doubtless guidance exists for a particular kind of person—those who want to protect themselves, the *muttaqeen*.

The connection can also be understood this way: There's no doubt about the fact that only in this Book is there true guidance. When people genuinely seeking to protect themselves look for guidance, they will only find it here. There's no doubt about that reality.

The word *muttaqeen* is crucial to understand. Someone might explore religions out of curiosity, like a shopping experience—"I like this aspect of this religion, that aspect of that religion." Some examine religions out of intellectual curiosity: "This one's peculiar, that one's interesting, this one's philosophically nuanced." But then there's a particular kind of person who says: "I'm looking for the truth because I know my safety and well-being lie in committing myself to the truth."

This is what *taqwa* means. The word comes from *wiqayah* (protection). A *muttaqi* is someone making an effort to protect themselves. When people are truly looking to protect themselves, to save themselves, when they genuinely go on this hunt—there's no doubt that they will find timeless guidance in this Book. This is the Book that will be guidance for those kinds of people, whoever they may be. They might even be people who haven't yet found this religion, but when they do, they will recognize it because they had the attitude of wanting to protect themselves. They will be the ones who discover the truth of this faith.

Think about what *muttaqeen* implies about this audience. These are people concerned with protecting themselves from something unseen. Protecting yourself from weather or physical danger—that's obvious. Even an animal does that. But these people have a higher sense of understanding. They realize there may be greater danger to their well-being than just what they see in front of them.

translation: Who believe in the unseen, establish prayer, and spend out of what We have provided for them
commentary:
**ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ**

Now Allah ﷻ describes these *muttaqeen*: *Alladheena yu'minoona bil-ghayb* (Those who believe in the unseen). Notice that *alladheena* (those who) is in the present tense form in Arabic. The present tense doesn't just refer to something happening now—it carries the connotation of the future as well. This encompasses those who have already come to believe and those who will come to believe in the unseen.

The connection between taqwa and belief in the unseen must be clearly understood. These are people who want to protect themselves, and their first quality is that they believe in the unseen. In other words, they want to protect themselves from something that is not in the seen world but in the unseen. Their taqwa itself is proof that they are convinced of what they cannot see.

Consider this: imagine two people backbiting about someone. One says, "*Ittaqillah*—be cautious of Allah ﷻ!" If the other person truly believes in the unseen, they'll stop immediately because that danger is real to them. Now imagine those same two people talking, and suddenly the third person they're discussing walks into the room. "Watch out, he's here!" What happens? They stop immediately because the danger is real, the embarrassment is tangible. Someone who stops themselves because of taqwa demonstrates that same level of conviction—the danger of the unseen is as real to them as if it were visible.

But here's the challenge: caution is like being a guard, and a guard can doze off. A guard can get tired. A guard can waver. You can be on guard for a long time and then get lazy. So what keeps this guard alert? What maintains this security system?

*Wa yuqeemuna as-salah* (And they establish the prayer). They've put something in place in their lives that keeps them on guard, that keeps their consciousness in check. Allah ﷻ gave us salah, and salah constantly reminds them of the reality that cannot be seen.

The word *salah* literally means connection. It's beautiful because it connects us to Allah ﷻ, but it does so by connecting us to the Quran itself. The longest part of salah is the *qiyam*, when we stand and recite Quran. So *yuqeemuna as-salah*—they establish and maintain that connection with Allah ﷻ through His Book.

Think about any relationship: when you're connected with someone all the time, you think about them all the time. The English saying goes, "Out of sight, out of mind." When you're with your children all the time, you think of them constantly. They disappear for a few minutes: "Where did they go?" When you sit with the same friend every day in class, one day they're missing: "Where are you? What's going on?" You send a text trying to find out. When there's a constant connection, you feel the loss. But if the connection gets weak, even when they're not there, you won't realize it until much later.

That's what happens in all human relationships, and it's what happens between us and Allah ﷻ. Salah keeps that connection strong. The cautiousness stays alive, and our conviction in what we cannot see—our *iman bil-ghayb*—stays fresh. These unseen realities take extra effort to remember. It takes very little effort to take caution with the weather—you step outside for a few seconds, you know it's hot or cold, and you dress accordingly. But the dangers and realities of the unseen require you to go out of your way, to check yourself, to remind yourself. This is what salah does.

Finally, Allah ﷻ describes the third quality: *Wa mimma razaqnahum yunfiqoon* (And from what We have provided them, they spend). This is profound. Everything in the seen world—your paycheck, your house, your car, your refrigerator and what's in it—it's all visible and tangible. But Allah ﷻ makes it a matter of the unseen. What you see is the food on your plate. What you don't see is that Allah ﷻ provided it. You don't necessarily see that connection.

But these people, because they're so connected to Allah ﷻ, whenever they see *rizq* (provision), they don't think of it as something they got themselves. It's *razaqnahum*—something We have in fact provided them. And because they see all the things they have in life through this lens—as something that has been given to them, not something that belongs to them—the next step becomes easy for them. It becomes easy to spend.

Think of it this way: imagine I give my two-year-old son a lollipop. He's very demanding sometimes. He looks at me: "Can I have it?" He screams. I say, "No, you can't have it." "Please, please!" Finally I say, "Okay, just two licks, that's it." He takes it and starts enjoying it. Two licks later, I say, "Can I have it back?" What does he say? "Mine! No!" He can't let go. I gave it, and then I ask for it back, and what does he say? "Mine!"

This is exactly how we are with our wealth: "My house, my money, my savings." But the one who believes in the unseen never forgets that it's not his, it's not hers. That's a logical consequence of truly believing in the unseen. Each quality Allah ﷻ mentions flows naturally from the one before it.

These are the people of taqwa. Their consciousness of Allah ﷻ is rooted deeply in their belief in what they cannot see. That belief manifests in maintaining their connection with Allah ﷻ through salah. And that connection transforms how they view everything they possess, making it natural for them to spend in His path. These three qualities—belief in the unseen, establishing prayer, and spending from what Allah ﷻ has provided—are not separate traits but interconnected realities that define those who truly seek to protect themselves through guidance from this Book.

translation: And who believe in what has been revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what was revealed before you, and of the Hereafter they are certain [in faith]
commentary:
**وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ وَبِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ**

*"And those who believe in what has been revealed to you and what was revealed before you, and in the Hereafter they are certain."*

This ayah continues describing the qualities of those who possess Taqwa. Allah ﷻ identifies them as people who believe in what has been sent to the Prophet ﷺ and what was revealed to the messengers who came before him.

Before exploring this further, we should understand that the Quran highlights three core areas of faith: belief in Allah ﷻ, belief in the message, and belief in the Afterlife. The middle category—belief in the message—encompasses belief in the angels who deliver revelation, the books themselves, and the messengers who received them. 

Notice which area of faith this ayah emphasizes: belief in the message. This pattern begins at the very opening of Surah Al-Baqarah. When Allah ﷻ says, *"That is the Book about which there is no doubt,"* He is reinforcing our faith in what? The message. This becomes a central theme throughout this Surah. When Allah ﷻ later mentions faith generally, we must understand it within this context—the climate of Al-Baqarah constantly draws our attention back to belief in the message.

The choice of the preposition *ilayaka* (إِلَيْكَ - "to you") rather than *'alayka* (عَلَيْكَ - "upon you") is significant. The word *'ala* typically implies responsibility or burden—something placed upon you that you must carry. But *ila* indicates privilege, something gifted to you that nobody else has been given. The revelation that came to the Prophet ﷺ is not merely a burden to bear, but an immense honor and privilege.

This ayah also closes an important theological door. There are some who claim that revelation is possible after the passing of the Prophet ﷺ, or that another messenger could arise who might receive revelation. The response to such claims lies right here in the beginning of the Quran. Those who will successfully become people of Taqwa believe in only two types of revelation: what has been sent to the Prophet ﷺ and what was sent to the messengers before him. There is no mention of anything after. If you want to be a person of Taqwa, you have two options for belief—this revelation and the previous revelations. That's it. There is no third option, and that door is completely closed.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement about conviction in the Afterlife: *wa bil-aakhirati hum yooqinoon* (وَبِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ). The word *yaqeen* (يَقِين) describes a unique level of certainty—conviction so strong it's as if you've experienced something firsthand. 

Consider the difference between types of belief. Someone might tell you an athlete is exceptional, and you believe them. You might read the statistics yourself and believe even more. But when you actually watch that athlete perform, you experience a different level of belief entirely. Similarly, someone might describe a flavor of ice cream as amazing. You trust their taste, you believe them. You read the ingredients and think it sounds good. But once you taste it yourself, your belief transforms into something unshakeable.

This experiential kind of belief is *yaqeen*—conviction so deep that no matter what anyone says to contradict it, you remain firm because you've experienced the truth. Allah ﷻ is describing believers whose faith in the Afterlife, in a life that comes after we leave this Earth, is that powerful. Their conviction is comparable to experiential belief, as if they've already witnessed it.

This isn't the first time the concept of the Afterlife appears in this passage. Earlier, Allah ﷻ mentioned those who have Taqwa protect themselves from something in the unseen (*al-ghayb*). What is the primary danger in the unseen that we protect ourselves from? The consequences of the Afterlife. Everything else pales in comparison. This ayah brings us back to that original sentiment, but now with revelation in hand, that feeling transforms from mere sentiment into solid conviction. Now they possess *yaqeen* in the eventual life to come.

translation: Those are upon [right] guidance from their Lord, and it is those who are the successful
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ**

*"Those are upon guidance from their Lord, and it is those who are the successful."*

Allah ﷻ uses the distant demonstrative pronoun *ulaika* (أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ) rather than the near pronoun *haaula* (هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ). This choice is deliberate and meaningful. The same distant pointer was used for the Book at the beginning: *Dhaalika al-Kitaab* (ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ) - "That is the Book," pointing to something far away.

Why point to these believers as if they're distant? When someone has reached a very high level of achievement, when they've accomplished something truly remarkable, and you want to present them as an example to others, you point to them as if they're far away. It's as though you're saying to your audience, "You see them over there? You need to be like them. They're up there, and you're over here. You have a long way to travel, much progress to make, because those people have reached heights you haven't yet attained."

*Ulaika 'ala hudan min rabbihim* - "Those are the ones truly upon guidance from their Master." The Surah began with *Dhaalika al-Kitaabu laa rayba feehi, hudan* - "That is the Book in which there is no doubt, a guidance." Then Allah ﷻ described the qualities of people who have Taqwa through the phrases *alladheena yu'minoona* and *alladheena yu'minoona*. Now He returns to the concept of guidance: these are the people who are genuinely upon guidance.

Why hammer this point again? Because there will be many who think they possess guidance, many who claim they are upon the right path. But the way to distinguish who is genuine and who is not is through the litmus test outlined in these opening ayahs. Allah ﷻ is certifying that these—and only these—are the ones truly committed to guidance from their Lord.

*Wa ulaika humu al-muflihoon* - "And those are the ones who are truly successful." Notice that Allah ﷻ gives two permanent qualities, two defining characteristics to the people of guidance. The first is *muttaqeen* (مُتَّقِينَ) - those who have Taqwa, those who protect themselves. The second is *muflihoon* (مُفْلِحُونَ) - those who are successful. Remember these two qualities: Taqwa and success. They will become increasingly important as we continue through this Surah.

The implication is clear: there will be people who fake it, who pretend to have guidance, and they will not succeed. There will be people who claim commitment to guidance but don't meet the criteria already outlined, and they won't achieve true success either.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve - it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them - they will not believe
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ**

*"Indeed, those who disbelieve - it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them - they will not believe."*

This ayah stands in stark contrast to those who believe, but before we explore that contrast, notice the connection to what came before. The Book was introduced as having *laa rayba feehi* - no doubt whatsoever in it. Now, when Allah ﷻ mentions *alladheena kafaroo* - those who have disbelieved - He is referring to those who harbor even a shred of doubt in this Book. Either you believe in this Book entirely without any doubt, or if you maintain even a little skepticism, you fall into disbelief. The contrast between these two states is absolute.

We often think of *inna alladheena kafaroo* as referring exclusively to non-Muslims. However, this is an incomplete understanding. Remember that the earlier phrase *alladheena yu'minoona* implied both those who currently believe and those who have the potential to come to faith. Similarly, it's not fair to label every non-Muslim as a *kaafir* in the Quranic sense. 

However, the Quran does make a clear assessment of a particular type of person: those who were given abundant opportunity to examine this Book, to experience the character of the Messenger ﷺ, who had a full decade to observe and question, who received answers to their inquiries in the most eloquent and powerful way—and still chose not to believe in this message. You couldn't ask for a better educational opportunity. You couldn't find more convincing responses to your questions. Yet they still turned away. For such people, it becomes evident that their window of opportunity has closed. The door that Allah ﷻ Himself has shut for them indicates they've reached a point of no return.

*Sawaa'un 'alayhim* - their outcome will be the same. The word *sawaa'* comes from the root meaning to bury a seed in the ground. These individuals have buried the seed of truth deep within themselves. That small light (*fitrah*) that Allah ﷻ gave them has been choked to death, suffocated under layers of rejection. At this point, they've become terminal cases—patients beyond treatment.

*A-andhartahum am lam tundhirhum* - "Whether you warn them or you don't warn them." Both present and past tenses are captured here: *laa yu'minoona* (they don't believe) and they won't believe in the future either. The message is clear: for such people, whether the Prophet ﷺ continues to warn them or not makes no difference.

Consider the weight of this statement. All of us have encountered effective speakers, powerful orators, convincing arguers. But you will not find a more persuasive speaker than the Messenger ﷺ himself. You will not find more credible speech than the Quran itself. For us, we experience it through translation, with all the limitations that entails. But for those people, it was in their own eloquent Arabic, without any filter, without needing to look up meanings. The entire dictionary was already in their hearts, the full context was there before them. The best of all speakers delivered the best of all messages in the best of all ways—using the most effective means of communication possible. And yet it didn't work. Why? Because they had turned their inner conscience away. They shunned it. They choked it to death.

Once a person reaches that point, even the finest message won't make any difference. There's no need to waste your breath on them anymore. This is a profound and heavy statement, because if we mistakenly think it's talking about all non-Muslims, we create a serious problem—it would suggest there's no point in calling people to Islam, no point in sharing the message. But that's not what this ayah means. It's not referring to all non-believers universally.

This ayah was revealed in Medina about a specific group from within the Quraysh who had actively committed themselves to disbelief. It's not even about all of the Quraysh. There were people among the Quraysh who never really took the message seriously, who didn't give it a fair examination and therefore didn't believe—not because they consciously rejected it, but because they hadn't truly considered it. Such people might still come to faith later. This ayah describes a particular category: those bent upon disbelief, determined in their rejection. Only Allah ﷻ knows the hearts.

translation: Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing, and over their vision is a veil. And for them is a great punishment
commentary:
**خَتَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ ۖ وَعَلَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ غِشَـٰوَةٌ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ**

*"Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing, and over their vision is a veil. And for them is a great punishment."*

This ayah describes the consequences for those who persist in rejection. Allah ﷻ says *khatama Allahu 'ala quloobihim* - "Allah has placed a seal upon their hearts." Notice the order: hearts are mentioned first, then hearing. In other passages, like Surah Al-Jaathiyah (45), you'll find Allah ﷻ mentions sealing their hearing first, then their hearts. Why does this ayah mention the heart first?

Because everything discussed so far relates to the heart. *Dhaalika al-Kitaabu laa rayba feehi* - where does doubt reside? In the heart. *Hudan lil-muttaqeen* - where is Taqwa located? The Messenger ﷺ said, *"Al-taqwa haa hunaa"* - "Taqwa is here," pointing to his chest. *Alladheena yu'minoona* - where does *iman* settle? In the heart. Everything mentioned up to this point has been a matter of the heart. Therefore, when these people are punished in this worldly life, their punishment begins where the crime originated—in the heart. So Allah ﷻ mentions sealing the heart first.

The word *khatama* (خَتَمَ) is significant. It means to fill something to the brim and then seal it completely. In ancient times, people didn't just cork a bottle to seal it. They would pour wax over the opening, and it would melt and spread over the sides, creating a complete covering that nothing could penetrate or escape. This is the kind of sealing Allah ﷻ describes—absolute, total, impenetrable.

*Wa 'ala sam'ihim* - "and upon their hearing." When a person's heart is sealed, they lose interest in the message. Once they're no longer interested in the message, no matter what you say to them, it won't matter. The warning the Prophet ﷺ gives is experienced through the ear. When the heart is sealed, that warning loses all impact. So Allah ﷻ placed a seal on their hearing as well. They won't hear anything beneficial anymore. All they'll hear is material they can mock, twist, or misquote.

But it goes even further: *wa 'ala absaarihim ghishaawah* - "and over their vision is a veil." This is remarkable because it moves beyond the unseen into the realm of the visible. These people could witness the Prophet's ﷺ character—the most beautiful, most flawless character imaginable. Yet they still couldn't see it for what it was. That's not a matter of the unseen (*ghayb*)—that's right there in the seen world (*shahaadah*), observable reality. But even that has been taken from them. If there was one thing they could still do, it would be to simply observe: see how he treats his family, see how he interacts with his companions, see the principles he calls people toward. But now, they can't even perceive that anymore.

*Wa lahum 'adhaabun 'adheem* - "And for them, exclusively, there is tremendous punishment." The suffering awaiting such people is not ordinary—it is described as great, immense, befitting the magnitude of their willful rejection.

translation: And of the people are some who say, "We believe in Allah and the Last Day," but they are not believers
commentary:
**وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَمَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ**

*"And among the people are some who say, 'We believe in Allah and the Last Day,' but they are not believers."*

The Quran has now introduced us to three categories of humanity. We saw at the end of Surah Al-Fatihah: *Alhamdulillahi Rabbil 'aalameen* - "All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds." The beginning of Al-Baqarah reveals that there are three types of populations in this world: those who believe or have the potential to believe, those who disbelieve or are determined in their disbelief and won't accept the truth regardless of evidence, and now a third category—people of weak character, hypocrites.

Notice how Allah ﷻ introduces them: *wa mina an-naasi man yaqool* - "And among the people is someone who says..." This is fascinating. Someone who claims to believe should logically be described as being "from among the believers." But Allah ﷻ says "from the people" instead. Why? Because by the end of this very ayah, Allah ﷻ will make clear they're not believers at all. So He doesn't even grant them the dignity of being categorized with the believing community. They're just "people"—that's all.

Also notice the singular *man yaqool* - "someone who says." It's not *alladheena yaqooloona* (those who say). The singular form indicates this isn't describing an organized group, but rather scattered individuals here and there who exhibit this characteristic. You'll find this type of person lurking within communities, but they don't constitute a unified movement.

*Aamannaa billahi* - "We believe in Allah." Interestingly, this person doesn't say "I believe in Allah" using the singular. He says "we" - *aamannaa*. Why? Because that's what hypocrites do. They try to blend in. They say "we" to create the impression they're part of the group: "We believe, we're all the same, guys. We're all in this together. We all believe in Allah."

Then notice the addition: *wa bil-yawmil-aakhir* - "and in the Last Day." There's a second *ba* (the preposition "in"). He doesn't say, "We believe in Allah and the Last Day" with a single preposition covering both. Instead, he adds a second *ba*, creating emphasis: "We believe in Allah. And IN the Last Day too." 

Why this over-emphasis? When someone volunteers information you didn't ask for, when they insist on clarifying something unprompted, it often reveals a guilty conscience. If a child approaches you and declares, "I didn't punch anyone, by the way, just so you know"—what does that tell you? That very emphasis betrays them. "We believe in Allah, and we definitely, absolutely believe in the Last Day too!"—it's compensation, overcompensation for what's missing inside.

Allah ﷻ calls them out immediately: *wa maa hum bi-mu'mineen* - "But they are not believers at all." The *bi* here is emphatic—"in any way, shape, or form." Don't be deluded for even a moment. Don't think they are believers in the slightest. Their words are empty, their claims hollow, their declarations meaningless. True faith requires more than words—it requires the qualities outlined in the earlier ayahs: belief that transforms the heart, produces Taqwa, and results in genuine conviction in the unseen, particularly in the Afterlife.

translation: They [think to] deceive Allah and those who believe, but they deceive not except themselves and perceive [it] not
commentary:
يُخَـٰدِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَمَا يَخْدَعُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ

*They seek to deceive Allah and those who believe, but they deceive none except themselves, yet they perceive not.*

This ayah introduces us to the defining characteristic of the hypocrites: deception. يُخَـٰدِعُونَ (yukhaadi'oon) – they attempt to deceive Allah ﷻ and those who believe. This is the first time hypocrisy appears in the Quran, and its placement at the very beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah tells us something profound. If the central message of this surah is guidance, and if the thing we asked for in Al-Fatihah was guidance, then anything that can destroy that guidance must be made crystal clear so we stay far away from it. Just as a doctor highlights the symptoms of diseases to help us stay healthy, Allah ﷻ highlights the symptoms of spiritual diseases so we can protect ourselves.

Before we examine what this ayah teaches about hypocrisy, we must establish some essential principles about how to understand this subject.

**The Believer's Relationship with Hypocrisy**

First and most importantly, you and I can never judge someone as a hypocrite. We simply do not have that right. It doesn't matter how clear their actions appear to be – this religion did not give us the license to make such judgments about others.

There are two types of hypocrites. The first type knows they are not Muslim and are only pretending, like spies. If such a person gets exposed and admits their deception, then obviously they can be identified. But the second type is far more dangerous: hypocrites who have no idea they are hypocrites. This type could be any one of us. None of us are safe.

This brings us to the second principle: you cannot keep yourself safe from that label. You cannot assume, and I cannot assume, that we are protected from hypocrisy. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that no one feels safe from hypocrisy except a hypocrite, and no one fears it except a believer. To be a believer, you must be afraid of hypocrisy. It is a constant struggle within the conscience of every believer.

The third principle is that the symptoms of hypocrisy will always be present. You will see them in people and in attitudes, but you are still not permitted to label anyone. You might find something that perfectly describes someone you know, but even if it seems 100% clear, you cannot make that judgment.

**The Case of Usamah bin Zaid (RA)**

Consider the example of Usamah bin Zaid (RA), whom the Prophet ﷺ loved dearly. During a battle, he fought against a man who was about to be killed. The moment the man fell and lost his sword, realizing his life was in danger, he suddenly proclaimed the shahada. Usamah (RA) thought to himself: just seconds ago you were trying to kill me, and now that you've fallen and lost your weapon, Islam suddenly makes sense to you? This man is clearly faking it to save his life. So Usamah (RA) killed him.

When the Prophet ﷺ found out, he was deeply upset. In one narration, he asked Usamah (RA): "Did you open his heart and see whether he meant it or not?" In another narration, he said: "What will you do when 'La ilaha illAllah' comes to complain about you on the Day of Judgment?"

If that clear-cut case did not give Usamah bin Zaid (RA) – the beloved of the Prophet ﷺ – the license to make such a judgment, then you and I certainly do not have that right. There will never be a more obvious case, and still the lesson stands.

**The Right Attitude**

When we read these ayahs, our attitude should not be: "I know someone who fits this description exactly." Instead, the believer's attitude is: "Is this me?" Not anyone else – me.

To illustrate this mindset, consider what happened during the expedition to Tabuk. Some hypocrites attempted to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ at night. They covered their faces so they couldn't be identified. Allah ﷻ revealed their names to the Prophet ﷺ, who then told Hudhayfah (RA) these names but instructed him not to share them with anyone else. Now only Allah ﷻ, His Messenger ﷺ, and Hudhayfah (RA) knew who these hypocrites were.

Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA) – one of the ten people guaranteed Paradise – came to Hudhayfah (RA) and asked: "Is my name on that list?" Hudhayfah (RA) replied that he had sworn not to reveal the names, but he could tell him that Umar's name was not among them. Think about this: Umar (RA), guaranteed Paradise, was so concerned about hypocrisy that he worried his name might be on a list of hypocrites revealed by Allah ﷻ.

This is the character we must embody. May Allah ﷻ protect us from hypocrisy.

**Understanding the Deception**

Returning to the ayah: يُخَـٰدِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ (yukhaadi'oona Allah) – they try to deceive Allah ﷻ, وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (walladheena aamanoo) – and those who believe. Who do they think they're fooling? Allah ﷻ knows all that they conceal. The hypocrites try to impress Allah ﷻ and the believers while harboring entirely different loyalties and intentions.

But then Allah ﷻ delivers His devastating response: وَمَا يَخْدَعُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ (wa ma yakhda'oona illa anfusahum) – they deceive none except themselves. For all their scheming and plotting, they succeed only in harming themselves. وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (wa ma yash'uroon) – and they have no realization whatsoever. This may indicate that this ayah speaks particularly about the second category of hypocrites: those who are not even aware that they may be hypocrites.

translation: In their hearts is disease, so Allah has increased their disease; and for them is a painful punishment because they [habitually] used to lie
commentary:
فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَرَضًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ

*In their hearts is a disease, so Allah has increased them in disease. And for them is a painful punishment because they used to lie.*

فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ (fee quloobihim marad) – in their hearts is a disease. Notice that Allah ﷻ specifies: the disease is only in their hearts, nowhere else. But what is this disease, and how does it manifest practically?

**The Disease of Dual Loyalty**

This disease is hypocrisy itself, but let's understand what that truly means. Hypocrisy happens when you try to save yourself from someone other than Allah ﷻ, or when you try to gain rewards from somewhere other than Allah ﷻ.

The story of the hypocrites began with people trying to protect themselves. Interestingly, the Arabic word for hypocrite – مُنَافِق (munafiq) – comes from نَفَق (nafaq), which refers to a lizard's hole. A lizard's burrow has two openings: if danger comes from one side, the lizard escapes through the other side. If danger approaches from that side, it escapes back through the first opening. This is the essence of نِفَاق (nifaq): always keeping two doors open for protection.

The hypocrite maintains friends among the believers and friends among the disbelievers. If things go poorly for the believers, he has his disbelieving friends to fall back on. If the believers prevail, he claims he was with them all along. He keeps two doors open for his own protection.

There is a legitimate way to protect yourself: protecting yourself from the displeasure of Allah ﷻ through taqwa. That is real safety. But the hypocrite tries to protect himself at the expense of loyalty to Allah ﷻ. This dual loyalty – this playing both sides – is their disease.

**The Example of Abdullah ibn Ubayy**

Consider Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the leader of the hypocrites in Madinah. Before the Prophet ﷺ arrived, this man was about to be crowned king of Madinah. His throne had already been prepared. The festival was planned. He had won, essentially, and was the uncontested ruler-to-be.

Then the Prophet ﷺ arrived, and suddenly the entire campaign was canceled. Overnight, the new and uncontested ruler of Madinah became the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Abdullah ibn Ubayy went from winning everything to becoming a nobody – not even part of any cabinet or council.

The only way he could stay relevant in public life was to remain visible among the Muslims. So he would stand in the front row of every prayer, right behind the Prophet ﷺ. Before the Prophet ﷺ would even speak, Abdullah would stand up and announce: "O people, this is the Messenger of Allah! Listen carefully. You should pay attention!" Then he would sit down. Everyone was going to listen anyway, but this man just needed his moment in the spotlight.

This is part of his attempting to deceive the people. Yet this same man, after the Battle of Uhud when his hypocrisy was becoming more exposed, sat in a gathering where the Prophet ﷺ was mentioned. He became so angry that he got up and left. On his way out, someone told him to go back, but he pushed past and kept going. He exposed himself – you didn't even need to point him out. He revealed his own true feelings.

**The Increase in Disease**

فَزَادَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَرَضًا (fazaadahumu Allahu maradan) – Allah ﷻ has increased them in disease. This is a profound principle: when someone persists in their spiritual sickness, refusing to seek healing, Allah ﷻ may increase them in that disease. They sink deeper into their hypocrisy, their hearts becoming harder, their deception becoming more elaborate, until they can no longer recognize truth from falsehood.

**The Painful Punishment**

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ (wa lahum 'adhaabun aleem) – and for them is a painful punishment. بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ (bima kaanoo yakdhiboon) – because of the lies they continuously made. This refers to multiple types of lies: the lies they told themselves, the lies they told Allah's Messenger ﷺ, the lies they spread about the Muslims, and the lies they spread among the Muslims about each other.

**Testimony as Deception**

In Surah Al-Munafiqoon, Allah ﷻ reveals how hypocrites would approach the Prophet ﷺ: "When the hypocrites come to you, they say, 'We testify that you are the Messenger of Allah.'" This is profoundly insightful. There is a difference between saying "You are the Messenger of Allah" and saying "We testify that you are the Messenger of Allah."

When you say "You are the Messenger of Allah," that statement is true regardless of who says it. But when they say "We testify that you are the Messenger of Allah," that testimony itself could be true or false. Do you see the difference? The fact that he is Allah's Messenger is true, but their testimony – their claim to believe it – is a lie.

Allah ﷻ responds in that surah: "Allah testifies that the hypocrites are indeed liars." Their testimony that the Prophet ﷺ is Allah's Messenger is objectively true, but their testimony that they believe in Allah and the Last Day is false. This is why Allah ﷻ says here: بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ – because of the lies they continuously made.

They also spread lies about individual Muslims. They fabricated the slander against Aisha (RA). They spread lies about the Prophet ﷺ himself, claiming he only wanted to expand his territory and gain more land. They created lies between Muslims, going from group to group sowing discord. Allah ﷻ says about them regarding the expedition to Tabuk: if they had gone with you, they would have created fitnah – going from one group of travelers to another, starting trouble, spreading gossip, making one group angry at another, then returning to say "You know what they said about you?"

This continuous lying – to themselves, to Allah ﷻ, to His Messenger ﷺ, about the believers, and among the believers – is why they will receive painful punishment.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Do not cause corruption on the earth," they say, "We are but reformers
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ

*And when it is said to them, "Do not cause corruption on the earth," they say, "We are only reformers."*

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ (wa idha qeela lahum) – and when it is said to them, لَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (la tufsidoo fil-ard) – do not cause corruption on the earth.

**Understanding Fasad**

فَسَاد (fasad) means corruption, and here we learn something remarkable: whatever you do that is not genuine is corruption. Even if on the surface someone is doing good deeds, if those deeds are not sincere, they constitute corruption on the earth.

Consider someone who builds a masjid not for the sake of Allah ﷻ, but to get their name recognized, to say "We built the biggest masjid in America" or "We're number one." On the outside, a masjid is being built – people will pray there, society will benefit. But what about the fights that will happen in that masjid? The disputes, the lawsuits, the division? All of this occurs because even though the outward deed appears good, the intention behind it is corrupt.

The hypocrite's outward actions look righteous, but they constitute fasad on the earth because they lack sincerity. The corruption is in the heart, and it spreads from there.

**The Hidden Corruption**

Another meaning of لَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ relates to the conspiring that hypocrites would do behind the scenes. In front of Muslims, they performed good deeds and appeared righteous. But when alone, they would plot and scheme and create genuine corruption.

Many scholars of tafsir comment that the fasad referenced here specifically refers to how hypocrites would conspire with non-Muslims to create problems for the Muslims. They would send messages to the leaders of Quraysh saying, "Look, we're with the Muslims outwardly, but we're really with you. We'll undermine them from within."

**The Response of Self-Righteousness**

When confronted about this corruption, what is their response? قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ (qaaloo innama nahnu muslihoon) – they say, "We are only reformers. We are only peacemakers."

Notice the audacity: "Oh no, we're not causing corruption! We're maintaining good relations with everyone. We're engaged in interfaith dialogue. We're just trying to help everyone get along and understand each other. That's all we're doing." They covered their cowardice and their dual loyalty with the garment of peace and reform.

But think about this logically: Allah ﷻ has already declared regarding the disbelievers that whether you warn them or not, they will not believe. What peace is left to make? What reconciliation remains possible? The Messenger of Allah ﷺ was ultimately instructed regarding such people: لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ – "For you is your religion, and for me is my religion." He completely separated from those who persisted in rejection. So why would the hypocrites try to maintain these ties and call it peace?

**The Manifestation of Previous Lies**

This response – إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ – is another lie, a manifestation of all the lies mentioned before. But there's something else notable here: hypocrites have no shame in declaring their self-righteousness. "We're doing very good things." "Actually, I'm very religious too." "We're just trying to be part of society." This kind of self-justifying rhetoric is a telltale sign.

translation: Unquestionably, it is they who are the corrupters, but they perceive [it] not
commentary:
أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدُونَ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ

*Unquestionably, it is they who are the corrupters, but they perceive not.*

أَلَآ (ala) – listen up! Unquestionably! This word demands attention. When you hear أَلَآ in the Quran, your ears should perk up like a rabbit's.

إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدُونَ (innahum humu al-mufsidoon) – they are the ones who are the corrupters. Not just corrupt themselves – notice the difference – but مُفْسِدُونَ (mufsidoon), those who cause corruption. They are not merely afflicted with corruption; they actively spread it. They are not just corrupt individuals; they are the source and root of corruption itself.

وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ (wa laakin la yash'uroon) – however, they have no realization. They lack any acknowledgement that they are the real problem. They perceive themselves as reformers, as peacemakers, as righteous, while in reality they are the very fountainhead of corruption on the earth.

This is the ultimate tragedy of hypocrisy: the inability to see oneself clearly. The disease has progressed so far that they can no longer distinguish between reform and corruption, between sincerity and deception, between guidance and misguidance.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from being of this kind. May He grant us sincere hearts, truthful tongues, and the ability to constantly examine ourselves with honesty. And may He make us of those who fear hypocrisy and seek refuge in Him from it, for that fear itself is a sign of true faith.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Believe as the people have believed," they say, "Should we believe as the foolish have believed?" Unquestionably, it is they who are the foolish, but they know [it] not
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ءَامِنُوا۟ كَمَآ ءَامَنَ ٱلنَّاسُ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ كَمَآ ءَامَنَ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ

*And when it is said to them, "Believe as the people have believed," they say, "Should we believe as the foolish have believed?" Unquestionably, it is they who are the foolish, but they know not.*

In the sequencing of these ayahs, Allah ﷻ first revealed the verdict about what lies in the hearts of the hypocrites: there is a disease. But when they're criticized, it's not for what's in their hearts—because that's impossible for us to judge—but rather for their corrupt actions in the earth. This reinforces a critical principle: hypocrisy as a disease of the heart is not something for us to judge. However, when someone is clearly engaged in corrupt action, it needs to be called out.

The ayah presents an interesting shift in tone. The hypocrites are not being rejected or labeled; they're being invited with words of encouragement: "Believe like everybody else has." The language is positive, almost saying, "Look, everyone is doing it. You can do it too. It's not that hard." The word الناس (an-nas, "people") is deliberately general—not highlighting the best of the Companions (RA) or making it seem like some extraordinary feat. Just people. Regular people.

But their response exposes something far worse than their earlier self-righteousness. Now their hatred and condescending attitude toward the believers becomes visible. They say, "Should we believe like these سُفَهَآء (sufaha, fools) have believed?" They call those who have made genuine sacrifices for Allah ﷻ stupid and unintelligent.

To understand why they use this insulting language, we need to recognize the different contexts of Islam's arrival in Makkah versus Madinah. When people came to Islam in Makkah, they gained nothing worldly. They lost family ties, tribal respect, and social standing. People called them crazy, accused them of being under magic spells or possessed by jinn. They faced verbal and physical assault, boycotts, and eventually had to leave their homes entirely. You got nothing worldly from becoming Muslim in Makkah.

In Madinah, by contrast, the Muslims were a rising force from day one. They had social recognition and power. Islam hadn't been tested by hardship in this new city. Those who came to Islam in Madinah—particularly the hypocrites—saw a movement gaining ground and thought, "We should join the winning team to get our share of the goods."

When told to embrace the faith that requires sacrifice, to look at the established Iman of the Muhajiroun who came from Makkah, they looked at these migrants as fools. "These people left their homes. They had so much going for them. You want us to become homeless like them?" This insulting attitude toward the Muhajiroun appears multiple times in the Quran. In Surah Al-Munafiqun, the hypocrites say, "We will expel the more humiliated ones," and according to some narrations, they even called the Makkans "the dogs from Makkah" because they were homeless and wandering.

Allah ﷻ responds directly: أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ (Ala innahum humu as-sufaha)—"Indeed, they are the fools." Let's connect this to what we've already learned. This story began with one of the implications of believing in the unseen: whatever they've been provided, they spend from it. That spending could not have happened without absolute conviction. What's the greatest example of spending? Giving up your entire house and home, exhausting everything you have for the sake of Allah's Messenger ﷺ and migrating to a new land where you have no stability, nowhere to turn, going only because the Messenger ﷺ commands it. The Muhajiroun are the highest example of people who truly know the truth for what it is.

Allah ﷻ concludes: وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (wa lakin la ya'lamun)—"but they do not know." They have no knowledge of how foolish they've become by using this kind of language. There's something else powerful here: when they insult the Companions (RA) of Allah's Messenger ﷺ, Allah ﷻ Himself responds to defend them. Insulting the Companions (RA) is an offense to Allah ﷻ Himself.

translation: And when they meet those who believe, they say, "We believe"; but when they are alone with their evil ones, they say, "Indeed, we are with you; we were only mockers
commentary:
وَإِذَا لَقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ شَيَـٰطِينِهِمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُسْتَهْزِءُونَ

*And when they meet those who believe, they say, "We believe"; but when they are alone with their evil ones, they say, "Indeed, we are with you; we were only mocking."*

Now Allah ﷻ exposes another dimension of their hypocrisy: their two-faced behavior. In private settings, when someone tells them to be like everyone else, they respond, "You want me to be like those fools? Give up everything? No thanks." But in public settings, when they meet those who truly believe, they declare, "We believe too. We're with you guys."

Then comes the phrase وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ شَيَـٰطِينِهِمْ (wa idha khalaw ila shayateenihim)—"when they are alone with their devils." This can mean when they're by themselves, but it also refers to those they conspire with. Some among the People of the Book, particularly certain Jews and Christians, were conspiring against the Muslims. Allah ﷻ calls them شَيَـٰطِين (shayateen, devils). The word comes from the root that also means to engage in evil activities in secret, and from shaṭana, which means to be overwhelmed with jealousy. These are the overwhelmingly jealous devils.

When the hypocrites return to their conspirators, they say, إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ (inna ma'akum)—"We are with you, no doubt about it. Trust us." The إِنَّا (inna) is for emphasis, for reassurance: "Look guys, we're with you. I'm guaranteeing you. I'm telling you. We're on your side." Then they add, إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُسْتَهْزِءُونَ (innama nahnu mustahzi'un)—"We're just mocking them. We're just humoring them. We're just playing along when we're there. We're just joking to keep face with them."

translation: [But] Allah mocks them and prolongs them in their transgression [while] they wander blindly
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ وَيَمُدُّهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ

*Allah mocks them and prolongs them in their transgression while they wander blindly.*

The response from Allah ﷻ is severe: ٱللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ (Allahu yastahzi'u bihim)—"Allah mocks them." Allah ﷻ is the One who's actually poking fun at them, letting them keep playing their game. This is Allah's punishment for the hypocrite: He gives them more and more opportunities to disobey, extends their leash.

Consider this example: if you put a one-foot leash on a wild dog that's pulling and straining, eventually it gives up. But if you put that same dog in the middle of a field with a 300-foot leash, for 299 feet it feels completely free, running as fast as it can. What happens at foot 300? The chokehold. Is the impact harder at one foot or at 300 feet? At 300 feet, it almost dies—it causes its own destruction.

This is the meaning of وَيَمُدُّهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ (wa yamudduhum fee tughyanihim)—"He extends them in their transgression." Allah ﷻ loosens their leash: "Go ahead. Dig your hole deeper. It'll hurt even more this way." The word طُغْيَان (tughyan) refers to rebellion, to crossing a line out of an urge to disobey. Everyone who crosses the line asking "Why?"—because they can't help themselves, like an untamed stallion. Allah ﷻ says they will keep extending themselves further into arrogant rebellion.

Then there's the word يَعْمَهُونَ (ya'mahun), which comes from fascinating vocabulary. There's عَمَى ('ama) and عَمَه ('amah). العَمَى فِي البَصَر كَالعَمَه فِي البَصِيرَة—'ama with an alif is blindness of the eye, while 'amah with a ha is blindness of insight. There's sight—what you see with your eyes—and insight—what you see with your heart and mind. It's not physical; it's abstract, on the inside. Deeper understanding comes from insight. 'Amah means someone blind of insight, blind of the heart, blind of the mind. They cannot see deeper things; they become shallow.

'Amah also carries the meaning of wandering back and forth, going to and fro without being sure which way to turn. Allah ﷻ says He extends them in their rebellion, and they're so unsure all the time: "Which way should we go?" They have no clarity of direction. People of kufr have very clear clarity and direction. People of genuine Iman have real clarity and direction. But the hypocrites? No clarity. They don't know where to turn.

This furthers an image Allah ﷻ already presented: وَعَلَىٰ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ غِشَـٰوَةٌۭ (wa 'ala absarihim ghishawa)—"And on their eyes is a veil." Now He describes that veil keeping them from seeing the truth, leaving them without any direction, just going around endlessly.

translation: Those are the ones who have purchased error [in exchange] for guidance, so their transaction has brought no profit, nor were they guided
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ فَمَا رَبِحَت تِّجَـٰرَتُهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ

*Those are the ones who have purchased error in exchange for guidance, so their transaction has brought no profit, nor were they guided.*

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ (Ula'ika alladheena ishtarawu ad-dalalata bil-huda)—"Those are the ones who have purchased misguidance in exchange for guidance." They sold off guidance to buy error.

The concept of trade—purchasing, buying, selling—is powerful throughout the Quran. Allah ﷻ uses it to compare this worldly life with the next, to illustrate our priorities. Let's understand the dynamics of this metaphor.

Human beings prefer cash or credit? If you're the seller, you prefer cash, because the rewards are immediate. Someone offers you $20 now for your goods; another person offers $25 but will pay next month. What do you take if you're a good businessperson? Twenty dollars now is worth far more than $25 later. A bird in hand. This is human nature. When we understand trade, we understand the better deal is what you get right away.

Allah ﷻ offers us a deal: "I'll give you Jannah. I'll give you a house. I'll give you everlasting youth. I'll give you the best company." Getting a nice house isn't hard; getting a nice house in a good neighborhood is hard. We know this reality—you can get a nice house in the middle of nowhere dirt cheap. You want that same house in a safe neighborhood, in a real development, around excellent neighbors? That's expensive. So it's not just a nice house being offered to us—it's a nice house with the best possible neighbors, a great neighborhood, complete security. All of it is being offered.

Now or later? This is the question. Is this transaction Allah ﷻ is offering us cash or credit? It's credit. And in credit, there's a level of trust involved. When someone is selling something on credit, don't they have to trust the customer? "I'll take your goods now and pay you back later." You really have to rely on them, which is why dealerships do background checks, ask for pay stubs, all that verification.

Who's offering us this deal of Jannah? Allah ﷻ. That means if someone takes this deal, they've displayed an incredible amount of trust in Allah ﷻ. When you make this kind of deal, you usually have something in writing, some receipt, at least a picture of what's being offered. What do you get from Allah ﷻ? Just His word: "Trust Me. I'll take care of you." 

What do you have to give? Your life and your money. How long? Until you die. When do you get paid? After you die. Once you're being buried in the ground, your payment begins. That's real credit—long-term payment. You don't get paid right away; you get paid way after.

Now Shaytan comes along: "I'm a customer too. I've got a payment plan. If you listen to me, if you give me your life and your money, what will I give you? Rewards right now. Cash. One click away. Right here. Take it. What are you waiting for? Just do it."

Marketing and advertising study what's called "point of sale"—how do you get a customer who visits a webpage or walks into a store to make the purchase right away? Car salesmen don't let you leave the dealership. They'll give out candy, play exciting music: "Wait, before you go, just drive it a little. Just touch it. Just feel it. Take it for a spin." They want you to commit immediately. To make the sale, you have to make whatever it is very tempting, very beautiful.

Human beings naturally have a tendency to go for cash, not credit—to choose immediate gratification over delayed reward. It takes tremendous trust to take the long-term deal and skip out on the immediate one.

This terminology runs throughout the entire Quran. Now it's being applied to the hypocrites. They were given a credit deal, but they took misguidance, which brought them benefits immediately—or so they thought. They gave up guidance, which would have brought benefits in the Hereafter. They were protecting themselves from harm that would have come now while failing to save themselves from harm that would come forever.

فَمَا رَبِحَت تِّجَـٰرَتُهُمْ (fama rabihat tijaratuhum)—"So their transaction brought no profit." There's a difference between نَفَعَ (nafa'a, benefit) and رَبِحَ (rabiha, profit). رَبِحَ actually means استقرّ (istaqarra)—for the deal to go through, to reach conclusion, to materialize. We say "we're going through negotiations; it'll go through in a couple of days." Allah ﷻ says their trade didn't materialize at all. They gave their part; they never received anything on the other end.

وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (wa ma kanu muhtadin)—"And they were not guided at all." The لَام (lam) here is لام التأكيد (lam at-ta'keed)—the lam of emphasis. They gave up that guidance, and they weren't committed to it at all. Allah ﷻ mentions this at the end because the hypocrite pretends to be committed to guidance. Allah ﷻ says no, they're not.

translation: Their example is that of one who kindled a fire, but when it illuminated what was around him, Allah took away their light and left them in darkness [so] they could not see
commentary:
مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَوْقَدَ نَارًا فَلَمَّآ أَضَآءَتْ مَا حَوْلَهُۥ ذَهَبَ ٱللَّهُ بِنُورِهِمْ وَتَرَكَهُمْ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتٍ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ

*Their example is that of one who kindled a fire, but when it illuminated what was around him, Allah took away their light and left them in darkness so they could not see.*

Now Allah ﷻ gives us a parable to illustrate what He's been describing. مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَوْقَدَ نَارًا (mathaluhum kamathali alladhi istawqada nara)—"Their example is like someone who lit a fire." Picture the middle of the night, complete darkness. People are lost. Someone traveling with his family doesn't know where to go. What happens when he sees a fire? Everyone knows where to go when there's fire. Then there's light around you. At least you become aware of your surroundings, where you are yourself.

This is the example of the Messenger ﷺ giving them the light of the Quran. He lit a blaze, and in the middle of darkness, a light is now present. Now you can finally have some access to guidance.

فَلَمَّآ أَضَآءَتْ مَا حَوْلَهُۥ (falamma ada'at ma hawlahu)—"When it illuminated what was around him." Whatever the fire was lighting up in its surroundings—

ذَهَبَ ٱللَّهُ بِنُورِهِمْ (dhahaba Allahu binurihim)—"Allah took away their light." Notice the contrast here: there's ضَوْء (daw', illumination with heat) versus نُور (nur, pure light). You've heard these terms before. أَجْعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِيَآءً وَالْقَمَرَ نُورًا (ja'ala ash-shamsa diya'an wal-qamara nura)—"He made the sun a source of light with heat, and the moon just light." Daw' combines two things: heat and light. Nur is one thing: light itself.

Nur in Arabic is used for several things, including your eyes. It's used for your eyesight. Your eyes sparkle; your eyes reflect light—that's how we're able to see. So the Arabic term for eyes is also nur.

Now there's this hot light around them, but what's taken away? Their own eyesight. What good is a fire if you don't have eyes anymore? ذَهَبَ ٱللَّهُ بِنُورِهِمْ (dhahaba Allahu binurihim)—"Allah took their sight away." In other words, revelation came. They came close to the revelation, but they weren't able to appreciate it. Allah ﷻ took their power of appreciation away, made them blind to it.

This connects to the previous ayah: يَعْمَهُونَ (ya'mahun)—they have no insight left, no vision left. Now a practical example is being given, which leads to another critically important concept in the Quran about guidance and misguidance.

When Allah ﷻ offers guidance, a good human being listens and says, "I should take this seriously." But imagine they don't. Imagine they heard something good and didn't take it seriously. Our human body is made up of muscles. If you don't exercise a muscle, what happens? It gets weaker and weaker until it atrophies and dies. People who are bedridden for six, eight, nine months or even years—they don't move their legs. Even though the legs were fine, because they don't move them for that long, they lose them. If you keep your eyes closed for six or seven months straight, you'll go blind. You lose it.

Allah ﷻ gave us that muscle, that ability to accept guidance. When you don't take advantage of it once, then a second time, a third time, a fourth time—every time you deny that opportunity, your ability to accept guidance gets weaker and weaker, until your light is altogether gone.

وَتَرَكَهُمْ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتٍ (wa tarakahum fee dhulumatin)—"And He left them in darkness"—but notice it's plural: ظُلُمَـٰت (dhulumat, darknesses). It's not like they got one problem; they've got multiple problems. They've got hypocrisy. They've got fear of creation. They've got greed, anger, jealousy, materialism—darkness upon darkness upon darkness.

لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ (la yubsirun)—"They cannot see." This completes the image. لَا يُبْصِرُونَ truly implies لَا بَصِيرَةَ لَهُمْ (la baseera lahum)—they have no insight. They can't see physically, and they don't have insight anymore. They're no longer able to think about guidance at all.

translation: Deaf, dumb and blind - so they will not return [to the right path]
commentary:
صُمٌّۢ بُكْمٌ عُمْىٌ فَهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ

The condition of the hypocrites deteriorates dramatically in this ayah. Allah ﷻ describes them as **صُمٌّ** (deaf), **بُكْمٌ** (mute), and **عُمْىٌ** (blind). Consider the severity of this situation: imagine being lost in the middle of the desert at night. Your light has gone out completely, leaving you in total darkness. 

If you were merely blind, at least you could hear someone calling out to help you. But these people are deaf—they cannot hear anyone trying to guide them. If you were deaf and blind but could speak, at least you could cry out so others might find you. But they are mute—they cannot even call for help. Their ability to ask for guidance has been completely stripped away.

Allah ﷻ took their light away in the previous example, which means they have no illumination around them whatsoever. In the darkness, they cannot see, but more than that—they cannot even be seen by others. They are in a state where they cannot hear and cannot be heard, cannot see and cannot be seen. They are completely abandoned.

This connects directly to the earlier statement: **فَتَرَكَهُمْ فِي ظُلُمَاتٍ** (He abandoned them in darkness). The final devastating reality comes in **فَهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ** (they will not return). 

The question arises: return to what? When the fire was lit, they were right there beside it. Then their light was taken away and they wandered off into darkness. The return mentioned here is a return to the Messenger ﷺ and the Quran—they will not be able to find their way back. 

Later in this same surah, Allah ﷻ describes how He brings people **مِنَ الظُّلُمَاتِ إِلَى النُّورِ** (out of darkness into light). This ayah presents the reverse tragedy: these people were in the light but went into darkness, and now they do not deserve to come back.

translation: Or [it is] like a rainstorm from the sky within which is darkness, thunder and lightning. They put their fingers in their ears against the thunderclaps in dread of death. But Allah is encompassing of the disbelievers
commentary:
أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فِيهِ ظُلُمَـٰتٌ وَرَعْدٌ وَبَرْقٌ يَجْعَلُونَ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِم مِّنَ ٱلصَّوَٰعِقِ حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ مُحِيطٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ presents a second parable that should be carefully compared with the first. The word **صَيِّب** refers to intensely heavy rain falling from the sky. While the previous example highlighted one aspect of revelation, this parable reveals another dimension.

In the cold desert night, fire provides not just light but also comforting warmth. The first parable showed revelation as something gentle and comforting—a source of guidance that also brings psychological and spiritual warmth to the heart. But this second parable reveals the other side of revelation: it can also be intense, powerful and even frightening. The Quran contains both aspects—it comforts the believers while warning the deniers with severity.

The scholars explain that this heavy rain, along with thunder and lightning, represents the intense and frightening aspects of revelation—particularly its warnings about the consequences of disbelief. These hypocrites find themselves caught in the middle of these powerful warnings.

**يَجْعَلُونَ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِم مِّنَ ٱلصَّوَٰعِقِ** (They stick their fingers in their ears because of the thunderbolts). The word **صَاعِقَة** (sa'iqah) refers specifically to explosive, deafening thunder—not just a rolling sound, but the kind of sharp, crackling explosion that wakes children from sleep and makes them cry. It is thunder so loud and frightening that a person instinctively covers their ears.

Picture the scene: caught outside in darkness, heavy rain pouring down, and thunder exploding around you so violently that you fear for your life. **حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ** (in fear of death)—the word **حَذَر** implies extreme caution, like someone carefully watching every step, afraid of being attacked at any moment.

What does this represent? When the hypocrite hears the warnings of the Quran about punishment and accountability, something in his heart tells him it is true. But he finds it too depressing, too uncomfortable to accept. So he blocks it out. You are sitting with him and begin to recite or discuss verses about the Day of Judgment. "Can you change the subject? Can we listen to something else? This is too depressing." He metaphorically sticks his fingers in his ears.

Notice the illogical nature of this response. If lightning is about to strike you, will putting your fingers in your ears protect you from death? Of course not. This reveals that these people have not only abandoned the means of perception—hearing and seeing—but they have lost their intellect as well. Their response to truth has become completely irrational.

**وَٱللَّهُ مُحِيطٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ** (And Allah has completely encircled the disbelievers). They cannot escape. Notice that Allah ﷻ calls them **كَافِرِين** (disbelievers) here, though they appear outwardly as Muslims. This is because Allah ﷻ judges what is in the heart, and if there is no true conviction inside, there can only be kufr. There is nothing in between. On the outside, we may call them hypocrites, but on the inside, Allah ﷻ knows them as disbelievers. On the Day of Judgment, they will not be raised as believers—they will be raised with the disbelievers.

Interestingly, this parable connects to a later passage in the Quran where someone will ask on Judgment Day: **رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنتُ بَصِيرًا** (My Lord, why have You raised me blind when I used to see?). The response comes: **كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ آيَاتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمَ تُنسَىٰ** (Just as Our signs came to you and you ignored them, so today you are forgotten). Here in this parable, they are left abandoned in the desert. On Judgment Day, they are raised blind and forgotten—a terrifying continuity between this world and the next.

translation: The lightning almost snatches away their sight. Every time it lights [the way] for them, they walk therein; but when darkness comes over them, they stand [still]. And if Allah had willed, He could have taken away their hearing and their sight. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
يَكَادُ ٱلْبَرْقُ يَخْطَفُ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أَضَآءَ لَهُم مَّشَوْا۟ فِيهِ وَإِذَآ أَظْلَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ قَامُوا۟ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَذَهَبَ بِسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

**يَكَادُ ٱلْبَرْقُ يَخْطَفُ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ** (The lightning almost snatches away their sight). After the deafness caused by covering their ears against the thunder, now another kind of blindness is mentioned. The lightning is so brilliant and intense that it nearly takes away their eyesight completely. This represents how the light of revelation can be so strong and clear that those who reject it become blinded by its very clarity.

The previous example showed them refusing to listen by sticking their fingers in their ears. Now we see the visual counterpart—they are overwhelmed by light itself. These are two different expressions of their rejection: refusing to hear the truth and being unable to face its illuminating power.

**كُلَّمَآ أَضَآءَ لَهُم مَّشَوْا۟ فِيهِ** (Every time it lights up for them, they walk in it). This phrase can also be understood as "whenever it becomes easy for them." This reveals something crucial about the hypocrites: it is not that they never follow revelation at all. Rather, they follow it when it is convenient, when it benefits them, when the conditions are favorable.

**وَإِذَآ أَظْلَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ قَامُوا۟** (And when darkness falls upon them, they stand still). The moment following the truth becomes difficult or inconvenient, they stop completely. They cannot commit to the path consistently—they are fair-weather believers who only practice when circumstances make it easy.

This is remarkably different from the first parable, where their sight and hearing were completely taken away. Here, at least they are walking a little, even if inconsistently. This subtle difference carries profound meaning.

**وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَذَهَبَ بِسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ** (And if Allah had willed, He could have taken away their hearing and their sight). Notice the difference in tone. In the previous parable, Allah ﷻ actually took these faculties away. Here, He says only that He could have taken them away—but He has not done so yet. 

This carries an implication of hope. Perhaps these individuals have not fully committed themselves to revelation as they should, but that does not mean it is too late for them. There remains a possibility that they can still return, that they can still get their act together and commit themselves properly. This is why Allah ﷻ uses the conditional form rather than stating it as an accomplished fact.

**إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** (Indeed, Allah is capable of all things). This is both a warning and a reminder—He has complete power to remove or restore these faculties, to guide or to leave astray. The choice that remains for these people is urgent, because His power is absolute.

translation: O mankind, worship your Lord, who created you and those before you, that you may become righteous
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ

After the intense parables about the hypocrites, Allah ﷻ now issues a universal call: **يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ** (O humanity!). This address is not limited to believers or to Arabs—it encompasses all of mankind.

**ٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ** (Enslave yourselves to your Master). The word **اعْبُدُوا** is profound. It comes from the root **عِبَادَة** ('ibadah), which means both worship and complete servitude. The command form used here connects to two different meanings: to worship your Lord, and to become slaves of your Lord. These are distinct concepts that many people miss.

Many people are willing to worship Allah ﷻ, but they do not accept Him as their complete Master. They do not fully enslave themselves to Him. True 'ibadah requires both dimensions—you worship Him and you accept that you are His slave in every aspect of your life. There is no such thing as part-time slavery. No one in history has ever been a slave only on weekends. Once you are a slave, it is a complete commitment that encompasses your entire existence.

Why does this ayah appear here, immediately after the parables of the hypocrites? Because the previous ayahs described people who pretended to enter into this slavery but only did so partially, conditionally, when convenient. Now, at the end of these descriptions, the call is made again from the beginning: let us start over. Enter into complete servitude.

**ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ** (The One who created you and those who came before you). This description of Allah ﷻ as Creator establishes the fundamental basis for why He deserves our complete submission. He is not just any master—He is the Master who brought you into existence, who gave you life itself.

**لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** (so that you may become people of taqwa). This brings us full circle. Remember the two parables—in both, people attempted to protect themselves. In the first, someone was left blind, deaf and mute in the desert, unable to find safety. In the second, people were surrounded by a violent storm, desperately trying to shield themselves from death. Both scenarios showed failed attempts at self-protection.

Allah ﷻ is saying: you truly want to protect yourself? Here is how—enslave yourself completely to your Master. This is the only real protection, the only genuine safety. The surah began with guidance for **الْمُتَّقِينَ** (those who have taqwa), and now we are told how to actually become from among them.

translation: [He] who made for you the earth a bed [spread out] and the sky a ceiling and sent down from the sky, rain and brought forth thereby fruits as provision for you. So do not attribute to Allah equals while you know [that there is nothing similar to Him]
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِرَٰشًا وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ continues describing Himself to explain why He alone deserves our worship and submission. **ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِرَٰشًا** (The One who made the earth a resting place for you). The word **فِرَاش** (firash) refers to something that has been spread out, laid down, made level and comfortable—like a carpet or bedding.
