The implication is powerful: Allah ﷻ did not make the earth unstable, rough or painful to walk upon. He humbled it and smoothed it for our benefit. Every step we take on this earth should remind us of His mercy and provision.

**وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً** (and the sky as a ceiling/structure). Now understand how this connects to the parables. The first parable described trouble on the earth—people abandoned on the ground with no light. The second parable described trouble from the sky—the storm, the thunder, the lightning coming down. But Allah ﷻ says: originally, if you had truly been My slaves, the earth was meant to be comfortable for you and the sky was meant to be protection over you, like the floor and ceiling of your home where you feel completely safe.

**وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً** (And He sent down water from the sky). Water was coming down in the second parable too, but there it came as part of destruction—heavy rain in the storm that threatened them. Here, Allah ﷻ reminds us that originally, water from the sky was meant as pure mercy.

**فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ** (Then He extracted with it from the fruits as provision for you). The earth and the sky work together in perfect harmony to provide your sustenance. The water from above nourishes the earth below, and from this blessed union comes the fruit that keeps you alive.

**فَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا** (So do not set up rivals to Allah). After describing His unique favors, the command comes: do not give anyone or anything else the status that belongs to Him alone. The word **أَنْدَاد** (andad) refers to competitors, equals, rivals—anything that competes with Allah ﷻ for your devotion, obedience and priority.

These rivals can be external idols made of stone, but they can also be internal idols—your ego, your desires, your greed, your pride. Any time you prioritize something over what Allah ﷻ wants from you, you have made it a competitor to Him.

**وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** (while you know). This is a devastating conclusion. The issue is not that you lack knowledge or evidence. Deep inside, you know the truth. Your intellect recognizes it. The problem is not intellectual—it is that you are allowing your desires, emotions and temptations to override what you know to be true.

This connects to an important principle: the lack of iman in a person's heart stems from two types of problems. First, there are emotional and psychological barriers—jealousy, ego, desires, temptations. Second, there are intellectual barriers—genuine doubt or lack of conviction about the truth. Allah ﷻ addresses the intellectual side here by saying **وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ**—you do know. Your problem is not a lack of knowledge. You are letting your emotions and temptations take you away from what your own intellect confirms.

translation: And if you are in doubt about what We have sent down upon Our Servant [Muhammad], then produce a surah the like thereof and call upon your witnesses other than Allah, if you should be truthful
commentary:
وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

The surah began with **لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ** (There is no doubt in it)—a definitive statement that this Book contains no doubt whatsoever. But now Allah ﷻ says **وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ** (And if you are in doubt). This is a conditional statement addressing those who claim to have doubts.

The previous ayah said **وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** (you know). So Allah ﷻ is saying: You do know the truth. But if it is the case that you claim to be in doubt—if you insist that you are uncertain—then here is a challenge for you.

**مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا** (about what We have sent down upon Our slave). Notice the profound honor given to the Prophet ﷺ here. Allah ﷻ does not mention him by name. He says **عَبْدِنَا** (Our slave)—emphasizing that the highest status a human being can achieve is to be a slave of Allah ﷻ. This is not a title of humiliation, but of ultimate honor.

**فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ** (Then bring a surah like it). The challenge is specific: produce even one surah—the shortest chapter—that comes anywhere close to this Quran. The phrase **مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ** means "anything like it" or "anything remotely similar to it."

It is crucial to understand when this challenge appears in the revelation. This is Madani Quran, revealed years after the Prophet's ﷺ mission began in Makkah. In the Makkan period, the primary call was to wake up the human conscience, to recognize the purpose of life, to see evil for what it truly is, to acknowledge the Creator. The intellectual challenge to produce something like the Quran comes later, after people have had ample opportunity to reflect and after they have become completely obstinate in their rejection.

With deep grief, we must acknowledge that many Muslims today misunderstand this challenge. We approach da'wah by immediately confronting people with "Produce a surah like it!" as if this is the first argument we should make. But Allah ﷻ did not structure His revelation this way. This challenge appears after prolonged exposure to the message, after people have had every opportunity to recognize truth through their own conscience and intellect.

When this challenge finally comes, it is actually a curse of sorts—Allah ﷻ is essentially inviting them to seal their own fate. If you insist on competing with what Allah ﷻ has revealed, go ahead. Produce your own revelation. But by attempting to do so, you demonstrate the absolute limits of human ability compared to divine speech.

**وَٱدْعُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ** (And call upon your witnesses other than Allah). The word **شُهَدَاء** (shuhada') means witnesses—those who testify. This is deeply ironic, because originally your own conscience was supposed to testify to the truth. But since you have killed your conscience, since you have silenced that inner voice that knows right from wrong, now Allah ﷻ says: go find some other witnesses. Bring whatever external support you think you have.

**إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ** (if you are truthful). The phrase **شَهَادَة** (testimony) and **صِدْق** (truthfulness) appear together in this ayah. True testimony is rooted in truth. If you claim to be truthful in your doubts, if you claim to have your own version of truth that contradicts what Allah ﷻ has revealed, then prove it. Bring your evidence. Produce something comparable.

translation: But if you do not - and you will never be able to - then fear the Fire, whose fuel is men and stones, prepared for the disbelievers
commentary:
فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَلَن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ ۖ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

**فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟** (Then if you do not do so). This addresses their failure up to the present moment. From the time this challenge was issued until now, no one has been able to meet it.

**وَلَن تَفْعَلُوا۟** (and you will not do so). This is a prophecy—a statement about the future. Allah ﷻ is declaring with absolute certainty that they will never be able to produce anything like the Quran. Not now, not tomorrow, not in a thousand years. This is both a challenge and a miracle of prophecy itself.

Notice the two-part structure: first, acknowledging that they have not done so thus far, and second, declaring that they will never be able to do so in the future. This completely closes the door on any possibility of success in their challenge.

**فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ** (then protect yourselves from the Fire). Earlier in the passage, the word **تَقْوَىٰ** (taqwa) appeared by itself: **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** (so that you may have taqwa). That usage was open-ended—you could protect yourself from disappointment, from punishment, from losing your decency, from any number of spiritual and moral harms.

But now, after the challenge has been issued and their failure declared, the object of taqwa is specified in the starkest terms: protect yourselves from the Fire. These people have descended to the lowest level. They are at rock bottom, and now the only protection that matters is protection from eternal punishment. This is the minimum standard, the last warning.

**ٱلَّتِى وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ** (whose fuel is people and stones). The word **وَقُود** (waqud) means fuel—what keeps a fire burning. This Fire is not fueled by ordinary wood or coal. Its fuel consists of people and stones.

The stones mentioned here carry multiple meanings. First, they refer to the idols that people worshipped—made of stone, now burning together with their worshippers. The very objects of their devotion become instruments of their punishment. Second, when stones burn, they produce an even more intense heat than wood—they create lava, the hardest and most devastating kind of flame. This intensifies the horror of this Fire.

There is also a profound connection to a later passage in this surah, where Allah ﷻ describes how people's hearts became **قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ فَهِيَ كَالْحِجَارَةِ** (hard like stones). Those hearts that became as hard as stone in this world will burn like stone in the next. The spiritual hardness they cultivated becomes their literal fuel in the Hellfire.

**أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ** (prepared for the disbelievers). The word **أُعِدَّت** means carefully prepared, made ready. This Fire has been specifically created and prepared for those who disbelieve, for those who are ungrateful. It is not a random punishment—it is a precisely designed consequence for those who rejected the clearest signs and the most obvious truth.

The progression in these ayahs is devastatingly clear: You claim to have doubts? Prove your truth. You cannot prove it and you never will? Then protect yourself from the Fire whose fuel you yourself may become. This is the final warning, the ultimate call to return before it is too late.

translation: And give good tidings to those who believe and do righteous deeds that they will have gardens [in Paradise] beneath which rivers flow. Whenever they are provided with a provision of fruit therefrom, they will say, "This is what we were provided with before." And it is given to them in likeness. And they will have therein purified spouses, and they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِن ثَمَرَةٍ رِّزْقًا ۙ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى رُزِقْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَأُتُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَهُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

The Messenger ﷺ is commanded to deliver glad tidings to those who believed and performed righteous deeds. The word بَشِّرِ (bashshir) carries the meaning of congratulations for an exceptional transaction—as though they've made the deal of a lifetime. Allah ﷻ tells them they will have جَنَّـٰتٍ (jannat), gardens in the plural, multiple gardens awaiting them.

Consider the human fascination with finding the perfect home. Whether searching online or touring properties with an agent, people examine every detail—the backyard, the neighborhood, whether there's a pool or waterfront view. Young families saving for their first home will browse listings years in advance, saving pictures and imagining their future. We even create entire television shows dedicated to this obsession with beautiful homes. Allah ﷻ is telling the believers: you have secured the most magnificent residence imaginable.

These gardens have rivers flowing beneath them—تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ. Picture homes built atop mountains with waterfalls cascading from underneath, or perhaps floors of glass revealing flowing streams below like an aquarium house. Notice the parallel structure in the Quran: earlier, Allah ﷻ mentioned sending rain from the sky to produce fruits on earth. Now in Jannah, He mentions rivers flowing, and immediately after—fruits again.

كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِن ثَمَرَةٍ رِّزْقًا—whenever they are provided with fruits, their response will be fascinating. They'll say, "This is what we were given before!" The fruits will appear remarkably similar to what they knew in the worldly life. You might pick up what looks like a familiar banana or apple. But here's what's unspoken: it only appears similar. When you taste it, the experience transcends anything you knew before. 

The phrase وَأُتُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا (brought to them in resemblance) contains deeper layers. Not only do the fruits resemble earthly ones, but each bite of the same fruit offers a new experience. You bite into an apple, and it's extraordinary. The second bite isn't identical—it's better. The third bite surpasses the second. Unlike the tasteless, genetically modified produce we've grown accustomed to—which now requires labels like "organic" to indicate what should be natural—the fruits of Jannah offer perpetual renewal and intensification of pleasure.

We've developed tremendous technology, yet ultimately discover that the best things were what Allah ﷻ created in the first place. We simply return to the original.

وَلَهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ—and for them therein are purified spouses. The word مُّطَهَّرَةٌ (mutahharah) means purified in multiple dimensions: physically clean, spiritually clean, and perfectly suited to you. In this world, even in the best marriages, you might appreciate most qualities in your spouse, but there's always something you must simply live with. Perhaps in the early months everything seems perfect, but as life continues, you recognize certain realities. In Jannah, everything about your spouse will be exactly as you desire, and for believing spouses from this life who are reunited there, all the difficulties that once existed between them will be removed. They'll be perfect for each other.

You know those rare, peaceful moments after conflict when couples experience genuine harmony and wonder, "Why can't we be like this all the time?" That calm never lasts—often not even until the end of that very conversation. وَهُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ—they will remain in this state permanently. That perfect state of contentment, uninterrupted, eternal.

translation: Indeed, Allah is not timid to present an example - that of a mosquito or what is smaller than it. And those who have believed know that it is the truth from their Lord. But as for those who disbelieve, they say, "What did Allah intend by this as an example?" He misleads many thereby and guides many thereby. And He misleads not except the defiantly disobedient
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَسْتَحْىِۦٓ أَن يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا مَّا بَعُوضَةً فَمَا فَوْقَهَا ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَيَقُولُونَ مَاذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا مَثَلًا ۘ يُضِلُّ بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا وَيَهْدِى بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا ۚ وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

Allah ﷻ has already given us examples in the previous passages. Now He declares: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَسْتَحْىِۦٓ—certainly Allah is not embarrassed at all—أَن يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا مَّا—to strike any example whatsoever—بَعُوضَةً فَمَا فَوْقَهَا—be it a mosquito or even something above it, whether something more insignificant or something sitting atop a mosquito.

This contains a profound lesson about the nature of divine teaching. A teacher's purpose is not to impress students with extensive knowledge, but to ensure understanding. A preschool teacher will stand on their head, juggle balls, make funny faces—whatever it takes to reach the students. You judge a speaker's intelligence by their speech, but can you judge a preschool teacher's intelligence by how they speak to children? They may know vastly more than they reveal, but they communicate at the child's level.

The Quran is the perfect speech of Allah ﷻ, yet fundamentally it serves as teaching. This represents a glimpse of His infinite wisdom, simplified so that we—limited human beings—can comprehend. He began the Surah by establishing this principle: الم (Alif Lam Mim), ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ—He knows infinitely more, but He gives us what we can handle. When someone offers examples to aid understanding, critics might say, "Look at these simplistic examples—couldn't He think of something more sophisticated?" But He gives these examples not because they benefit Him, but because they benefit us.

Human beings should be deeply grateful that Allah ﷻ has given us this Quran in language and forms we can process. Beyond that, He declares elsewhere, وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ—"We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance." Generally, the more knowledgeable someone becomes, the more complex their speech. Advanced courses in philosophy, law, or microbiology can become completely inaccessible. Yet Allah ﷻ, who knows everything, has clarified and simplified to a degree where we can understand—this is tremendous mercy.

This teaches us something essential about teaching itself. If you become an instructor, you shouldn't feel inhibited or concerned with impressing students. Your role is to teach. There's a significant difference between a scholar and a teacher. A scholar may speak extensively, but audiences won't grasp it. A teacher descends to the people's level, making even calculus feel like elementary mathematics. A good teacher is measured by how accessible they can make their subject, how much they can simplify so people can process it.

Allah ﷻ gave us the Quran so we can learn—وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ ۖ وَمَا يَعْقِلُهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْعَـٰلِمُونَ—"We present these examples to humanity, but none will understand them except those who have knowledge." Those with even a little knowledge can appreciate what Allah ﷻ is conveying.

With this foundation, Allah ﷻ addresses two contrasting responses to His examples.

فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ—As for those who have iman, they know this is the truth from their Master. One of the most essential attitudes for successfully learning the Quran is intellectual humility. You must accept that Allah ﷻ knows and you don't. Sometimes you'll encounter examples you don't immediately understand. You'll study and reflect, and ultimately you may have to say Allahu A'lam (Allah knows best), while maintaining certainty that whatever it is, it is truth from your Master.

وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَيَقُولُونَ مَاذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا مَثَلًا—And as for those who disbelieve, they say, "What did Allah intend by this as an example?" Notice the precision here. This isn't asking, "What does this example mean?"—which would be a legitimate question from a sincere seeker. Rather, they're saying, "What did He intend by giving this as an example?"—criticizing and being condescending toward the very concept of using such examples. This highlights their arrogant attitude when approaching this Book.

The response comes: يُضِلُّ بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا—He misguides by means of it many. The "it" could refer to the entire Quran or to the specific examples. وَيَهْدِى بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا—and He guides by means of it many. The same text that provides guidance for some becomes a means of increased misguidance for others.

Some will read the Quran and find reasons to intensify their hatred. They'll quote it selectively, produce critical content, and spread it widely. The more they study it with corrupt intentions, the more their disbelief increases. Allah ﷻ clarifies: وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ—He doesn't misguide anyone by it except the fasiqeen, the inherently corrupt.

The word فَـٰسِقِينَ (fasiqeen) comes from the root meaning to cross a boundary improperly, like a rat leaving its hole to go where it shouldn't. This describes someone who crosses a line they're not supposed to cross. There's a line in attitude you must not cross toward the Quran. When you cross it with arrogance and criticism, you no longer receive guidance. The criticism of the Quran itself—feeling intellectually capable of critiquing it—is the antithesis of faith and guarantees misguidance.

translation: Who break the covenant of Allah after contracting it and sever that which Allah has ordered to be joined and cause corruption on earth. It is those who are the losers
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

Allah ﷻ now defines who these fasiqeen are through three characteristics.

ٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ—Those who violate the covenant of Allah after its confirmation. The word مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ (meethaq) indicates this covenant was established long ago. What promise is this? Allah ﷻ describes taking a covenant with us at the very beginning, when we were created and asked, "Am I not your Lord?" and we all testified, "Yes, we bear witness." When you violate that promise—when someone who testified that He is their Master then questions the Master—they break that contract. A slave by definition is humble and doesn't question the master. Questioning the examples in the Quran is questioning the Master, thereby violating that primordial covenant.

وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ—And they cut apart what Allah commanded to be joined together. This is a profound statement with multiple manifestations.

Allah ﷻ placed our body and soul together. There are needs of the body and needs of the soul—they should remain in harmony. Some focus on spirituality at the expense of physical needs; others do the opposite. They're meant to stay together.

Our religion balances individual and communal concern. Some become entirely individualistic, others entirely collectivist. Both dimensions should coexist.

Some say, "I take the Quran seriously, but the Sunnah isn't that important." The Quran and Sunnah are meant to stay together. Allah ﷻ commands us to believe in all the prophets and messengers—لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِۦ—"We make no distinction between any of His messengers." Yet some people accept certain messengers and reject others, cutting apart what should remain together.

Similarly, some take portions of the Book seriously while disregarding others—تُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ—dividing what Allah ﷻ intended as a unified whole.

One of the most accessible examples is family ties. Allah ﷻ commanded that we maintain relationships with kin—brothers, sisters, spouses, children, parents, extended family. You may not like certain relatives, but the command remains to preserve these bonds. When families break apart, corruption naturally follows.

Examine neighborhoods with the highest crime rates—you'll consistently find the most broken families. When what's supposed to stay together falls apart, all kinds of societal corruption emerge. Consider economic systems that emphasize individual concern at the expense of collective responsibility. When that balance breaks, you see rising rates of suicide, heart disease, and social disintegration despite material prosperity and abundant places of worship. A society falls apart when foundational bonds are severed.

وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—And they cause corruption in the earth. This is the inevitable result of cutting apart what Allah ﷻ commanded to keep together. When natural, divinely ordained connections are broken, corruption spreads.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ—Those are the losers. After describing the winners who earn Jannah, Allah ﷻ now identifies the losers: those who violate His covenant, sever what He commanded to join, and spread corruption. They are the ultimate losers, having exchanged eternal success for temporary rebellion.

translation: How can you disbelieve in Allah when you were lifeless and He brought you to life; then He will cause you to die, then He will bring you [back] to life, and then to Him you will be returned
commentary:
كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَٰتًا فَأَحْيَـٰكُمْ ۖ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ

"How could you disbelieve in Allah ﷻ—and how could you be ungrateful to Him—while all of you were dead, and then He gave you life?"

This question carries both dimensions of meaning: how can you reject faith, and how can you be ungrateful? We were nonexistent, essentially dead, and Allah ﷻ brought us into being. This connects directly to the earlier discussion about breaking covenants "min ba'di mithaqihi" (after it has been taken). We were nothing, Allah ﷻ brought us to life, and in doing so, He took a covenant from us.

Then comes the progression: "thumma yumitukum" (then He will give you death), "thumma yuhyikum" (then He will bring you back to life), "thumma ilayhi turja'un" (and then to Him you will all return). Some scholars interpret this as referring to our original nonexistence, our life in this world, our death, and our resurrection in the next. This cycle of existence itself should inspire both faith and gratitude—how can we be ungrateful when we recognize that every stage of our existence comes from His mercy?

translation: It is He who created for you all of that which is on the earth. Then He directed Himself to the heaven, [His being above all creation], and made them seven heavens, and He is Knowing of all things
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَسَوَّىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ ۚ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

"He is the One who created for you whatever is on the earth, all of it together."

The theme of gratitude continues and deepens. The word "jami'an" (all together) carries two significant meanings. First, everything on earth was created for all of us—all of humanity. This establishes a profound humanitarian principle: the resources of this world are meant to be shared by all people. No single nation or group has the right to hoard or manipulate these resources at the expense of others. The earth's blessings are for universal benefit.

Second, "jami'an" can refer to all the things created on earth—every resource, every blessing, comprehensively placed here for our benefit.

"Thumma istawa ila as-sama'" (Then He rose to the sky). "Fa-sawwahunna sab'a samawat" (And He fashioned them as seven heavens). "Wa huwa bikulli shay'in 'alim" (And He is fully knowledgeable of everything).

This ayah addresses a dangerous misconception that has plagued human societies throughout history: the idea that religion deals only with otherworldly matters while worldly affairs are beyond its scope. Even today, this attitude persists—the notion that religious people don't understand "how the world really works," that they're disconnected from reality, only concerned with Paradise and Hellfire.

Allah ﷻ demolishes this false dichotomy. When He teaches us this religion, He begins by reminding us that He created everything on this earth for us. Then He rose to the sky and established the heavens. And He is fully knowledgeable of everything—not just spiritual matters, but all things. The Muslim doesn't have the luxury of separating religion from worldly knowledge. Islam doesn't deal only with spiritual concerns while leaving worldly matters to secular wisdom. The deen encompasses everything.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when your Lord said to the angels, "Indeed, I will make upon the earth a successive authority." They said, "Will You place upon it one who causes corruption therein and sheds blood, while we declare Your praise and sanctify You?" Allah said, "Indeed, I know that which you do not know
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى جَاعِلٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ ٱلدِّمَآءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

Now we reach the profound narrative of humanity's origins. Allah ﷻ has described three categories of people in the opening passages of Surah Al-Baqarah: the believers (al-muttaqun), those who reject guidance (alladhina kafaru), and the hypocrites (al-munafiqun). These three groups represent all of humanity. But all these people—despite their differences—are children of the same father. This conflict between guidance and misguidance is not new. It began at the very dawn of human history, even before humans set foot on earth.

"Wa idh qala rabbuka lil-mala'ikah" (And when your Master said to the angels). "Inni ja'ilun fil-ardi khalifah" (Indeed, I am placing on the earth a khalifah).

The word "khalifah" deserves careful attention. In Arabic, it means to come behind someone, to follow after another. A khalifah is one who comes after, again and again and again—generation following generation. So the primary meaning here is that Allah ﷻ is placing on earth a being who will have generation after generation after generation, each one leaving successors behind.

The modern political usage of "khalifah" actually derives from this original meaning. After the Prophet ﷺ passed away, those who led the community were called khulafa' because they came after the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. They were the ones left behind to carry on his mission. But the original meaning in this ayah is not political—it refers to the continuous succession of human generations.

The angels responded: "Ataj'alu fiha man yufsidu fiha wa yasfiku ad-dima'" (Are You going to place in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood?). Notice the intensity of the language. "Yasfiku ad-dima'" doesn't just mean to shed blood—the word "yasfiku" implies pouring out rivers of blood, mass carnage, genocide, wars of devastating scale.

"Wa nahnu nusabbihu bihamdika wa nuqaddisu lak" (While we declare Your perfection, praising You, and we sanctify You). The angels are making a contrast. They're saying: this creature You're creating will commit these terrible acts, yet You are too sanctified, too perfect for Your creation to behave this way. And we're already here, glorifying You perfectly.

But there's something deeper in their words. The act of tasbeeh—declaring Allah's ﷻ perfection—is actually the antithesis of causing corruption on earth. When we truly engage in tasbeeh of Allah ﷻ, it's not merely words on our tongues. True tasbeeh means recognizing Allah's ﷻ perfection in such a way that it transforms our character and how we carry ourselves in society.

Today, when we think of dhikr, we often think of what we do with our prayer beads or our tongues after Salah. But tasbeeh is far more comprehensive. If real tasbeeh is being done—if the declaration of Allah's ﷻ perfection is genuine—its impact extends beyond the individual in the masjid. It begins to bring goodness to society itself. Real obedience and real declaration of Allah's ﷻ perfection should prevent the very corruption and bloodshed the angels mentioned.

Allah ﷻ responded: "Inni a'lamu ma la ta'lamun" (Indeed, I know what you do not know).

What does Allah ﷻ know that the angels don't? The angels raised a legitimate concern—one that echoes the earlier description of "alladhina yanquduna 'ahd Allah" (those who violate the covenant of Allah ﷻ) after it has been taken. The violation of that covenant manifests as corruption on earth and the shedding of blood. This wasn't a new concern; even the angels saw this potential in humanity before we even arrived on earth.

Yet Allah ﷻ says: I know what you don't know. Some scholars interpret this to mean that Allah ﷻ knows the true potential of the human being—a potential so great that even angels will one day send salawat upon a human being, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Human beings will reach such heights of nobility that they will be honored above the angels themselves.

Others interpret Allah's ﷻ words as a subtle correction to the angels: You think you're so perfect? Actually, there's someone among you who isn't pure. You don't even realize who he is yet—referring to Iblis, who was present among the angels and would soon reveal his true nature.

translation: And He taught Adam the names - all of them. Then He showed them to the angels and said, "Inform Me of the names of these, if you are truthful
commentary:
وَعَلَّمَ ءَادَمَ ٱلْأَسْمَآءَ كُلَّهَا ثُمَّ عَرَضَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ فَقَالَ أَنۢبِـُٔونِى بِأَسْمَآءِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

"And He taught Adam (AS) the names, all of them."

This is our gateway into understanding the origins of human knowledge, particularly language and terminology. Allah ﷻ taught Adam (AS) "al-asma'"—the names. Names form the foundation of language. All language begins with nouns—names of things. Then, by manipulating these nouns, we develop verbs and more complex linguistic structures. Allah ﷻ literally taught Adam (AS) to name things, and this naming ability became the foundation of all human language.

But notice the shift in pronouns: "al-asma' kullaha" (all of them—using the feminine plural "ha"), but then "thumma 'aradahum" (then He presented them—using the masculine plural "hum"). When referring to names alone, Arabic uses the feminine form. But when Allah ﷻ brought these things before the angels, He switched to the masculine plural that encompasses all categories—living beings, non-living objects, every type of creation.

Things were shown to Adam (AS), and then all of these things together were brought before the angels. "Anbi'uni bi-asma'i ha'ula'" (Tell Me the names of these). Again, note "ha'ula'"—these. If these were only inanimate objects, Arabic would use "hadhihi." But the use of "ha'ula'" indicates that the things presented to the angels had magnitude, including both animate and inanimate creation.

"In kuntum sadiqin" (If you are indeed truthful). If your claim holds weight—if what you said about this creature causing bloodshed has any merit—then demonstrate your knowledge. Tell Me the names of these things.

The connection might not seem obvious at first, but it's profound. Allah ﷻ is revealing that this human being will be given knowledge, and that knowledge can lead to extraordinary advances. The teaching of names to Adam (AS) represents the foundation of all systematic human knowledge.

Consider this: every field of knowledge today—regardless of the subject—ultimately comes down to terminology. Whether you're studying psychology, physiology, biology, architecture, engineering, or any other discipline, what are you really learning? You're learning terms. You're mastering concepts that are defined by specific terminology. Everything reduces to nouns, to a glossary, to an index of specialized vocabulary.

To work in any specialized field, you must be fluent in its terminology. I once overheard two doctors talking to each other. At first, their conversation was normal, but then it accelerated—like a plane starting its takeoff. Suddenly they were speaking a completely different language, using medical terms I couldn't even repeat because I don't know them. To anyone outside their field, it sounded like a foreign language. But to them, this specialized vocabulary was essential for discussing their work.

This phenomenon exists in every profession. All modern knowledge, in its essence, is built upon what Adam (AS) was originally given: the ability to label things, to name them, to create terminology.

This is crucial to understand: Allah ﷻ is not referring here to revealed knowledge. He's referring to what we might call worldly or secular knowledge—all fields of study beyond religious sciences. The origins of this knowledge were planted in Adam (AS), to the point where even the angels had no understanding of it.

And Allah ﷻ praised this knowledge too. This carries enormous implications. Many young people who become zealous about religion think, "I'm going to study the deen—that's the real knowledge." Yes, studying Islam is essential. But biology, architecture, history, sociology, political science, psychology—these fields are also forms of knowledge that Allah ﷻ honored from the very beginning. They too are studies of Allah's ﷻ ayat.

Remember what we discussed about the hypocrites—how they cut apart what is meant to be together? What's meant to be together and work in harmony for society's benefit? The knowledge of guidance and the knowledge of science. The knowledge of sociology and revealed wisdom. Political science and Islamic principles. Psychology and spiritual insight. All these fields of knowledge were never meant to be divorced from revealed knowledge. They're supposed to be married to it, to work hand in hand. They're two sides of the same coin.

We can call one type "acquired knowledge"—what human beings discover through observation and study. The other is "revealed knowledge"—what Allah ﷻ has taught us through His messengers. These two are meant to interact with one another, to inform one another.

Consider a contemporary example. In many parts of the Muslim world today, you either attend a university to study Islam, or you attend a different institution to study political science, history, or other academic subjects. The college and university system exists separately from the madrassa system. They're two parallel tracks that rarely intersect.

But originally, these two types of knowledge were meant to interact continuously. Look at our most sacred text—the Quran itself, which represents the pinnacle of revealed knowledge. How much of the Quran directs our attention to creation? Look at the sky. Observe the mountains. Consider the ocean. Reflect on travel and trade. Study history. Allah ﷻ constantly pushes us to engage with the world, to study worldly phenomena. But this study is meant to be in harmony with revealed guidance.

What's happened in the modern world is that Muslims have split into two camps. Some pursue only religious knowledge and remain ignorant of how the world actually functions. Others pursue only worldly knowledge and disconnect it from divine guidance.

But think about the practical implications. If you don't understand how modern finance works at its core—if you don't have a deep, technical understanding of contemporary economic systems—how can you possibly produce an Islamic economic solution that's actually viable? Not just for Muslims, but for the world? You can't. You need both types of knowledge.

If you don't understand how society is organized—if you haven't studied sociology—how can you develop solutions from our deen that genuinely help society improve? You need to understand both dimensions.

Take another example: in Islamic tradition, we acknowledge the existence of jinn possession and spiritual afflictions. In modern science, there's psychology, psychiatry, and diagnoses like schizophrenia. Sometimes Muslims do dangerous and illegitimate things in the name of exorcism—even physically harming people. But how do you know whether someone is suffering from jinn possession or from a psychological disorder? I'm not denying that spiritual afflictions exist—they do. But how can you distinguish between the two if you don't study both sciences?

These fields of knowledge are meant to work together. Our scholars should be well-rounded. If we can't produce such comprehensive scholars, then at the very least we need think tanks where experts from different fields collaborate. Islamic legal scholars and lawyers working together. Scholars of hadith and sociologists sitting at the same table. Family therapists and fuqaha discussing issues side by side.

When these two areas of knowledge become divorced from one another, society descends into chaos.

Here's a critical point: science is blind. Science, in and of itself, has no guidance. You can use scientific knowledge to create nuclear weapons. You can use it to develop chemical warfare. Science is simply the study of how materials and forces interact with one another. Think of science as power—as the saying goes, "knowledge is power." Modern knowledge certainly represents power. But like any powerful tool or weapon, everything depends on where you point it, how you use it.

Guidance from revealed knowledge gives science its direction. It answers the question: How should you conduct research to make the world a better place? What's happening today? Most scientific research is driven by institutions that receive massive funding from corporations that are entirely motivated by greed. So science is being pushed forward by what force? Greed.

Look at pharmaceutical research. The primary factor behind much medical research isn't "How can we help humanity?" It's "How much can we sell this for? What kind of profit margin can we achieve?" The corruption in the pharmaceutical industry is staggering. The markup from production cost to retail price can be criminal—a pill that costs one cent to manufacture sold for $300. It's unconscionable that human beings can do this to other human beings.

If divine guidance were the driving factor behind such research, the goal would be "li-n-nasi jami'a"—for all people, for mass benefit, for universal accessibility.

I once witnessed a gathering where professionals from the medical field were complaining about corrupt imams and scholars who only care about money. One person responded, "So why don't all of you work pro bono? Start working without insurance companies taking their cut. You should live up to that standard too." Silence. Because they knew that to maintain their careers in certain fields, they have to cross certain ethical lines, compromise certain principles.

These two areas of knowledge must come back together. Here in the United States, alhamdulillah, we have a maturing Muslim community. It's growing stronger. Our masajid are expanding. Our younger generation is heading to college in larger numbers. We have a genuine opportunity—a historic opportunity—to create institutions that marry religious knowledge with secular knowledge.

If someone comes to me and says, "I want to study psychology from an Islamic perspective—where can I go?" I honestly don't know what to tell them. "I want to study political science within an Islamic framework." Where do you go for that? "I want to specialize in youth counseling with Islamic principles integrated." Does such a program even exist? Currently, no.

But we can't just sit and complain about this absence. We're living in a country built on entrepreneurship, on creating new institutions. We have the freedom and the resources to make this happen. We can create these programs, these institutes, these degrees. We have the opportunity to bring revealed knowledge and acquired knowledge back together again.

I'm telling you, we're sitting on a gold mine. We just need the vision and determination to make it real. May Allah ﷻ help us.

translation: They said, "Exalted are You; we have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed, it is You who is the Knowing, the Wise
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

The angels replied: "Subhanak" (Glory be to You, You are perfect). "La 'ilma lana illa ma 'allamtana" (We have no knowledge except what You have taught us). "Innaka anta al-'Alim al-Hakim" (Indeed, You are the All-Knowing, the All-Wise).

The angels' response reveals their humility and their recognition of their own limitations. They couldn't name what Allah ﷻ had shown them. They acknowledged that all their knowledge—everything they know—comes only from what Allah ﷻ has taught them. They possess no independent knowledge. And they affirmed that He alone is Al-'Alim (the All-Knowing) and Al-Hakim (the All-Wise)—He knows everything, and His wisdom is perfect.

translation: He said, "O Adam, inform them of their names." And when he had informed them of their names, He said, "Did I not tell you that I know the unseen [aspects] of the heavens and the earth? And I know what you reveal and what you have concealed
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ أَنۢبِئْهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَنۢبَأَهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ

Allah ﷻ then commanded: "Ya Adam, anbi'hum bi-asma'ihim" (O Adam, inform them of their names).

"Falamma anba'ahum bi-asma'ihim" (So when he informed them of their names). Adam (AS) demonstrated the knowledge Allah ﷻ had given him, naming everything the angels could not name.

Then Allah ﷻ said: "Alam aqul lakum" (Didn't I say to you?). "Inni a'lamu ghayba as-samawati wal-ard" (Indeed, I know the unseen of the heavens and the earth).

This statement carries profound meaning. There isn't just unseen knowledge in the heavens—there's also unseen knowledge in the earth. The earth contained knowledge that was hidden from the angels but revealed to Adam (AS). The angels couldn't figure it out, but Adam (AS) was taught these names.

"Wa a'lamu ma tubdun" (And I know what you reveal). "Wa ma kuntum taktumun" (And what you have been hiding).

The use of the past continuous tense here is significant: "kuntum taktumun"—you have been hiding. The angels had been concealing something, perhaps without even realizing what they were hiding. They had been harboring Iblis in their ranks.

The implication is clear: You thought this human being would cause corruption? Let Me tell you—something far more corrupt is already here, right among you. And you've been hiding him, unknowingly protecting him in your midst, all this time.

translation: And [mention] when We said to the angels, "Prostrate before Adam"; so they prostrated, except for Iblees. He refused and was arrogant and became of the disbelievers
commentary:
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

**When We said to the angels: Prostrate to Adam, they all prostrated except Iblis. He refused and was arrogant, and he was of the disbelievers.**

Allah ﷻ commanded the angels to make a ceremonial prostration to Adam (AS) as a show of loyalty to this newest and greatest creation. Much has been speculated about the nature of this prostration, but what we know with certainty is that prostration to anyone other than Allah ﷻ is now forbidden. This was a unique command for that time, demonstrating the honor bestowed upon Adam (AS).

The angels obeyed immediately, but Iblis refused. The Quran tells us he did two things: أَبَىٰ - he refused, and اسْتَكْبَرَ - he sought his own greatness. The word استكبار comes from the root meaning "to be big." One of Allah's names is المتكبر - He who is truly Great. But istikbar means wanting to be great, seeking recognition of one's own superiority.

What makes Iblis's rebellion particularly instructive is what he was seeking. He wasn't after wealth or worldly power. He already had a position of worship and devotion to Allah ﷻ. What he craved was religious recognition - to be acknowledged as the greatest worshiper, to hold the highest status in the eyes of Allah ﷻ and the other angels. This wasn't just arrogance; it was spiritual arrogance.

This carries a profound warning for our time. Just as capitalism can turn anything into an industry, it can commodify religious knowledge and status. Scholars and speakers can find themselves competing for recognition, appreciation, first-class accommodations, and celebrity-like treatment. This is no different from Iblis vying for acknowledgment of his superiority.

The entire purpose of human creation is to recognize ourselves as slaves of Allah ﷻ and to humble ourselves before Him. Knowledge should increase humility, not pride. Think of a fruit tree - the more fruit it bears, the lower its branches hang toward the earth. Similarly, the more we learn about our faith, the more we should be humbled by it.

We live in an unfortunate era where we're turning our scholars into celebrities. People line up for pictures and autographs at Islamic conferences, creating uncomfortable situations where refusing seems rude, but accepting feeds into something dangerous. The most precious thing a scholar possesses isn't knowledge - knowledge is relatively easy to acquire. The most precious and volatile possession is sincerity. It's easy to gain knowledge and speaking ability, but maintaining sincerity is extraordinarily difficult, especially when constantly praised and given accolades.

This doesn't mean we shouldn't appreciate our teachers and scholars - we absolutely should. But when appreciation transforms into a culture of celebrity worship, it creates serious spiritual dangers. Iblis served the faith for ages, yet one moment of seeking recognition destroyed him. The best thing we can do for those who teach us is make dua for them, particularly for the preservation of their sincerity.

The ayah ends with a chilling observation: وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ - "he was of the disbelievers." Iblis became a disbeliever because he refused one prostration. How many prostrations does a lazy Muslim refuse when neglecting their prayers? The parallel should shake us.

translation: And We said, "O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise and eat therefrom in [ease and] abundance from wherever you will. But do not approach this tree, lest you be among the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

**And We said: O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise, and eat from it freely wherever you wish. But do not approach this tree, lest you become of the wrongdoers.**

The word اسْكُنْ means to settle down after being in motion. This tells us that Adam (AS) was created somewhere else, and Jannah was not yet his home. Now Allah ﷻ was telling him to station himself there, to make it his residence.

Notice the structure of the command: "O Adam, dwell, you and your wife." The command is directed to Adam (AS), then mentions his spouse. The Arabic imperative form that follows - كُلَا (kula) "both of you eat" - addresses them together, but the initial instruction was given to Adam (AS). He was to relay Allah's command to his wife. This establishes a pattern within the household where divine instructions are conveyed through the head of the household.

We see this principle elsewhere in the Quran, where the Prophet ﷺ was commanded: "Command your family to prayer and be patient in maintaining it yourself" (20:132). The maintainer of the house, the husband, serves as the conduit for Allah's guidance to reach the family members.

This doesn't negate women's rights or their direct relationship with Allah ﷻ. Rather, it recognizes that Allah ﷻ has given men and women different rights and responsibilities. They're not the same creature, so giving them identical rights wouldn't be justice - it would be uniformity, which is different from equity. Justice means each person receives what suits their nature and circumstances. There's a difference between equality and justice. Equality means everyone gets the same thing; justice means everyone gets what they deserve and what fits their role.

Allah ﷻ has given men advantages in certain areas and women advantages in others. At the end of the day, Allah ﷻ has promised both men and women the best and most beautiful reward. Within the household structure, the husband holds a managerial position - but he's not the owner. He's an employee who happens to be a manager. The true Owner is Allah ﷻ. This becomes crystal clear in the later verses about divorce in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ directly addresses men who abuse their wives, reminding them that they are not the ultimate authority.

Allah ﷻ tells Adam and his wife: وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا - "eat from it freely" - رَغَدًا means carefree, without worry or restriction. حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا - "wherever you wish." Everything was permissible. No need to check ingredients, worry about what's halal, or investigate the source. Everything was not just halal, but better than halal - it was pure Paradise.

Then comes the prohibition: وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ - "do not approach this tree." The word هَـٰذِهِ is a close demonstrative pronoun, meaning "this one here." If the tree were far away, Allah ﷻ would have used تِلْكَ - "that one there." But He said "this tree," indicating they were already close to it. They were meant to move away immediately, but instead they remained in proximity to the forbidden.

This is the nature of prohibition. When you stay close to what's forbidden, you become familiar with it. Eventually, you can't resist. Getting close to trouble means falling into trouble. You might think you can control yourself - "we're just talking," "I can handle this" - but once you're drawn in, the pull becomes irresistible. Bad company works like this. Bad habits work like this. You start small and get consumed entirely.

فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - "you will be of the wrongdoers." Everything is permissible except this one thing. This is the blueprint for Islamic law. The default is permission; prohibition is the exception. Everything on earth is halal for you except a small list of things you must avoid. Don't approach those few trees; everything else is yours to enjoy. This is the mercy of Allah ﷻ - broad permission with narrow restriction.

translation: But Satan caused them to slip out of it and removed them from that [condition] in which they had been. And We said, "Go down, [all of you], as enemies to one another, and you will have upon the earth a place of settlement and provision for a time
commentary:
فَأَزَلَّهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ عَنْهَا فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ ۖ وَقُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ

**Then Shaytan caused them to slip from it and brought them out of what they had been in. And We said: Descend, some of you enemies to others. And for you on earth is a place of settlement and provision for a time.**

Shaytan caused them both to slip - أَزَلَّهُمَا from the command to stay away from the tree. He made them forget the warning and drew them close to what was forbidden. Once they approached, they couldn't resist. This is the danger of proximity to sin.

But there's a deeper dimension to this story that's crucial to understand. Remember that before Adam (AS) was even created, Allah ﷻ announced to the angels: إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً - "I am placing on the earth a khalifah" (2:30). The very first announcement about Adam (AS) was that he was being created for the earth. Who heard this announcement? The angels heard it. Who was in charge of the angels at that time? Iblis. So who knew that Adam (AS) was destined for earth? All the angels knew, and Iblis certainly knew.

Yet Allah ﷻ didn't place Adam (AS) on earth immediately. He placed him in Jannah first. This is significant. Iblis knew Adam's ultimate destination was earth, so when he came to tempt him, he could make a very specific offer: "Eat from this tree and perhaps you'll become من الملائكة - from the angels, or من الخالدين - from those who remain permanently."

The implication was clear: "You were created for earth, but if you eat from this tree, maybe you can stay here in Paradise permanently like the angels." The drive to eat from the tree was the desire to avoid going to earth.

What was the consequence of eating from the tree? اهْبِطُوا - "Descend to earth." But wait - the transfer papers were already issued before the temptation. Adam (AS) was going to earth regardless. The punishment seems to be exactly what was already planned.

This brings us to one of the oldest questions in human thought: If Allah ﷻ already knew what would happen, how is it our fault? If Allah ﷻ knew Adam (AS) would go to earth, how is it Adam's fault that he ate from the tree?

Here's what's profound: the first human being could have asked this very question. Adam (AS) himself could have argued, "The destination was predetermined - how am I responsible?" But he didn't. This tells us everything we need to know about the question of predestination and free will. The question isn't new or clever; it's as old as humanity itself. Every philosophy textbook deals with it on the first page. But the first human being, who had the most legitimate reason to ask it, chose not to.

Both Iblis and Adam (AS) disobeyed Allah ﷻ. Both were honored creatures. Both were given the opportunity to either repent or rationalize their mistake. Iblis rationalized: خَلَقْتَنِي مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِن طِينٍ - "You created me from fire and created him from clay" (7:12). His argument was logical - fire is superior to clay; he had seniority and a superior nature. It was a rational complaint.

Adam (AS) could have made a logical complaint too, but he didn't. One chose to justify; the other chose to repent. One was cursed for eternity; the other was forgiven. This is the choice presented to every human being who reads this story. We encounter it seven times in the Quran, a constant reminder: Will you make the choice of Adam (AS) or the choice of Iblis?

When you sin, you face this fork in the road. Either you rationalize, justify, and present logical explanations for why you did what you did, or you simply say: "I was wrong. I should stop. I hope Allah ﷻ forgives me." That's the bottom line of this entire narrative.

وَقُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ - "Descend, some of you enemies to others." The plural form indicates this addressed Adam, his wife, and Iblis - they would all descend, and some would be enemies to others. Earth would be a battleground between their descendants.

وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ - "And for you on earth is a place of settlement and provision for a time." The earth wasn't presented as a prison, but as a temporary residence where you'll have what you need. مُسْتَقَرٌّ means a place to rest and settle. مَتَـٰعٌ means useful provisions, things to benefit from. إِلَىٰ حِينٍ - for a time, temporarily. This is your temporary residence until you return to your permanent home.

translation: Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful
commentary:
فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَـٰتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

**Then Adam received words from his Lord, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.**

The word تَلَقَّىٰ means to receive or come into contact with something. Adam (AS) received كَلِمَـٰتٍ - words - from his Lord. These weren't just any words; they were the words of repentance, the exact phrases to say when seeking forgiveness.

We have the same gift. The Quran contains the words from our Master that we recite to earn His forgiveness. رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا - "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves" (7:23). رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَا إِن نَّسِينَا أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا - "Our Lord, do not take us to account if we forget or make a mistake" (2:286). All these beautiful supplications we make to Allah ﷻ to earn His forgiveness - where did they come from? He taught them to us.

Adam (AS) didn't even know how to articulate his remorse. When you're overwhelmed with embarrassment and shame, words fail you. You don't know what to say. Allah ﷻ is so merciful that He didn't just accept an apology - He provided the very words to say. "Here's what you should say. If you just say this, I'll forgive you."

Notice something significant: فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ - "Adam received." The ayah mentions Adam (AS) by name, but doesn't explicitly mention his wife. Why? Because the father took responsibility for the household. This doesn't mean she didn't repent - of course she did. But the Quran highlights the husband's role because he should have been the one leading his family, the one who was directly commanded even though the prohibition applied to both of them.

The head of the household is mentioned first in repentance because he's responsible. This teaches men that they must take care of their families, because they'll need to make tawbah for their shortcomings in leadership too. If they fall short in their responsibilities, they're accountable.

فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ - "He turned back toward him in acceptance." Allah ﷻ accepted his repentance. The phrase indicates Allah ﷻ turning toward His servant with mercy, opening the door that the servant thought might be closed.

إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ - "Indeed, He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful." 

التَّوَّابُ - the One who continually accepts repentance. This isn't a one-time acceptance; it's a constant attribute. He's always ready to accept those who turn back to Him.

الرَّحِيمُ - the Merciful. There's a distinction between رَحِيم and رَحْمَن Rahman shows mercy universally and immediately. Raheem shows continuous mercy over time. Someone might show you mercy once, but Raheem is the One who shows you mercy again and again and again.

Your belief in these names is tested when you fall short, when you sin, when you fail to meet expectations. You might think: "I did this, then I was good for a while, then I messed up again. Am I a hypocrite? Is there still room for me?" 

You don't need to ask a scholar these questions. Ask Allah ﷻ directly. No one will answer you better than He will. And that answer is clear: the doors of repentance are always open. His mercy is constantly available. He's not Raheem in the past tense, showing mercy once upon a time. He's Raheem in the continuous present - showing mercy right now, at this moment, and in every moment to come. If you ask Allah ﷻ for forgiveness, it will always be there for you.

translation: We said, "Go down from it, all of you. And when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance - there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
قُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّى هُدًى فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَاىَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

**"We said: Descend from here, all of you together. Then if and when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows that guidance—they will have no fear, nor will they grieve."**

Allah ﷻ addresses Adam (AS) and his descendants with a fundamental promise that establishes the relationship between humanity and divine guidance. The command to descend—*ihbitu*—marks the transition from paradise to earth, but more significantly, it introduces the concept of هُدًى, guidance.

This represents the second type of knowledge Allah ﷻ teaches humanity. Earlier, He taught Adam (AS) the names of things—acquired knowledge, practical information. Now He introduces a different category: guidance that will come from Him directly. This guidance is not merely information; it is divine direction for how to live.

The promise attached to this guidance is powerful and precise: **فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** "no fear upon them, nor will they grieve." These two qualities mirror the description of the Muttaqun at the beginning of this surah. When Allah ﷻ mentioned the people of Taqwa, He described them with two essential characteristics: those who protect themselves and those who succeed.

Consider the connection: someone who protects himself has nothing to fear—he's been saved from danger. That's the first part of this promise: لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ —there's no fear upon them. Someone who's safe has no reason for anxiety about what lies ahead.

The second quality relates to success and failure. When you succeed at something, happiness follows. When you fail, sadness comes. Success at a job interview brings a smile; failure brings disappointment. The Muflihun—those who succeed—are characterized by happiness, while those who fail are marked by grief. Here Allah ﷻ says وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ —they will not be the ones grieving. 

This establishes that guidance is not a new promise to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ—it began with Adam (AS), the first human being. The covenant made with him serves as the foundation for all future covenants with humanity.

translation: And those who disbelieve and deny Our signs - those will be companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

**"And those who disbelieve and deny Our signs—those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally."**

The opposite outcome is now presented with equal clarity. Earlier in the surah, Allah ﷻ described the reality of the disbelievers: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ —their hearts have been sealed, their case closed. Here, the consequence is made explicit: these are the people of the Fire, and in that Fire they will remain eternally.

This ayah serves as a reiteration and intensification of the earlier warning. The story of Adam (AS) and Iblis reminds us that the promise of following guidance and being in a position of safety has always existed. Those who violate Allah's promise after it was made—وَٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ (those who break the covenant of Allah)—fall into the same pattern as Iblis himself.

With the individual covenant established through Adam (AS), the narrative now transitions to the societal level. After Adam (AS) had children and societies began to form, Allah ﷻ chose one society to make them an example for all of humanity—a nation that wouldn't receive guidance just once, but repeatedly. He wouldn't send them a single messenger, but messenger after messenger, prophet after prophet. This society would be given chance after chance to become the role model for the rest of humanity. That nation was Bani Israel, the Children of Israel.

This transition is profound because it connects the hypocrites—those claiming to believe while not truly believing, not showing loyalty to the messenger—with this historical pattern. It's also significant because the early Muslim community in Medina came into direct contact with the Jewish tribes. The warning وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ (those who disbelieved and lied against Our signs) leads directly to addressing the Israelites: speaking of which, O Children of Israel...

translation: O Children of Israel, remember My favor which I have bestowed upon you and fulfill My covenant [upon you] that I will fulfill your covenant [from Me], and be afraid of [only] Me
commentary:
يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِعَهْدِىٓ أُوفِ بِعَهْدِكُمْ وَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱرْهَبُونِ

**"O Children of Israel, remember My favor which I bestowed upon you, and fulfill My covenant, and I will fulfill your covenant, and of Me alone should you be in awe."**

Allah ﷻ directly addresses the descendants of Israel with a command to remember. Earlier, when Allah ﷻ mentioned creating the earth and providing sustenance, He was instilling a sense of gratitude. But if any nation should be grateful above all others, it's not merely the one that received food and drink on this earth—it's the one that received guidance upon guidance, messenger after messenger.

ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ —make mention of My favor, the favor I showered upon you. This isn't just about material blessings; it encompasses the spiritual wealth of prophetic guidance from Musa (AS) all the way to Isa (AS). How much more grateful should they be? And by extension, how grateful should they be for the final messenger ﷺ?

The covenant is then invoked: وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِعَهْدِىٓ أُوفِ بِعَهْدِكُمْ —fulfill My promise, and I will fulfill My promise to you. What was the original promise to Adam (AS)? Whenever guidance comes, you will be protected from fear and grief. The logical corollary follows: if you don't follow the guidance, what will you get? Fear and grief.

Study the history of the Israelites, and you'll find their entire narrative can be characterized by these two conditions: fear and grief. The very things promised protection from became their defining experiences due to their repeated violations of the covenant.

The ayah concludes with powerful emphasis: وَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱرْهَبُونِ —and of Me alone, exclusively, should you be in awe. The word appears twice in this construction—إِيَّـٰىَ  at the beginning and the implicit return to it at the end. The term رَهْبَة combines two concepts: خَوْف (fear) and فَزَع (shock, being shaken). 

Allah ﷻ is telling them: you've done things before that go beyond normal human error. Most people need to be cautious of Allah ﷻ, mindful and afraid. But you—given the level of ingratitude you've shown, the amount of guidance you've received and rejected—you should go above and beyond ordinary fear. You should have rahbah, a profound terror of Allah's ﷻ response to your violations.

translation: And believe in what I have sent down confirming that which is [already] with you, and be not the first to disbelieve in it. And do not exchange My signs for a small price, and fear [only] Me
commentary:
وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلْتُ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ وَلَا تَكُونُوٓا۟ أَوَّلَ كَافِرٍۭ بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا وَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱتَّقُونِ

**"And believe in what I have sent down confirming what is with you, and do not be the first to disbelieve in it, and do not exchange My signs for a small price, and of Me alone should you be conscious."**

The command centers on revelation: وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلْتُ —believe in what I have sent down. Remember, there are three areas of belief that Allah ﷻ highlights: belief in Allah ﷻ Himself, belief in the messenger, and belief in the revelation. This ayah emphasizes the third category, but with an important recognition—at the time of the Prophet ﷺ, believing in the message and believing in the messenger cannot be separated. The Quran is coming from the mouth of the Messenger ﷺ, so when you say "I believe in revelation," it's the same as saying "I believe in Muhammad ﷺ."

The key phrase is مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ —confirming, validating the truth of what you already have. When you hear this revelation, your faith in your own scripture should be strengthened. You read it and recognize: "What Musa (AS) told us was true."

Consider Deuteronomy 18:18-20, where Allah ﷻ says through Musa (AS): "I will send among them from within their brethren a prophet who will speak in My name, as I have commanded him." This prophet speaks in Allah's ﷻ name—whenever he does something, he says بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ. Remarkably, some modern translations have inserted "Israelites" after "brethren," though it wasn't there before. Who were their original brethren? The people of Ismail (AS), from whom the Prophet ﷺ descends.

The command continues: وَلَا تَكُونُوٓا۟ أَوَّلَ كَافِرٍۭ بِهِۦ —don't be the first in line to disbelieve in it. How could they be among the first to disbelieve? They saw the prophecies, they recognized the signs, yet they rejected him outright. Others might have doubts or confusion, but for the People of the Book to reject what they know to be true—that places them at the front of the line of disbelief.

Notice the parallel: just as Iblis was characterized by استِكْبَار (arrogance), the root cause of the Israelites' rejection is the same. "I'm from a superior nation. How can I accept someone from what I consider an inferior people?"

The warning becomes specific: وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا —don't sell off My revelations, My miraculous signs, for such a small price. What small price could be worth trading divine truth? Social status? Maintaining position among your people? Worldly wealth?

The ayah concludes: وَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱتَّقُونِ —and of Me alone, only Me, should you be protecting yourselves from. What are you protecting yourself from—social ramifications? What your fellow Israelites might say? You should worry about protecting yourself from Me alone.

translation: And do not mix the truth with falsehood or conceal the truth while you know [it]
commentary:
وَلَا تَلْبِسُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

**"And do not mix truth with falsehood, and do not conceal the truth while you know."**

The grammatical structure here reveals a profound insight. In Arabic, you would expect وَلَا تَلْبِسُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَلَا تَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ —with the conjunction وَ repeated. Instead, the structure shows these as two parts of the same process: don't dress up, don't disguise truth with falsehood, and by doing so, hide the truth altogether.

The first step in hiding truth is mixing it with falsehood. Then Allah ﷻ describes the ultimate goal: وَتَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ —you do this specifically to hide the truth itself.

This highlights a critical concept about knowledge and power. People who possess knowledge have power, especially when others rely on them for that knowledge. This gives them the ability to mix truth with falsehood. Consider a mechanic who knows everything about fixing cars while you know very little. You bring your car in for an oil change—a simple, honest need. He can mix truth with falsehood: "Actually, you need a new transmission, your timing belt is gone, you need a new engine, change this, replace that..." Before you know it, he's extracted five thousand dollars from you. He can do this because he has knowledge you don't have.

The learned scholars of the Israelites possessed scriptural knowledge their general population didn't have. When common people came asking—"What do you think of this new prophet? It sounds like the messenger you've been giving us hope about. You told us the messenger is coming and he'll have these signs. We've been listening to this Muhammad ﷺ and it seems like he's the one. What do you think?"—they could mix truth with falsehood. "No, actually it doesn't say that..."

The knowledgeable ones knew the truth, but they hid it from their own masses through this technique of mixing. And the clause وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ —while you know—makes it clear this isn't innocent error or confusion. This is deliberate concealment by those who know better.

translation: And establish prayer and give zakah and bow with those who bow [in worship and obedience]
commentary:
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱرْكَعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ

**"And establish prayer, and give zakah, and bow with those who bow."**

The command returns to fundamental acts of worship: وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ —establish the prayer—and وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ—give the zakah.

Some scholars note that the Israelites had violated the laws of zakah and had changed the prescribed form of salah. They're being told to reestablish these practices correctly. Consider the connection: what does salah do? As explained at the beginning of this surah, it connects you to Allah ﷻ and develops Taqwa. What does zakah do, as its very name indicates? يُزَكِّي —it purifies you.

They had become corrupt, spiritually contaminated through their violations and concealments. Now they need to get clean, to purify themselves so they can properly receive this new revelation. وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ—establish prayer and give zakah for this purpose.

Then comes a remarkable phrase: وَٱرْكَعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ —bow with those who bow. This implies there's your way of doing salah, but there's now an official version, the authorized form. More significantly, it means join the congregation—the congregation of the Muslims. You need to join this new Jama'ah.

The word رُكُوع (ruku') itself carries profound linguistic meaning. One of its primary definitions is الخُضُوع الغَالِب —to be overwhelmingly humbled, overpowered. Ruku' is an expression that you have been overpowered by Allah ﷻ. You recited His words from the Quran, and they were so overwhelming that you declared اللّٰهُ أَكۡبَرُ (Allahu Akbar)—Allah is the Greatest—and it humbled you so completely that you went down into ruku'.

The classical Arabs used this word in another fascinating context. When a man who was previously wealthy and independent became impoverished, losing everything, they would say أَرْكَعَهُ الدَّهْرُ —time has brought him to ruku'. He's been brought down. He lost everything, became bankrupt after being rich. Someone who was wealthy, who didn't need anybody, and then lost it all—at that point they say this person is مُرْكِع —he's got nothing left.

All his comforts have declined, everything has deteriorated—his family situation, his financial circumstances, every aspect of his life has collapsed. That level of powerlessness, that complete absence of self-sufficiency—that's what you and I are supposed to embody before Allah ﷻ. There's no room left for arrogance because he's lost everything and all he's got left is his ruku'.

If someone is rich and his situation is comfortable, he might have reason for arrogance. But if he's lost everything, what's left for him? وَٱرْكَعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ. How profound is that concept embedded in this simple phrase about bowing with those who bow.

translation: Do you order righteousness of the people and forget yourselves while you recite the Scripture? Then will you not reason
commentary:
۞ أَتَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبِرِّ وَتَنسَوْنَ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَتْلُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

**"Do you order people to righteousness and forget yourselves, while you recite the Scripture? Will you not then use reason?"**

This is one of the most frightening ayahs in the Quran for anyone engaged in calling to Islam. When we read about المُنَافِقُون (the hypocrites), we shouldn't think of someone else—we should examine ourselves. When we read about the behavior of بَنِي إِسْرَائِيل, we shouldn't distance ourselves from it—we should see the warning for our own communities.

The proof of this principle comes from Surah Al-Fatihah itself. When Allah ﷻ teaches us to say صِرَاطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ ٱلْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (the path of those You have blessed, not of those who have incurred anger nor of those who are astray), the Prophet ﷺ explained that المَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ refers to certain behaviors of the Jews, and الضَّآلِّينَ to certain behaviors of the Christians. The fact that we ask Allah ﷻ not to be like them means we have the real possibility of becoming like them.

The ayah begins with a powerful rhetorical question: أَتَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبِرِّ —are you the people commanding others to goodness? Are you the ones telling people to do good? البِرّ encompasses all forms of righteousness and virtue—always do good, be righteous, be pious.

Then the devastating contrast: وَتَنسَوْنَ أَنفُسَكُمْ —and you forget about yourselves. This can happen to anyone engaged in Islamic work, but it happens most dangerously to those who are constantly commanding others to good. They're always telling others what to do, how to improve, what Islam requires—but they never diagnose themselves.

It becomes just a performance, something you deliver. The only time you learn something new is "Oh, I've got to use this next time I give a talk." You acquire knowledge not for your own transformation but for regurgitating it to someone else. You're ready to tell people impressive things—"That's good, I need to memorize that, it'll sound really impressive in the khutbah"—but وَتَنسَوْنَ أَنفُسَكُمْ—you forget about your own self.
