The medical metaphor is apt: يَا أَيُّهَا الطَّبِيبُ—hey doctor, diagnose yourself. What about you? This represents one of the most dangerous spiritual diseases for anyone active in da'wah—commanding people to do good while forgetting one's own condition.

It's easy to talk about. It's extraordinarily difficult to live by. This is why those engaged in the work of Allah ﷻ, in any capacity, must surround themselves with genuine friends who can keep them in check—especially elders and scholars who can provide honest accountability.

The phrase وَأَنتُمْ تَتْلُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ —and you're the ones who are in fact reading and following the Book—adds another layer. You're not ignorant people. You're people of knowledge, people who study, people who recite scripture.

Then comes the devastating question: أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ —why don't you then understand? Why don't you apply your intellect?

This marks the first appearance of the word عَقْل in the mushaf, and it's a term of profound importance that deserves extensive discussion. For now, understand the basic question: why don't you try to understand?

The question is entirely legitimate because these are people of knowledge. How can knowledge not lead to understanding? How can someone know something so well they can give speeches about it, yet still not understand it? How can you master information without comprehending its implications?

You know it thoroughly enough to lecture others, you've memorized it, you can articulate it beautifully—yet you still don't truly understand it. That's the paradox Allah ﷻ exposes here, and it's a mirror every person of knowledge must look into honestly.

translation: And seek help through patience and prayer, and indeed, it is difficult except for the humbly submissive [to Allah]
commentary:
وَٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلْخَـٰشِعِينَ

Allah ﷻ says, "And seek help through patience and prayer." This command addresses the emotional bias mentioned in the previous ayah. People who don't understand something don't need patience and prayer - understanding is an intellectual matter. But here we're learning that the real problem is emotional and spiritual bias, and that can only be cured by two things: patience (صبر) and prayer (صلاة).

The word استعينوا means to seek aid from something by putting yourself to work. You can only qualify for Allah's help when you make the effort of getting that help by being patient and by establishing prayer.

This command takes on special significance when we remember that these passages are addressing the Children of Israel. If they were to accept Islam, they would face tremendous backlash from their own community. At that point, they would desperately need to seek help through patience. They were also commanded earlier to establish prayer - Salah itself would aid them in making this difficult decision.

The ayah continues: وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ - "And indeed, it is difficult." What does the pronoun "it" refer to? There are two interpretations. The first is that it refers to Salah itself - getting help from prayer is very difficult. Praying itself might not be difficult, but making the kind of prayer that actually helps you, that transforms you, is accomplished only by those who have true خشوع.

Many people pray, but those whose prayer actually helps them become better people and commit themselves to truth and guidance are the ones who have khushu' in their prayer.

The second interpretation is that the pronoun refers to الاستعانة - the act of seeking help itself. Seeking help through patience and prayer is inherently difficult for anyone who doesn't have khushu'.

Khushu' is related to words for fear in Arabic, but it's a very specific kind of fear. It's an overwhelming sense of awe that overtakes you completely. It's the feeling of standing before a great power and realizing how utterly powerless you are - a spiritual state where even your muscles become numb in recognition of that majesty. This is khushu'.

translation: Who are certain that they will meet their Lord and that they will return to Him
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ

Those who possess khushu' are described as الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ - "those who are convinced that they will meet their Master." Notice the beautiful connection between khushu' in prayer and the certainty of meeting Allah ﷻ.

Every single Salah is a meeting with our Master, but only one with khushu' truly realizes this. For someone without khushu', prayer is just standing, bowing and prostrating - ups and downs and motions. But someone who genuinely understands what they're doing in Salah recognizes it as a practice drill for the Day of Judgment.

The word يَظُنُّونَ indicates conviction. Earlier, the Quran used يُوقِنُونَ for certainty. Here, dhann in Arabic represents something you're so convinced of that you don't even have to think twice about it. It becomes a cornerstone of your thoughts.

وَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ - "And that they will return to Him." Earlier, we read كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَاتًا فَأَحْيَاكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (2:28) - "Then to Him you will be returned," using the passive form تُرْجَعُونَ. But here the ayah uses the active form رَٰجِعُونَ - they will actively return.

This distinction is meaningful. Because these believers have khushu' in their prayer, they return to Allah ﷻ voluntarily in this life. They won't be herded toward Allah ﷻ on Judgment Day - they'll be actively going to Him. They're already doing that through their prayers. Instead of saying they will be returned to Allah ﷻ, the ayah says they will return to Allah ﷻ.

The word رَٰجِعُونَ is also significant grammatically - it's an ism (noun), not a verb, signifying permanence. Just as they are constant in their prayers, their khushu' is permanent. They are absolutely convinced they're going back to Allah ﷻ, and every time they return to prayer, it reminds them they will return to Him ultimately. Salah itself is a form of returning to Allah ﷻ.

translation: O Children of Israel, remember My favor that I have bestowed upon you and that I preferred you over the worlds
commentary:
يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَنِّى فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

For the second time, Allah ﷻ calls out: يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - "O Children of Israel." Something has fundamentally shifted since the first call. Everything said to them initially was positive reinforcement - establish prayer, give charity, bow with those who bow. Now comes a different approach.

From this point forward, eight extended passages will be dedicated to recounting the crimes and failures of the Children of Israel. This is negative reinforcement: you had better come to the truth and not repeat these catastrophic mistakes.

The tone has changed, but the message remains: ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ - "Remember My favor which I bestowed upon you." This invitation to make dhikr of Allah's blessings carries an important lesson for us today.

The Quran constantly invites the Children of Israel to remember and reflect. We are meant to be engaged in active da'wah with the Jewish community. This eternal Book maintains an ongoing conversation with them. However, because of current political situations, there exists in the Muslim psyche a universal hatred toward Jews that creates a barrier. You cannot invite someone you hate - that's not how da'wah works. Da'wah requires dialogue with a community you're engaged with, and that dialogue is meant to continue. It didn't end with the Prophet ﷺ, just as dialogue with the Christian community continues.

وَأَنِّى فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "And that I preferred you above the worlds." The question naturally arises: how were they preferred? The answer was already given to Adam (AS): "Whenever guidance comes to you from Me..." That open invitation was extended, and no nation received as much guidance as the Children of Israel did. This was their preference.

But in their minds, being "preferred" meant they were a superior race, that everyone else was gentile, that they were guaranteed Heaven where there would be no gentiles, that they had guaranteed salvation. The passages that follow will correct this misunderstanding: "Let Me show you from your own history what it truly means that I gave you preference. I made concessions for you that I would not have made for other nations."

translation: And fear a Day when no soul will suffice for another soul at all, nor will intercession be accepted from it, nor will compensation be taken from it, nor will they be aided
commentary:
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِى نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ وَلَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ

Before recounting their history, Allah ﷻ gives them a stark warning: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا - "Be cautious of a Day." Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't say "have taqwa" as He did earlier. That higher expectation is no longer placed on them. They were previously told إِيَّايَ فَارْهَبُونِ - "Be afraid of Me alone." Taqwa is an elevated spiritual state. Now they're simply told to fear a Day - even that basic level of caution would be enough.

لَّا تَجْزِى نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا - "When no soul will avail another soul in anything." Most nations that become corrupt - including Muslim nations, for the Children of Israel were a Muslim nation that went corrupt - develop false concepts of salvation. They begin to feel they're no longer individually responsible because someone will intervene on their behalf.

Allah ﷻ is reminding them: no nation, no individual will be able to benefit anyone else in any way whatsoever on that Day.

وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ - "And no intercession will be accepted from it." To understand this, imagine you're an employee who gets in trouble. Between you and the boss is a manager who happens to be your uncle. Instead of facing consequences, your uncle covers for you: "He's with me, I've got you covered." So you start taking your job casually because you have connections.

This is the concept of شفاعة - intercession - when corrupted: "I can do whatever I want because someone else will deal with Allah ﷻ for me. I just have to keep the middleman happy." Whether that middleman is Prophet Isa (AS), whether it's an idol, whether for some Muslims it becomes "we just have to praise the Messenger ﷺ and he'll take care of everything" - this mentality develops in corrupt communities.

The Children of Israel developed this mindset: "Prophet Musa (AS) will make our case. All the prophets will intercede for us. We don't have to deal with Allah ﷻ directly."

Allah ﷻ says: not only will no intercession be accepted, even if someone wanted to intercede for you, they wouldn't be permitted. The interesting grammatical point here is that شَفَـٰعَةٌ is feminine while يُقْبَلُ uses a masculine form. This seeming contradiction in gender creates a more comprehensive meaning: no intercessor (masculine) and no intercession (feminine) will be accepted.

وَلَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ - "And no compensation will be taken from it." When people get in trouble, the first thought is: "I'll get a good lawyer to make my case." That's ruled out. The next thought: "I'll just pay a penalty, pay a fine - I won't have to face real consequences." Allah ﷻ says no compensation, no equivalent payment will be accepted either.

وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ - "And they will not be helped." Their intercession isn't accepted, their compensation is rejected, even their lawyer has abandoned them. The only thing left is to cry out for help. Allah ﷻ says they won't be among those who are helped.

translation: And [recall] when We saved your forefathers from the people of Pharaoh, who afflicted you with the worst torment, slaughtering your [newborn] sons and keeping your females alive. And in that was a great trial from your Lord
commentary:
وَإِذْ نَجَّيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ يُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ

Notice the beautiful connection: immediately after saying "they will not be helped," Allah ﷻ reminds them: وَإِذْ نَجَّيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ - "And when We saved you from the people of Pharaoh."

The connection is profound. The fact that you won't receive help on Judgment Day doesn't mean I never wanted to help you. I've been helping you all along, but you kicked that help aside. You did that to yourselves. You are the same people I used to help constantly - We rescued you over and over again from the clutches of Pharaoh.

يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ - "They were afflicting you with the worst torment." The word يَسُومُونَكُمْ means to humiliate, to blacken faces, to put mud on your honor - an Arabic expression for degrading a nation. سُوٓءَ comes from سوء, related to a corpse. A corpse is disgusting, revolting. سُوٓءَ doesn't just mean evil - it means the ugliest, nastiest form of evil. It's the kind of thing you see on the news and want to change the channel because it's too disturbing to watch.

What was this unthinkable punishment? يُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ - "Slaughtering your sons." Pharaoh's army had been commanded because Pharaoh saw a dream that one of the sons of this land would end his kingdom. So he issued a decree: every other year, go door to door and execute every baby boy. Complete genocide of children, every other year. This is truly سُوٓءَ - unthinkable evil.

وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ - "And sparing your women." They let the girls live. Why would they spare the women? I don't need to spell that out - you understand what happens to the women of oppressed nations.

There is no greater humiliation for a people than the violation of their women. Today we speak of women being victims of war crimes - Stalin didn't invent this; Pharaoh was the master of this tactic. This is how you keep a nation completely subdued, stripped of all respect and dignity.

Our dignity comes from our women. The honor of our household resides in the honor of our women. People can say whatever they want about me personally, but if they say anything about my daughters, my wife, my mother - that's where the line is drawn. When the women of a nation are humiliated, that nation has no respect left, no honor remaining. This is how Pharaoh kept them undermined and destroyed, crushing their self-respect completely.

وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ - "And in that was a tremendous trial from your Lord." Even though it was a horrific ordeal, Allah ﷻ uses the word بَلَآءٌ (bala') - trial or test. This is the same word used for the trials of Ibrahim (AS), which shows you the magnitude of what the Children of Israel endured. Allah ﷻ is acknowledging: this was an incredibly difficult test, comparable even to the trials of Ibrahim (AS).

translation: And [recall] when We parted the sea for you and saved you and drowned the people of Pharaoh while you were looking on
commentary:
وَإِذْ فَرَقْنَا بِكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰكُمْ وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ

وَإِذْ فَرَقْنَا بِكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ - "And when We parted the sea for you," or "because of you." Let's understand both meanings. The word بِكُمُ - "with you" or "by means of you" - creates a powerful image.

Think of cutting a cake with a knife. The knife itself becomes the divider between both sides. Allah ﷻ is describing the parting of the sea as though the Children of Israel themselves were the dam, keeping the two walls of water on either side. They themselves were the divider. The other meaning is straightforward: We parted the water because of you, for your sake - as you approached the water, We split it open.

فَأَنجَيْنَـٰكُمْ - "Then We saved you." Earlier, Allah ﷻ used نَجَّيْنَـٰكُم - "We used to rescue you" (repeatedly, over and over). Now He uses أَنجَيْنَـٰكُمْ - We saved you once and for all, definitively. Because once the water parted and the Israelites crossed, and Pharaoh was crushed in those waters, that trial was over forever.

وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ - "And We drowned the people of Pharaoh." Again, the verb form indicates: We drowned them once and for all.

وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ - "While you were watching." A question naturally arises: the Prophet ﷺ is speaking to Children of Israel living in Arabia thousands of years after this event. How is "you were watching" a blessing for them? They weren't the ones watching - this happened generations before them.

The answer reveals something important about the Israelites as a nation: they take tremendous pride in their narrative. They are a people who pass down their history generation after generation with great care. The proudest moments of their history are being rescued from Pharaoh's clutches, with their hero Musa (AS) helping them when God came to their aid.

Just as the Quraysh celebrated the Year of the Elephant when Abraham's army was destroyed, the Israelites celebrated the day Pharaoh's oppressive armies were destroyed by divine intervention. This was their glory day. When they thought of their legacy, they didn't say "our ancestors survived." They said "WE survived. Allah ﷻ was on OUR side."

This is like when Muslims today speak of past Islamic civilizations: "We were in Rome, we were in Persia, we conquered Spain" - taking pride in a shared legacy. Allah ﷻ is capitalizing on that pride, addressing them in their own framework: "You say it like that, right? You say YOU were the ones watching? You believe it was your own people, and you have solidarity with them? Very well - then own all of it, including what comes next."

translation: And [recall] when We made an appointment with Moses for forty nights. Then you took [for worship] the calf after him, while you were wrongdoers
commentary:
وَإِذْ وَٰعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰٓ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ

وَإِذْ وَٰعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰٓ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً - "And when We appointed for Musa forty nights." If there was ever one reason to rely completely on Allah ﷻ, if there was ever one thing that should have made them say "we will trust Allah ﷻ no matter what," it was what they had just witnessed.

They had been rescued from an impossible situation. An undefeated army approached from behind, a cloud of dust rising as the soldiers advanced. Water stood before them. There was either death by drowning or death by swords - no escape. And then Allah ﷻ parted the water before their very eyes.

If anything could make a people say "I will rely on Allah ﷻ and no one else," it was that moment. They crossed through walls of water held back by divine power, and they watched their oppressors drown when those walls collapsed.

ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ - "Then you took the calf." The verb اتَّخَذَ requires two objects - you take something AS something else. "Allah took Ibrahim as a friend" (4:125) - there's Ibrahim and there's friend. "I took you as a student" - there's you and student.

But notice: ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ - "You took the calf" - and then nothing. What did you take the calf AS? That second object isn't mentioned because it's not even worth dignifying with words. You took it as a god, as an object of worship - and it's so unthinkable, so unspeakable, so disgusting to Allah ﷻ that He doesn't even complete the sentence. The omission itself expresses the disgust.

مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ - "After that." After everything - after the rescue, after watching Pharaoh drown, after seeing miracle upon miracle - you could turn to worship a calf?

وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ - "And you were wrongdoers." Just as earlier Allah ﷻ said وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ - "you were the ones watching" - now He says "you were the ones doing wrong." The same people who witnessed the greatest miracle became the same people who committed the gravest betrayal.

The word ظَـٰلِمُونَ is in the noun form, indicating a permanent state. This wasn't a one-time mistake - you made yourselves into wrongdoers. You became oppressors, not just of others, but of your own souls.

translation: Then We forgave you after that so perhaps you would be grateful
commentary:
ثُمَّ عَفَوْنَا عَنكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

Then We pardoned you after that so perhaps you would be grateful.

The word عَفَوْنَا (We pardoned) carries profound meaning beyond simple forgiveness. عَفْو represents a loving pardon, one that comes with complete restoration of relationship. Consider when a husband and wife argue: one spouse may say "it's okay," but the tone reveals it's not really okay. A mother may accept her daughter's apology with words, yet her expression shows lingering disappointment. But afw is when forgiveness is absolutely complete—when you receive not just words but a hug, reassurance, perhaps even dessert. This is the nature of Allah's pardon ﷻ—totally accepting, completely loving.

This loving pardon becomes even more remarkable when we consider what crime preceded it. Allah ﷻ does not typically forgive shirk—associating partners with Him. Yet these were the very people who had just committed this gravest of sins by worshiping the golden calf. Allah ﷻ reminds us at the beginning of this passage that He gave the Children of Israel preference over other nations. Can you imagine what would happen to any other nation if they received a messenger, were saved by Allah ﷻ, and then still returned to shirk? 

But Allah ﷻ made an exception for them: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (so perhaps you would be grateful). The entire conversation from the beginning of this surah—when Allah ﷻ spoke about creating the earth for humanity—has carried the underlying tone of gratitude. Now with the final revelation having come, something greater than ever before has been given to humanity to be grateful for. The Quran represents the ultimate gift.

Think of a child constantly asking when they can drive: "When can I get my permit? When can I get a car?" The parent says, "You're not ready yet. When you're ready, I'll give it to you." Humanity reached the point where they were ready for this final gift. Who should have been in greater anticipation of this than the people who received revelation before and were promised the best one was still coming? They should have been the most grateful. Yet Allah ﷻ is saying: I gave you previous opportunities. Even though you committed shirk—even though you were supposed to be role models for all nations—I pardoned you lovingly. Why? Because there was potential you would be grateful.

translation: And [recall] when We gave Moses the Scripture and criterion that perhaps you would be guided
commentary:
وَإِذْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْفُرْقَانَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ

And when We gave Musa the Scripture and the criterion so perhaps you would be guided.

Even after they failed so dramatically, Allah ﷻ says: We gave Musa (AS) the Book and Al-Furqan. You can understand الْفُرْقَانَ in two ways. It could mean the distinction between right and wrong that enabled Musa (AS) to pass judgments among his people. Or you can understand الْكِتَابَ وَالْفُرْقَانَ together as one concept: We gave Musa the Book which itself serves as the criterion for right and wrong.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ—so that you all may be committed to guidance. In this world, you and I will enjoy no greater gift than guidance. When Musa (AS)—your own messenger, the one who rescued you from the clutches of Pharaoh—was given this greatest gift of guidance, you should have been grateful for it. Instead, in his absence, you committed the ultimate crime.

translation: And [recall] when Moses said to his people, "O my people, indeed you have wronged yourselves by your taking of the calf [for worship]. So repent to your Creator and kill yourselves. That is best for [all of] you in the sight of your Creator." Then He accepted your repentance; indeed, He is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّكُمْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنفُسَكُم بِٱتِّخَاذِكُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ فَتُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ بَارِئِكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ عِندَ بَارِئِكُمْ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

And when Musa said to his people, "O my people, indeed you have wronged yourselves by taking the calf. So repent to your Creator and kill yourselves. That is best for you in the sight of your Creator." Then He accepted your repentance; indeed, He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

Later in the Quran, we learn that when Musa (AS) came back with revelation and found them worshiping the calf, he became furious. His anger was so intense that he grabbed his brother Harun's (AS) head, demanding to know how he let this happen. Musa (AS) has a temper. But we also know that Harun (AS) had been with him through thick and thin—he was with him when facing Pharaoh. Harun (AS) knew how to calm his brother down. When Harun (AS) opened his mouth, he used very particular words: يَا ابْنَ أُمَّ (O son of my mother). "Brother from the same mother, come on now." We learn from this that Harun (AS) had a calming effect on Musa (AS).

Once Musa (AS) composed himself, he addressed the people publicly: يَا قَوْمِ إِنَّكُمْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنفُسَكُم (O my people, indeed you have wronged yourselves). This emphatic language suggests they were trying to shift blame: "It's your fault. You left us. You went away for forty days. What were we supposed to do? We got bored. Someone was very convincing." But Musa (AS) made it absolutely clear: "No doubt about it—you are the ones who wronged yourselves. Nobody else. You cannot blame this on anyone else. You cannot put this on anyone."

بِاتِّخَاذِكُمُ الْعِجْلَ—by having taken the calf. فَتُوبُوا إِلَىٰ بَارِئِكُمْ—then you must repent to your Creator. But how? فَاقْتُلُوا أَنفُسَكُمْ—kill your own selves.

What does this mean? There were twelve tribes of Israel. Some members of every tribe had committed this crime of shirk. Each tribe was to take their own مُرْتَدِّين —those who had abandoned their religion and become apostates—and execute them. This would serve as collective repentance and purification for the nation as a whole.

This law of punishing apostasy with execution did not originate with the shariah of Muhammad ﷺ. It existed in the shariah of Musa (AS), as we see clearly in this ayah. The Children of Israel knew this law very well.

But they questioned: How is this going to help? Why can't You just forgive us again? Allah ﷻ responds: ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ عِندَ بَارِئِكُمْ (That is best for you in the sight of your Creator). This is not hatred toward you. This is the verdict of Allah ﷻ. He is letting you know this is better for you. How is it better? Because once you carry it out, He will accept your repentance.

You committed the ultimate crime of shirk, which cannot be easily forgiven. Something must compensate for that. You have done many crimes before, but this one is unprecedented. You will have to pay a serious penalty. But then: فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ—He accepted your repentance. Certainly He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Ever-Merciful.

translation: And [recall] when you said, "O Moses, we will never believe you until we see Allah outright"; so the thunderbolt took you while you were looking on
commentary:
وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّىٰ نَرَى ٱللَّهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْكُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ

And when you said, "O Musa, we will never believe you until we see Allah outright." So the thunderbolt seized you while you were looking on.

When iman is used with the letter ب, as in يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ (they believe in Allah), it indicates belief. But when it comes with the letter ل, as in لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ, it means to cave in, to surrender, to give in to somebody's demand. "We will not give in to what you're saying. We will not accept what you're claiming."

Their condition? حَتَّىٰ نَرَى اللَّهَ جَهْرَةً—"until we see Allah face to face." Their sarcastic commentary was essentially: "If Allah wants us to kill our own people, let Him tell us Himself. You just hate our people. So we're only going to believe when we see Allah directly."

Allah ﷻ was deeply offended by this statement, and for two reasons. First, it was blasphemy against Allah ﷻ—demanding to see Him physically. But before that, it was complete disregard and doubt that the messenger speaks on behalf of Allah ﷻ. This entire surah centers on iman in revelation, and here they showed utter disrespect for both the message and the messenger. They did not even spare Musa (AS).

فَأَخَذَتْكُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ—a thunderbolt, an explosive sound, seized all of them. It killed every single one. وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ—while you were watching. They were just standing there when this loud explosion came, and everyone was gone.

translation: Then We revived you after your death that perhaps you would be grateful
commentary:
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَـٰكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

Then We raised you after your death so perhaps you would be grateful.

Notice the progression. The first time, Allah ﷻ lovingly pardoned them—عَفَوْنَا (We pardoned)—so they could be grateful. The second time, He said He would accept their tawbah, but they had to kill their own people. A little harsher. The third time? He killed them all and brought them back to life. The bar keeps rising. Sometimes when a teacher sees that softness is not producing results—when the student takes advantage of kindness—the teacher must become progressively firmer.

If Allah ﷻ had not raised them, what would have been their fate? The Day of Judgment was next, and that would have been the end. The fact that He raised them again gave them still another opportunity—not to avoid Hell (talking back to Allah's messenger was enough for that), but to have a chance to change.

translation: And We shaded you with clouds and sent down to you manna and quails, [saying], "Eat from the good things with which We have provided you." And they wronged Us not - but they were [only] wronging themselves
commentary:
وَظَلَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْغَمَامَ وَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ ۖ وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ

And We shaded you with clouds and sent down to you manna and quails, [saying], "Eat from the good things with which We have provided you." And they did not wrong Us, but they were [only] wronging themselves.

Now Allah ﷻ takes us chronologically backward in the narrative. The moment the Israelites crossed into the desert, they faced an environment with no water, no vegetation, no shade. Thousands of men, women and children were in the middle of the desert. Can you imagine the chaos? How would they have rationed water?

Allah ﷻ sent الْغَمَامَ—dark, sheltering clouds. For Arabic students, this is an interesting word. Its plural is غَمَامٌ, its singular is غَمَامَةٌ—the same root as the word used in غُلَامٌ (young boy), because both provide covering and protection. These were friendly, protective clouds that shaded them from the scorching desert sun.

وَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَنَّ وَالسَّلْوَىٰ—We sent down upon you manna and salwa. المَنّ was a grain-like substance. Fine particles would settle on the sand like pollen. Normally sand produces nothing, but somehow this substance would produce vegetation for them in the middle of the desert. They now had crops to make wheat and bread from.

السَّلْوَىٰ was a type of quail-like bird. Naturally, if your children try to catch a pigeon, what happens? It flies away. But unnaturally, these birds would come to their camp and stay seated until captured. It was like free food delivered to them. They had bread and meat—carbohydrates and protein both covered.

كُلُوا مِن طَيِّبَاتِ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ—consume from the good and pure things We have provided you. There is a beautiful blessing embedded in this phrase. When you consume good and pure things, it should lead to your own spiritual purity. The path to becoming grateful to Allah ﷻ is to eat and drink from the good and pure provisions He provides.

وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا—they did not wrong Us. وَلَٰكِن كَانُوا أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ—however, they were the ones who were continually wronging themselves.

They spent forty years in diaspora—a famous period in Jewish history—there in the desert with manna and salwa. That cowardly generation which had refused to fight when Musa (AS) commanded them eventually passed away. A new generation was raised in the desert, tougher than their enslaved parents.

translation: And [recall] when We said, "Enter this city and eat from it wherever you will in [ease and] abundance, and enter the gate bowing humbly and say, 'Relieve us of our burdens.' We will [then] forgive your sins for you, and We will increase the doers of good [in goodness and reward]
commentary:
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا ٱدْخُلُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ رَغَدًا وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا وَقُولُوا۟ حِطَّةٌ نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطَـٰيَـٰكُمْ ۚ وَسَنَزِيدُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

And when We said, "Enter this city and eat from it wherever you will in [ease and] abundance, and enter the gate bowing humbly and say, 'Relieve us of our burdens.' We will [then] forgive your sins for you, and We will increase the doers of good [in goodness and reward]."

In this new generation, Allah ﷻ raised up a new messenger—most commonly identified as Yusha (Joshua). He was commanded to conquer that same city their fathers had been too afraid to enter, the city full of tyrannical people, powerful fighters whom Allah ﷻ called الْجَبَّارِينَ (the tyrants). But this youth, raised in the desert, were tougher. They had not been enslaved like their fathers. They said, "We've had enough of this desert life. We're going in."

Allah ﷻ gave them victory. Then He commanded: فَكُلُوا مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ رَغَدًا—eat from it wherever you wish, freely and abundantly. The word رَغَدًا describes carefree, unrestricted eating. This echoes an earlier command from Surah Al-Baqarah regarding Adam (AS) in Paradise: وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا (eat from it freely wherever you wish). But notice the order is reversed here. In Paradise, the carefree nature (رَغَدًا) was mentioned first because resources in Paradise are truly unlimited. Here, it comes after because even though they can eat freely, the resources of this world are still limited compared to Paradise.

وَادْخُلُوا الْبَابَ سُجَّدًا—and enter the gate in a state of prostration. Old cities had massive gates, and conquerors would parade through them in victory. Allah ﷻ commanded them to enter with humility, bowing low. This doesn't mean crawling on your hands and knees, but rather with your head on the neck of your animal—your horse, camel or donkey—entering in a posture of complete humility.

وَقُولُوا حِطَّةٌ—and say "hitta" (forgiveness). Forgiveness for what? When Allah ﷻ originally commanded your fathers to fight, what did they say? "You and your Lord go fight!" For forty years you spent in the desert because of that cowardice. Now Allah ﷻ has given you another opportunity, and this time He has granted you victory. But victory does not equal automatic forgiveness. Victory is still a test. You should enter the city asking Allah ﷻ for forgiveness.

نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطَايَاكُمْ—We will forgive your sins. وَسَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ—and We will increase those who excel in goodness. Forgiveness is the first step. Once sins are forgiven, someone with a sinful past can still become among the محسنين —those at the highest state of faith, those who truly recognize Allah's presence at all times. Someone who lived a life of sin might think, "I can never reach that level." But look at what Allah ﷻ says: Start with seeking forgiveness, and I will increase you to the level of ihsan. You have that potential.

translation: But those who wronged changed [those words] to a statement other than that which had been said to them, so We sent down upon those who wronged a punishment from the sky because they were defiantly disobeying
commentary:
فَبَدَّلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى قِيلَ لَهُمْ فَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ

But those who wronged changed [those words] to a statement other than that which had been said to them, so We sent down upon those who wronged a punishment from the sky because they were defiantly disobedient.

The word they were told to say was حِطَّةٌ —forgiveness. But فَبَدَّلَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا قَوْلًا—those who did wrong replaced the word. They changed it to حِنْطَةٌ —wheat. "We want more wheat!" They made it into a joke. As they entered the city, they whispered and chuckled to each other, mocking the command of Allah ﷻ.

Notice how the punishments have escalated. The first time they committed a major crime, Allah ﷻ told them to execute their own people. The second time, Allah ﷻ did not ask that of them—He would punish them Himself.

فَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا رِجْزًا مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ—We sent down upon those who wronged a plague from the sky. The word رِجْز refers to an incapacitating disease. Some scholars describe it as a camel disease that leaves the animal unable to move, shaking and useless. Others say this disease was so severe that people's skin turned inside out.

بِمَا كَانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ—because of the corruption they continually engaged in. Allah ﷻ did not simply say بِمَا فَسَقُوا (because they sinned). He said بِمَا كَانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ—because they had been persistently engaged in corruption. This harsh punishment came because this last act of mockery was merely the final straw. They had been continuously sinning, and this was just the culmination.

translation: And [recall] when Moses prayed for water for his people, so We said, "Strike with your staff the stone." And there gushed forth from it twelve springs, and every people knew its watering place. "Eat and drink from the provision of Allah, and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption
commentary:
۞ وَإِذِ ٱسْتَسْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ فَقُلْنَا ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْحَجَرَ ۖ فَٱنفَجَرَتْ مِنْهُ ٱثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا ۖ قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ ۖ كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ

And [recall] when Musa asked for water for his people, so We said, "Strike with your staff the stone." And there gushed forth from it twelve springs, and every people knew its watering place. "Eat and drink from the provision of Allah, and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption."

Now we move further back chronologically, to before the death of Musa (AS). The Israelites were still in the desert. The clouds had come, the manna and salwa were being provided, but there remained a shortage of water. Musa (AS) asked Allah ﷻ for water for his nation.

اضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ الْحَجَرَ—strike with your staff the rock. Strike this particular rock, and only with your staff. فَانفَجَرَتْ مِنْهُ اثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا—then twelve springs burst forth from it.

Notice the word انفَجَرَتْ. It comes from the root فَجَرَ, and it is passive in form. Allah ﷻ did not say "Musa made the water come out" or "the staff made the water come out." Instead, He says the twelve springs exploded or burst out on their own. He did not make Musa (AS) the subject of this verb because this was a miracle from Allah ﷻ alone.

قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ—every group of people knew their drinking place. Why? Because there were twelve tribes, so twelve separate bodies of water emerged. Each tribe had its designated spring.

كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا مِن رِّزْقِ اللَّهِ—eat and drink from the provision of Allah. Earlier, Allah ﷻ said كُلُوا مِن طَيِّبَاتِ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ (consume from the good and pure things We have provided). Now He simply says: eat and drink from Allah's provision.

وَلَا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ—and do not go about committing abuse in the land, spreading corruption. The word عَثَا comes from عَاثٍ—a description of a person who is foolish and reckless, someone who does stupid things. In old folktales, there is always the character of the big, goofy person who constantly does foolish things. العَاثِي الغَبِي—the big, dumb guy.

Notice how even the language Allah ﷻ uses with them has become harsher. Before He said, "Eat from the good things." Now He says, "Eat and drink, but don't be idiots anymore." The word مُفْسِدِينَ (spreaders of corruption) is mentioned alongside فِي الْأَرْضِ (in the earth), so there is a doubling: corruption mentioned twice. Don't be corrupt yourselves, and don't cause corruption in society as a result.

translation: And [recall] when you said, "O Moses, we can never endure one [kind of] food. So call upon your Lord to bring forth for us from the earth its green herbs and its cucumbers and its garlic and its lentils and its onions." [Moses] said, "Would you exchange what is better for what is less? Go into [any] settlement and indeed, you will have what you have asked." And they were covered with humiliation and poverty and returned with anger from Allah [upon them]. That was because they [repeatedly] disbelieved in the signs of Allah and killed the prophets without right. That was because they disobeyed and were [habitually] transgressing
commentary:
وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَن نَّصْبِرَ عَلَىٰ طَعَامٍ وَٰحِدٍ فَٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُخْرِجْ لَنَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مِنۢ بَقْلِهَا وَقِثَّآئِهَا وَفُومِهَا وَعَدَسِهَا وَبَصَلِهَا ۖ قَالَ أَتَسْتَبْدِلُونَ ٱلَّذِى هُوَ أَدْنَىٰ بِٱلَّذِى هُوَ خَيْرٌ ۚ ٱهْبِطُوا۟ مِصْرًا فَإِنَّ لَكُم مَّا سَأَلْتُمْ ۗ وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ وَٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ

### The Complaint: Missing What They Left Behind

After some time in the wilderness, the Israelites approached Prophet Musa (AS) with a complaint: "We can no longer be patient with one kind of food." The repetition of manna and salwa had become tiresome to them. They said, "Make dua to your master, call upon your Rabb for us, that He may bring out for us from what the earth grows—its herbs, cucumbers, garlic, lentils and onions."

Notice the telling detail in their request: "Make dua to *your* master." They could have made dua themselves. They should have approached Allah ﷻ directly. But they positioned themselves as intermediaries who needed Musa (AS) to petition on their behalf. This reveals their lack of genuine connection with Allah ﷻ. They had no real iman, no actual relationship with their Creator. They still operated under a false conception that only their prophet could access Allah ﷻ, that they didn't need to deal with Him directly.

Allah ﷻ is teaching us through this narrative that we must never develop such an attitude. We cannot think that the messenger will take care of our relationship with Allah ﷻ while we remain distant. Yes, we honor and follow the Prophet ﷺ, but we also maintain a direct, intimate connection with Allah ﷻ. From the very beginning of this surah, the believers are characterized by their direct establishment of salah—a connection with whom? With Allah ﷻ Himself, five times every single day.

### The Deeper Problem: Longing for Slavery

The list they presented—herbs, cucumbers, garlic, lentils and onions—reveals something troubling. How did they know the names of these specific vegetables? Not from the desert. They learned these names and developed these tastes back when they lived in Egypt. They were sitting in the desert one day eating manna, and nostalgia overtook them. They gave this request serious thought before approaching Musa (AS), creating quite an elaborate menu.

But here's the devastating reality: when you miss the food from prison, you're essentially saying you miss prison itself. When they asked for the food of Egypt, they thought they were simply requesting a dietary change. What they failed to realize was that the simple food Allah ﷻ had given them—food they should have been grateful for—was a small price to pay for something priceless: freedom.

In asking for their old food, they were unknowingly asking for slavery again. They didn't even recognize how foolish this was. The previous ayat had already warned: don't be stupid, don't act foolishly. Now they were saying possibly the most foolish thing yet.

### Musa's Penetrating Response

Musa (AS) is highly intelligent and spiritually perceptive. He saw this request for what it truly was, and he called it out immediately: "Do you seek to replace that which is inferior with that which is better?"

Notice that he didn't say "food for food" or even "vegetables for manna." He used the singular form—*alladhi* (that which). For Musa (AS), this wasn't about comparing food items. It was about freedom, dignity, respect and independence versus subjugation. They were proposing to trade away all of that for familiar vegetables.

He was essentially asking: Have you forgotten? You used to eat those vegetables, yes, but your babies were killed every other year. Have you not forgotten what that life actually meant?

For someone who has been traumatized by an experience, even the slightest reminder of it should be disturbing. The fact that they were sitting back reminiscing about "that time" revealed that Firaun had truly done a number on them psychologically. Modern psychology recognizes what's called "hostage syndrome"—when a hostage, after prolonged captivity, begins to identify with their captor, becomes submissive to them, even develops a strange loyalty to them. This is the mentality the Israelites had developed. They were missing "back in the day," longing for the Egyptian menu.

### The Sarcasm in the Response

Musa's response contains a subtle but cutting sarcasm: "Go down into *misran*." This requires some explanation of Arabic grammar. *Misr* (مِصْر) with tanween means "a town" or "countryside" in Arabic. *Misr* (مِصْر) without tanween is a proper noun—it means Egypt specifically.

Musa (AS) didn't directly say "Go back to Egypt," but he came remarkably close. He said *misran*—"go to some countryside"—using the word that sounds nearly identical to Egypt. It's as if he was saying, "What you're asking for is like asking for Egypt again." The sarcasm is evident: "Go find a countryside somewhere—you'll definitely get what you asked for."

But notice he didn't say "you'll get the food." He said "you'll get *what you asked for*." Musa (AS) understood the deeper implications of their request and knew the consequences that would follow.

### The Consequences: Three Curses Return

Before examining those consequences, we need to recall what made life in Egypt unbearable. There were three elements:
1. **Dhillah (الذِّلَّة)** - Humiliation
2. **Maskanah (الْمَسْكَنَة)** - Powerlessness, being trapped and unable to change their situation
3. The rage of a tyrannical king

The word *maskanah* comes from the root meaning "to stop" or "to be stationary." It describes someone stuck somewhere, unable to get out, completely trapped. Not only were they humiliated in Egypt, but they couldn't do anything about it. They couldn't change their circumstances. And to make matters worse, every other year they experienced a raging king's wrath.

Now observe what Allah ﷻ says happened when they entered a new town: "Humiliation was struck upon them." The first problem of Egyptian life—humiliation—returned immediately. "And maskanah"—they became powerless again, incapable of changing their situation, just as in Egypt.

But the third element became far worse: "They drew upon themselves rage from Allah ﷻ." Not from a king this time—from Allah ﷻ Himself. They went from the frying pan into the fire. The ramifications of their foolishness grew progressively more severe with each consequence.

### The Root Causes: Disbelief and Transgression

Allah ﷻ then explains why these punishments befell them: "That is because they used to disbelieve in the signs of Allah ﷻ."

There are three core aspects of iman: faith in Allah ﷻ, faith in the message and faith in the afterlife. Correspondingly, there are three types of kufr: rejecting Allah ﷻ, rejecting the message and rejecting the afterlife.

This ayah specifically highlights: "They used to disbelieve in the ayat of Allah ﷻ." Which aspect of iman does this address? Faith in the message. But the ayat didn't come to them as a book on paper. How did Allah's ayat reach them? Through prophets. So disbelieving in Allah's ayat necessarily implies their disregard for and rejection of prophets.

This is a historical account, but it carried immediate relevance. Jews were standing before the Prophet ﷺ listening to this recitation. Allah ﷻ was essentially warning them: "Are you doing this again? Do you remember what happened last time? Do you remember what you went through? Why are you repeating the same mistakes?"

"And they used to kill the prophets without any right to do so." The ayah uses the word *nabiyyeen* (prophets) rather than *mursaleen* (messengers). Some scholars argue that one distinction between prophets and messengers is that prophets could be killed, while messengers were always granted dominance by Allah ﷻ, at least in a worldly sense.

Were they plotting to kill a prophet in Medina during this very revelation? SubhanAllah, they were being called out: this isn't the first time you've attempted this. You've done it before, and look at what happened. All those trials you cite as evidence of your victimhood didn't just happen to you—you brought them upon yourselves.

"That is because they disobeyed." Disobeyed whom? While we could say they disobeyed Allah ﷻ, the practical disobedience they engaged in was specifically toward the message and the messengers sent to them.

"And they used to transgress." The Arabic يَعْتَدُونَ carries the meaning of crossing boundaries, violating limits repeatedly. This wasn't occasional transgression—it was a pattern of behavior.

I've spent significant time highlighting these elements because the ayah that follows is one of the most commonly misunderstood passages in the entire Quran.

translation: Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews or Christians or Sabeans [before Prophet Muhammad] - those [among them] who believed in Allah and the Last Day and did righteousness - will have their reward with their Lord, and no fear will there be concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

### The Universal Criteria

"Indeed, those who believed, and those who were Jews, and the Christians, and the Sabians—whoever believed in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day and did righteous deeds, then they will have their reward with their Rabb, and there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve."

This ayah has become one of the most misunderstood verses of the Quran. Let me address the common misinterpretation first, then unpack the proper understanding within its context.

### The Common Misinterpretation

Some people look at this ayah and conclude: "Look, Allah ﷻ mentions Jews, Christians, Sabians and Muslims—four groups. Then He ﷻ lists three criteria: belief in Allah ﷻ, belief in the Last Day and doing good deeds. If you meet these three expectations, there's no fear and no grief. Therefore, it doesn't matter which religion you follow—so long as you believe in God, believe in some afterlife and try to be a good person, you're fine. We're all one big happy family, all 'children of God.'"

This interpretation represents a fundamental misreading that tears the ayah from its surrounding context.

### Understanding Within Context

First, examine what this ayah actually says. Four groups are mentioned, but three expectations are explicitly stated:
1. Belief in Allah ﷻ
2. Belief in the Last Day  
3. Righteous deeds

Notice what seems to be missing compared to the three core aspects of faith we discussed: faith in Allah ﷻ, faith in the message and faith in the Last Day. This ayah mentions Allah ﷻ and the Last Day, but instead of explicitly mentioning "the message," it says "righteous deeds."

We need to explore what "righteous deeds" means in the context of this surah.

### The Context of Revelation Throughout the Surah

From the very beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, the concept of iman has been intrinsically connected to revealed guidance. Let me trace this theme:

- "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those who are conscious of Allah ﷻ" (2:2) - Revelation
- "Those who believe in what was revealed to you and what was revealed before you" (2:4) - Revelation  
- "Believe as the people have believed" (2:13) - Following revelation
- "Those who purchased misguidance in exchange for guidance" (2:16) - Rejecting revelation
- "Allah ﷻ took away their light" (2:17) - What was that light? Revelation
- "If you are in doubt about what We have sent down upon Our slave, then produce a surah like it" (2:23) - Revelation, with the challenge that disbelieving it makes one a kafir
- "As for those who truly believe, they know it is truth from their Rabb" (2:26) - Revelation
- "He misguides many with it and guides many with it" (2:26) - With what? Revelation
- "Whenever guidance comes to you from Me" (2:38) - What comes? Revelation
- "Fulfill My covenant and I will fulfill your covenant" (2:40) - A covenant made where? In revelation
- "Believe in what I have sent down, confirming what you already have" (2:41) - Revelation, with the warning that rejection equals kufr
- "Musa (AS) said: 'Allah ﷻ commands you to sacrifice a cow'" (2:67) - Revelation
- "You wronged yourselves by worshiping the calf; turn in repentance to your Creator" (2:54) - Revelation showing them how to restore faith

The ayah immediately preceding our passage states: "They used to disbelieve in the ayat of Allah ﷻ and kill the prophets." Everything centers on revelation.

The ayah immediately following continues: "When We raised the mountain of Tur above you and took your covenant: 'Hold firmly to what We have given you with great strength, and remember what is in it so that you may be protected'" (2:63). What were they to hold onto? Revelation. How would they stay safe? By remembering revelation—specifically, the promise of the coming messenger found within it.

### What "Righteous Deeds" Really Means

So when this ayah says "whoever believed in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day and did righteous deeds," we must ask: what defines a righteous deed? Throughout this entire surah, the contrast has been between deeds that align with revelation and deeds that violate it. All the bad deeds highlighted so far have been violations of Allah's ayat, transgressions against revealed guidance.

When Allah ﷻ says "righteous deeds," He doesn't mean your personal concept of good deeds or society's evolving standards. He means deeds defined as righteous by revelation itself. The standard for what constitutes righteous action comes exclusively from Allah's guidance delivered through His messengers.

### The Three Criteria Properly Understood

Now let's examine each criterion more carefully:

**1. Belief in Allah ﷻ**

Does Allah ﷻ accept just any vague concept of a divine being? A Christian says they believe in God. A Jew says they believe in God. A Hindu says they believe in God. Even an agnostic might concede "there may be some deity."

But has Allah ﷻ been vague about who He is? Has He been particular about His nature and attributes since the time of Adam (AS)? Absolutely. Every prophet came with a specific, consistent understanding of Allah's nature—His Oneness, His attributes, His rights over creation.

When this ayah says "whoever believed in Allah ﷻ," it doesn't mean any concept of God that someone might hold. It means the specific, accurate understanding of Allah ﷻ that He has revealed about Himself through His messengers. You cannot truly believe in Allah ﷻ the way you're supposed to until revelation teaches you who He is.

**2. Belief in the Last Day**

Has the afterlife been described only to us, or was it taught to Musa (AS), Ibrahim (AS) and all the prophets before and after? Is it a different afterlife that we're supposed to believe in versus what those messengers taught? Of course not.

Just because someone claims "I believe in an afterlife" doesn't automatically fulfill this criterion. The Last Day must be believed in according to how Allah ﷻ has described it through revelation—not according to your own imagination or cultural traditions. The concept of the afterlife, resurrection, judgment, Paradise and Hellfire must align with what the prophets taught.

**3. Righteous Deeds**

You cannot perform truly righteous deeds on your own because you don't inherently know what they are. You may have some general sense of ethics, but you don't have complete, divine guidance about what truly constitutes righteousness. That comprehensive understanding only comes from revelation.

### The Essential Unity of the Criteria

Here's the critical point: even looking at this ayah in isolation, you cannot ignore the necessity of consulting revelation for all three criteria. You need revelation to understand:
- Who Allah ﷻ truly is
- What the Last Day actually entails  
- What deeds are genuinely righteous

### The Meaning in Historical Context

What, then, is this ayah actually saying? It's stating that throughout history—whether people were followers of earlier prophets, or were Jews in the time of Musa (AS), or were Christians following Isa (AS), or were Sabians who maintained remnants of Ibrahim's teachings—*whoever among them believed these three things according to how they were supposed to be believed at that time*, they were saved.

The word translated as "Christians" is actually نَصَارَىٰ, which literally means "helpers" or "those who turned toward Allah ﷻ in seeking aid." Similarly, هَادُوا۟ means those who were committed to guidance, who turned toward it. صَّـٰبِـِٔينَ has among its meanings "those who maintained their fitrah," the natural state of purity.

The ayah is saying: throughout all eras, whoever followed the revelation given to their time, believed in Allah ﷻ as He revealed Himself, believed in the Last Day as described in that revelation and performed righteous deeds as defined by that revelation—they achieved salvation.

### The Continuity of Revelation

This brings us to the present reality: we now have the final revelation, the Quran, and the final messenger, Muhammad ﷺ. The same principle applies. Salvation requires:
- Believing in Allah ﷻ as He has described Himself in this final revelation
- Believing in the Last Day as described in this revelation  
- Performing righteous deeds as defined by this revelation

The ayah doesn't undermine the necessity of following revelation—it affirms it across all ages. Every nation that was saved was saved by following the revelation given to them. Now that the final revelation has arrived, salvation requires following it.

### The Comfort in the Conclusion

The ayah concludes with the same terms used at the beginning of this series of narratives: "No fear concerning them, nor will they grieve." These are the two promises made to the believers—*la khawfun alayhim* (no fear) and *wa la hum yahzanun* (and they won't grieve). This connects back to the original promise of what iman protects us from.

This ayah, far from being a universalist statement that undermines the importance of prophethood and revelation, actually reinforces that the path to salvation has always been—and will always be—through believing in and following the guidance that Allah ﷻ sends to humanity through His chosen messengers. The revelation may come in different forms across different eras, but the principle remains constant: true faith requires submission to Allah's revealed guidance, not to our own preferences or the customs we've inherited.

translation: And [recall] when We took your covenant, [O Children of Israel, to abide by the Torah] and We raised over you the mount, [saying], "Take what We have given you with determination and remember what is in it that perhaps you may become righteous
commentary:
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ ٱلطُّورَ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ

Before examining this ayah, we should recognize something essential about the opening passages of Surah Al-Baqarah. The surah began by offering guidance to all those who meet the criteria of taqwa. Those who commit themselves to this guidance are considered among the muflihoon - the ultimately successful. These principles emerge repeatedly throughout these accounts, whether discussing the Jews, followers of Shaytan, or any other community. The criteria never change. The principles of salvation remain constant.

This stands as one of the most fundamental distinctions between Islam and other religions. The Jews essentially eliminated the concept of salvation for practical purposes - a significant portion of their population denies the afterlife altogether, while others believe only Jews will enter it. The majority Christian position holds that salvation belongs exclusively to those who believe in Christ. Yet Allah ﷻ reveals that originally, salvation was one unified concept, and Islam continues this original teaching.

The word مِيثَـٰق deserves special attention. While the most general word for promise in Arabic is وَعْد, and the word for agreement is عَهْد, mithaq comes from the root withaq - meaning to tie something, to chain things together. A mithaq represents a contract in which both parties clearly understand what they're getting into, including all the fine print. Allah ﷻ calls our relationship with revelation a mithaq because He made everything clear. If something remains unclear to someone, the fault lies with them alone, not with the clarity Allah ﷻ provided.

Picture this scene: a nation unaccustomed to listening, rebellious like an unruly student who refuses to settle down. Allah ﷻ literally lifts Mount Tur and suspends it above them. Imagine the terror, the immediate attention this commands. Now He says: take revelation seriously. خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ - "Hold on to what We have given you with strength." If there was ever a moment when you would say "Yes, absolutely, I understand," this would be it.

The ayah concludes with وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ - "And remember what is in it so that you may protect yourselves." Why does protecting yourself become particularly relevant in this ayah? Because there's a mountain hanging over your head. When you genuinely want to protect yourself, the answer is to hold onto revelation and remember what's contained within it.

This truth applies to us as well. Just because the mountain isn't physically hovering over our heads doesn't mean we shouldn't be people committed to revelation who remember what's in it.

translation: Then you turned away after that. And if not for the favor of Allah upon you and His mercy, you would have been among the losers
commentary:
ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ فَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَكُنتُم مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ - "Then all of you turned away even after that." Even after witnessing the mountain suspended above them, even after Allah ﷻ directly addressed them with such a dramatic sign, they still turned away.

فَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ - "Had it not been for the special favor of Allah upon you and His mercy." Notice the dual mention: both fadl (special favor) and rahmah (mercy). These are gifts that can only come from Him, mercies that belong exclusively to Allah ﷻ.

لَكُنتُم مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ - "You would surely have been from among the losers." There's no doubt about this outcome. Without Allah's intervention, their fate was sealed.

translation: And you had already known about those who transgressed among you concerning the sabbath, and We said to them, "Be apes, despised
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱعْتَدَوْا۟ مِنكُمْ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ فَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ

Allah ﷻ addresses the Jews of Madinah directly: "You already knew about those who violated among you on the Sabbath." This isn't new information - they know this famous story from their own history. You already know what happened to those who played games with the Sabbath commandment.

The message becomes clear: playing with new revelation is no different from playing with old revelation. If you learned the lessons from that story, why don't you apply them here?

فَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ - "We said to them: Become rejected apes." They were literally transformed into monkeys. In ancient cities, apes and monkeys were pests that would come and steal fruit, constantly being shooed away - khasi'een, turned away and humiliated. Those who violated the Sabbath were turned into these very creatures.

This represents a critical lesson about revelation. Violating revelation leads to humiliation. You know this from your own revelation, Allah ﷻ is saying. Why would you think the results would be any different for you?

Notice something significant here: the violation of Sabbath is an evil deed, while its observation is a good deed. The previous ayah mentioned وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - "and they did good deeds." Here's an example of such a good deed. But here's what makes it fascinating: you can only know that Sabbath should be observed and not violated through revelation itself. You cannot discover this truth through independent reasoning. The obligation to refrain from work on Saturday can only be known through revelation.

translation: And We made it a deterrent punishment for those who were present and those who succeeded [them] and a lesson for those who fear Allah
commentary:
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا نَكَـٰلًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهَا وَمَا خَلْفَهَا وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

Allah ﷻ made their punishment even more severe by turning it into a lasting lesson. فَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا نَكَـٰلًا - "We made it a deterrent." The word nakal refers to making an example of someone so that everyone else corrects their behavior. A teacher might single out one student to make their life difficult, creating a deterrent effect for all the others. When one student gets expelled, it sends shockwaves through the entire school.

لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهَا وَمَا خَلْفَهَا - "For whoever was right in front of it and whoever would come afterwards." This punishment served as a terrifying warning for those who witnessed it directly, and for all future generations. Parents would teach this story to their children: Don't do this. Allah ﷻ transformed our grandfathers. We saw it with our own eyes.

وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ - "And a hard-hitting advice for people of taqwa." This lesson penetrates deep, but only for those who truly want to protect themselves, those committed to consciousness of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And [recall] when Moses said to his people, "Indeed, Allah commands you to slaughter a cow." They said, "Do you take us in ridicule?" He said, "I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تَذْبَحُوا۟ بَقَرَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَتَّخِذُنَا هُزُوًا ۖ قَالَ أَعُوذُ بِٱللَّهِ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ

A murder has occurred among the Bani Israel. The perpetrator remains unknown, and the situation threatens to explode into civil war. Accusations fly back and forth. They come to Musa (AS) seeking a verdict. He turns to Allah ﷻ for revelation, then returns with an answer that seems completely unrelated: Allah ﷻ commands you to slaughter a cow.

Before we continue, notice this is the first time a cow is mentioned in this context. Earlier in the surah, another calf was mentioned - the golden calf they worshiped. Part of Allah's test involves seeing where their loyalties truly lie. He doesn't command them to slaughter a goat or a chicken. He says to slaughter a cow. Later we'll discover what color this cow must be - yellow. This detail is remarkable because their loyalties are being tested. Allah ﷻ wants to ensure the calf-worship is completely purged from their system.

Their response reveals their spiritual state: أَتَتَّخِذُنَا هُزُوًا - "Are you taking us as a joke?" There's a murder investigation happening and you want us to go slaughter a cow? What's the matter with you? They assume their prophet is either joking with them or speaking unintelligently.

Musa's (AS) response teaches us something profound about prophetic character. قَالَ أَعُوذُ بِٱللَّهِ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ - "He said: I seek Allah's refuge that I should become from the ignorant." The word jahileen here doesn't just mean those who lack knowledge. It refers to those who lose their temper, who cannot control their emotions.

We know from other passages that Musa (AS) had a temper. When they accuse him of joking around after everything they've put him through, he could easily lose his composure. So he genuinely asks Allah's refuge - not just declaring "I'm not ignorant," but actually seeking divine protection from losing his cool with his people.

The patience required to deal with such a nation is extraordinary. They tested the limits of a prophet known for his passionate nature. Eventually, Musa (AS) would reach the point where he says to Allah ﷻ: just get me away from these people. I can't deal with them anymore. I want nothing to do with them. For a messenger to say this about his own nation is incredible.

Some might say Nuh (AS) was tested for 950 years. True, but Nuh's people didn't believe in him. These people believed in Musa (AS) and still gave him this torment. In another passage, Musa (AS) asks them: "Why are you causing me pain? You already know that I am a messenger of Allah ﷻ. How can you do this?" It doesn't make sense. How far can people take their rebellion?

translation: They said, "Call upon your Lord to make clear to us what it is." [Moses] said, "[Allah] says, 'It is a cow which is neither old nor virgin, but median between that,' so do what you are commanded
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا فَارِضٌ وَلَا بِكْرٌ عَوَانٌۢ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ فَٱفْعَلُوا۟ مَا تُؤْمَرُونَ

قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ - "They said: Call upon your master to clarify for us what it is." They need further elaboration about this cow.

Remember, Musa (AS) didn't just speak to Allah ﷻ casually like "hold on, let me get back to you." He had to ascend Mount Tur - a full day's journey - speak with Allah ﷻ, then descend and relay the message. Now they're asking for clarification, which means another day's journey up the mountain.

قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ - "He said: Indeed, He - He in fact is the one who says." Notice how Musa (AS) keeps emphasizing this. إِنَّهُۥ - It is Allah ﷻ. The double emphasis is crucial. He keeps telling them: this isn't coming from me, it's coming from Allah ﷻ. That's why their question "Are you making us a joke?" was itself a form of disbelief. They thought this was Musa's idea, when Musa (AS) keeps insisting it's directly from Allah ﷻ.

إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا فَارِضٌ وَلَا بِكْرٌ عَوَانٌۢ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ - "It is a cow that is neither too old nor too young, right in the middle between the two."

فَٱفْعَلُوا۟ مَا تُؤْمَرُونَ - "Just do what you've been commanded." The verb fa'ala means to do something without overthinking it - just execute the command. Amina means to think about something and perform it as a conscious action. Here, Musa (AS) is telling them: stop analyzing and just do it. Stop asking questions and simply obey.

translation: They said, "Call upon your Lord to show us what is her color." He said, "He says, 'It is a yellow cow, bright in color - pleasing to the observers
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا لَوْنُهَا ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ صَفْرَآءُ فَاقِعٌ لَّوْنُهَا تَسُرُّ ٱلنَّـٰظِرِينَ

But they're enjoying this. They whisper to each other: "This is fun. He's going to go on another trip. Let's ask him again."

قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا لَوْنُهَا - "They said: Call upon your master to clarify for us what color it is." They got the age requirement, but the color seems important too.

قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ صَفْرَآءُ - "He said: Indeed, He says it is a yellow cow." Again, the emphasis: Allah ﷻ is the one saying this.

فَاقِعٌ لَّوْنُهَا - "Brilliant in its color." The word faqi' describes something brilliant in texture or color, like brilliant gold.

تَسُرُّ ٱلنَّـٰظِرِينَ - "It pleases those who look at it, it captures the eye." The word nadhara means to stare at something, not just to see it (basara). When you're on a road trip and spot an unusual farm animal, you don't just glance and move on. You stare. Children point and exclaim. A yellow cow that's brilliant in color - people can't take their eyes off it.

Here's what this means: this won't be some obscure cow that's impossible to find. If there was a yellow cow somewhere, it would be famous. Everyone would know about it. Yet notice what's happening - the more questions they ask, the harder the instructions become. Had they simply accepted the initial command and sacrificed any cow, it would have been easy. But each additional question narrows the criteria and makes their task more difficult.

translation: They said, "Call upon your Lord to make clear to us what it is. Indeed, [all] cows look alike to us. And indeed we, if Allah wills, will be guided
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ إِنَّ ٱلْبَقَرَ تَشَـٰبَهَ عَلَيْنَا وَإِنَّآ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَمُهْتَدُونَ

قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ - "They said: Call upon your master to clarify for us what it is."

إِنَّ ٱلْبَقَرَ تَشَـٰبَهَ عَلَيْنَا - "This cow business is really confusing us. Cows look similar to us."

وَإِنَّآ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَمُهْتَدُونَ - "And we're telling you, if Allah wills, we will definitely be guided." Notice they didn't say "We're going to do this." They said "If Allah wills, we'll be guided."

This phrase reveals something dangerous about their mindset. We say insha'Allah about future events as an acknowledgment of Allah's control. But we don't say things like "May Allah make us believers, insha'Allah" in our supplications. We don't say "O Allah, make us believers if You will" - because that sounds like we're putting the blame on Allah ﷻ.

That's exactly the language they're using here. "If Allah wants, we'll be guided. If we're not guided, it's because Allah ﷻ didn't want us to be." They're creating a backup plan, an excuse. It's like saying "It's not our fault; it's Allah's fault if we're not guided."

translation: He said, "He says, 'It is a cow neither trained to plow the earth nor to irrigate the field, one free from fault with no spot upon her.' " They said, "Now you have come with the truth." So they slaughtered her, but they could hardly do it
commentary:
قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا ذَلُولٌ تُثِيرُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَا تَسْقِى ٱلْحَرْثَ مُسَلَّمَةٌ لَّا شِيَةَ فِيهَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ جِئْتَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ فَذَبَحُوهَا وَمَا كَادُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ

Musa (AS) returns once more: قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ - "He said: Indeed, He is the one who says."

إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا ذَلُولٌ تُثِيرُ ٱلْأَرْضَ - "It is a cow that has not been put to work, not domesticated to till the land."

وَلَا تَسْقِى ٱلْحَرْثَ - "And it doesn't water the crops." Farmers would put buckets on either side of a cow and make it carry water across the fields.

مُسَلَّمَةٌ - "It is well-built, strongly constructed." When a cow is well-built and muscular, that's precisely when you're supposed to put it to work. Remember the previous specifications: it's not too young and not too old - it's middle-aged. If it's too young, you can't make it work. If it's too old, it's ready to be slaughtered. The prime time to put a cow to work on the farm is during its middle age.

So this cow is middle-aged, strongly built, muscular - exactly the type that should be working - yet it's never been put to work. It's like having a powerful engine that's never been revved.

لَّا شِيَةَ فِيهَا - "It doesn't have a single blemish." Perfect in every way.

قَالُوا۟ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ جِئْتَ بِٱلْحَقِّ - "They said: Now you've come with the truth. Okay, okay, we got it."

Some narrations tell us that they finally found this cow. The situation had become so serious that they turned to this as a last resort. They didn't do it out of sincere faith - they just figured the murder problem wasn't getting solved, so they might as well try this whole slaughtering-a-cow thing.

They found this cow owned by a boy and his mother. He had inherited it from his father and refused to sell it at any price. Eventually they agreed: they would pay its weight in gold. What could have been a simple instruction - take any cow and be done with it - became incredibly expensive and difficult because they kept asking questions. They made it harder upon themselves.

This teaches us something the Prophet ﷺ emphasized in a famous hadith: "Indeed Allah ﷻ loves three things for you and hates three things for you." One of the three things He hates is kathrat as-su'al - asking too many questions. When you ask excessive questions, you only make your own life difficult. Look at the instructions and just follow them. Don't try to find exceptions or loopholes.

فَذَبَحُوهَا وَمَا كَادُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ - "Then they slaughtered it, and they almost didn't do it." Even then, with everything specified, they were still thinking twice about whether to obey.

translation: And [recall] when you slew a man and disputed over it, but Allah was to bring out that which you were concealing
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَتَلْتُمْ نَفْسًا فَٱدَّٰرَْٰٔتُمْ فِيهَا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ tells us why this entire procedure was necessary.

وَإِذْ قَتَلْتُمْ نَفْسًا - "When you killed a person."

فَٱدَّٰرَْٰٔتُمْ فِيهَا - "And you were pointing fingers at each other, sharing the blame with each other." Everyone stood around the corpse accusing others. "You did it. No, you didn't like him. You had a motive." Back and forth, the accusations flew.

وَٱللَّهُ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ - "And Allah is bringing out what you have been hiding." Notice the grammatical form - this is a nominal sentence (ismiyyah), indicating permanence and continuous action. Allah ﷻ is the One who brings out what people hide, always and forever.

The beauty of the rhetoric here is profound. Allah ﷻ didn't just say "Allah brought out who did it." The implication is that many people in this crowd already knew who the murderer was and were hiding it. Some of the criminals were standing right there among those who came to Musa (AS) seeking justice.
