But there's another layer. This passage is being revealed to the Jews of Madinah. They're hiding something too - their confirmation that Muhammad ﷺ is the messenger of Allah ﷻ. So even at the immediate level of revelation, the message is clear: bring out what you've been hiding.

translation: So, We said, "Strike the slain man with part of it." Thus does Allah bring the dead to life, and He shows you His signs that you might reason
commentary:
فَقُلْنَا ٱضْرِبُوهُ بِبَعْضِهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُحْىِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

فَقُلْنَا ٱضْرِبُوهُ بِبَعْضِهَا - "Then We said: Strike him with some of it." Strike the murdered corpse with the flesh of this slaughtered animal.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُحْىِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ - "That is how Allah brings the dead back to life. He gives them life."

وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ - "And He shows you His miraculous signs."

لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ - "So that you may apply your intellect, so that you may think clearly."

Remember what I mentioned about 'aql (intellect)? It's not merely an intellectual exercise. To truly apply your 'aql, you must put certain biases aside. When the Quran discusses biases, it typically refers to matters of the heart - whether greed, jealousy, anger, or any emotional prejudice. All of these are matters of the heart.

In this ayah, Allah ﷻ says "I did this so that you may be able to apply your intellect." But of course, they weren't able to apply their intellect properly. Why? Because to truly apply your 'aql, your heart has to be sound. Your heart has to be clean. The next ayah will explain what happened to their hearts.

translation: Then your hearts became hardened after that, being like stones or even harder. For indeed, there are stones from which rivers burst forth, and there are some of them that split open and water comes out, and there are some of them that fall down for fear of Allah. And Allah is not unaware of what you do
commentary:
ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَهِىَ كَٱلْحِجَارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنَ ٱلْحِجَارَةِ لَمَا يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَشَّقَّقُ فَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهُ ٱلْمَآءُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ - "Then your hearts became hard even after that." Even after witnessing a dead person return to life, you still didn't take Allah ﷻ and His message seriously.

فَهِىَ كَٱلْحِجَارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً - "And they became like stone, or even tougher in their stiffness."

This isn't the first time stone is mentioned in the Quran. Earlier, Allah ﷻ said: فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ - "Protect yourselves from the Fire whose fuel is people and stones" (2:24). You and your stone hearts can burn together. The stones - referring to idols - burn in Hell, and so do hardened hearts.

The heart is meant to be soft. Think of the parable in Surah An-Nur where the heart is compared to a glass lamp. Light is supposed to enter and shine through. When the heart becomes like a rock, what happens? No light enters and no light emanates. Someone becomes completely blocked off - nothing goes in, nothing comes out.

Your hearts became completely hard. At that point, no matter what advice you receive, what counsel you hear, what you witness with your own eyes - none of it penetrates.

Remember what Allah ﷻ said earlier: وَعَلَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ غِشَـٰوَةٌ - "And upon their eyes is a veil" (2:7). You would think that seeing a dead person return to life would lift any veil from anyone's eyes. If anything could remove spiritual blindness, it would be that. But once the veil is placed, once the heart is sealed, none of these faculties - the hearing, the seeing, the speech that should aid us toward guidance - none of them help anymore. No arguments penetrate. No miracles convince.

The heart is the center of it all. When the heart goes, everything goes with it.

وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ - "And Allah is not unaware of what you do." Despite their hardened hearts, despite their turning away, Allah ﷻ sees everything. He remains fully aware of all actions, and nothing escapes His knowledge.

translation: Do you covet [the hope, O believers], that they would believe for you while a party of them used to hear the words of Allah and then distort the Torah after they had understood it while they were knowing
commentary:
۞ أَفَتَطْمَعُونَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا۟ لَكُمْ وَقَدْ كَانَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُحَرِّفُونَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا عَقَلُوهُ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

This ayah addresses the Muslims directly, particularly the Companions (RA) who had moved to Madinah with certain expectations. The Quraysh of Makkah had no experience with scripture, prophets or divine revelation—these concepts were entirely foreign to them. Making them understand required building from scratch, which is why the call in Makkah was so difficult.

### The Companions' Hope

When the Prophet ﷺ and his Companions (RA) migrated to Madinah, they encountered the People of the Book—Jews who already had scripture, prophets and a connection to divine revelation. The Companions (RA) naturally thought this would make their task easier. These are people who already know about prophecy, revelation and divine guidance. They're waiting for a final messenger. What easier audience could there be for Islam?

The Companions (RA) were genuinely hopeful. Surely these people who possess knowledge of previous scriptures would recognize the truth when they heard it. They're already halfway there.

### A Reality Check

Allah ﷻ gives them a reality check: "Do you then hope that they will believe in you?" After everything that's been described—the miracles they witnessed, the hardening of their hearts—are you realistically hopeful?

Then comes the explanation: "When there was already a group among them who used to hear the speech of Allah ﷻ, then they would alter it after they had understood it, while they knew [what they were doing]."

This is the crucial point. Allah ﷻ emphasizes that they altered His words "after they understood it." If someone is copying a transcript of the Torah and makes an honest mistake, that's one thing. But these people changed it deliberately, fully understanding what they were doing. Their problem wasn't a lack of understanding—the previous ayah told us what was missing: their hearts had become hard. Understanding was present, but fear of Allah ﷻ and consciousness of consequences had vanished.

### When Hearts Override Minds

This teaches us something essential about human nature: your mind can understand perfectly, but if your heart is corrupted, that understanding means nothing. The heart drives everything. When the heart is good, the Prophet ﷺ taught us, everything is good. The heart is the driver, and the mind is the vehicle.

Think about students exhausting themselves to learn Arabic grammar, morphology and Quranic sciences. Why do they persist through mental exhaustion? Because their hearts say, "I love the Quran. I want to understand it." The smartest medical student exhausts their mind studying—but why? Because their heart says, "One day I'll save lives," or "One day I'll make my parents proud." The heart drives the mind's efforts.

When the heart is corrupted or hardened, even the sharpest intellect becomes a tool for distortion rather than guidance.

translation: And when they meet those who believe, they say, "We have believed"; but when they are alone with one another, they say, "Do you talk to them about what Allah has revealed to you so they can argue with you about it before your Lord?" Then will you not reason
commentary:
وَإِذَا لَقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتُحَدِّثُونَهُم بِمَا فَتَحَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لِيُحَآجُّوكُم بِهِۦ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

This ayah reveals a two-faced behavior, exposing what happened when some Jews encountered the Prophet ﷺ and heard his message. The scene unfolds in two parts: what they said publicly to the believers, and what they said privately among themselves.

### The Public Face

"And when they meet those who believe, they say, 'We believe.'" Some Jews came to the Prophet ﷺ and heard him narrate stories from the life of Musa (AS). Their reaction was genuine surprise: "Wait, you have that story too? We have the same account!" They were excited by the commonality, by how much Islam and their own tradition seemed to align. "We believe just like you do. Our beliefs are very similar."

These weren't necessarily the corrupt leadership—these were often sincere, naive followers who genuinely saw the truth in what they heard. They thought, "This is remarkable—we have so much in common with these Muslims. I need to tell our scholars about this."

### The Private Confrontation

"And when they are alone with one another, they say, 'Do you talk to them about what Allah ﷻ has revealed to you so they can argue against you with it before your Lord? Then do you not reason?'"

Picture the scene: these excited followers return to their religious leadership, enthusiastic about what they've learned. "You won't believe what I heard from the Muslims today! They have exactly what we believe, word for word. It's the same! We should engage with them—we have so much in common."

The leadership's response was immediate and harsh: "Be quiet. Don't tell them anything more."

### The Leadership's Fear

The leaders weren't concerned about truth—they were concerned about consequences. Their warning reveals their calculation: "Are you telling them things that will be used against you on the Day of Judgment? Are you giving them evidence that Allah ﷻ will use as a case against you?"

Think about what they're actually saying: "If you admit to them that yes, we have these same teachings in our books, if you confirm with your own tongue that the Prophet ﷺ is teaching what we have in our scripture, then on the Day of Judgment, Allah ﷻ will have witnesses. Your own admission will condemn you. Just don't talk to them. Stay silent."

### The Rhetorical Question

The ayah ends with a stinging question: "Then do you not reason?" This could be understood in two ways. It might be the leadership questioning their followers: "Why don't you understand what we're telling you? Are you so naive that you don't see the danger in admitting these truths?"

Or it could be Allah ﷻ questioning the leadership themselves: "Don't you realize how foolish this is? You think you can hide the truth from Allah ﷻ? You're more concerned about covering up your knowledge than about standing before your Lord?"

Either way, the irony is devastating: they're using their intellect (aql) not to pursue truth, but to suppress it.

translation: But do they not know that Allah knows what they conceal and what they declare
commentary:
أَوَلَا يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ

The question in this ayah cuts through all their scheming and calculations: "Do they not know that Allah ﷻ knows what they conceal and what they declare?"

This is the fundamental flaw in their entire strategy. The leadership told their followers to hide the truth, to keep quiet about the confirmations they found between Islam and their own scriptures. They were operating under the assumption that silence would protect them, that concealing evidence would somehow shield them from accountability.

But Allah ﷻ was present in their private meetings. He heard what the leadership said to their followers behind closed doors. The very conversation they thought was secret—the warning not to share what they knew—Allah ﷻ is now revealing it in the Quran for all time.

### The Futility of Concealment

This ayah demolishes any notion that humans can hide their knowledge or intentions from their Creator. Whether they speak openly or whisper in private, whether they admit the truth or suppress it, Allah ﷻ knows. The leadership's entire strategy of concealment was built on forgetting this basic reality of tawhid: nothing is hidden from Allah ﷻ.

It's a reminder not just for them, but for anyone who thinks they can hide their knowledge of truth, their recognition of what's right, their understanding of guidance. You might fool people. You might maintain appearances. But "Allah ﷻ knows what they conceal and what they declare."

translation: And among them are unlettered ones who do not know the Scripture except in wishful thinking, but they are only assuming
commentary:
وَمِنْهُمْ أُمِّيُّونَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّآ أَمَانِىَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ

This final ayah in this passage adds another dimension to understanding why the corruption described in previous ayahs could occur. It's not just about deliberate distortion by knowledgeable leaders—there's also the problem of widespread ignorance among the masses.

### The Unlettered Among Them

"And among them are unlettered ones who do not know the Scripture except in wishful thinking." The word أُمِّيُّونَ refers to those who are unlettered, unable to read or write. But this illiteracy isn't just about lacking the skill to read—it's about not truly knowing what their Book says.

### The Meaning of Wishful Thinking

The phrase أَمَانِىَّ is particularly significant. It means "wishful thoughts" or "desires." Ibn Abbas (RA) explained this ayah in a way that should give us pause: these people's only relationship with their Book is that they recite it. They've memorized portions of it. They can chant it beautifully. But they have no understanding of what it contains. They simply assume they know, make wishful guesses about its meanings, and confidently hold opinions about what they've never actually studied.

### A Warning for Every Generation

This interpretation from Ibn Abbas (RA) carries a sobering lesson for Muslims. Imagine surveying the educated Muslim community and asking: "How many of us love the Quran, recite it regularly, have portions memorized, celebrate its recitation, love listening to beautiful Quranic recitation—yet have little to no knowledge of what it actually says?"

The parallel is uncomfortable but necessary to confront. A people can have a deep emotional connection to their Scripture, can honor it and celebrate it, can recite it in beautiful voices—and yet be utterly disconnected from its meanings and guidance. They operate on assumptions, on wishful thinking about what they believe it says, without ever engaging with its actual content.

### Assumptions Without Knowledge

"And they are not except assuming." The ayah ends by characterizing their relationship with knowledge: it's all assumption, speculation and confident guesses built on nothing solid. They're certain about things they've never actually verified. They hold firm opinions about a Book they've never understood.

This is perhaps one of the most dangerous spiritual states: confident ignorance. Not knowing that you don't know. Making assumptions and treating them as knowledge. Having a relationship with Scripture that's entirely emotional and ritualistic, divorced from understanding and implementation.

The warning echoes through time: Don't let your relationship with the Quran be one of wishful thinking and assumptions. Don't be among those who love to hear it but never engage with what it says. The Quran was revealed for transformation, not just for beautiful recitation.

translation: So woe to those who write the "scripture" with their own hands, then say, "This is from Allah," in order to exchange it for a small price. Woe to them for what their hands have written and woe to them for what they earn
commentary:
فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ يَقُولُونَ هَـٰذَا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَشْتَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لَّهُم مِّمَّا كَتَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَوَيْلٌ لَّهُم مِّمَّا يَكْسِبُونَ

The Quran has been describing two groups: those who don't know and the criminals who do know. This ayah addresses the worst of these criminals—those who possess knowledge of their scripture yet deliberately corrupt it. The word وَيْلٌ refers to the worst possible destruction and punishment.

Allah ﷻ describes how these individuals يَكْتُبُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ—they write the book with their own hands. This isn't passive ignorance but active corruption of divine revelation. Then they compound this crime by claiming هَـٰذَا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ—"this is from Allah." They attribute their own fabrications to the Creator.

What drives this corruption? لِيَشْتَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا—so they can sell it for a small price. They exchange divine truth for worldly gain, trading the eternal for something temporary and insignificant. This isn't just about money, though that's certainly included. It's about any worldly benefit they prioritize over divine guidance—status, political power, or maintaining their position of authority.

The severity of this crime is emphasized through repetition. Allah ﷻ declares wayl three times: destruction for them because of what their hands have written, and destruction for them because of what they continue to earn through this corruption. This threefold warning underscores the gravity of altering Allah's book and deceiving people about His guidance.

translation: And they say, "Never will the Fire touch us, except for a few days." Say, "Have you taken a covenant with Allah? For Allah will never break His covenant. Or do you say about Allah that which you do not know
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً ۚ قُلْ أَتَّخَذْتُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَهْدًا فَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ عَهْدَهُۥٓ ۖ أَمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

After committing such grave sins, what excuse do these people offer? They claim لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً—"The Fire will not touch us except for a few numbered days." They acknowledge their wrongdoing but minimize its consequences. Yes, we've messed up, they admit, but our punishment won't be that severe or long.

This represents a dangerous delusion: the belief that one can corrupt divine guidance yet face only minimal accountability. Historically, some strands of Jewish thought evolved to minimize or even eliminate belief in the afterlife altogether. The attitude became one of moral convenience—we'll face some temporary discomfort, but ultimately we'll be fine.

Allah ﷻ responds with a devastating question: أَتَّخَذْتُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَهْدًا—Have you taken some kind of guarantee from Allah? Do you possess a special covenant promising you limited punishment? فَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ عَهْدَهُۥٓ—Allah never breaks His promise, so if you truly had such a guarantee, it would be honored. But you don't have one.

The alternative is even worse: أَمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ—Or are you saying about Allah what you have no knowledge of? You're making assumptions about divine judgment based on wishful thinking rather than revelation. You're deciding for yourselves what Allah's judgment will be, creating your own comfortable version of accountability.

translation: Yes, whoever earns evil and his sin has encompassed him - those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
بَلَىٰ مَن كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَأَحَـٰطَتْ بِهِۦ خَطِيٓـَٔتُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

Allah ﷻ corrects their false assumption with بَلَىٰ—"Rather, the reality is..." He then describes a critical spiritual principle: مَن كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً—whoever earns a single sin. The focus isn't on committing many sins but on how a person relates to even one sin.

The key phrase is وَأَحَـٰطَتْ بِهِۦ خَطِيٓـَٔتُهُۥ—and his mistake surrounded him. The word أَحَـٰطَتْ comes from the root meaning to encircle or encompass completely. This describes someone who allows a sin to invade their entire life. They commit a wrong and then justify it: "I do so many good things—this one flaw doesn't matter." They don't feel remorse anymore. They've rationalized it, made peace with it, perhaps even defended it.

When someone tells themselves, "This is just one of my weaknesses, what can I do?" without genuine struggle to change, that sin begins to encircle them. They've essentially created their own categories of halal and haram, deciding for themselves what's acceptable rather than submitting to what Allah ﷻ has commanded.

For such people, فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ—those are the companions of the Fire, هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ—they will remain in it eternally. One sin, left unchecked and unjustified, can have devastating consequences. The issue isn't human weakness or occasional mistakes—it's the attitude of making your own rules and refusing to seek Allah's forgiveness and guidance.

translation: But they who believe and do righteous deeds - those are the companions of Paradise; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

In perfect contrast to the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ describes the believers: وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ—those who believe and do righteous deeds. Notice the beautiful simplicity. Allah ﷻ doesn't demand perfection or an endless list of accomplishments. He asks for genuine faith and the few righteous deeds He has prescribed.

These are people who submit to Allah's guidance without making their own exceptions, who strive to do good without ulterior motives. Their good deeds aren't performed to earn worldly recognition or status—they're done purely for Allah's sake.

For such people, أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ—those are the companions of Paradise, هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ—they will remain in it eternally. The structure mirrors the previous ayah: companionship and eternal residence. But while the former described eternal punishment for those who rationalize sin, this describes eternal reward for those who sincerely believe and strive.

The parallel structure emphasizes the choice before every person: allow sin to encircle you through justification and arrogance, or embrace faith and righteous action with humility and submission.

translation: And [recall] when We took the covenant from the Children of Israel, [enjoining upon them], "Do not worship except Allah; and to parents do good and to relatives, orphans, and the needy. And speak to people good [words] and establish prayer and give zakah." Then you turned away, except a few of you, and you were refusing
commentary:
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا وَذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَقُولُوا۟ لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنكُمْ وَأَنتُم مُّعْرِضُونَ

Allah ﷻ reminds the Israelites of the covenant He took from them: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ. The Bani Israel were given numerous commandments, but this ayah summarizes the essentials—the fundamental principles that, if followed, would create a healthy, thriving society. These same principles offer us guidance for building strong communities today.

The first command: لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ—worship none but Allah. Tawhid is the foundation. Everything else builds upon this bedrock of pure monotheism.

Second: وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا—and to parents, excellence. The word إِحْسَان means to be the best you can possibly be, to excel in goodness. After establishing the right relationship with Allah ﷻ, the next building block of a healthy society is the family. Excellence toward parents strengthens the fundamental unit from which everything else grows.

Third: وَذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ—and to those who possess closeness. This literally refers to close relatives but extends to neighbors and those with whom you share bonds. The family circle expands outward, creating concentric rings of care and responsibility.

Fourth: وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ—and to the orphans. But how do you know who the orphans are? They don't wear signs. You only know them when you truly know your family and your community. When we reduce "community" to people we pray beside but never speak to beyond saying "Assalamu alaikum," we remain strangers. We can't care for orphans we don't even know exist. Real community requires knowing people's situations, their struggles, their needs.

Fifth: وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ—and to those in need. These are people incapable of helping themselves, those facing genuine hardship. Again, how do you identify them? Only through real connection. The person praying next to you in the masjid might be struggling financially, but you won't know unless you build genuine relationships. Too often, we only discover someone's difficulty when they've been pushed to the point of humiliation, forced to beg. It shouldn't reach that point.

Sixth: وَقُولُوا۟ لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا—and speak to people good things, speak beautifully. Notice this command isn't limited to believers—لِلنَّاسِ means all people. Yet somehow this becomes one of the hardest commands for Muslims to follow. We struggle to speak kindly, especially to each other.

The place where we should be most careful about our speech is the masjid, yet conflicts and harsh words are commonplace there. Someone takes "your spot" and you respond with attitude. We've become experts at making even "Assalamu alaikum" sound like an insult through tone and body language. This condescending attitude toward one another destroys community from within. Consider how many family conflicts stem not from disagreements themselves but from how things are said.

Only after establishing these social foundations does Allah ﷻ mention the ritual acts: وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ—establish the prayer and give the zakat. These two acts appear repeatedly throughout the Quran because they serve as ambassadors representing two categories of good deeds.

Salah represents all deeds done purely for Allah ﷻ—acts of worship that bring you closer to Him. Zakat represents deeds that benefit others but are done for Allah's sake. These are the two directions of our actions: vertical (toward Allah ﷻ) and horizontal (toward His creation), but both rooted in seeking His pleasure. Dawah helps others but is done for Allah ﷻ. Charity benefits people but earns Allah's reward. This is the comprehensive picture of righteousness.

Yet despite receiving these beautiful, balanced instructions, the Israelites failed: ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنكُمْ—then you turned away, except for a few among you, وَأَنتُم مُّعْرِضُونَ—and you were deliberately ignoring. They possessed clear guidance but chose to reject it, consciously turning their backs on these divine principles.

translation: And [recall] when We took your covenant, [saying], "Do not shed each other's blood or evict one another from your homes." Then you acknowledged [this] while you were witnessing
commentary:
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَآءَكُمْ وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنفُسَكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ

The connection between the previous ayah's social commands and this one is profound. Allah ﷻ continues describing the covenant: لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَآءَكُمْ—you will not shed your own blood. Notice He doesn't say "each other's blood" but "your own blood." This language emphasizes that when you harm someone from your ummah, you're harming yourself. The believers are like one body, as the Prophet ﷺ taught.

Similarly: وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنفُسَكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ—and you will not expel yourselves from your homes. When you exile people from your community, you're expelling part of yourself. They are an extension of you; their homes are your homes in the spiritual sense of communal belonging.

Why does Allah ﷻ mention violence immediately after the social commands of the previous ayah? Because when we fail to be good to family, when we don't speak kindly to people, when we neglect the orphans and the needy—these failures create fractures. Small fractures grow into divisions. Divisions harden into factions. Factions lead to conflict. And conflict, left unchecked, eventually leads to violence.

We might think, "We don't have Muslims killing each other here in America." But look at Muslim communities across the world—Muslims attacking other Muslims, fighting amongst themselves. Where does it start? It starts with the erosion of basic social bonds and common decency. It builds from there.

Even in places protected by law and order, the hatred exists beneath the surface. Fistfights break out in masajid. If circumstances were different, if restraints were removed, would it escalate further? These ayat warn us about this progression.

ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ—Then you acknowledged this, and you bore witness. You agreed to these terms. You understood the covenant. Yet what happened next?

translation: Then, you are those [same ones who are] killing one another and evicting a party of your people from their homes, cooperating against them in sin and aggression. And if they come to you as captives, you ransom them, although their eviction was forbidden to you. So do you believe in part of the Scripture and disbelieve in part? Then what is the recompense for those who do that among you except disgrace in worldly life; and on the Day of Resurrection they will be sent back to the severest of punishment. And Allah is not unaware of what you do
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتُخْرِجُونَ فَرِيقًا مِّنكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ تَظَـٰهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَإِن يَأْتُوكُمْ أُسَـٰرَىٰ تُفَـٰدُوهُمْ وَهُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْرَاجُهُمْ ۚ أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ ۚ فَمَا جَزَآءُ مَن يَفْعَلُ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَشَدِّ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

Despite their covenant, Allah ﷻ exposes their hypocrisy: ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ—then you are these very people killing yourselves. You're killing your own. The historical context shows Jewish tribes fighting against each other, shedding the blood they swore not to shed.

وَتُخْرِجُونَ فَرِيقًا مِّنكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ—and you expel a group among yourselves from their homes. But first, notice the process: you make them a faction. You cast them out ideologically before physically. You label them as deviants, as having left the religion, as a disease in your society. Once you establish that they're fundamentally different, that they're outcasts, it becomes easier to justify their expulsion.

تَظَـٰهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ—you advance against them with sin and animosity. You justify your sin by saying these are deviants, these are supposed to be fought. How easily the label of munafiq (hypocrite) can be thrown at another Muslim, yet how serious that allegation is!

Then comes the stunning contradiction: وَإِن يَأْتُوكُمْ أُسَـٰرَىٰ تُفَـٰدُوهُمْ—and if they came to you as prisoners, you would ransom them. Here's what happened: when the Jews fought each other, their infighting weakened them as a nation. Weakened nations become easy targets. Outsiders attacked—the Babylonians, the Assyrians, later the Romans. When these external forces captured Jews as prisoners, suddenly those same people they had fought against, expelled, and hated became "our brothers" who needed to be ransomed.

This pattern sounds familiar, doesn't it? Throughout the last century, how many conflicts between Muslim factions have we witnessed? How many civil wars where Muslims fought Muslims? And then when external forces attacked or captured Muslims, suddenly there were calls for unity and brotherhood.

وَهُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْرَاجُهُمْ—but it was forbidden to you to expel them in the first place. Allah ﷻ exposes the hypocrisy: you violated the covenant by expelling them, yet when they became prisoners to outsiders, you remembered your religious duty to ransom them.

أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ—Do you believe in some parts of the Book and disbelieve in other parts? This is selective religion at its worst. You quote the parts of scripture that serve your agenda and conveniently ignore everything else. You cite passages about ransoming prisoners but ignore commandments against fighting your own people.

It's easy to have a cause and only reference the parts of revelation that support it. When it comes to your particular agenda, those ayat are front and center. But passages that contradict your course of action or challenge your behavior? Those are forgotten, set aside, never mentioned.

The critical point: they don't explicitly deny that other parts are scripture. They simply don't use them, don't apply them, don't let them guide their actions. And Allah ﷻ makes clear that ignoring parts of the Book is itself a form of kufr—covering up and rejecting divine guidance.

فَمَا جَزَآءُ مَن يَفْعَلُ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا—So what should be the recompense for whoever does this among you except humiliation in worldly life? Those who pick and choose from divine guidance will face disgrace in this world.

وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَشَدِّ ٱلْعَذَابِ—and on the Day of Resurrection they will be returned to the most severe punishment. The consequences don't end in this world. Those who displayed such hypocrisy face the worst punishment in the hereafter.

وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ—and Allah is not at all unaware of what you continue to do. Don't think your selective application of religion goes unnoticed. Allah ﷻ sees everything—the parts you follow and the parts you ignore, the commandments you emphasize and those you conveniently forget.

translation: Those are the ones who have bought the life of this world [in exchange] for the Hereafter, so the punishment will not be lightened for them, nor will they be aided
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ

Allah ﷻ describes these hypocrites with a powerful image: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ—those are the ones who purchased worldly life in exchange for the afterlife. They made a transaction, a terrible trade. They sold their hereafter to buy temporary worldly benefits.

What did they gain? Perhaps political power, perhaps wealth, perhaps the satisfaction of defeating their rivals. What did they lose? Everything eternal.

The consequences of this trade: فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ—punishment will not be lightened for them. Those who attacked members of their own ummah, who displayed such blatant hypocrisy, who cherry-picked divine guidance to suit their desires—for them, there is no reduction in punishment.

وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ—and they will not be aided. No help will come to them. In this world, they sought to dominate others through sin and animosity. In the hereafter, they will find themselves utterly without support, facing the full weight of their choices.

translation: And We did certainly give Moses the Torah and followed up after him with messengers. And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit. But is it [not] that every time a messenger came to you, [O Children of Israel], with what your souls did not desire, you were arrogant? And a party [of messengers] you denied and another party you killed
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَـٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقًا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ

Allah ﷻ emphasizes His continuous guidance to the Israelites: وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ—We swear, We had certainly given Musa (AS) the Book. The use of the oath emphasizes the certainty and significance of this gift.

وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ—and We fortified with messengers after him, one after another. The word قَفَّيْنَا suggests following in succession, reinforcing and supporting. Messenger after messenger came, each one confirming and building upon what Musa (AS) had brought.

The passage then mentions the final of these messengers to the Israelites: وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ—and We gave Isa, son of Maryam, the clear proofs. The word ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ refers to the clearest, most obvious signs—miracles and teachings so evident that no one could deny them in good faith.

وَأَيَّدْنَـٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ—and We aided him with Ruh al-Qudus, the Holy Spirit. This is the title used for Jibreel (AS), the angel who brought revelation and supported the prophets.

So here's the summary: Musa (AS) was given the Book at the beginning, and Isa (AS) was given clear proofs at the end, with countless messengers in between. The Israelites had no shortage of guidance.

Yet what was their response? أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ—Is it that every time a messenger came to you with what your souls did not desire, you sought your own greatness? The word تَهْوَىٰٓ refers to personal preference, what you find agreeable or suitable. The message didn't align with your desires, didn't suit your personality, didn't jive with your agenda.

So instead of submitting, ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ—you sought your own greatness. This is istikbar—thinking you're greater than the text, above divine guidance. "I'm not comfortable with this ruling. I don't like what the scripture says here. This doesn't fit my worldview." This arrogance, this elevation of personal preference over divine command, is the essence of istikbar.

What were the consequences? فَفَرِيقًا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ—Then a group among the messengers you lied against, and a group you even went as far as to kill. Some prophets you slandered and rejected. Others you murdered. The verb for killing is in the present tense (تَقْتُلُونَ), suggesting this became their characteristic behavior—a pattern repeated across generations.

translation: And they said, "Our hearts are wrapped." But, [in fact], Allah has cursed them for their disbelief, so little is it that they believe
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌۢ ۚ بَل لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَقَلِيلًا مَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ

When confronted with these crimes—the lying, the killing, the rejection of messenger after messenger—what excuse did they offer? وَقَالُوا۟ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌۢ—They said, "Our hearts are wrapped up."

Picture the Prophet ﷺ recounting all their violations of the covenant. Their response? "Our hearts are sealed, wrapped, protected. Nothing you say is getting through to us. Allah has veiled our hearts from this kind of talk."

They tried to claim divine protection as an excuse for their stubbornness. As if Allah ﷻ had somehow made them incapable of receiving guidance, and therefore they bore no responsibility for their rejection.

Allah ﷻ responds: بَل لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ—Rather, Allah has cursed them because of their disbelief. It's not that Allah protected their hearts from truth. It's that their persistent disbelief earned them Allah's curse. The sealing of their hearts wasn't arbitrary divine action—it was the natural consequence of their choices.

فَقَلِيلًا مَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ—How little it is that they believe. How few of them will ever come to faith. This can mean both that very few individuals from among them will believe, and that even those who claim belief will have only minimal faith. Their hearts have become so hardened through repeated rejection that genuine belief becomes nearly impossible.

translation: And when there came to them a Book from Allah confirming that which was with them - although before they used to pray for victory against those who disbelieved - but [then] when there came to them that which they recognized, they disbelieved in it; so the curse of Allah will be upon the disbelievers
commentary:
وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ فَلَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

This ayah reveals a particularly painful irony. وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ—And when a Book came to them from Allah confirming what they already had with them. The Quran came as a confirmation of the Torah and Injeel, validating the core message the Israelites had possessed all along.

But here's the twist: وَكَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟—and they had been, much before this, seeking victory and boasting about it against those who disbelieved. The word يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ implies calling for a decisive victory, a final triumph.

What does this mean? When the Jewish tribes in Medina lost battles against the pagan Arab tribes, they would console themselves: "Just wait. The final messenger is coming. Our books have told us about him. When he arrives, we will have guaranteed victory. You'll see—we'll crush you all." They used the prophecy of the final messenger as a source of hope and a threat against their enemies.

Their own scriptures had informed them about this coming prophet. They knew the signs, they knew the descriptions, they recognized the truth when it came. Yet فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ—when what they recognized came to them, they disbelieved in it.

This is the tragedy: they knew. They weren't confused or uncertain. They recognized the truth of the Prophet ﷺ and the Quran. But they rejected it anyway.

Why? The next ayah will explain their motivation. But the consequence is clear: فَلَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ—then the curse of Allah is upon those who disbelieve. They had knowledge, they had preparation, they had prophecies that came true before their eyes. Yet they chose rejection. For such deliberate disbelief in the face of clear truth, they earned Allah's curse.

translation: How wretched is that for which they sold themselves - that they would disbelieve in what Allah has revealed through [their] outrage that Allah would send down His favor upon whom He wills from among His servants. So they returned having [earned] wrath upon wrath. And for the disbelievers is a humiliating punishment
commentary:
بِئْسَمَا ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بَغْيًا أَن يُنَزِّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ فَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ عَلَىٰ غَضَبٍ ۚ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

Allah ﷻ describes the terrible nature of their choice: بِئْسَمَا ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ—How wretched is that for which they sold themselves. They made a transaction, and what a horrible trade it was.

What exactly did they sell themselves for? أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بَغْيًا—That they should disbelieve in what Allah sent down, purely out of rebellious envy. The word بَغْيًا is powerful—it means the urge to dominate, to rebel, to transgress out of spite and jealousy.

أَن يُنَزِّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ—Because Allah sent down from His special favor upon whoever He wills from among His slaves. This is the heart of their problem: the final messenger came to an Arab, not to one of them. They were the "chosen people," or so they believed. Why would Allah ﷻ give this tremendous honor to someone outside their nation?

Their pride couldn't accept it. The message was true—they knew it. The messenger was authentic—they recognized him. But he wasn't one of them, so they rejected it all. They sold divine guidance, they sold truth, they sold their own salvation—all to preserve their ethnic and religious pride.

فَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ عَلَىٰ غَضَبٍ—Then they drew upon themselves rage upon rage. Earlier in Surah Al-Baqarah, we learned they had already earned Allah's anger through previous disobedience. Now, by rejecting the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ despite recognizing his truth, they drew upon themselves another layer of divine wrath. Anger upon anger. Rage upon rage.

The ayah concludes: وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ—and for the disbelievers there is humiliating punishment. The word مُّهِينٌ (humiliating) connects back to their motivation. They saw humiliation in accepting a "gentile" messenger, in accepting an Arab prophet. They thought it would be humiliating to their status as the chosen nation.

So what does Allah ﷻ promise them? True humiliation. They sought to avoid imagined humiliation by rejecting truth, so they will face real humiliation as punishment. The very thing they feared in accepting guidance will come to them through their rejection of it—but far worse, and eternal.

This concludes a powerful passage examining the depths of human arrogance, the tragedy of recognizing truth yet rejecting it for pride, and the devastating consequences of selective faith. These ayat serve as warnings not just about past nations but about patterns of behavior that can emerge in any community that places pride, preference and ethnic superiority above divine guidance.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Believe in what Allah has revealed," they say, "We believe [only] in what was revealed to us." And they disbelieve in what came after it, while it is the truth confirming that which is with them. Say, "Then why did you kill the prophets of Allah before, if you are [indeed] believers
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ نُؤْمِنُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَيَكْفُرُونَ بِمَا وَرَآءَهُۥ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ ۗ قُلْ فَلِمَ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنۢبِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ مِن قَبْلُ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

This ayah introduces a devastating three-step argument that progressively exposes the contradictions in the claims of those who reject new revelation. When told to believe in what Allah ﷻ has sent down, their response is immediate and seemingly pious: "We believe in what was sent down upon us." They present themselves as committed believers, faithful to their own scripture, with no need for a new message.

But Allah ﷻ immediately dismantles this claim. The phrase وَيَكْفُرُونَ بِمَا وَرَآءَهُۥ (and they disbelieve in what is behind it) reveals the reality: they don't even believe their own book. The expression "behind their backs" indicates something they have cast aside, ignored, abandoned. So when they say "we already have faith," Allah ﷻ responds: "No, you don't even believe your own book."

Then comes the powerful confirmation: وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ (and it is the truth confirming what is with them). The Quran doesn't contradict their scripture; it confirms it. Their book contains truth, and this revelation confirms what they already have. This makes their rejection even more inexcusable.

The ayah concludes with a devastating question: فَلِمَ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنۢبِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ مِن قَبْلُ (then why did you kill the prophets of Allah before?) If you are truly committed to the truth, if your faith is genuine, how does your history include the murder of prophets? What kind of faith commands you to kill the messengers of Allah ﷻ? This rhetorical question exposes the hollowness of their claim to believe. True faith doesn't lead to killing prophets—it leads to following them.

translation: And Moses had certainly brought you clear proofs. Then you took the calf [in worship] after that, while you were wrongdoers
commentary:
۞ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُم مُّوسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ

The argument intensifies. "All right," Allah ﷻ says, "forget all the other prophets. What about the one prophet who saved you from the clutches of Firaun?" Musa (AS) brought the clearest possible proofs—miracles that left no room for doubt. Yet even after witnessing these signs, even after being rescued from slavery, they took the calf as an object of worship.

The phrase ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ (then you took the calf after him) emphasizes the timing and the betrayal. After everything Musa (AS) did for them, after all the miracles, they still dishonored him by worshiping an idol. The ayah ends with a simple declaration: وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (and you were wrongdoers). This isn't a matter of confusion or honest mistake—it's deliberate wrongdoing.

translation: And [recall] when We took your covenant and raised over you the mount, [saying], "Take what We have given you with determination and listen." They said [instead], "We hear and disobey." And their hearts absorbed [the worship of] the calf because of their disbelief. Say, "How wretched is that which your faith enjoins upon you, if you should be believers
commentary:
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ ٱلطُّورَ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَأُشْرِبُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ بِكُفْرِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ بِئْسَمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنُكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

The argument reaches its peak. "You killed prophets. You dishonored Musa (AS). But forget all that—what about Allah ﷻ Himself?" This ayah recalls the moment when Allah ﷻ took a binding covenant from them and raised Mount Tur above them. The command was clear: خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ (hold firmly to what We have given you and listen).

Their response is shocking in its audacity: قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا (they said, "We hear and we disobey"). This wasn't ignorance or confusion. They heard the command directly from Allah ﷻ and chose to disobey. They announced their rebellion openly.

The phrase وَأُشْرِبُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ (and they were made to drink the calf into their hearts) uses powerful imagery. The love of this idol was absorbed into their hearts like a drink is absorbed into the body. This happened بِكُفْرِهِمْ (because of their disbelief)—as a consequence of their rejection.

Allah ﷻ then tells the Prophet ﷺ to respond to their claim of faith: قُلْ بِئْسَمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنُكُمْ (say, "How evil is that which your faith commands you"). What kind of faith tells you to kill prophets, dishonor Musa (AS), and tell Allah ﷻ to His face that you will disobey? This is the devastating conclusion of the three-step argument: their claimed faith is exposed as hollow, contradicted by their own history and actions.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "If the home of the Hereafter with Allah is for you alone and not the [other] people, then wish for death, if you should be truthful
commentary:
قُلْ إِن كَانَتْ لَكُمُ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَالِصَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ issues a powerful challenge that cuts to the heart of their false confidence. The Jewish community in Medina held a particular belief: that Paradise belonged exclusively to them. They considered themselves the chosen nation, believing all gentiles would burn in hell while they alone enjoyed eternal bliss.

Allah ﷻ says: if the final home truly belongs only to you, خَالِصَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ (exclusively for you, pure from all other people), then فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ (wish for death). The logic is simple and devastating: if you have a guaranteed mansion waiting for you, free of all problems, why are you clinging to this difficult life? Why deal with persecution, poverty and hardship when Paradise awaits?

The challenge ends with إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (if you are truthful). This isn't just about Paradise—it's about whether their claim of exclusive salvation is genuine or just empty arrogance.

translation: But they will never wish for it, ever, because of what their hands have put forth. And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَلَن يَتَمَنَّوْهُ أَبَدًۢا بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ answers His own challenge before they can respond: وَلَن يَتَمَنَّوْهُ أَبَدًۢا (they will never, ever wish for it). The emphasis is absolute—not just "they won't," but "they will never, ever."

The reason? بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ (because of what their hands have sent forward). This is a beautiful Quranic expression referring to investments made for the future. When a farmer plants seeds, his hands send that work forward in time, investing in a future harvest. In spiritual terms, this refers to the deeds we send ahead of us—our investments in the Hereafter.

The profound implication is this: they will never wish for death because all their investments are in this world. They have nothing waiting for them in the next life. If someone has spent their entire life building wealth, status and comfort here, with no investment in the eternal home, why would they want to leave? They have no incentive to go where they have no assets.

The believer, by contrast, has accounts in two banks—the worldly bank and the eternal bank. Every time we spend in Allah's path, we're not losing wealth; we're transferring it from an account with limited returns to one where Allah ﷻ multiplies every dollar by 700 or more. The believer looks forward to meeting Allah ﷻ because that's where the real wealth is stored.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (and Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers). Allah ﷻ knows full well why they cling to this life. He knows the wrongdoing they've committed, the lack of investment in the Hereafter. Some people can't even stand to discuss the afterlife—they immediately want to change the subject because the thought itself is depressing to them. This ayah describes exactly that psychology.

translation: And you will surely find them the most greedy of people for life - [even] more than those who associate others with Allah. One of them wishes that he could be granted life a thousand years, but it would not remove him in the least from the [coming] punishment that he should be granted life. And Allah is Seeing of what they do
commentary:
وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَوٰةٍ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۚ يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ وَمَا هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ أَن يُعَمَّرَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ

Allah ﷻ now describes their desperate attachment to life with striking precision. وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَوٰةٍ (and you will certainly find them the most zealous of people concerning life). This isn't ordinary attachment—it's extreme, desperate clinging to earthly existence.

Then comes a remarkable comparison: وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ (and even more than those who associate partners with Allah). The mushrikun also don't believe in the afterlife, yet they're often fearless about death. They'll die for honor, for tribal glory, to be remembered as heroes. But these people who claim to have the truth are more attached to life than even the mushrikun. They kept losing in battle because while their enemies were fearless, they were terrified of death.

The ayah continues: يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ (each one of them wishes he could live a thousand years). Even an impossibly long life would satisfy them. But then comes the cold reality: وَمَا هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ أَن يُعَمَّرَ (but he would not be removed from the punishment even if he were given that life). Living longer doesn't save anyone from accountability. The punishment they fear is coming regardless.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (and Allah is Seeing of what they do). Nothing is hidden from Him—not their desperate clinging to life, not their real motivations, not their deeds.

translation: Say, "Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel - it is [none but] he who has brought the Qur'an down upon your heart, [O Muhammad], by permission of Allah, confirming that which was before it and as guidance and good tidings for the believers
commentary:
قُلْ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُۥ نَزَّلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

After being called out for their crimes and contradictions, they needed a new line of attack. They decided to criticize the Prophet ﷺ himself: "Which angel brings you revelation?" When he answered "Jibreel (AS)," their response was immediate: "Oh, not him. Anyone else would have been fine, but we have a problem with Jibreel (AS)."

This petty excuse reveals their desperation. Allah ﷻ responds with devastating logic: قُلْ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ (say, whoever is an enemy to Jibreel). The response doesn't entertain their excuse but exposes its absurdity.

فَإِنَّهُۥ نَزَّلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ (then indeed, he brought it down upon your heart by Allah's permission). Jibreel (AS) is merely the messenger carrying out Allah's command. Being angry at the angel is like being angry at the mailman for delivering a letter you don't like. The message comes from Allah ﷻ, and Jibreel (AS) acts only by His permission.

The description of what Jibreel (AS) brought is beautiful: مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ (confirming what is before it). This revelation confirms what came before—including the Torah and Injeel. It also means the Prophet ﷺ himself confirms the previous scriptures. Then: وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and guidance and good news for the believers). This Quran is guidance for those who want to be guided and good news for those who believe.

translation: Whoever is an enemy to Allah and His angels and His messengers and Gabriel and Michael - then indeed, Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers
commentary:
مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّلَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَجِبْرِيلَ وَمِيكَىٰلَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

This ayah establishes a critical principle of faith: you cannot pick and choose. مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّلَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَجِبْرِيلَ وَمِيكَىٰلَ (whoever is an enemy to Allah and His angels and His messengers and Jibreel and Mikael). Notice that Jibreel and Mikael (AS) are mentioned specifically after angels and messengers are mentioned generally. This emphasizes: you can't say "I accept most of it but not this one part."

If someone says, "I love all the prophets except I'm not a fan of one," they've rejected all of them. Faith is indivisible. You can't be selective about revelation, about prophets, about angels. The moment you reject any part, you've rejected the whole.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (then indeed Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers). This is the ultimate consequence: when you make yourself an enemy to any part of the faith, Allah ﷻ becomes your enemy. Not because He is petty, but because rejecting any part of His guidance is rejecting Him.

translation: And We have certainly revealed to you verses [which are] clear proofs, and no one would deny them except the defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ وَمَا يَكْفُرُ بِهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

Allah ﷻ makes a simple declaration: وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ (We have certainly sent down to you clear signs). The word بَيِّنَـٰتٍ emphasizes that these aren't ambiguous or unclear—they're crystal clear, miraculous signs that leave no room for honest doubt.

Then comes the devastating assessment: وَمَا يَكْفُرُ بِهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (and none disbelieve in them except the defiantly disobedient). The word الْفَـٰسِقُونَ refers to those who are inherently corrupt, who rebel against what they know to be true.

This teaches us something powerful about the Prophet's mission ﷺ. During his lifetime, after people came into contact with his revelations, there was no legitimate reason to disbelieve. The only reason to reject him was internal corruption. The evidence was overwhelming; only those who had corruption in their hearts could turn away from it.

translation: Is it not [true] that every time they took a covenant a party of them threw it away? But, [in fact], most of them do not believe
commentary:
أَوَكُلَّمَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ عَهْدًا نَّبَذَهُۥ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

This ayah addresses a pattern of behavior: أَوَكُلَّمَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ عَهْدًا نَّبَذَهُۥ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم (is it not that every time they make a covenant, a party of them throws it aside?). The word نَّبَذَ means to toss something away carelessly, to throw it behind your back as if it's worthless.

This isn't describing a one-time incident. كُلَّمَا (every time) indicates a repeated pattern. Every time they made a promise to Allah ﷻ or His messengers, a group among them would break it. Not necessarily the entire community, but فَرِيقٌ (a party) from among them would consistently betray their commitments.

The ayah concludes with a stark assessment: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (rather, most of them do not believe). This breaking of covenants isn't accidental or occasional—it stems from a fundamental lack of faith. The repeated betrayals reveal that most of them never had genuine belief to begin with.

translation: And when a messenger from Allah came to them confirming that which was with them, a party of those who had been given the Scripture threw the Scripture of Allah behind their backs as if they did not know [what it contained]
commentary:
وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ نَبَذَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

The pattern continues into the present: وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ (and when there came to them a messenger from Allah confirming what was with them). The Prophet ﷺ came with a message that confirmed their own scripture, that validated what they already believed to be true.

Their response? نَبَذَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ (a party of those who had been given the Scripture threw the Scripture of Allah behind their backs). The same word نَبَذَ appears again—they tossed it aside carelessly. And notice what they threw away: كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ (the Book of Allah) itself. Not just the new revelation, but their own Book, which confirmed the new messenger.

The ayah ends with a description of their pretense: كَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (as though they did not know). The Prophet ﷺ would recite verses to them, and they would immediately recognize them—"This is exactly what we've been studying, exactly what we memorized from our scripture. It's all coming true!" But then they would say, "I don't know what you're talking about." They threw their knowledge behind their backs and pretended ignorance.

This isn't about lacking information. It's about willful denial of what you know to be true.

translation: And they followed [instead] what the devils had recited during the reign of Solomon. It was not Solomon who disbelieved, but the devils disbelieved, teaching people magic and that which was revealed to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut. But the two angels do not teach anyone unless they say, "We are a trial, so do not disbelieve [by practicing magic]." And [yet] they learn from them that by which they cause separation between a man and his wife. But they do not harm anyone through it except by permission of Allah. And the people learn what harms them and does not benefit them. But the Children of Israel certainly knew that whoever purchased the magic would not have in the Hereafter any share. And wretched is that for which they sold themselves, if they only knew
commentary:
**وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَا تَتْلُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ عَلَىٰ مُلْكِ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۖ وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ ٱلسِّحْرَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَكَيْنِ بِبَابِلَ هَـٰرُوتَ وَمَـٰرُوتَ ۚ وَمَا يُعَلِّمَانِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَآ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تَكْفُرْ ۖ فَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مِنْهُمَا مَا يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِۦ بَيْنَ ٱلْمَرْءِ وَزَوْجِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا هُم بِضَآرِّينَ بِهِۦ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنفَعُهُمْ ۚ وَلَقَدْ عَلِمُوا۟ لَمَنِ ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ ۚ وَلَبِئْسَ مَا شَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ**

### The Corruption of Solomon's Legacy

The ayah begins with a powerful indictment: "And they followed whatever the devils used to follow in the dominion of Solomon." This immediately draws our attention to a historical distortion. The Israelites had been practicing various forms of magic and attributing these practices to Prophet Sulayman (AS). This was a grave injustice to his memory and a complete misrepresentation of his righteous kingdom.

Allah ﷻ declares the innocence of Sulayman (AS) categorically: **وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ** - "Solomon did not commit an act of disbelief at all." The grammatical structure here is significant. When Allah ﷻ negates something about one party and then affirms it about another, He's making a sharp distinction. The language implies: "You've got it completely wrong. It wasn't Solomon who disbelieved."

The ayah continues: **وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ كَفَرُوا۟** - "However, in fact, it's the devils who disbelieved." The structure emphasizes that the devils are the true culprits, not this noble prophet. The next phrase clarifies exactly how they disbelieved: **يُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ ٱلسِّحْرَ** - "They used to teach people magic."

### Understanding What Was Sent Down

The ayah then mentions: **وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَكَيْنِ بِبَابِلَ هَـٰرُوتَ وَمَـٰرُوتَ** - "And what was sent down upon the two angels in Babylon, Harut and Marut." There is scholarly consensus that Harut and Marut were angels who appeared in human form to teach people. However, understanding what exactly was "sent down" requires deeper reflection.

An important principle emerges here: whenever Allah ﷻ mentions something being "sent down" in the Quran, it is never directly associated with something inherently evil. Revelation that descends from Allah ﷻ, even when it involves trials or tests, contains goodness. Even divine punishment that is "sent down" serves a purifying purpose - it cleanses the earth of corruption.

Since Allah ﷻ explicitly describes magic (سِحْر) as an act of disbelief, something inherently wrong, how do we reconcile this with the concept of something being "sent down"? The scholar Amin Ahsan Islahi offered a thought-provoking perspective worth examining. He suggests that perhaps what was sent down to these angels wasn't magic itself, but rather knowledge meant to combat the magic that was widespread in Babylon at the time.

This interpretation finds support in understanding the historical context. When the Israelites were exiled to Babylon (present-day Iraq), they entered a region notorious for sorcery and spell-casting. As they grew distant from their own scripture, they began seeking solutions to their problems elsewhere - including in the magical practices prevalent in their new homeland.

### Primary vs. Secondary Benefits of Revelation

This brings us to a crucial distinction in understanding divine guidance: the difference between primary and secondary benefits of revelation. The primary purpose of revelation is guidance - to show us how to live, to distinguish right from wrong, to illuminate the path we should walk. The secondary benefits include blessings, protection, healing, and spiritual rewards.

When we recite Quranic supplications before traveling, we receive protection. When we recite upon entering our homes, we invite blessings. When someone is ill, Quranic recitation can serve as spiritual healing. Every letter we recite earns us ten good deeds. These are all genuine, legitimate secondary benefits.

However, when people lose connection with the primary function of revelation - guidance itself - all that remains is a transactional relationship focused solely on benefits. This is a dangerous shift that we can observe even in contemporary Muslim communities.

Many Muslims maintain distance from the Quran except when they need specific benefits: reciting at funerals to send blessings to the deceased, reciting at weddings to bless the gathering, or intensifying Quranic recitation during Ramadan primarily for the extra rewards. While none of these practices are wrong in themselves, when the foundational relationship with the Quran as guidance disappears, people become vulnerable to distortions.

### The Warning Against Misuse

The angels would warn anyone who came to learn: **إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تَكْفُرْ** - "We are nothing but a trial, so do not disbelieve." The word فِتْنَة is particularly instructive. In Arabic, fitna refers to the process of purifying gold by heating it intensely, causing impurities to burn away. In the Quran, fitna describes something that isn't evil in itself but has the potential to be misused, leading to harm.

Allah ﷻ says: "Your wealth and your children are a fitna." Wealth isn't inherently evil, nor are children, but both can be catastrophically misused. Similarly, the knowledge being taught by these angels, while potentially protective against harmful magic, could be twisted for corrupt purposes by those with wrong intentions.

The ayah describes how some people learned **مَا يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِۦ بَيْنَ ٱلْمَرْءِ وَزَوْجِهِۦ** - "what would cause separation between a man and his wife." They took what could have been protective knowledge and weaponized it against the most sacred of human relationships.

### The Reality of Harm and Benefit

Allah ﷻ then reminds us: **وَمَا هُم بِضَآرِّينَ بِهِۦ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ** - "And they cannot harm anyone with it except by Allah's permission." No matter how powerful any form of magic or harm appears, it operates only within the boundaries that Allah ﷻ permits. This is our fundamental protection and comfort.

The ayah continues with a striking observation: **وَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنفَعُهُمْ** - "And they learned what harmed them and did not benefit them." This reveals the tragedy of corrupted intentions. Even when someone learns something that could potentially be beneficial, if their intentions are false and their hearts corrupt, that knowledge will only harm them.

This principle extends to the Quran itself. Allah ﷻ tells us: **يُضِلُّ بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا وَيَهْدِى بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا** - "He misguides many with it, and He guides many with it." The same divine revelation guides those who come to it with sincerity and misguides those who approach it with corrupt intentions. The Quran itself doesn't change - what changes is the heart and intention of the one engaging with it.

### The Ultimate Exchange

The ayah concludes with a sobering assessment: **وَلَقَدْ عَلِمُوا۟ لَمَنِ ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ** - "And they certainly knew that whoever purchased it would have no share in the Hereafter." They knew the consequences. This wasn't ignorance - it was a deliberate choice despite knowing the price.

**وَلَبِئْسَ مَا شَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ** - "And wretched is that for which they sold themselves." They traded their souls, their eternal well-being, for temporary worldly gains through these corrupt practices.

The final phrase **لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ** - "If only they knew" - is particularly tragic. They had knowledge, but not the kind that transforms. They knew intellectually but didn't truly understand or internalize the weight of what they were doing.

translation: And if they had believed and feared Allah, then the reward from Allah would have been [far] better, if they only knew
commentary:
**وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَمَثُوبَةٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ ۖ لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ**

After exposing the corruption and tragic choices of those who pursued magic and misused divine knowledge, Allah ﷻ presents the alternative path - the one they should have taken.

**وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟** - "And if only they had believed and been conscious of Allah." This simple statement contains the entire solution. Instead of chasing after harmful knowledge and corrupt practices, if they had simply committed to faith and maintained God-consciousness, everything would have been different.

**لَمَثُوبَةٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ** - "The reward from Allah would have been better." The word مَثُوبَة refers to a reward, a recompense that comes directly from Allah ﷻ. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't even need to specify what this reward is. He simply says it's "better" - better than anything they sought through their corrupt practices, better than the temporary gains of magic, better than the false power they thought they were acquiring.

The phrase مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ (from Allah) emphasizes the divine source. When something comes from Allah ﷻ, it carries inherent blessing, purity, and lasting benefit. Compare this to what they were actually pursuing - knowledge that harmed them, practices that corrupted their souls, and choices that cost them the Hereafter.

The ayah concludes, like the previous one, with **لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ** - "If only they knew." This repeated phrase drives home the tragedy. The option was always there. The better path was never hidden from them. They simply failed to recognize what was truly in their best interest.

translation: O you who have believed, say not [to Allah 's Messenger], "Ra'ina" but say, "Unthurna" and listen. And for the disbelievers is a painful punishment
commentary:
**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقُولُوا۟ رَٰعِنَا وَقُولُوا۟ ٱنظُرْنَا وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ**

The address shifts dramatically. After discussing the corruption among the People of the Book, Allah ﷻ now directly addresses the believers: **يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟** - "O you who believe."

The instruction seems deceptively simple: **لَا تَقُولُوا۟ رَٰعِنَا وَقُولُوا۟ ٱنظُرْنَا** - "Do not say 'Ra'ina' but say 'Undhurna.'" Both phrases essentially mean "please attend to us" or "please give us your attention," commonly used when addressing the Prophet ﷺ during his teachings. So why does Allah ﷻ prohibit one and command the other?

The word رَٰعِنَا could be manipulated by those with ill intentions. Some among the People of the Book who harbored hostility toward the Prophet ﷺ would use this word in a way that, in their language or dialect, carried a mocking or disrespectful connotation. They found a way to appear as if they were being respectful while actually insulting him.

This teaches us something profound about Islamic etiquette and communication. It's not enough for our words to seem appropriate - we must ensure they cannot be twisted or misunderstood. We must close the doors to mockery and disrespect, even if it means adjusting our vocabulary. The alternative, انظُرْنَا, carries the same meaning but without the potential for manipulation.

**وَٱسْمَعُوا۟** - "And listen" - this command goes beyond physical hearing. It's a call to truly listen, to pay attention, to receive with the intent to understand and implement. When the Prophet ﷺ speaks, when the Quran is recited, listening isn't passive - it's an active, engaged state of receiving guidance.

The ayah concludes with a stark warning: **وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ** - "And for the disbelievers is a painful punishment." This isn't random. It's connected to the context. Those who mock, those who play word games with sacred communication, those who pretend respect while harboring contempt - they are warned that there are serious consequences for such behavior.

translation: Neither those who disbelieve from the People of the Scripture nor the polytheists wish that any good should be sent down to you from your Lord. But Allah selects for His mercy whom He wills, and Allah is the possessor of great bounty
commentary:
**مَّا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَلَا ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ**

After exposing the historical corruptions and current manipulations, Allah ﷻ now reveals the psychological state of those who oppose the truth. The ayah describes two groups: **ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ** - "those who disbelieved from among the People of the Book" and **ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ** - "those who commit shirk."

Notice the precise language. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "the People of the Book are disbelievers." He says "those who disbelieved from among the People of the Book." This is a critical distinction. Not every person from the People of the Book is automatically a disbeliever in the Quranic sense. The term كَافِر is very specific - it refers to someone who has been exposed to clear truth, recognizes it internally, yet deliberately rejects it.

The same precision applies to the polytheists of Makkah. Among them were those absolutely committed to rejection, but that didn't mean every single person in Makkah had genuinely been exposed to or understood the message of Islam, even after years of the Prophet's ﷺ mission there.

This understanding is powerfully confirmed by a later revelation, given after the conquest of Makkah. Allah ﷻ instructs: **وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ٱسْتَجَارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّىٰ يَسْمَعَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ** - "And if any one of the polytheists seeks your protection, then grant him protection so that he may hear the words of Allah." Even after twenty years, after all the battles and conflicts, if someone says, "I never really got to hear the message properly; I want to understand what this religion actually teaches," they must be given that opportunity without pressure or judgment.

### The Psychology of Opposition

The ayah reveals their inner state: **مَّا يَوَدُّ** - "They do not wish" or "they have no desire." The word يَوَدُّ speaks to deep-seated longing and desire. Allah ﷻ is exposing what's in their hearts: they actively do not want **أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ** - "that any good should be sent down upon you from your Lord."

After years of exclusive religious status, of being known as "the People of the Book," they now see this status being transferred. They see themselves being exposed - their hidden corruptions, their manipulation of scripture, their pursuit of harmful practices. The clouds are gathering. The storm they fear is approaching: the removal of their special status.

Their hostility isn't based on intellectual disagreement with Islamic teachings. It's rooted in the recognition that they're losing something they held onto as an identity marker. They failed the test. Opportunity after opportunity was given, but they squandered each one. And now they see divine favor moving elsewhere.

### Divine Choice and Infinite Grace

Allah ﷻ responds to their resentment with a declaration of His absolute sovereignty: **وَٱللَّهُ يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ** - "And Allah selects for His mercy whom He wills."

The word يَخْتَصُّ means to single out, to select specifically. Allah's mercy isn't distributed based on historical privilege or ethnic identity.
