His mercy goes where He wills it to go, and He grants it to whom He chooses. No people can claim monopoly over divine guidance. No community can assert that they alone deserve God's favor.

The ayah concludes with one of the most beautiful divine names: **وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ** - "And Allah is the possessor of great bounty." The word فَضْل (fadl) refers to bounty, grace, favor that goes beyond what's earned or deserved. It's giving that exceeds all expectation.

And it's not just fadl - it's **ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ** - great, tremendous, magnificent bounty. No matter how much divine favor has been distributed, no matter how many prophets have been sent, no matter how much revelation has been given, Allah's bounty hasn't diminished. It remains vast, infinite, available to all who turn to Him with sincerity.

This is our comfort and our hope. Whatever corruptions occurred in previous communities, whatever mistakes we ourselves make, Allah's doors remain open. His mercy continues to flow. His bounty never depletes. And He continues to select for His mercy those who turn to Him, regardless of their past or their lineage.

translation: We do not abrogate a verse or cause it to be forgotten except that We bring forth [one] better than it or similar to it. Do you not know that Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
۞ مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَآ أَوْ مِثْلِهَآ ۗ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

A new objection arose from the Israelites: "If this is truly revelation, why doesn't it match exactly what we were given? We have a complete legal system, specific laws revealed to Musa (AS). Why is the Quran different?"

Allah ﷻ responds by explaining the concept of abrogation—both between previous revelations and this final revelation, and potentially within the Quran itself. Before we proceed, we must handle this subject with extreme care. We're dealing with Allah's ﷻ words, with final revelation. For any human being to claim that part of Allah's ﷻ word is no longer applicable is an enormously weighty statement. Before anyone dares say an ayah is abrogated, they must exhaust every possible effort to find another explanation.

Unfortunately, in our time, this concept has been treated far too casually. Some extremist groups claim that all verses about patience, dialogue, and tolerance are abrogated by later verses about fighting. By this logic, two-thirds of the Quran—including the extensive discourse with the People of the Book—becomes irrelevant. This is absurd. You cannot simultaneously be in dialogue with people and at war with them. You cannot invite someone to dinner and shoot at them at the same time.

Our classical scholars approached this subject with profound caution. Imam al-Suyuti initially identified a certain number of abrogated verses, then other scholars reduced that number significantly. The trend among serious scholarship has been toward minimizing claims of abrogation, recognizing that Allah ﷻ left this matter open for human understanding, and we shouldn't close it prematurely.

مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ "Whatever We abrogate of a verse"—notice the special grammatical form here that creates a hypothetical: "We would not abrogate any verse whatsoever." The ayah continues: أَوْ نُنسِهَا "or cause it to be forgotten." There are two ways revelation can be removed: explicit abrogation or being forgotten over time. We know conversations Nuh (AS) had with his people because the Quran records them, but we don't possess his complete revelation. We have fragments of wisdom from the scriptures of Ibrahim (AS) that the Prophet ﷺ shared with us, but not the complete scrolls. Much has been forgotten.

Now, regarding the progression of revelation itself: previous scriptures weren't arbitrary. They represented stages in the evolution of human society. When a child is in first grade, you teach certain lessons. In second grade, the lessons evolve. In third grade, they develop further. Humanity itself was going through a maturation process. Society was becoming increasingly complex, so Allah ﷻ gave instructions appropriate to each stage.

Initially, prophets taught moral principles: goodness to parents, honesty in business, kindness, humility. Then, with Musa (AS), serious law arrived—constitutional law for a society. Regulations about worship were prescribed, legal punishments were established, detailed commandments were given. This represented advancement, greater complexity matching societal development.

But what happened? The Israelites received these laws but lost the spiritual heart that should animate them. They became obsessed with technical details while the purpose—connection to Allah ﷻ—faded away. Isa (AS) didn't come to replace the law but to restore its soul. He repeatedly emphasized: "I came to confirm what is in the Torah." His message was fundamentally spiritual, calling people back to the essence they had lost.

Consider prayer. We have laws about wudu, about the proper direction, about clean clothing, about what to recite and when, about the angle of ruku and the position in sujud, about which way to say salam first and which way second. All these technical aspects are important. But if all that remains is technique with no khushu, no heart, no connection to Allah ﷻ—what have we really preserved?

People fall into two extremes. Some say, "Prayer is just a physical exercise. I can connect to God in my own way, sitting on my bed, holding my hands up." Others become so consumed with technical perfection that they never emphasize khushu at all. The Israelites had reached one extreme, so a correction was needed.

Finally, humanity reached a point where Allah ﷻ chose to reveal the final message—guidance not just for Arabs but for all of humanity until the end of time. Some things in previous revelations had limited relevance, applicable to specific societies in specific times. With this final revelation, such elements were abrogated, and notably, most abrogations made things easier, not harder.

The entire Sabbath day—where the Muslims of Musa's (AS) time were forbidden from any work—was eased for this Ummah to just the time of Jumu'ah prayer on Friday. Certain foods the Israelites had made forbidden for themselves (like camel meat, which Ya'qub (AS) personally disliked) were made permissible. Allah ﷻ says: "All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel made forbidden for himself before the Torah was revealed."

What the Quran did was confirm valid laws from previous revelations, provide ease where there had been hardship, and complete what humanity wasn't yet ready for. This explains why differences exist.

Regarding abrogation within the Quran itself: we must be extraordinarily careful. Some scholars believe there is no abrogation within the Quran at all. Those who do accept it don't view it as cancellation but as progressive revelation leading to a final version.

Take the example of alcohol. The first revelation stated: "The sin in intoxicants and gambling is greater than their benefit." Even here, note the word: إِثْمُهُمَا (ithmuhuma)—their sin, not their harm. It's not a matter of medical harm versus benefit; it's about sin. This first statement acknowledged some benefit while establishing that sin outweighs it.

The next revelation commanded: "Don't approach prayer while intoxicated until you know what you're saying." Does this necessarily nullify the previous statement? Not at all. It's a further elaboration, a continuation of the same conversation. Consider: Muslims were praying five times daily as a bare minimum, and the Companions (RA) prayed far more frequently. With prayers every few hours, if someone is committed to salah, there's no window to become intoxicated without missing prayer while drunk. This effectively curtailed alcohol consumption without an outright ban.

Finally came the definitive command: "It is an abomination from the work of Shaytan, so avoid it." But even this doesn't mean the previous verses have no value. They represent stages of guidance, each building on the last.

Similarly with inheritance laws. Allah ﷻ first gave a general principle: "Leave something good for your parents and close relatives." Later, detailed fractions were revealed: quarters, sixths, eighths, thirds—specific shares based on whether you have children, parents, siblings, whether you're married or divorced. Are the earlier general statements abrogated? Or are they elaborated upon, explained in detail? The healthier way to view this is as elaboration, not cancellation.

نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَآ أَوْ مِثْلِهَآ "We bring better than it or similar to it." If something is forgotten from previous revelations, Allah ﷻ may restore it (something similar) or provide something superior. The ayah ends with a rhetorical question: أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ "Do you not know that Allah has power over all things?" Why would Allah ﷻ do this? Someone might ask, "Why is this necessary?" The answer: Don't you already know He's in complete control? This is part of His wisdom. He doesn't need to explain Himself to you.

translation: Do you not know that to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and [that] you have not besides Allah any protector or any helper
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ

أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ "Do you not know"—again, a rhetorical question building on the previous one. Don't you know that Allah ﷻ exclusively possesses the dominion of the heavens and the earth? The repetition of أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ emphasizes that these should be foundational truths already established in our consciousness.

The Israelites were claiming, "This should be our revelation, our status, our privilege." Allah ﷻ responds: Everything in the heavens and earth belongs to Him alone. Who are you to make claims of ownership over divine favor?

وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ "And you have no protector besides Allah, nor any helper." This is both warning and reassurance. For those opposing the truth, it's a warning: no one can protect you from Allah's ﷻ judgment. For the believers, it's reassurance: you don't need any protector or helper besides Allah ﷻ.

translation: Or do you intend to ask your Messenger as Moses was asked before? And whoever exchanges faith for disbelief has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way
commentary:
أَمْ تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ رَسُولَكُمْ كَمَا سُئِلَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۗ وَمَن يَتَبَدَّلِ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ

Now Allah ﷻ directly addresses the Muslims: أَمْ تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ رَسُولَكُمْ "Or do you intend to question your messenger?" Notice: He says "your messenger" (rasulakum), not "their messenger," because the Israelites refused to acknowledge the Prophet ﷺ as their messenger. They insisted he was only the messenger of the Arabs, not theirs.

كَمَا سُئِلَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ "As Musa was questioned before?" This raises an important point. Is asking questions wrong? We know from previous passages that the Companions (RA) were sometimes reluctant to ask the Prophet ﷺ questions out of respect. But the key distinction is between asking for clarification and questioning validity.

When Musa (AS) commanded the Israelites to slaughter a cow, they responded with skepticism and mockery: "Are you making fun of us?" That's not asking for clarification—that's questioning the integrity of the message, challenging the messenger's authority. That kind of questioning reveals a lack of faith. If someone truly believes this is revelation from Allah ﷻ and this is His messenger, there's no room to question its validity.

We are absolutely encouraged to ask questions about our religion—to understand, to learn, to seek clarity. But we should never question its integrity, never approach it with the attitude of "This doesn't make sense" or "I don't agree with this ruling." That very attitude demonstrates an absence of genuine faith.

وَمَن يَتَبَدَّلِ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ "And whoever exchanges faith for disbelief"—these challenging questions are symptoms of deeper disbelief—فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ "he has certainly gone astray from the soundness of the way."

The phrase ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ is profound. There are two words for going astray: ضَلَّ and أَضَلَّ. Dalla means you were on a path and took a wrong turn—you deviated but you know a path exists, you're just lost from it now. Adalla is worse: you don't even know the path exists. You're completely unaware there's a destination to reach. This ayah uses dalla as-sawa as-sabil: he's lost the most even, the straightest, the soundest path. It's not just any path—it's the clear, straight, perfect path, and he's lost it entirely.

translation: Many of the People of the Scripture wish they could turn you back to disbelief after you have believed, out of envy from themselves [even] after the truth has become clear to them. So pardon and overlook until Allah delivers His command. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كُفَّارًا حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَٱعْفُوا۟ وَٱصْفَحُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ "Many from among the People of the Book wish"—again, notice: many from among them, not all of them. لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كُفَّارًا "if only they could turn you back into disbelievers after your faith."

This is deeply human psychology. When someone is exposed as corrupt or failing, they hate standing out. Misery loves company. If you're a lazy student not studying, and your roommate is studying hard, you have two options: start studying yourself, or convince your roommate to come to the movie with you. The latter is easier. Every time you see him studying, you feel guilty, so you try to drag him down.

When the People of the Book were exposed—their hidden corruptions brought to light, their distortions of scripture revealed, their hypocrisies made plain—they couldn't stand that the Muslims remained guided. They wanted everyone tainted equally so they wouldn't feel their failure as acutely.

حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم "Out of jealousy from within themselves"—this jealousy emanates from deep within, from the core of their beings. مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ "even after the truth has become clear to them." This is the tragedy: they recognize the truth internally but let jealousy override it.

There's also historical context here. People who became Muslim in Madinah—whether previously Jewish or Christian—were leaving their communities. Religious leaders were losing their congregations, their followers, their community members. There was a concerted effort to win them back, to attack Islam as a way of recovering lost community. This is a natural human reaction when you feel your community disintegrating.

فَٱعْفُوا۟ وَٱصْفَحُوا۟ "So pardon and turn the page." The command to the Muslims is remarkable: just forgive, overlook it, turn the page. The word صَفْح literally refers to one side of a page. When you turn the page, you no longer see what was on the previous side. Don't dwell on it. Don't obsess over it. Don't get paranoid about their plots.

Muslims today often become overwhelmed by the plots of others. "They're making websites against Islam! They have radio shows! They're going to say things about us! They're trying to make us lose our faith!" Allah ﷻ tells us: relax. Ignore the noise. Don't let it consume you.

حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ "Until Allah brings His decision." There's wisdom in this timing. Historically, the clash with the Makkans was imminent. The Battle of Badr was approaching. The Muslims had bigger concerns than dealing with internal opposition while simultaneously facing external threats. Allah ﷻ sets priorities: focus on what matters most right now. Deal with the greater challenge first, then address the other issues.

This teaches us about prioritization in our own lives. We face many problems simultaneously, but we must identify the most critical issues and address them first. Don't get sidetracked. It's far too easy to get distracted by lesser concerns while greater challenges loom.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ "Indeed, Allah has power over all things." If Allah ﷻ is in complete control, then all these plots ultimately mean nothing. Why worry excessively? That statement alone should bring peace to the believers' hearts.

translation: And establish prayer and give zakah, and whatever good you put forward for yourselves - you will find it with Allah. Indeed, Allah of what you do, is Seeing
commentary:
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

After addressing what not to worry about, Allah ﷻ redirects attention to what deserves focus: وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ "Establish the prayer and give zakah." If you want to be preoccupied with something, be preoccupied with this. These two commands beautifully encapsulate the entirety of our religion.

Prayer isn't just about the physical act—it's about connection with Allah ﷻ. The word صلاة itself relates to connection. Our prayers are the means by which we connect to Allah ﷻ, and the purpose is to maintain that connection between prayers. This is why Allah ﷻ says: "Guard all the prayers and the middle prayer." Guard them, maintain them, and they will maintain you until the next connection point.

The longest gap between prayers is from Isha to Fajr—but you should be sleeping then, when you're least likely to be distracted from remembrance. The prayers during waking hours are distributed with profound wisdom, providing regular intervals to refuel, reconnect, and realign.

When you're truly connected to Allah ﷻ through salah, you naturally stay away from sin. On a macro level, zakah addresses your relationship with wealth and how you earn it. Giving zakah on impure income is pointless—if you're earning haram to begin with, zakah can't purify it. The two primary things—consuming what is halal and pure, and maintaining a clean income—are both summarized in these fundamental pillars: salah and zakah.

Salah takes care of your relationship with Allah ﷻ. Zakah ensures your finances are clean, which means your sources of income must be clean. These two pillars, maintained properly, encompass the essence of the entire religion.

وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ "And whatever you invest for yourselves of good"—I prefer "invest" over the common translation "send forward" because it captures the reality better. You're investing in your own future, your own account with Allah ﷻ.

تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ "You will find it with Allah." If there's one thing deserving constant preoccupation, constant thought, constant worry, it should be compiling good deeds that you will find with Allah ﷻ. That's what should occupy your mind.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ "Indeed, Allah is Seeing of what you do." This is deeply soul-searching. Whatever good you do, you will find it with Allah ﷻ, and Allah ﷻ is completely watching what you do. He sees the inside and the outside, the intention and the outcome. Everyone can see someone make a generous donation, but only Allah ﷻ knows whether their heart was truly connected to Him when they did it, or whether it was for show. بَصِيرٌ—He's in full view of everything.

translation: And they say, "None will enter Paradise except one who is a Jew or a Christian." That is [merely] their wishful thinking, Say, "Produce your proof, if you should be truthful
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ لَن يَدْخُلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَن كَانَ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۗ تِلْكَ أَمَانِيُّهُمْ ۗ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Having answered the question about abrogation, the People of the Book now fall back to their standard slogan: وَقَالُوا۟ لَن يَدْخُلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَن كَانَ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ "And they say: 'None will enter Paradise except those who are Jews or Christians.'" The verb كَانَ implies "has been"—suggesting longevity, establishment. "We've been here. You're the new kid on the block. We've got history, legacy, tradition."

This attitude persists today. I was recently listening to a discussion on NPR about Mormons, since some presidential candidates are Mormon. The commentator listed: "Judaism, Christianity, original religions... Islam, loophole... Mormonism, loophole." They lumped Islam with Mormonism as recent innovations, cults with their own prophets and books. This dismissive attitude toward newer revelation is nothing new.

تِلْكَ أَمَانِيُّهُمْ "Those are their wishful thoughts"—mere fantasies without foundation. قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ "Say: Bring your evidence." 

The word بُرْهَان is powerful—it means a proof so definitive that it completely stumps the opposition. You have no comeback after burhan is presented. It's an unanswerable argument.

إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ "If you are truthful." When you engage with people making grand claims about salvation and you ask them for evidence, what typically happens? Their voices get louder. Volume becomes the substitute for proof. Raising your voice doesn't constitute evidence—it's actually an indication that no evidence exists. All you have are raised voices.

translation: Yes [on the contrary], whoever submits his face in Islam to Allah while being a doer of good will have his reward with his Lord. And no fear will there be concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُۥ لِلَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ فَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

بَلَىٰ "However"—now comes the true criterion. مَنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُۥ لِلَّهِ "Whoever submits his face to Allah"—that's Islam. The face represents your entire being, your identity, your direction. Submission to Allah ﷻ is intensely personal, not communal. Submitting your face is about your individual relationship with your Creator.

وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ "And he is a doer of excellence"—this is the ultimate level. How far can you go in Islam? All the way to ihsan. The Prophet ﷺ defined ihsan: "That you worship Allah as though you see Him, and if you cannot see Him, then know that He sees you." Islam is the beginning; ihsan is the goal.

فَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ "Then he will have his reward with his Lord"—notice the singular. Your reward is yours individually, exclusively. But then comes a remarkable shift:

وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ "And there will be no fear upon them, nor will they grieve"—suddenly, plural. Why this switch from singular to plural?

Submission to Allah ﷻ, your personal Islam, is between you and Him alone. You're not doing it because others are watching; you're not performing for a community. On the Day of Judgment, you will stand before Allah ﷻ individually: أمتي، أمتي، أمتي ("My Ummah, my Ummah, my Ummah")—everyone comes before Him alone. Your compensation is exclusively, individually yours.

But when it comes to safety, when it comes to the negation of fear, collectivity is mentioned. What's the natural human instinct when we sense danger? To be around others, to find safety in numbers. "All those people are gathering there—I should go there too. I shouldn't be alone."

لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ "No fear upon them"—Allah ﷻ will keep them safe as a collectivity, and being in that collectivity will itself provide psychological comfort. When you're around people who don't have their books in their left hands, when you're surrounded by people whose foreheads aren't branded, you feel safer. You have your own book, your own record, but seeing others in good standing calms your nerves. "These people seem okay. I recognize some faces. Oh, you're here too! Alhamdulillah."

Interestingly, you will find people in Paradise you didn't particularly like in the world, but Allah ﷻ surgically removes ill feelings from hearts in Jannah. Those negative emotions wouldn't leave otherwise—they have to be extracted. Once removed, everyone exists in genuine harmony.

وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ "Nor will they grieve"—grief, like fear, is multiplied in collectivity. When two people cry, a third person who doesn't even know why starts crying. Sadness is infectious. But so is joy, so is happiness. When you're around happy people, you become even happier. The blessing multiplies.

The ayah begins with individual compensation but ends with collective blessing—you're rewarded individually, but you experience the joy of that reward together with countless others who have also submitted to Allah ﷻ and pursued excellence.

translation: The Jews say "The Christians have nothing [true] to stand on," and the Christians say, "The Jews have nothing to stand on," although they [both] recite the Scripture. Thus the polytheists speak the same as their words. But Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ
commentary:
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ لَيْسَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَقَالَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ لَيْسَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَهُمْ يَتْلُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ مِثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ

Earlier, they said nobody enters Paradise except Jews or Christians. Now they contradict themselves entirely. When Jews and Christians talk about Muslims, they unite: "Only we have the path to salvation." But when they talk about each other, everything changes.

وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ لَيْسَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ "The Jews say: The Christians have no basis whatsoever." Their critique is harsh: Jesus (AS) is not a legitimate prophet, not even a legitimate child, they killed Christ—accusations and dismissals.

وَقَالَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ لَيْسَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ "And the Christians say: The Jews have no basis whatsoever." They counter: the Jews are criminals, they rejected truth, they were given blessings but didn't benefit from them.

وَهُمْ يَتْلُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ "And they both recite the Book"—this is the tragedy. They're reading the same scripture, the same book, and arriving at completely opposite conclusions about each other. They're both reading, both claiming to follow divine revelation, yet completely dismissing each other's validity.

Does this sound familiar? Should we be looking in a mirror when we read this? It's an ugly picture, but one we need to confront.

كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ مِثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ "Just like that, those who have no knowledge say the same as what they say." Now there's a third group—completely ignorant people who don't follow any scripture—and they also say, "Everybody else is wrong. None of them have anything of value."

So we have three groups: two supposedly knowledgeable groups, both reading scripture, both hating on each other; and a third ignorant group dismissing everyone. This phenomenon exists throughout history and continues today.

Even among Muslims, we see this same disease. "Those people follow that scholar? They're not on anything." "That group? They have no basis." "This madhab versus that madhab"—dismissing each other while all claiming to follow the same Quran and Sunnah.

فَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ "So Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection." Ultimately, human disputes don't matter. All these arguments, all these dismissals, all these claims and counterclaims—Allah ﷻ will settle them all.

فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ "Concerning that in which they used to differ." Every difference, every dispute, every disagreement will be resolved. The truth will be made plain, and all arguments will cease.

This ayah serves as both warning and comfort. Warning to those who arrogantly dismiss others without basis, and comfort to those who are unjustly dismissed—your vindication will come. Don't waste energy on these disputes now. Focus on what truly matters: establishing prayer, giving zakah, investing in good deeds that you will find with Allah ﷻ. Let Him handle the judgment.

translation: And who are more unjust than those who prevent the name of Allah from being mentioned in His mosques and strive toward their destruction. It is not for them to enter them except in fear. For them in this world is disgrace, and they will have in the Hereafter a great punishment
commentary:
**وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن مَّنَعَ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ أَن يُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ وَسَعَىٰ فِى خَرَابِهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا كَانَ لَهُمْ أَن يَدْخُلُوهَآ إِلَّا خَآئِفِينَ ۚ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ**

This ayah opens significant themes that will unfold throughout the coming passages of Surah Al-Baqarah. To understand its depth, we must recognize the historical context in which it was revealed. This is an early Madani ayah, revealed shortly after the Prophet ﷺ migrated to Medina. The wounds of being expelled from Makkah were still fresh, and the violation of the House of Allah ﷻ by the Quraysh was still vivid in the hearts of the believers.

Allah ﷻ begins with a rhetorical question that establishes the gravity of the crime: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن مَّنَعَ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ - "Who could be a worse wrongdoer than the one who forbids the houses of Allah?" This is a direct reference to the Mushrikun of Makkah who prevented believers from worshiping at Al-Masjid al-Haram. They didn't just discourage worship - they physically attacked believers who wanted to pray there, including the Prophet ﷺ himself.

The crime is described in two parts. First, أَن يُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ - they prevented Allah's ﷻ name from being mentioned in His houses. Second, وَسَعَىٰ فِى خَرَابِهَآ - they actively worked toward its ruin and corruption. The word سَعَىٰ indicates deliberate effort and striving. They weren't passively allowing corruption; they were making dedicated efforts to destroy the sanctity of the House built by Ibrahim (AS).

Allah ﷻ then pronounces the consequences of this crime. The first punishment is psychological and immediate: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا كَانَ لَهُمْ أَن يَدْخُلُوهَآ إِلَّا خَآئِفِينَ - "Those people, it is not worthy of them, not appropriate for them to enter it except in a state of fear." The tables will be completely turned. They instilled fear in the believers; they tortured those who wanted to worship. But Allah ﷻ is foretelling that they will be the ones entering the House in fear. This is an allusion to the coming conquest of Makkah, which was still a decade away from this revelation.

The punishment continues: لَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ - "For them in this world is humiliation." At the time of this revelation, the Quraysh were not humiliated. They were in a position of power, so much so that they successfully expelled the Muslims. But Allah ﷻ is declaring what will come - their eventual defeat and disgrace. This is a glimpse of the future that Allah ﷻ grants to the believers early in the Madani period.

وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ - "And for them in the Hereafter is tremendous punishment." The worldly humiliation is only the beginning; the real reckoning awaits in the next life.

By mentioning this crime and its punishment, Allah ﷻ is signaling to the Muslim community that even though they have moved to Medina and are establishing themselves there, the mission objective hasn't changed. The target is still Makkah. Don't forget that the House of Allah ﷻ remains violated. It is only when Makkah is conquered that Allah ﷻ will declare the completion of the religion.

translation: And to Allah belongs the east and the west. So wherever you [might] turn, there is the Face of Allah. Indeed, Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing
commentary:
**وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا۟ فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ**

Immediately after mentioning the violation of the Kaaba, Allah ﷻ makes a profound declaration: وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ - "To Allah belongs the East and the West." This ayah addresses a significant test the believers were facing regarding the Qiblah.

When the Prophet ﷺ lived in Makkah, he prayed in such a way that he could face both the Kaaba and Al-Aqsa simultaneously. The Kaaba would be in front of him, and Al-Aqsa would be beyond it in the same direction. He had that geographic advantage in Makkah. The direction of prayer toward Al-Aqsa was still valid at that time, following the Sharia of Musa (AS) that hadn't yet been abrogated.

However, when the Prophet ﷺ migrated to Medina, this became impossible. Medina is positioned between Makkah and Al-Aqsa - you must face one or the other. Every time the Prophet ﷺ prayed toward Al-Aqsa, his back was toward the Kaaba, the house built by his forefather Ibrahim (AS).

This was an immense test for the believers. Their entire lives, they had prayed toward the Kaaba during the days of Jahiliyyah. They had loyalty and allegiance to that direction. Now they had moved to Medina and turned their backs on the very Kaaba they used to face. It wasn't just a question of faith - it was a question of loyalty and identity.

Allah ﷻ addresses this tension by declaring that He owns all directions. فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا۟ فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ ٱللَّهِ - "Wherever you may turn, there is the Face of Allah." The word ثَمَّ means "there" - wherever you turn, you'll find Allah's ﷻ pleasure. Don't think that by turning away from one direction, you're turning away from Allah ﷻ Himself.

This ayah plants the seeds for the change of Qiblah that will be discussed multiple times in the coming passages. Allah ﷻ is preparing the believers' hearts for this momentous change. He concludes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ - "Certainly Allah is all-encompassing, all-knowing." He is vast enough to know wherever you may be and whatever you may be doing. You cannot reduce Allah ﷻ to a particular direction.

translation: They say, "Allah has taken a son." Exalted is He! Rather, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. All are devoutly obedient to Him
commentary:
**وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ بَل لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥ قَـٰنِتُونَ**

The surah now shifts to address another community - the Christians. After discussing the idol worshipers of Makkah, Allah ﷻ addresses another form of shirk, one that exists without physical idols. وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا - "And they said Allah has taken a son." 

Allah's ﷻ response is immediate and decisive: سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ - "He is far above that." The word سُبْحَـٰنَ is used in the Quran when something inappropriate is said about Allah ﷻ. It means He is too perfect for such talk, way above what they are attributing to Him.

بَل لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - "Rather, to Him exclusively belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth." When the word لَّهُۥ ("to Him") comes by itself without anything following it, it emphasizes everything, all together. Every single thing in existence belongs exclusively to Him.

كُلٌّ لَّهُۥ قَـٰنِتُونَ - "All are in complete subservience exclusively to Him." The word قَـٰنِتُونَ describes a level beyond mere obedience. It indicates someone who is not just obedient but eager and ready to obey, standing by in complete willingness to serve their master. Everything in the heavens and earth exists in this state of qunoot before Allah ﷻ.

translation: Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is
commentary:
**بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ**

Allah ﷻ continues His response to the claim of divine sonship with a description of His absolute creative power. بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - "The Originator of the heavens and the earth." The word بَدِيعُ comes from the concept of creation ex nihilo - creation from absolute nothing. This is different from خَلْق (creation), which can include creating something from something else, like creating humans from clay. But بَدِيعُ specifically means He created the entire universe from nothing whatsoever.

وَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ - "And whenever He decides a matter, He only says to it 'Be,' and it is." This statement directly addresses the Christian claim. Allah ﷻ is teaching them: The One who created the entire universe from nothing - can't He create a human without a father? The One who can say "Be" and bring anything into existence - why would He need to physically father a child? Why do you have to go as far as to make shirk and say that He fathered? This is Allah ﷻ protecting their theology and inviting them to correct their understanding.

translation: Those who do not know say, "Why does Allah not speak to us or there come to us a sign?" Thus spoke those before them like their words. Their hearts resemble each other. We have shown clearly the signs to a people who are certain [in faith]
commentary:
**وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ لَوْلَا يُكَلِّمُنَا ٱللَّهُ أَوْ تَأْتِينَآ ءَايَةٌ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ ۘ تَشَـٰبَهَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ**

Now Allah ﷻ returns to address the Mushrikun of Makkah, documenting their constant criticisms. وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - "And those who have no knowledge said." The description "those who have no knowledge" is significant - it indicates their arguments come from ignorance, not genuine inquiry.

لَوْلَا يُكَلِّمُنَا ٱللَّهُ أَوْ تَأْتِينَآ ءَايَةٌ - "Why doesn't Allah speak to us, or why doesn't a sign come to us?" These were among the most constant objections of the Quraysh before migration. "What's so special about you? Your parents are dead, you're an orphan, you're not even wealthy, you're not a tribal leader. Why would Allah choose you? Why doesn't Allah talk to us directly? Why don't we get revelations? Why do they only come to you?"

These arguments might sound novel to modern critics, but Allah ﷻ reveals their timelessness: كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ - "Exactly like this, those who came before them said the exact same thing, just like their words." People think they're being original and clever with their criticisms of revelation and prophethood, but these are ancient arguments. What seems like modern skepticism is actually the same old arguments made by pagans in the desert centuries ago.

تَشَـٰبَهَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ - "Their hearts are very similar." Not only are their words similar, but their hearts carry the same disease. This is one of the Quran's remarkable features - it discusses people living in different societies, different centuries, different worlds, yet they say exactly the same things. We'll see this pattern with evildoers, but also with the righteous. Different prophets who never met each other will be quoted saying identical words, delivering the same message with perfect consistency.

قَدْ بَيَّنَّا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ - "We have made the ayat abundantly clear for a people who want to be convinced." The grammatical structure here is crucial. The word يُوقِنُونَ is actually a verb functioning as an adjective, which transforms the meaning. Instead of "a convinced people," it means "a people who want to be convinced."

Allah ﷻ is making a powerful argument: If you were really looking to be convinced, the ayat would be enough. The fact that you're making these demands shows you're not actually seeking conviction - you've already convinced yourself not to believe. You just want to make excuses and justify your rejection. This is captured beautifully in the contrast between ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (those who have no knowledge) at the beginning and لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (people who want certainty) at the end.

translation: Indeed, We have sent you, [O Muhammad], with the truth as a bringer of good tidings and a warner, and you will not be asked about the companions of Hellfire
commentary:
**إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُ عَنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ**

Having established that the revelation is sufficient, Allah ﷻ now addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly regarding his role and responsibilities. إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ - "We have sent you with the truth." Don't worry about what they say. What you have is the truth, and Allah ﷻ Himself is declaring this.

بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا - "As a bearer of good news and a warner." The Prophet's ﷺ job description is clear and limited: give good news to those who believe, and give warnings to those who don't. He doesn't plead, he doesn't beg, he doesn't become desperate. The messenger shouldn't be desperate because he doubts the message - rather, he might feel desperate out of genuine concern that people will end up in Hellfire if they don't listen.

Allah ﷻ addresses this concern directly: وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُ عَنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ - "You will not be asked about the people of Al-Jaheem." The word جَحِيم derives from the intense stare of a lion before it devours its prey - Hellfire stares down its victims before consuming them. Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ: You won't be questioned about those who end up there. Your job is only to deliver the message clearly.

translation: And never will the Jews or the Christians approve of you until you follow their religion. Say, "Indeed, the guidance of Allah is the [only] guidance." If you were to follow their desires after what has come to you of knowledge, you would have against Allah no protector or helper
commentary:
**وَلَن تَرْضَىٰ عَنكَ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَلَا ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَتَّبِعَ مِلَّتَهُمْ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۗ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۙ مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ**

Allah ﷻ now gives the Prophet ﷺ a crucial reality check about interfaith relations. وَلَن تَرْضَىٰ عَنكَ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَلَا ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَتَّبِعَ مِلَّتَهُمْ - "The Jews and Christians will never be pleased with you until you follow their way."

This ayah addresses an important reality for Muslims engaged in interfaith dialogue. It's valuable to share our faith with others and understand theirs. We should show the wonderful qualities of the Prophet ﷺ - his honesty, his character as a father, husband, leader, businessman. However, there's one quality that matters above all others: he is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ. That's the one thing he asked people to believe, and that's the one point of contention that will remain.

People will appreciate many things about the Prophet ﷺ, but they'll say, "He's a great guy, except he shouldn't have claimed to be a prophet." When we represent the Prophet ﷺ to non-Muslims, we must never sacrifice this fundamental truth. The modern discourse that suggests we should focus only on commonalities and avoid discussing prophethood itself contradicts the Prophet's ﷺ primary mission.

قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ - "Say: Indeed, the guidance of Allah - it is the guidance." The emphasis through repetition (guidance... it is THE guidance) makes it absolute. This isn't one path among many - it is THE path.

وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ - "And if you were to follow their desires after what came to you of knowledge." Their desires in this context mean compromising the message - being "a little bit more like us, just give in on some things." The word ٱلَّذِى (that which) emphasizes the clarity and prominence of the revelation that came.

مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ - "You will not find any protector from Allah, nor any helper." If even the Prophet ﷺ were to compromise on this fundamental truth after receiving such clear knowledge, there would be no protection for him. This establishes that theological clarity and firmness on core beliefs is non-negotiable, even while maintaining respectful relationships with people of other faiths.

translation: Those to whom We have given the Book recite it with its true recital. They [are the ones who] believe in it. And whoever disbelieves in it - it is they who are the losers
commentary:
**ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَتْلُونَهُۥ حَقَّ تِلَاوَتِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ**

This ayah distinguishes between two groups among the People of the Book. ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ - "Those to whom We gave the Book." Notice Allah ﷻ takes credit for giving them the Book by saying "We gave," which indicates He is pleased with this group.

يَتْلُونَهُۥ حَقَّ تِلَاوَتِهِۦٓ - "They read it and follow it as it deserves to be read and followed." The word تِلَاوَة encompasses both recitation and following. These people don't just read their scripture - they live by it properly. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ - "Those are the ones who believe in it."

This ayah teaches us something profound: if someone truly believes the Book is the most valuable thing in their life, they will give it their time. They will read it and follow it. When something has real value to you, it automatically gets your attention and time. If you have an exam on Chapter 5, you'll be spiritually connected to that textbook. When something is directly connected to your success, it naturally gets your focus.

If the Quran fails to get our time and attention, something hasn't been connected properly in our understanding. How can we claim to believe this is divine guidance, the most important thing for our success in both worlds, yet we don't make time to read it or follow it?

وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ - "And whoever disbelieves in it - those are the ultimate losers." This refers to those among the People of the Book who claim their community alone will enter Paradise. Allah ﷻ responds: those who reject this Book are the real losers.

translation: O Children of Israel, remember My favor which I have bestowed upon you and that I preferred you over the worlds
commentary:
**يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَنِّى فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ**

This is now the third time in Surah Al-Baqarah that Allah ﷻ addresses the Children of Israel with this same call. The first two times were invitations - fulfill your covenant, don't hide the truth, remember My favors. Now the door is beginning to close on them. The conversation is coming to its conclusion.

يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ - "O Children of Israel, remember My favor that I bestowed upon you." وَأَنِّى فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "And that I gave you preference over all other nations."

Notice the emphasis: أَنِّى (that I) appears twice. "I bestowed... I gave you preference." Allah ﷻ takes full credit for the favor they received. This is crucial - they didn't earn this preference on their own merit. They didn't deserve it inherently. Allah ﷻ chose to give it to them. Don't be deluded into thinking it was something special about you. I was the One who gave it to you.

translation: And fear a Day when no soul will suffice for another soul at all, and no compensation will be accepted from it, nor will any intercession benefit it, nor will they be aided
commentary:
**وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِى نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا تَنفَعُهَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ**

This ayah mirrors an earlier warning in the surah, but with significant changes in sequence and wording that carry profound meaning. وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا - "Protect yourselves from a Day." Earlier Allah ﷻ said "Protect yourselves from Me," but now He's established they have no fear of Him, so He simply says: get ready for that Day.

لَّا تَجْزِى نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا - "No soul will compensate for another soul anything at all." Earlier in the surah, intercession (شَفَاعَة) was mentioned first, then compensation (عَدْل). Here, compensation is mentioned first, then intercession. Why?

When you're in trouble, your first line of defense is having connections - someone who can intercede for you. When that fails, you try to pay your way out. That's the natural sequence mentioned earlier. But this time, the ayah begins with the worst situation - you're already trying to pay for yourself. If it's already at that desperate stage, there's no point mentioning intercession being of benefit, because you're past that point.

وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ - "No compensation will be accepted from it." وَلَا تَنفَعُهَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ - "Nor will intercession benefit it." Notice the verb changed from يُقْبَلُ (will be accepted) for compensation to تَنفَعُ (will benefit) for intercession. In the earlier ayah, the focus was on whether the intercessor would even be allowed to intercede. Here, even if there were an intercession, it wouldn't benefit.

وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ - "Nor will they be aided." The pronoun هُمْ (they) indicates that while they won't be aided, there will be others who will receive help. There will be others who will receive intercession. May Allah ﷻ make us worthy of the Prophet's ﷺ intercession.

The concept of شَفَاعَة (intercession) is significant here because if misunderstood, it leads to shirk. Most religions that practice shirk have corrupted this concept. The human being doesn't want to face Allah ﷻ directly on Judgment Day, so they want someone in between - idols, saints, or others who will "make their case" with Allah ﷻ. The Christians say, "We can't answer to God directly, so Jesus will intercede." Some Jews believed their status as the chosen people would automatically protect them.

This is why these discussions of intercession are strategically placed right before the mention of Ibrahim (AS), because all three communities - the Mushrikun of Makkah, the Jews, and the Christians - attribute themselves to the Abrahamic tradition. Yet if there's one prophet who taught uncompromisingly that no one stands between you and Allah ﷻ, it was Ibrahim (AS).

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when Abraham was tried by his Lord with commands and he fulfilled them. [Allah] said, "Indeed, I will make you a leader for the people." [Abraham] said, "And of my descendants?" [Allah] said, "My covenant does not include the wrongdoers
commentary:
**وَإِذِ ٱبْتَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ رَبُّهُۥ بِكَلِمَـٰتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّى جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا ۖ قَالَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى ۖ قَالَ لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ**

Ibrahim (AS) is strategically important in this surah because he represents the common link between all three audiences being addressed. Now Allah ﷻ recounts his story and his tests.

وَإِذِ ٱبْتَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ رَبُّهُۥ بِكَلِمَـٰتٍ - "And when his Master tested Ibrahim with certain words." The word كَلِمَـٰتٍ (words/commands) is grammatically feminine, and Allah ﷻ uses فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ (he completed them), where the pronoun هُنَّ is used for emphasis. This emphasizes the magnitude and weight of these commands - they were not light or easy instructions.

What were these tests? Jump into a blazing fire they've built for you. Take your wife and infant son and leave them in the middle of a barren desert with no food or water. We can barely handle a three-hour road trip with family, yet Ibrahim (AS) took them on an unimaginable journey to leave them in complete desolation. Imagine being five minutes late to pick up your children from the bus stop - those five minutes feel like torture. He left his wife and infant son in the desert and walked away.

Then came the ultimate test with his grown son. Fathers often place their hopes and dreams in their sons, wanting them to follow in their footsteps. After years of waiting, after finally having this son and building a relationship with him, the command came: take your boy and sacrifice him. And the remarkable quality of both father and son - both submitted completely.

These weren't easy tests. فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ - "He completed them perfectly." He submitted to every single one.

After completing these colossal tests, Allah ﷻ said: قَالَ إِنِّى جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا - "I am making you an imam for all people," a leader and role model for all of humanity. The word إِمَام means someone who stands ahead, someone people follow and look up to.

Ibrahim's (AS) immediate response reveals his character: قَالَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى - "He said: And what about my offspring?" The word ذُرِّيَّة doesn't just mean immediate children - it means all future generations, his children's children's children. When he heard the word "imam," an alarm went off in his heart. Being an imam means you're responsible for the people who follow you. If those people are corrupt, who will be questioned first? The imam will be.

He knows he'll be held accountable for his entire lineage on Judgment Day. He wants to ensure they're righteous. قَالَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى - "And from my offspring?" Make them leaders of righteousness as well.

Allah's ﷻ response was both a promise and a difficult truth: قَالَ لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - "My covenant does not extend to the wrongdoers." What a harsh reality! Allah ﷻ is telling Ibrahim (AS) that some of his descendants will be wrongdoers. The Mushrikun of Makkah were descendants of Ibrahim (AS), yet they violated the very house he built. Allah ﷻ doesn't guarantee all of his offspring will be righteous.

Despite this difficult answer, Ibrahim (AS) doesn't give up. That's where we learn perseverance in dua. He comes back with another request.

translation: And [mention] when We made the House a place of return for the people and [a place of] security. And take, [O believers], from the standing place of Abraham a place of prayer. And We charged Abraham and Ishmael, [saying], "Purify My House for those who perform Tawaf and those who are staying [there] for worship and those who bow and prostrate [in prayer]
commentary:
**وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا ٱلْبَيْتَ مَثَابَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَأَمْنًا وَٱتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن مَّقَامِ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ مُصَلًّى ۖ وَعَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ أَن طَهِّرَا بَيْتِىَ لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْعَـٰكِفِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ**

Before continuing with Ibrahim's (AS) dua, Allah ﷻ describes what He did with the House. وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا ٱلْبَيْتَ مَثَابَةً لِّلنَّاسِ - "And when We made the House a place of return for people," a place where people could come back over and over again. وَأَمْنًا - "And a place of peace and safety."

To this day, even the most disturbed people experience peace when they see the Kaaba. Unfortunately, we sometimes work to destroy that peace - people making phone calls, taking selfies, playing on devices, treating it like a tourist attraction rather than the sacred space it is. The state of Hajj reflects the state of the Ummah. If we fix how we approach Hajj, it's an indication that the Ummah is being fixed.

Hajj is supposed to be a simulation of Judgment Day - standing in simple burial clothes before Allah ﷻ in a massive crowd, focusing entirely on Him. Yet we've turned it into something else, constantly complaining about accommodations and comfort, when people used to undertake this journey under the harshest conditions.

وَٱتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن مَّقَامِ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ مُصَلًّى - "And take from the standing place of Ibrahim a place of prayer." وَعَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ - "And We made a covenant with Ibrahim and Ismail." Notice that Ismail (AS) is mentioned specifically. This is significant because the Jews refused to acknowledge his nobility and honor, even accusing him of being illegitimate. But Allah ﷻ mentions him in the same breath as Ibrahim (AS), commanding them together.

أَن طَهِّرَا بَيْتِىَ - "Purify My House." If Ismail (AS) were himself impure as they claimed, why would Allah ﷻ command him to purify His House?

لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْعَـٰكِفِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ - "For those who perform tawaf, and those who devote themselves in i'tikaf, and those who bow and prostrate." Notice the beautiful sequence: tawaf is the most exclusive worship - it can only be performed at the Kaaba. I'tikaf is less exclusive - it happens in mosques worldwide, but not as commonly as other worship. Then come ruku' and sujood, which occur in every salah performed by every Muslim multiple times daily. The sequence moves from the most exclusive act of worship to the most universal, and notice there are no conjunctions (و - "and") between tawaf and i'tikaf, because they're distinct acts. But ruku' and sujood are connected because they're parts of one act of worship - salah.

translation: And [mention] when Abraham said, "My Lord, make this a secure city and provide its people with fruits - whoever of them believes in Allah and the Last Day." [Allah] said. "And whoever disbelieves - I will grant him enjoyment for a little; then I will force him to the punishment of the Fire, and wretched is the destination
commentary:
**وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا بَلَدًا ءَامِنًا وَٱرْزُقْ أَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ مِنْهُم بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ قَالَ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُۥ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ**

Now we return to Ibrahim's (AS) dua. وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا بَلَدًا ءَامِنًا - "And when Ibrahim said: My Master, make this a secure city." He's building this House in the middle of a barren desert. There is no city there. He looks around at nothing and says, "Make this nothing into a city."

وَٱرْزُقْ أَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ - "And provide its people with fruits." Not only make it a city, but make sure it has economic prosperity. In his genius, Ibrahim (AS) asked for the two essential elements any society needs to survive: security and economic opportunity. Safety and prosperity must go hand in hand. If you have safety but no way to earn a living, society collapses. If you have prosperity but no security, society collapses. He understood this and asked for both.

But then he added a condition: مَنْ ءَامَنَ مِنْهُم بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ - "Whoever among them believes in Allah and the Last Day." Allah ﷻ had already told him some of his descendants would be wrongdoers. Ibrahim (AS) understood this point. Rather than have multiple generations of evildoers prospering, he asked that these blessings be limited to believers only.

But Allah ﷻ gave a harsh answer again: قَالَ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُۥ قَلِيلًا - "Even the one who disbelieves, I will let him enjoy a little." I'll give them provisions too, let them have their security and prosperity as well. ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلنَّارِ - "Then I will drag him to the punishment of the Fire." وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ - "What a horrible destination."

Despite this harsh response, Ibrahim (AS) still doesn't give up.

translation: And [mention] when Abraham was raising the foundations of the House and [with him] Ishmael, [saying], "Our Lord, accept [this] from us. Indeed You are the Hearing, the Knowing
commentary:
**وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ ٱلْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ**

وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ ٱلْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلُ - "And when Ibrahim was raising the foundations of the House, along with Ismail." Ismail (AS) is mentioned as helping his father - he's the assistant mentioned at the end.

رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّآ - "Our Master, accept from us." This is the first time in the conversation we see رَبَّنَا ("Our Master") instead of رَبِّ ("My Master"). Before this, it was always "Rabbi, Rabbi, Rabbi." Now it's "Rabbana" - "Our Master."

Who does "our" include? Ibrahim (AS) and Ismail (AS). Notice the strategy: You won't guarantee my future generations. At least guarantee me this boy right here. Give me this one. And the way he asks for it is remarkable - he's building the Kaaba, which he knows is Allah's ﷻ instruction. He knows this is an accepted deed. He knows Ismail (AS) has been commanded to help build it. So he makes this dua while performing an act he knows is pleasing to Allah ﷻ.

إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - "Indeed You, You are the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing." Notice the emphasis: إِنَّكَ (indeed You) and then أَنتَ (You). When you desperately want someone to listen, you say "Listen to me, listen to me, just listen to me." That's captured in this repetition. "You, You are the One who hears, who knows."

He's pleading: Ya Allah, You know he's a good boy. I just want You to accept from this one. The desperation is palpable in his words.

What makes this even more profound is understanding the timeline. Has Ishaq (AS) been born yet? No. This dua of Ibrahim (AS) includes a request for both him and Ismail (AS) to be accepted as Muslims - but Ishaq (AS) hasn't even been born yet. This shows Ibrahim's (AS) foresight and his desperate hope that Allah ﷻ will accept his righteous children.

translation: Our Lord, and make us Muslims [in submission] to You and from our descendants a Muslim nation [in submission] to You. And show us our rites and accept our repentance. Indeed, You are the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful
commentary:
**رَبَّنَا وَٱجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَآ أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ**

رَبَّنَا وَٱجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ - "Our Master, make both of us Muslims exclusively for You." Make us both in complete submission just to You, for no one else.

وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَآ أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ - "And from our offspring, one united group, one nation that is Muslim exclusively for You." He's refined his request. He's not asking that all his descendants be righteous - he understands Allah ﷻ has told him some will be wrongdoers. Now he's asking: Can I at least have one group, one unified nation from among my generations? Can You at least give me that, Ya Allah? Just one Muslim nation from my descendants?

That nation would be us. We're meant to be the answer to Ibrahim's (AS) prayer. What an incredible honor! This man is pleading with Allah ﷻ while building the House that billions face in prayer, and he's making dua for you and me.

وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا - "And show us our rituals." We're building this House, but how do we take care of it? How do we worship You here? We don't even know what to do. Show us.

وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَآ - "And accept our repentance." This is remarkable. What sin is he committing? He's building the Kaaba! If building a masjid brings tremendous reward, imagine the reward for building the Kaaba. If you get commission from every prayer made in a masjid you helped build, imagine the commission from every prayer ever made toward the Kaaba, from that day until the end of time.

Yet Ibrahim (AS) is making tawbah. Why? Because we learn that even when you do a good deed, it doesn't mean you've done it perfectly. Even if you prayed, it doesn't mean you prayed perfectly. Even if you made wudu, it doesn't mean your wudu was perfect. Even if you went to Hajj, it doesn't mean your Hajj was perfect. There are probably gaps and holes and imperfections throughout.

Ibrahim (AS) is saying: I know I did a terrible job. Please, with Your mercy, fill in all the holes and just accept it. If Ibrahim (AS) was this worried about making tawbah, how worried should we be?

We sometimes think, "I'm doing pretty good actually. I'm studying Arabic, listening to Quran commentary - I'm basically awesome." No. These prophets are showing us the attitude we should have before Allah ﷻ. What humility we should maintain. How constantly we should turn back to Him in repentance.

إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ - "Indeed You, You are the One who continually accepts repentance, the Especially Merciful." The word ٱلتَّوَّابُ means the One who accepts tawbah again and again and again. Don't be reluctant to make tawbah. Some people say, "I messed up, but I can't bring myself to make tawbah. It's too embarrassing. I know I'm not going to change anyway, so why bother?"

That's all from Shaytan. You've been deceived. You have to have no false dignity when you make tawbah. You have to believe that Allah ﷻ has the power to change you if you have sincerity. You have to believe that Allah ﷻ can make you better. That's what it means to turn to the One who is At-Tawwab Ar-Raheem - the One who continually accepts repentance, the Especially Merciful.

translation: Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
رَبَّنَا وَٱبْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

The supplication of Prophet Ibrahim (AS) continues here with the conjunction وَ (and), which carries meaningful implications. The presence of this "and" suggests that when Ibrahim (AS) made his previous request to Allah ﷻ, there was no counter response, no rejection. When Allah ﷻ does not counter a request from His beloved prophet, it indicates acceptance. So Ibrahim (AS), sensing this acceptance, naturally adds another request to his supplication.

The ayah begins: "Our Master, and raise among them a messenger from themselves." Notice the dual emphasis in the Arabic - رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ (a messenger from them) and فِيهِمْ (among them). These are two distinct aspects that serve different purposes. Being "from them" means sharing their lineage, their culture, their language. Being "among them" means remaining present with them, living in their midst. Someone could be from a people without being committed to staying with them, but Ibrahim (AS) asks for both qualities in this messenger.

This is precisely how the supplication was answered through Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He was raised among his people, which is why the Quran describes him as صَاحِبُكُم (your companion), the one who has been in your midst all along. Allah ﷻ says, "Your companion has not gone astray, nor has he erred" (53:2). The Prophet ﷺ lived among the Quraysh for forty years before prophethood, establishing trust and credibility. When he finally spoke with revelation, they were ready to listen because he was one of their own who had lived honorably among them.

Ibrahim (AS) then outlines four essential functions this messenger would perform:

**First: يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِكَ** - "He will recite upon them Your signs." The messenger would communicate Allah's ﷻ miraculous revelations directly to the people. The fact that he is from among them removes barriers to communication. When someone from outside addresses a group, there are immediate obstacles - suspicion, prejudice, lack of common ground. But when he is one of their own, there is already an opening, an ear willing to listen. The fact that he remains among them (فِيهِمْ) builds credibility over time, so that when he does recite the ayat, they are prepared and willing to receive them. For the Muslim Ummah, this was fulfilled through the Prophet's ﷺ recitation of the Quran.

**Second: وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ** - "And he will teach them the Book." The word الْكِتَـٰبَ (the Book) in Arabic also carries the meaning of law, of something written down and established. When something is law, it is etched in stone, permanent and binding. Even today we use this expression - "the judge threw the book at him" - meaning the judge applied every aspect of the law against someone. In the Quranic context, الْكِتَـٰبَ often refers to divine law and legislation, as in كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ (prescribed upon you), meaning the law mandated upon you. So this messenger would teach them the divine law and legislation.

**Third: وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ** - "And wisdom." Interestingly, wisdom is mentioned separately after knowledge, establishing a crucial distinction. We often think knowledge and wisdom are the same thing - that someone with extensive knowledge must be wise. But in Arabic, these are distinct concepts. Knowledge (عِلْم) is knowing what is right and wrong, understanding information. Hikma (wisdom) is not just knowing something beneficial, but actually acting upon that knowledge. Someone who knows fire burns but touches it anyway has knowledge without wisdom. Wisdom means you have matured in your knowledge and also matured in how you apply and commit to that knowledge. The messenger would therefore teach not just the laws, but also teach people how to make themselves apply those laws, how to become wise in implementing them.

**Fourth: وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ** - "And he will purify them." The word تَزْكِيَة refers to spiritual purification. There are different words in Arabic for purification - physical cleanliness uses different terms, while tazkiyah specifically addresses spiritual and moral purification of the heart and soul. The messenger would cleanse them spiritually, elevating their character and inner state.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ - "Indeed, You alone are the All-Powerful, the All-Wise." These two divine names beautifully correspond to what was just mentioned. الْعَزِيزُ (the Ultimate Authority) relates to الْكِتَـٰبَ (the law) - laws come from an authority, and Allah ﷻ is the ultimate source of all law. الْحَكِيمُ (the All-Wise) relates to الْحِكْمَةَ (wisdom) - all wisdom flows from the One who is perfectly wise.

translation: And who would be averse to the religion of Abraham except one who makes a fool of himself. And We had chosen him in this world, and indeed he, in the Hereafter, will be among the righteous
commentary:
وَمَن يَرْغَبُ عَن مِّلَّةِ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ إِلَّا مَن سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ وَلَقَدِ ٱصْطَفَيْنَـٰهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

The question posed here is profound: "And who would turn away from the millah of Ibrahim except one who makes a fool of himself?" The word يَرْغَبُ with the preposition عَن is significant. In Arabic, when you are attracted to something, you say يَرْغَبُ إِلَىٰ, but when you are repelled from something, turned off by it, the preposition changes to عَن. So this speaks of someone actively turning away from, being repelled by, the legacy of Ibrahim (AS).

Before proceeding, it's important to understand what millah means. The word مِلَّة is sometimes confused with قَوْم (people) or أُمَّة (nation), but there are distinctions. A millah is a nation unified by some kind of emotion or shared feeling - perhaps patriotism, perhaps love for their founder or their land, perhaps even unified by hatred of another group. The emotional bond is what defines a millah. An ummah, on the other hand, is a nation unified in the strongest possible way with a shared direction and mission, unified by a common cause. 

Here, millah implies that we should have an emotional attachment to Ibrahim (AS), an emotional affinity to being from the legacy of our father Ibrahim. This is about feeling that connection, that loyalty.

The rhetorical question answers itself: only someone who سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُۥ (makes a fool of himself) would turn away. The language is perfectly chosen. Ibrahim's (AS) legacy was one of exposing the foolishness of shirk through brilliant reasoning. His conversations with his people, as recorded in the Quran, display the height of intelligence and wisdom. So the only person who would not appreciate that legacy and not want to be part of it is someone engaged in self-foolery, someone making themselves foolish.

Some people misunderstand the story where Ibrahim (AS) looked at the moon, then the sun, then the stars, thinking he was discovering monotheism through observation. This is a shallow reading. When Ibrahim (AS) said "This is my lord" about the sun, did he not already know it would set? He was one of the most intelligent human beings who ever lived. He was demonstrating the absurdity of his people's beliefs, showing them the foolishness of worshiping created things that appear and disappear. Allah ﷻ defends His prophet here, saying وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (and he was never of the polytheists). He was never confused about tawheed; he was guiding others to recognize their confusion.

The ayah continues: وَلَقَدِ ٱصْطَفَيْنَـٰهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا - "And certainly We had already chosen him in this world." The word اصْطَفَيْنَـٰهُ implies special selection, that Allah ﷻ chose Ibrahim (AS) exclusively for a unique role. And the completion: وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ - "And indeed in the Hereafter he will be among the righteous."

This ending carries an invitation. It's not just describing a quality of Ibrahim (AS), but inviting us to join him. Those who truly have an affinity for Ibrahim (AS) should strive to be from among الصَّـٰلِحِينَ (the righteous), because that's who he'll be with in Paradise. Try to be counted among them.

translation: When his Lord said to him, "Submit", he said "I have submitted [in Islam] to the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥٓ أَسْلِمْ ۖ قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"When his Master said to him: 'Submit,' he said: 'I have submitted to the Lord of all the worlds.'" Earlier in Surah Al-Baqarah, when discussing the People of the Book, Allah ﷻ said, "And they say none will enter Paradise except Jews or Christians. Say: Bring your proof if you are truthful. Rather, whoever submits his face to Allah..." (2:111-112). That was speaking in general terms about submission to Allah ﷻ.

Now Allah ﷻ makes it specific: You claim loyalty to Ibrahim (AS), but he was from the legacy of someone who did Islam, who submitted himself completely. The conversation is direct and immediate. When his Master said أَسْلِمْ (submit), Ibrahim (AS) instantly responded أَسْلَمْتُ (I have submitted).

Notice the remarkable nature of this exchange. Allah ﷻ doesn't specify what Ibrahim (AS) should submit to - no particular command is mentioned. He simply says "Submit," and that is enough. Whatever comes from Allah ﷻ, Ibrahim (AS) is ready to submit to it without hesitation, without question, without needing to know the details first. This is the essence of Islam - complete submission.

Also notice there is no قَالَ (he said) before أَسْلَمْتُ. In Arabic narrative, the absence of this transitional phrase suggests immediate response with no time gap. Ibrahim (AS) doesn't pause, doesn't deliberate, doesn't need time to think it over. The moment he's certain the command is from Allah ﷻ, he obeys. This is the same attitude we see when he saw the dream about sacrificing his son - once he confirmed it was from Allah ﷻ, he proceeded without hesitation.

And Ibrahim (AS) doesn't just say "I submit" - he specifies لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (to the Lord of all the worlds). He submits to the Master of all peoples, all nations, all of creation. His submission isn't limited or conditional; it's absolute and universal.

translation: And Abraham instructed his sons [to do the same] and [so did] Jacob, [saying], "O my sons, indeed Allah has chosen for you this religion, so do not die except while you are Muslims
commentary:
وَوَصَّىٰ بِهَآ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ بَنِيهِ وَيَعْقُوبُ يَـٰبَنِىَّ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰ لَكُمُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ

Now we arrive at the heart of this passage. The Bani Israel refused to accept Islam, claiming loyalty to their ancestral religion. But Ibrahim (AS), the very ancestor they claim loyalty to, commanded his descendants to embrace Islam. If they truly had loyalty to their father, accepting this message would be the way to demonstrate it, not rejecting it.

The verse says وَوَصَّىٰ بِهَآ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ بَنِيهِ - "And Ibrahim instructed his sons with this," meaning with Islam, with submission to Allah ﷻ. But notice the ayah doesn't stop there. It immediately adds: وَيَعْقُوبُ (and so did Yaqub).

This is significant. The millah of Ibrahim (AS) includes both his sons - Ismail and Ishaq (AS). From Ishaq (AS) came Yaqub (AS), and from Yaqub came the Bani Israel. So Allah ﷻ could have mentioned Ishaq (AS) as the direct link, but He goes directly to the grandson, Yaqub (AS). Why? Because Yaqub (AS), whose other name is Israel, is the direct father of the Bani Israel. They have the most immediate connection to him. He is their father, even though Ibrahim (AS) is their grandfather.

Here's the beautiful point: both the grandfather Ibrahim (AS) and the grandson Yaqub (AS) gave the exact same instruction to their children. At the time of death, both patriarchs said identical words to their descendants. Imagine that continuity, that consistency in the family legacy. The grandfather trained his children in something so profound that when his grandson became a grandfather himself, he passed down the exact same teaching. This shows incredible spiritual training and inheritance.

What did they both say? يَـٰبَنِىَّ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰ لَكُمُ ٱلدِّينَ - "O my sons, indeed Allah has chosen for you this deen (religion/way of life)." The emphasis here is powerful: إِنَّ (indeed) stresses certainty, and it is Allah ﷻ who has chosen this path for them. It's not their choice to make between options; Allah ﷻ has already selected what is best for them.

Then comes the urgent command: فَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ - "So do not die except while you are Muslims." The phrasing is remarkable - "do not die except..." as if to say, don't let death even come near you unless you are in a state of complete submission to Allah ﷻ. Guard your Islam with your life. Make sure that whenever death arrives, it finds you as a Muslim.

The instruction to be Muslim was given by Ibrahim (AS) to his children and by Yaqub (AS) to his children. So when Allah ﷻ calls the Bani Israel to Islam through Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, He's actually calling them back to their original legacy, to what their own fathers commanded them. This is what makes their rejection so tragic - they're abandoning their own heritage.

translation: Or were you witnesses when death approached Jacob, when he said to his sons, "What will you worship after me?" They said, "We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham and Ishmael and Isaac - one God. And we are Muslims [in submission] to Him
commentary:
أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِذْ قَالَ لِبَنِيهِ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِى قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ إِلَـٰهَكَ وَإِلَـٰهَ ءَابَآئِكَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ

The ayah asks: "Or were you witnesses when death approached Yaqub?" This is Allah ﷻ presenting historical testimony. Let's go even further into the legacy, to the actual moment when Yaqub (AS) faced death.

The imagery is powerful: إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ ٱلْمَوْتُ - "when death presented itself to Yaqub." Death is portrayed as a visitor, a guest who has arrived at the door. When a guest knocks unexpectedly, what do you do? You quickly straighten up the house, put things away, make immediate preparations before opening the door.
