When the guest of death knocks at Yaqub's (AS) door, what are his immediate preparations? Not about property, not about wealth, not about business affairs. His only concern: "O my sons, what will you worship after I'm gone?"

The question itself is telling: مَا تَعْبُدُونَ (what will you worship) - he says "what" not "who." Using "what" can indicate expecting a potentially problematic answer, or it can be completely open-ended. He's making no assumptions; he wants to hear it from them directly, from the beginning.

And these are the sons of Yaqub (AS) - you know their story. These are the same brothers from the story of Yusuf (AS), the ones who threw their brother into the well, who lied to their father, who caused him years of grief. But they had made tawbah (repentance). By this point, they're living in Egypt with Yusuf (AS), and they've become righteous, committed Muslims.

Yet even knowing all this, at his deathbed, Yaqub (AS) still needs reassurance. He's still concerned. Why? Because he understands a profound truth: guidance is not guaranteed by parentage. Guidance is guaranteed only by Allah ﷻ, and every individual must make their own choices. Even righteous children of prophets must continually choose faith. So at his dying breath, when people often talk about worldly affairs until their last moment, what is Yaqub (AS) discussing? "I'm about to be gone. My problems will be over. But you - what will you do after I'm gone? Just take care of this one thing: what will you worship?"

Their answer is absolutely beautiful: قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ إِلَـٰهَكَ - "They said: We will worship your Ilah." The word إِلَـٰه is difficult to translate with a single English word - it means the Entity worthy of worship and complete obedience simultaneously. So they say: "We will worship and enslave ourselves to your Ilah."

Then they continue: وَإِلَـٰهَ ءَابَآئِكَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ - "And the Ilah of your fathers: Ibrahim, Ismail, and Ishaq." The order here is critical and deliberate. They mention Ibrahim (AS) first - of course, he's the patriarch. But then notice: they mention Ismail (AS) before Ishaq (AS). This is chronological and factual - Ismail (AS) was the older brother. Yet this also carries deep significance for the Bani Israel.

These are the sons of Yaqub (AS), who came through the line of Ishaq (AS). Naturally, Ishaq (AS) is their direct grandfather. But they honor Ismail (AS) first, even though he's their grand-uncle, not their direct ancestor. They legitimize Ismail (AS). They recognize his status and honor it.

This becomes important because later generations among the Bani Israel corrupted this view. They began to claim that Ismail (AS) was somehow illegitimate, that he wasn't really the older son, that he didn't deserve equal honor. But Allah ﷻ is exposing the truth: their own ancestors, the actual sons of Yaqub (AS), testified at the deathbed of their father that they honored Ismail (AS), mentioning his name before even their own grandfather Ishaq (AS).

They conclude: إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا - "One single Ilah" - there is no difference, no division. Whether we're speaking of the God of Ibrahim (AS), or Ismail (AS), or Ishaq (AS), it's the same One God. وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ - "And we are exclusively Muslims to Him."

This statement is profound. The sons of Yaqub (AS), the original Bani Israel, declared "We are Muslims." What an incredible piece of history, what an excerpt from the true heritage of the Israelites.

translation: That was a nation which has passed on. It will have [the consequence of] what it earned, and you will have what you have earned. And you will not be asked about what they used to do
commentary:
تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ ۖ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

"That was a nation that has passed away. For them is what they earned, and for you is what you earn. And you will not be asked about what they used to do."

This ayah appears twice in this passage, and its message is critical. Don't just sit back and reminisce about how great your fathers were, how awesome they were, while you yourself do nothing. You will not be interrogated about them on the Day of Judgment. 

Learning about the history of the prophets and their righteous followers is absolutely critical, but it becomes pointless if you're learning it merely for information, merely to know stories. Why should we learn that history? To change ourselves. That's the only valid reason. Allah ﷻ makes that history known to us so that we can use it to commit ourselves to guidance, to transform our own lives.

This is why Allah ﷻ doesn't tell us every detail of their lives - not their full names, not what addresses they lived at, not every person they married, not every mundane detail. Even the story of Yusuf (AS), which is the most detailed story narrative in the Quran, think about how many details are actually given. We don't know the minister's name. We don't know his wife's name. We don't know the names of Yusuf's (AS) brothers (except Binyamin by implication). We don't even know the prisoners' names. The mother of Yusuf (AS) isn't mentioned. 

Only information that will make you a better human being is provided from those stories. That's all Allah ﷻ wants from us. Don't get lost in the details and miss the point. You won't be asked about the finer points of their biographies. What's implied? You will be asked what you did. Did you read the story of Ibrahim (AS)? Did you learn the story of Yusuf (AS)? Did you enjoy listening to it? Was the storyteller entertaining? That's wonderful, but what did you do after that? What changed in your life? That's the question that matters.

translation: They say, "Be Jews or Christians [so] you will be guided." Say, "Rather, [we follow] the religion of Abraham, inclining toward truth, and he was not of the polytheists
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ تَهْتَدُوا۟ ۗ قُلْ بَلْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

Now we return to the present argument. "And they said: Be Jews or Christians, and you will be guided." The claim from the People of the Book is that their path is the path to guidance. 

Allah ﷻ instructs His Messenger ﷺ: قُلْ (Say) - respond to them: بَلْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ حَنِيفًا - "Rather, the millah of Ibrahim, purely devoted." Don't tell us to become Jews or Christians. Instead, let's talk about the legacy of Ibrahim (AS), who was حَنِيفًا - solely and purely devoted to Allah ﷻ.

The word حَنِيف is powerful. It means someone who is solely dedicated, undistracted, focused only on what is before them: Allah ﷻ alone. No divisions, no distractions, complete single-minded devotion to the One God. This word could grammatically refer to either Ibrahim (AS) or to the millah itself, or even to the pronoun "be" - in other words, "be like the millah of Ibrahim, being purely devoted," or "the millah of Ibrahim, which is purely devoted," or "Ibrahim who was purely devoted." The flexibility of Arabic grammar here encompasses all these meanings.

The ayah concludes: وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ - "And he was never from among the polytheists." This emphatic defense comes because some people, reading the story of Ibrahim (AS) superficially, think that when he looked at the sun and said "this is my lord," he was actually discovering monotheism through trial and error. This is an absurd misreading.

When Ibrahim (AS) observed the sun and said "this is my lord" to his people, did he not already know the sun would set? Of course he did. This was one of the most intelligent human beings who ever lived. He wasn't making a genuine discovery; he was using a teaching method, demonstrating to his people the sheer foolishness of their belief system. When the moon changed phases and disappeared, he wasn't shocked that morning happened. He was showing his people: "Look, if this is your god, what happens when it disappears? That makes no sense, does it?"

Allah ﷻ comes to the defense of His prophet, stating clearly: he was never from among the mushrikeen, not even for a moment, not in any way whatsoever. He was simply guiding people to a logical conclusion, helping them see their error.

translation: Say, [O believers], "We have believed in Allah and what has been revealed to us and what has been revealed to Abraham and Ishmael and Isaac and Jacob and the Descendants and what was given to Moses and Jesus and what was given to the prophets from their Lord. We make no distinction between any of them, and we are Muslims [in submission] to Him
commentary:
قُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ

Notice the command here: قُولُوٓا۟ (All of you, say). This is addressed to the Muslim Ummah collectively. We see the word قُل (say) directed at the Prophet ﷺ individually in the previous ayah, but now it's قُولُوٓا۟ - plural. The entire Ummah in Madinah was instructed to say this collectively to the Jewish community.

What are we to say? ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ - "We have believed in Allah," وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا - "and in what has been sent down to us" - meaning the Quran. وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ - "and what was sent down to Ibrahim," وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ - "and Ismail and Ishaq and Yaqub," وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ - "and the Asbat" (the descendants, the future generations of prophets from their lineage).

Ibrahim (AS) is the common link between Muslims and the People of the Book. Then the two lines of ancestry are mentioned: Ismail (AS) and Ishaq (AS), and from Ishaq (AS) comes Yaqub (AS), and then the Asbat who followed. But then the ayah mentions وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ - "and what was given to Musa," وَعِيسَىٰ - "and Isa."

Why mention Musa and Isa (AS) separately after already covering the lineage? Because while Ibrahim (AS) is the common ancestral link, the specific revealed law (Shariah) for the Bani Israel began with Musa (AS) and was completed with Isa (AS). All the prophets who came to the Bani Israel between these two are encompassed. So by mentioning the beginning and end of their prophetic legacy, Allah ﷻ includes all of them.

Then the statement becomes even more comprehensive: وَمَآ أُوتِىَ ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ - "and whatever was given to the prophets from their Lord" - meaning all prophets, wherever they were sent, whatever they were given.

Then comes our declaration: لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ - "We do not make any distinction between any of them." The word تَفْرِيق here is crucial to understand. Later in this passage, we'll encounter another word: تَفْضِيل. These are different concepts.

Tafdheel (giving preference) means recognizing that Allah ﷻ gave certain prophets special favors and gifts that He didn't give others. Allah ﷻ Himself says this in the Quran: "These are the messengers; We favored some over others" (2:253). Some were given miracles others weren't given, some were given books others weren't, some were given special ranks. Our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was given the highest rank among all prophets. This recognition of Allah's ﷻ distribution of special gifts is tafdheel, and it's valid and true.

But tafriq (making distinction) is something else entirely. Tafriq means we believe some of them are prophets and some aren't, that we accept some as legitimate messengers and reject others. That's what we don't do. We don't say, "We believe in this prophet but not that one." We accept all of them as true messengers from Allah ﷻ without making any such distinctions.

The ayah ends: وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ - "And we are Muslims to Him alone." Notice what we're saying now: نَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (we are Muslims to Him). Do you remember who else in this very conversation said exactly these words? The sons of Yaqub (AS), the original sons of Israel, said at their father's deathbed: وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ.

Centuries have passed. All that history has unfolded. And now the followers of Muhammad ﷺ are saying exactly what the original sons of Israel said. We are the true inheritors of that legacy. We are saying what they said: "We are Muslims to Him alone."

translation: So if they believe in the same as you believe in, then they have been [rightly] guided; but if they turn away, they are only in dissension, and Allah will be sufficient for you against them. And He is the Hearing, the Knowing
commentary:
فَإِنْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ مَآ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ ۖ وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا هُمْ فِى شِقَاقٍ ۖ فَسَيَكْفِيكَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

"Then if they believe in the like of what you have believed in, then they have been rightly guided." They might say, "Well, we believe too. We have our own way though, our own version." But Allah ﷻ clarifies: بِمِثْلِ مَآ ءَامَنتُم - until they believe in exactly the like of what you believe in, precisely the same belief. If they do that, فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ - then they have indeed committed themselves to true guidance.

This word اهْتَدَوا۟ is used specifically because earlier they said كُونُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ تَهْتَدُوا۟ - "Be Jews or Christians and you will be guided." Allah ﷻ responds: No, if they believe exactly as you believe, then تَهْتَدَوا۟ - then they will be committed to guidance.

وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ - "And if they turn away," refusing to accept the truth, فَإِنَّمَا هُمْ فِى شِقَاقٍ - "then they are only in schism." The word شِقَاق means division, friction, being in difficulty and conflict. They're caught in this state of opposition.

فَسَيَكْفِيكَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ - "Then Allah will be sufficient for you against them." Notice the remarkable Arabic here: there are two attached pronouns in this verb, which is rare. Usually you see unattached pronouns with verbs, but here both are attached: سَيَكْفِيكَهُمُ - "He will suffice you against them." The compression of language intensifies the meaning - Allah ﷻ will be enough for you; you don't need to worry about their plots or opposition.

وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - "And He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing." He hears everything they say in front of you and everything they say behind your back. He knows everything they harbor inside themselves, all their real intentions. السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ together mean: nothing escapes His awareness, neither their speech nor their hidden thoughts.

translation: [And say, "Ours is] the religion of Allah. And who is better than Allah in [ordaining] religion? And we are worshippers of Him
commentary:
صِبْغَةَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ صِبْغَةً ۖ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ عَـٰبِدُونَ

صِبْغَةَ ٱللَّهِ - literally "the color of Allah" - is a powerful figure of speech. Notice that صِبْغَةَ ends with a fathah (صِبْغَةَ not صِبْغَةُ or صِبْغَةٍ). This grammatical form (mansub) implies there's a verb before it that's been omitted. The implied statement is: "We have adopted the color of Allah" - اصْطَبَغْنَا صِبْغَةَ ٱللَّهِ.

What does this metaphor mean? Imagine someone who has been completely drenched in paint - they've had a bucket of paint poured over them. When you see this person walking by, what's the first thing you notice? Their color. "Look, there's the blue guy. That orange person over there." The color is the most immediately obvious thing about them.

The statement صِبْغَةَ ٱللَّهِ means: We have drenched ourselves in tawheed, in belief in Allah ﷻ, in submission to Him. We've immersed ourselves so completely in Islam that when somebody sees us, the first thing they notice is: these are people who are وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ - completely submitted to Allah ﷻ. This is our identifying mark, our most visible characteristic. This is the "color" that identifies us as people belonging to Allah ﷻ.

Then the rhetorical question: وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ صِبْغَةً - "And who is better than Allah in coloring?" What better identity could we possibly have than being identified by our submission to Allah ﷻ? What better "color" could distinguish us?

وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ عَـٰبِدُونَ - "And we are worshipers of Him." After taking on this color, this identity, our worship helps us reinforce it, strengthen it, deepen it. We're exclusively engaged in worship to Him, and this worship maintains and intensifies our "drenching" in His obedience.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "Do you argue with us about Allah while He is our Lord and your Lord? For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds. And we are sincere [in deed and intention] to Him
commentary:
قُلْ أَتُحَآجُّونَنَا فِى ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ وَلَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُخْلِصُونَ

The Prophet ﷺ is now told: قُلْ (Say) - respond to them: أَتُحَآجُّونَنَا فِى ٱللَّهِ - "Are you arguing with us about Allah?" The word فِى here means "about" or "concerning." But notice the question: how can you argue about Allah ﷻ in this manner?

وَهُوَ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ - "When He is our Master and your Master?" This is key. You're not just talking about some abstract philosophical entity. You're talking about your Master, the One who has authority over you. Watch how you speak about Him.

I often give this example: imagine you're sitting in an office cafeteria with coworkers at lunch. One employee starts making fun of the boss - "The manager is so incompetent, look how he dresses, he doesn't know what he's doing" - just running his mouth with insults. He's very confident the manager isn't listening. But if he's doing this and doesn't realize the manager is standing right behind him, what happens? Or even if the manager isn't there, but another employee jokingly says "Hey, how's it going, Bob?" (the manager's name), what does the person talking trash do? "Oh God, is he really here? Thank God you were joking!" 

You see, you watch how you talk about an authority if you have any awareness that the authority is listening, that they're aware of what you're saying. Here Allah ﷻ is saying: أَتُحَآجُّونَنَا فِى ٱللَّهِ - you're debating about Allah ﷻ? He is your Master. He has power over you. How dare you be so casual in how you discuss Him?

وَلَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ - "And for us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds." This isn't saying "You go your way, we'll go ours, and we're all fine." Rather, it's saying: We have deeds that speak for ourselves. The only assets you truly have are your deeds. We've shown the صِبْغَةَ ٱللَّهِ - we've adopted the color of Allah ﷻ, we've embraced tawheed entirely in our lives. And you're arguing with us about Allah ﷻ? Look at what you've done. Look at your track record that Allah ﷻ has exposed in these ayahs. Now you want to argue about how loyal you are to Him?

وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُخْلِصُونَ - "And we are the ones who are sincere to Him exclusively." Mentioning sincerity and intention here is critical because in debate, intentions become everything. What happens in argument? Sometimes your intention shifts from seeking truth to just wanting to win. It's no longer about the other person benefiting, no longer an act of worship - it becomes about projecting your ego, demonstrating your superiority. That's why mention of being مُخْلِصُونَ (sincere, making intention pure for Allah ﷻ alone) is essential when debate is mentioned.

translation: Or do you say that Abraham and Ishmael and Isaac and Jacob and the Descendants were Jews or Christians? Say, "Are you more knowing or is Allah?" And who is more unjust than one who conceals a testimony he has from Allah? And Allah is not unaware of what you do
commentary:
أَمْ تَقُولُونَ إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطَ كَانُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۗ قُلْ ءَأَنتُمْ أَعْلَمُ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَتَمَ شَهَـٰدَةً عِندَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

أَمْ تَقُولُونَ - "Or do you say" - are you actually claiming - إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطَ كَانُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ - "that Ibrahim, Ismail, Ishaq, Yaqub and the Asbat were Jews or Christians?"

This is absurd on its face. These prophets lived centuries before Judaism as a named religion and millennia before Christianity. How can you claim they were Jews or Christians when those religious labels didn't even exist in their time? Ismail (AS) lived long before Judaism developed as a distinct community. How could he possibly have been Jewish?

The Catholics developed alternative theologies to deal with these contradictions. For instance, they said you can't go to heaven except through Jesus (AS). But then what about all the righteous people praised in their own Bible - Abraham, Noah, Isaac and others? Are they in hell? To solve this, they developed the concept of purgatory - an "elevator stuck between floors" where these righteous people wait until Judgment Day when Jesus (AS) comes and releases them. They had to create such theories to justify their corrupted theology.

قُلْ (Say to them): ءَأَنتُمْ أَعْلَمُ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ - "Are you more knowing, or is Allah?" Who knows history better - you with your corrupted accounts, or Allah ﷻ who witnessed it all?

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَتَمَ شَهَـٰدَةً عِندَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ - "And who is more unjust than one who conceals a testimony he has from Allah?" They knew about Makkah. The Jews of Madinah knew about Makkah as the site Ibrahim (AS) built the House. But they changed it in their books, calling it "Sakka" instead of "Makkah." Where is Sakka? Nobody knows, but according to their altered version, Ibrahim built a house in "Sakka." 

They knew it was Ismail (AS) who was to be sacrificed, but they changed it to Ishaq (AS). Why? Because if they legitimized Ismail (AS), then the Arabs - his descendants - wouldn't be an inferior people anymore. They would be equals, family. That couldn't be allowed. So they changed the story, just a little adjustment here and there to serve their prejudices.

The great historian Shah Waliullah Dehlawi wrote a letter to Christians about this very issue - who was the son to be sacrificed? He pointed out that the pagan Arabs, who were not people of scripture and had no influence from Christianity or Judaism, had been sacrificing animals at the Kaaba for centuries. In their ancient poetry and centuries-old documents, if you ask them why they perform this sacrifice, they'll say: "Our father Ismail taught us this." Even they, without any book, preserved the tradition. Where did they get it from? This is Abrahamic tradition passed down. Why would they celebrate the sacrifice of Ishaq (AS)? He's not even their ancestor. The evidence is clear.

وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ - "And Allah is not at all unaware of what you do." Whatever they're trying to do, whatever manipulation of scripture, whatever concealment of truth - Allah ﷻ is fully aware of all of it.

translation: That is a nation which has passed on. It will have [the consequence of] what it earned, and you will have what you have earned. And you will not be asked about what they used to do
commentary:
تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ ۖ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah appears again, word for word, repeating what was said in ayah 134. "That was a nation that has passed away. For them is what they earned, and for you is what you earn. And you will not be asked about what they used to do."

The repetition is significant. This concludes the address Allah ﷻ has had with the Bani Israel. Let's track the progression: First, Allah ﷻ was speaking to them directly - يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ (O Children of Israel). He called them by name and addressed them face to face. Then, after listing their crimes and their rejection, He stopped talking to them directly. Instead, He told the Prophet ﷺ and the Muslims: "You tell them this." Everything became third person. This shift demonstrates Allah's ﷻ displeasure - He's no longer willing to address them directly.

Now, with this repeated ayah, that conversation is truly concluded. Allah ﷻ has made the case about the true legacy of Ibrahim (AS), about how Muslims should be loyal to that legacy, and how the Bani Israel have abandoned it despite their claims. 

This conclusion prepares us for a major shift. We've discussed Ibrahim (AS) and his legacy. We've established the legitimacy of the Muslim claim to that heritage. Now we're ready for the next major event in Islamic history: the change of the Qibla. This will be the third major shift in the surah's narrative flow.

translation: The foolish among the people will say, "What has turned them away from their qiblah, which they used to face?" Say, "To Allah belongs the east and the west. He guides whom He wills to a straight path
commentary:
۞ سَيَقُولُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَا وَلَّىٰهُمْ عَن قِبْلَتِهِمُ ٱلَّتِى كَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا ۚ قُل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

Until this revelation came, the Prophet ﷺ used to pray in the same direction as the Jews - toward Al-Masjid Al-Aqsa in Jerusalem. He also fasted on the same days they did. Deep down, some knowledgeable Jews knew this was indeed the final prophet, but as long as he was praying toward their holy site and following laws similar to theirs, they felt they could deal with him on their terms. There was no state of emergency, no urgency to submit.

Then the command came: the Qibla must change. Now Muslims must pray toward the Sacred Mosque in Makkah. Some Muslims were already in the middle of prayer when a Companion ran by announcing the command. Right there, mid-prayer, while in ruku, they turned direction. This was the attitude of the Sahaba (RA) - immediate, unquestioning obedience.

Now think about this: if the Jews truly believed Muslims were just a deviant cult with no connection to real revelation, they shouldn't care which direction Muslims pray. If you're a different religion entirely, what does it matter to me whether you face east, west, north or south? Christians aren't offended when Muslims face the Qibla because it's not their religion - they have no stake in it.

But if you know deep inside that Islam is true, and part of what gave you comfort was that Muslims were still praying toward the house that honored your people in Jerusalem - Al-Masjid Al-Aqsa - that was a thread of connection. Now that thread is cut. When Muslims in Madinah pray toward the Kaaba, are they facing Al-Aqsa anymore? No - their backs are to it.

The Jews were offended. And the fact that they were offended is itself proof that they believed the Messenger ﷺ to be true. If they truly thought Islam was just a cult, a false religion, they wouldn't care about the direction. Their anger exposed what they knew and were hiding inside.

When the Qibla changed, it was deeply symbolic. The intelligent people understood: it's as if the capital of Islam just changed. The center of attention shifted. The favor of Allah ﷻ has moved. The house that Ibrahim (AS) built with the help of which son? Ismail (AS). That's now the center. This isn't a Bani Israel contribution anymore - this is the contribution of Ismail (AS). Their "chosen" status, their centrality, is being transferred.

Allah ﷻ says: سَيَقُولُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ - "The foolish among the people will soon say..." Why does He call them foolish when they were so clever at hiding their doubts until now? Because they're about to expose what they were smart enough to conceal. Their reaction will reveal their true knowledge and their true rejection.

مَا وَلَّىٰهُمْ عَن قِبْلَتِهِمُ ٱلَّتِى كَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا - "What has turned them away from their Qibla which they used to face?" Notice: قِبْلَتِهِمُ - "their Qibla." They're claiming ownership. "It's supposed to be our direction. What do you mean you're not praying that way anymore? That's your Qibla!" 

Qibla just means direction, but they're making it possessive, making it about religious authority and legitimacy.

Allah ﷻ instructs: قُل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ - "Say: To Allah belongs the East and the West." Didn't Allah ﷻ already hint at this earlier in the surah? "Wherever you turn, there is the Face of Allah" (2:115). That was preparing us, dropping a hint that something significant was coming regarding direction.

And notice: He doesn't address them directly. He doesn't say يَا أَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ... or يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ. He no longer speaks to them face to face. Instead: قُل (You, Prophet, tell them) - You go tell them that to Allah ﷻ belongs the East and the West. All directions are His.

يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ - "He guides whom He wills to a straight path." The straight path here works on both figurative and literal levels. Figuratively, it's the path of Islam, the path of guidance. But literally, there's also a hint about physical direction - Allah ﷻ will guide the Muslim Ummah back to Makkah on the straight path, so they can eventually conquer it and return to their spiritual home.

translation: And thus we have made you a just community that you will be witnesses over the people and the Messenger will be a witness over you. And We did not make the qiblah which you used to face except that We might make evident who would follow the Messenger from who would turn back on his heels. And indeed, it is difficult except for those whom Allah has guided. And never would Allah have caused you to lose your faith. Indeed Allah is, to the people, Kind and Merciful
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا لِّتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا ۗ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلْقِبْلَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنتَ عَلَيْهَآ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يَتَّبِعُ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِمَّن يَنقَلِبُ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ ۚ وَإِن كَانَتْ لَكَبِيرَةً إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَـٰنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا - "And thus We have made you a middle nation." The word وَسَطًا carries layers of meaning. Most obviously, it means "middle" or "central." But before exploring that, notice something remarkable about the Quran's structure: Surah Al-Baqarah was revealed over 12 years in Makkah and Madinah. The Sahaba (RA) didn't have verse numbers - they knew ayahs by their opening words or distinctive phrases. Surah Al-Baqarah has 286 ayahs total. And right in the middle - ayah number 143, the exact mathematical center after 12 years of gradual revelation - Allah ﷻ mentions "middle nation."

Think about this. I've been speaking for a couple of hours now. Do I remember what the middle sentence of my speech was? Does anyone? If I spoke to you over the course of years, would you remember the middle sentence of our entire conversation? How is such precision even possible? Yet Allah ﷻ places this phrase about being a middle nation precisely at the middle point of the surah. SubhanAllah.

Now, what does أُمَّةً وَسَطًا mean? وَسَط means both "middle" and "balanced." The nation mentioned before - Bani Israel - had knowledge. Allah ﷻ favored them with tremendous knowledge, with prophets, with scripture. But what happened? That knowledge didn't balance with action. Their hearts became hard. They knew but didn't practice.

We spent time in Surah Al-Fatihah discussing balance - the entire surah is about balance. Allah ﷻ made you the balanced nation: balanced between knowledge and action, between understanding the faith and practicing it, between علم (knowledge) and حكمة (wisdom). Remember earlier in this very passage? يُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ - knowledge and wisdom together.

Balance between concerns of the mind and concerns of the heart. The Christians became almost entirely a people of spirituality, of emotional connection to faith, while abandoning intellectual rigor. The Jews became almost entirely intellectual, developing intricate legal systems while their hearts became hard - so hard that Allah ﷻ describes their hearts in the Quran as hardened. You, Muslim Ummah, must keep your minds sharp and open AND keep your hearts soft. You walk the middle path between these extremes.

صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ - the path of those You have favored - غَيْرِ ٱلْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ - not of those who earned anger nor of those who went astray. Right in the middle. That's أُمَّةً وَسَطًا.

لِّتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ - "So that you may be witnesses over humanity." Who was witness over humanity before? Which nation? Bani Israel. "We favored you over all the nations" - so the world could see what a nation looks like when they truly submit to Allah ﷻ, when they properly utilize the blessings Allah ﷻ gives them, when they establish justice and peace and beauty in life. That's what you were supposed to be, Bani Israel. You failed. 

Now it's your turn, Muslims. Congratulations - you're the middle nation, witnesses over humanity. This is a tremendous honor. But it comes with serious, weighty responsibility.

وَيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا - "And so the Messenger will be a witness over you." The Messenger ﷺ has a monumental task - he has 23 years to accomplish the most important mission in human history: revealing Allah's ﷻ final word to humanity. Allah ﷻ says إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا - "We will send down to you a weighty word" (73:5). This is not easy. He must deliver something that will guide humanity's future, and he's already 40 years old when it begins. Most of his life is behind him. This immense mission rests on his shoulders.

And when he departs from this world, his final address to the Ummah includes: "Let those who are present convey to those who are absent." The responsibility passes to us. How do you think you and I became Muslims? All of us are descendants of converts. Our ancestors somewhere back were pagans, Hindus, Buddhists, Zoroastrians, and Islam reached them. Someone became a witness to them. One person in my ancestry, maybe 1,000 years ago, maybe just a teenager, disobeyed his pagan parents and accepted Islam. And I'm taking Shahada today because of that one decision way back then, because somebody was a witness to humanity for him.

That's us. We're one-fifth of the world's population now. We're supposed to be witnesses over humanity. But we have to ask ourselves: what kind of witness are we? The proof of this deen was supposed to be how beautifully Muslims live their lives, how magnificent Muslim society is. What Bani Israel failed to do was given to us as our task. Allah ﷻ charged us with this, and He said we'd do it before all of humanity - so humanity sees they're in darkness and we're the light. We're what light looks like.

And if we don't fulfill this? Then on the Day of Judgment, who will be the prosecuting witness against us? وَيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا - the Messenger will be a witness against you. The ayah says عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا - not لَكُمْ (for you) but عَلَيْكُمْ (against you, over you). If it said لَكُمْ, it would mean the Messenger ﷺ testifies in your favor. But عَلَيْكُمْ means he must testify concerning what you did, and if we failed, that testimony won't be in our favor.

That Messenger ﷺ - Allah ﷻ changed the entire direction of prayer just because He saw the Prophet ﷺ looking toward the sky. He didn't even have to ask out loud. Just the turning of his face toward the sky was enough for Allah ﷻ to change divine legislation. The Quran says this explicitly - we'll read it in the next ayah. When you make that Messenger angry on Judgment Day, where do you stand? Where do I stand?

The Prophet ﷺ loved his Ummah so much. There are very few places in the Quran where Allah ﷻ mentions he'll have to testify against us - this is one of them. There's another in Surah An-Nisa that affected the Prophet ﷺ deeply. He once asked Ibn Mas'ud (RA), "Recite Quran for me. I love listening to it from others." Ibn Mas'ud (RA) began reciting Surah An-Nisa. When he reached the ayah, "How will it be when We bring a witness from every nation and bring you as a witness over these people?" - فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَـٰؤُلَآءِ شَهِيدًا - the Prophet ﷺ said, "Stop! I can't listen anymore." His eyes were filled with tears. The thought of having to testify concerning his Ummah was overwhelming for him.

When we talk about the Muslim Ummah being in crisis, there is no bigger crisis than the possibility of standing on the wrong side of the Prophet's ﷺ testimony. That's the ultimate crisis.

translation: We have certainly seen the turning of your face, [O Muhammad], toward the heaven, and We will surely turn you to a qiblah with which you will be pleased. So turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram. And wherever you [believers] are, turn your faces toward it [in prayer]. Indeed, those who have been given the Scripture well know that it is the truth from their Lord. And Allah is not unaware of what they do

And if you brought to those who were given the Scripture every sign, they would not follow your qiblah. Nor will you be a follower of their qiblah. Nor would they be followers of one another's qiblah. So if you were to follow their desires after what has come to you of knowledge, indeed, you would then be among the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَلَئِنْ أَتَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِكُلِّ ءَايَةٍ مَّا تَبِعُوا۟ قِبْلَتَكَ ۚ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَتَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا بَعْضُهُم بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَةَ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۙ إِنَّكَ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly: "Even if you were to bring to the people of the book all kinds of miraculous proofs, they would never, ever be following your Qibla." The repetition and emphasis in the Arabic makes clear the finality of their stance. They have made their decision, and no amount of evidence will change it.

The ayah continues: "And you are not going to be following their Qibla at all ever again." This establishes a complete separation. The direction has been changed, and there is no going back. "And no group will be following the Qibla of another." From this point forward, all communities will be distinct and separate in their direction of prayer.

Then comes a startling warning: "And even if you were to follow their desires, after knowledge has come to you, you, in that case, would be from the wrongdoers." This is the first major instance in the Quran where the Prophet himself ﷺ is being explicitly warned in such strong terms. Previous warnings existed, but this one is remarkably direct.

This divine reprimand of the Messenger ﷺ serves as one of the proofs of the Quran being revelation. If these were the Prophet's own words ﷺ, why would he reprimand himself? It makes no logical sense. Rather, Allah ﷻ establishes His authority over His Messenger ﷺ. From this we learn that if Allah ﷻ is so strict in His standards with His beloved Messenger ﷺ, then the Master is still the Master in the end.

This balance is crucial because just a few ayahs before, Allah ﷻ displayed extreme love for His Messenger ﷺ. It's very easy for a believing community to take that love and go too far with it. This is what happened with the followers of Isa (AS). The followers of Muhammad ﷺ also have intense, powerful love for our Messenger ﷺ, which means there's also a danger of that love exceeding proper bounds. So the Quran maintains the balance between displaying extreme love for the Messenger ﷺ and then suddenly saying, "If even you were to follow their desires, then you would be from the wrongdoers."

translation: Those to whom We gave the Scripture know him as they know their own sons. But indeed, a party of them conceal the truth while they know [it]
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنْهُمْ لَيَكْتُمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

"Those who We have given the book to, they know it just as well as they know their own children." The word "it" here has been interpreted in multiple ways by scholars. Some say it refers to the Kaaba, others say it refers to the Quran, and still others say it refers to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself. All three interpretations are linguistically possible and contextually sound.

The key point is the certainty of their knowledge. When Allah ﷻ says they know it "as they know their own children," He's using the most intimate, unquestionable knowledge as the standard. You cannot mistake your own child. Similarly, these scholars were very well-read and educated. They recognized the Messenger ﷺ when they saw him, they recognized the legitimacy of the Kaaba as a direction of prayer, and they recognized the truth of the Quran. They already testified internally that the Kaaba is legitimate. They already know that it's the truth from their Master.

Yet despite this knowledge, "there is a distinct group among them that really does hide the truth, and they know—they in fact know." The repetition emphasizes their full awareness of what they're doing. This isn't ignorance or misunderstanding. This is deliberate concealment of truth despite having complete knowledge.

translation: The truth is from your Lord, so never be among the doubters
commentary:
ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ

"The truth is from your Master." This declaration comes at a critical moment. The learned people of the book were using their credentials, their educational background, as a stepping stone to instill doubt into the believing community. They would say, "We have scoured our books, and we know what we're talking about. The Kaaba is not a legitimate direction of prayer. We are the scholars in this community. You're an unlettered prophet anyway—you don't even know how to read. So when we say something, it has weight."

Does this happen today? Absolutely. Academics and intellectuals use their credentials as weapons to instill doubt into believing communities. The same problem manifests in every era.

Allah ﷻ responds to this assault with simple clarity: "The truth is from your Master. Don't you dare become from those who fall into doubt." Despite their arguments, despite their credentials, despite their scholarship, the truth remains the truth. It comes from your Master, and that's all that matters.

translation: For each [religious following] is a direction toward which it faces. So race to [all that is] good. Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you forth [for judgement] all together. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا۟ يَأْتِ بِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

"And for every person there is a particular place they turn, which they are constantly turning towards." This is one of those universal statements in the Quran—a figure of speech that extends beyond the immediate context. The Quran is filled with these expressions that carry wisdom applicable across all times and situations.

Every individual finds a direction in life and sticks to it. It's not just a matter of religion—it's bigger than that. Somebody's direction might be becoming an athlete, so their days and nights are spent perfecting their sport, working their muscles, training relentlessly. Somebody else's goal in life becomes purchasing a house, and they eat, sleep, drink, and think constantly about how to get that house or how to pay it off. People find a direction in life and commit to it. Psychologists call this an ideal to aspire toward, a goal you want to achieve in life.

We live in an interesting time where there's a huge number of people who have no goal in life whatsoever. Their entire aspiration is sitting on a couch. Ask them what they want to do: "Chill." What do you want later? "Chill some more." If you could have anything you want in life, what would you do? "Chill." That's it. There's no drive, no direction, no purpose.

I've met Muslims like this who think there's something wrong with them. They say, "I'm not motivated to do anything. Even when I hear about Jannah, I'm not motivated to go there. I'm not motivated to go to school, to work, to do anything." It's a strange phenomenon when you lose drive for everything. But Allah ﷻ has naturally placed in everyone a drive, a direction they turn towards, something they work towards. Everyone has something they face, a direction their entire lives are oriented around.

Then Allah ﷻ says: "So all of you should compete with each other in good deeds, all manner of good deeds." The direction to the Kaaba has been established. Now what about a direction in life that is marked by you competing in good deeds with each other?

"Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together." Imagine a race going on towards goodness. Some people got way ahead. Some people are a little behind. Some people kind of got off the road. Wherever you may be, Allah ﷻ will bring you all back together. The race will stop—the timer is running, though you don't know when it stops. One day it will stop, and He will bring you all back, and we'll see how much progress you made, how far you got. Everybody will get their respective rewards. "Certainly Allah is in complete control over everything."

translation: So from wherever you go out [for prayer, O Muhammad] turn your face toward al- Masjid al-Haram, and indeed, it is the truth from your Lord. And Allah is not unaware of what you do
commentary:
وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

"And wherever you may come out from, turn your face towards the direction of Al-Masjid Al-Haram, the sanctified mosque." The command is repeated for emphasis. No matter where you are, no matter what situation you find yourself in, this is your direction.

"And certainly it really is the truth from your Master." Again, the affirmation that this is truth, not opinion, not preference, but absolute truth from your Lord.

"And Allah is not unaware at all of what you're up to." There were some among the hypocrites thinking that maybe when nobody else is around, they could go back to praying in the old direction. You know, just to keep up appearances—the Muslims are there watching, but when they're not, we'll do our own thing. Allah ﷻ says He's not unaware of what you're up to. Nothing is hidden from Him.

translation: And from wherever you go out [for prayer], turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram. And wherever you [believers] may be, turn your faces toward it in order that the people will not have any argument against you, except for those of them who commit wrong; so fear them not but fear Me. And [it is] so I may complete My favor upon you and that you may be guided
commentary:
وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَحَيْثُ مَا كُنتُمْ فَوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُۥ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَيْكُمْ حُجَّةٌ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِى وَلِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ

"Wherever you may come out from, turn your face towards the direction of Al-Masjid Al-Haram. And wherever the rest of you maybe, all of you should turn your directions, your faces, in that direction as well."

Why this insistence? "So that there may be no case left in favor of the people against you." If they see you praying back in the old direction, they'll say, "What happened? I thought you changed the Qibla. Are you changing it back again? You can't commit to anything. This religion changes like the seasons." No more should there be a case against you. You should commit to this direction from now on.

"Except for those who do wrong among them—those who are wrongful will try to make a case against you no matter what." Some people will criticize regardless of what you do. Their opposition isn't based on logic or evidence; it's based on their own wrongdoing.

"So don't be afraid of them. Be fearful of Me." The emphasis shifts from human opinion to divine command. Don't let their criticism or their arguments move you. Your concern should be with Allah ﷻ alone.

"And so that I may complete My favor upon you." What favor? The favor of cleaning Allah's house. When you're constantly turning towards the Qibla, aren't you thinking about it? Wouldn't it bother you that the Kaaba is full of idols? You're facing that direction to pray to Allah ﷻ all the time, and it's surrounded by idols. Allah ﷻ is giving you this focus constantly so you will never forget that your mission is to clean that house. The Muslims in Medina—that is their mission. Even though they're in Medina, their eyes, their focus, is on Makkah.

"So that I may complete My favor upon you, and so that you may be committed to guidance."

translation: Just as We have sent among you a messenger from yourselves reciting to you Our verses and purifying you and teaching you the Book and wisdom and teaching you that which you did not know
commentary:
كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيكُمْ رَسُولًا مِّنكُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا وَيُزَكِّيكُمْ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَيُعَلِّمُكُم مَّا لَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْلَمُونَ

"Just as I sent among you a messenger, just like the messenger was a part of you." The word "among you" emphasizes that he's one of you, from your own people, speaking your language, understanding your context.

Now notice the sequence carefully. Allah ﷻ says the Messenger ﷺ "recites onto you Our miraculous signs, purifies you, teaches you the book and the wisdom, and teaches you what you couldn't possibly have known."

This sequence is significant. Remember the dua of Ibrahim (AS)? He asked for a messenger who would recite the signs, teach them the book, teach them wisdom, and purify them. Purification was mentioned at the end in his supplication.

But look at what Allah ﷻ says here: He recites the miraculous signs, purifies you, and then teaches you the book and wisdom. The purification is mentioned earlier now. The teaching of the book and wisdom comes later. Allah ﷻ didn't just listen and accept the prayer of Ibrahim (AS)—He perfected it. He fine-tuned the sequence. Ibrahim (AS) asked for all the right things, but in Allah's wisdom, the sequence could be bettered, and Allah ﷻ perfected it.

How is it perfected? Ibrahim (AS) only had experience with good children. Who was with him when he made this dua? His son Ismail (AS)—an already pure, righteous child. So Ibrahim (AS) thought: teach them the book, teach them wisdom, and keep purifying them to maintain their purity.

But Allah ﷻ knew that later on, children wouldn't be as pure as Ismail (AS). Once the signs are recited to them, what will they need first? Purification, before they even learn something. Because when they're not pure and they learn something, they won't benefit from it. Forget wisdom—wisdom happens when you learn something and act on it. If they're not pure, they won't even be interested in learning.

Purification has two dimensions, like two sides of a coin. On one hand, there's spiritual purification—purification of the heart from jealousy, greed, lust, laziness, lying, cheating, cowardice, anger. These are diseases inside our hearts. Lack of remembrance of Allah ﷻ, love of entertainment—all this requires cleansing.

The other dimension is intellectual purification. Who is Allah ﷻ? What does He expect from me? What is my goal in life? You should have clarity. Your thoughts should be purified. In a society like ours, we're bombarded with useless information constantly—through social media, TV, billboards, radio, blogs, comments under videos, endless streams of content. Our minds have to be purified too. We have to be able to think clearly.

Our teacher used to say: if you eat healthy food and at the same time eat junk food, it's no good. If you're taking in good—learning the Quran, seeking knowledge—but at the same time taking in filth constantly, your mind is not going to be purified. Not to mention your heart, but your mind also needs intellectual clarity.

What is shafa'ah (intercession)? Who qualifies for the Prophet ﷺ to make a case for them on Judgment Day? How will I be successful on that Day? You should have clarity about these fundamentals. This is purification—having a clear idea of what you're getting into. Once you have that clarity, then you can learn the finer details of the law. Then comes wisdom.

"And he teaches you what you couldn't possibly have known yourselves." Knowledge that is completely beyond your capacity to discover on your own.

translation: So remember Me; I will remember you. And be grateful to Me and do not deny Me
commentary:
فَٱذْكُرُونِىٓ أَذْكُرْكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِى وَلَا تَكْفُرُونِ

"So keep remembering Me, and I will make mention of you." The beauty of Allah's mercy shines through in this promise. Elsewhere in the Quran, He says about those who forget Him: "Don't be like those who forgot Allah, so Allah made them forget themselves" (59:19). You'd expect the parallel to say "so Allah forgot them," but He didn't say that. He said Allah made them forget themselves—which is actually worse, because you lose your own identity, your own purpose.

But here, the flip side shows pure mercy: "You remember Me, and I will remember you." He doesn't say, "If you forget Me, I'll forget you." He just says, "Remember Me, and I will remember you."

Think about this. People who are important—we remember them, but they don't remember us. You meet someone significant, you mention them all the time, but they don't mention you. They're too important, too busy, they have too many things to take care of. There's a scholar you talk about constantly, you read about them, you quote them, but they have no idea you exist.

Who could be more important than Allah ﷻ? Who could have more things to do than Allah ﷻ? And you're mentioning Allah ﷻ—no big deal, right? But what's truly remarkable is that Allah ﷻ mentions you. The fact that He, in His infinite majesty, remembers you when you remember Him.

"So be grateful to Me, and don't you dare be ungrateful to Me." The command for gratitude, and the prohibition against ingratitude—both equally emphasized. Don't disbelieve, don't be ungrateful to the One who remembers you when you remember Him.

translation: O you who have believed, seek help through patience and prayer. Indeed, Allah is with the patient
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

"O you who have believed, seek help from patience and prayer." Was this advice given before? Yes—to Bani Israel. "Seek help through patience and prayer" (2:45). It was their command. Now it's your turn.

But notice what happened before this address. Allah ﷻ gave Bani Israel an invitation to come to Islam, then made a list of all the mistakes they made. What is He doing now? He's giving us—the Muslim Ummah—the invitation to become the Ummah we're supposed to be. He just inaugurated us, didn't He? When He said the Messenger ﷺ will be a witness against you, and you will be witnesses to humanity—that's the deal. You're up next.

Before, He told them: "Fulfill your promise, I'll fulfill My promise to you." Here, He's telling us: "You're witnesses to humanity, the Messenger will be witness against you." That's literally what it means—we're up next.

"O you who have believed"—this is the beginning of your journey as a community with a mission. "Seek help through patience and prayer. No doubt about it, Allah is with those who have sabr." Allah is on the side of those who persevere, who remain patient, who endure.

translation: And do not say about those who are killed in the way of Allah, "They are dead." Rather, they are alive, but you perceive [it] not
commentary:
وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَن يُقْتَلُ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتٌۢ ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَشْعُرُونَ

"Don't you dare say about the one who gets killed in Allah's path that they're dead." Fighting hasn't even happened yet in Medina. Allah ﷻ is preparing this new community: you should be ready to get killed in Allah's path.

The only kind of being killed in Allah's path that the Muslims knew at that point was what happened in Makkah—Muslims who didn't even fight back but were killed just because they believed. That was also being killed in Allah's path. The people in Medina haven't seen that. They haven't experienced Muslims being killed for their faith. But Allah ﷻ wants them to know from the very beginning: this is what you're getting into. You need to understand what you've signed up for.

"Don't say about those who are killed in Allah's path that they're dead. Rather, they're alive—but you don't realize it." You have no perception of how they're alive. They're taken immediately to Paradise, experiencing a life you cannot comprehend in your current state.

translation: And We will surely test you with something of fear and hunger and a loss of wealth and lives and fruits, but give good tidings to the patient
commentary:
وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُم بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلْخَوْفِ وَٱلْجُوعِ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَنفُسِ وَٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

"We swear to it—We will definitely, definitely test you." The emphasis in the Arabic is unmistakable. This is a promise, a certainty.

"With anything We want from fear." Political difficulties that create fear. Military difficulties that create fear. Fear of various kinds—fear for your safety, fear for your family, fear for your future.

"Or hunger." Economic difficulties that lead to hunger and scarcity.

"And We will test you if We want with loss in wealth and loss of manpower." You'll lose money. You'll lose people. And losing people doesn't just mean people dying—people might walk away from the faith. People might leave the community. You'll lose both wealth and people.

"And loss of fruits." In Arabic, fruits is an expression used for children, but it also means the fruits of your investment. You'll put all this work into building something, investing in something, and then you lose it.

This became literal reality later in Madinah. At the Battle of Tabuk, the dates were staring at the believers, ripe for picking. The farmers are looking at their harvest—the fruits are perfect, ready to be collected. But the command comes: you cannot pick them this week. You have to go to Tabuk, to an impossible battle. If you don't pick them now, they'll go bad. Only men with special skills in climbing those trees could harvest them. But they couldn't get them—they had to leave for battle. Allah ﷻ tested them with fruits, exactly as He promised.

"And give good news to those who will have patience." Not a warning—good news. To be tested and to have patience is good news, because of what follows.

translation: Who, when disaster strikes them, say, "Indeed we belong to Allah, and indeed to Him we will return
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَصَـٰبَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّآ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ

"Those who, when a calamity would strike upon them—" The word مُصِيبَة is very precise. أَصَابَ means to hit something with an arrow accurately. مُصِيبَة is when you hit a target precisely, exactly on point. A museebah is a calamity that comes from Allah ﷻ and hits exactly where it's supposed to hit. It's a surgical strike, in military terms. These calamities are precise—they're supposed to happen right then and there, to that person, in that way, at that time. It's no accident.

So whenever a precise difficulty strikes them and hits them, they say: "إِنَّا لِلَّهِ—no doubt about it, we belong to Allah."

When a problem happens, we usually talk about loss. Allah ﷻ talked about loss of money, loss of food, loss of peace, loss of children, loss of assets. These are losses. But when calamity strikes, these believers say, "We belong to Allah"—because loss implies you owned something. They're declaring: No, forget what I own that I lost. I myself am owned by Allah ﷻ, so how can I complain about losing these things? They're not mine in the first place. I myself am property, I myself am in the ownership of Allah ﷻ.

"وَإِنَّآ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ—and only to Allah am I going to be returning." All those things that were taken away—what to speak of them? I'll be taken away anyway. Everything returns to Him.

translation: Those are the ones upon whom are blessings from their Lord and mercy. And it is those who are the [rightly] guided
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَٰتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُهْتَدُونَ

"Those are the people—especially upon them there are prayers, salutations from their Master." Scholars interpret this to mean Allah ﷻ commands angels specifically to make dua for these people. Special instruction from Allah ﷻ: these people deserve it. An army of angels making dua for people who respond to calamity with "إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّآ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ."

"And they will have mercy." Not just prayers from angels, but mercy directly from Allah ﷻ.

"أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ—and those, they are the ones that are committed to guidance." Notice Allah ﷻ uses the distant pointer أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ. He often does this when talking about a really high expectation. The distant pointer makes the listener think: those people are up there. I should work to get there. I should be like them. You should feel like you're not close to that yet, that you need to get there. The distant pointer serves that purpose—to inspire you to elevate yourself to that level.

Those are the ones committed to guidance, the ones who have truly understood what it means to belong to Allah ﷻ.

translation: Indeed, as-Safa and al-Marwah are among the symbols of Allah. So whoever makes Hajj to the House or performs 'umrah - there is no blame upon him for walking between them. And whoever volunteers good - then indeed, Allah is appreciative and Knowing
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ ٱلصَّفَا وَٱلْمَرْوَةَ مِن شَعَآئِرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَنْ حَجَّ ٱلْبَيْتَ أَوِ ٱعْتَمَرَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِ أَن يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِمَا ۚ وَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَاكِرٌ عَلِيمٌ

Context is essential here. The house—the Kaaba—is to be cleansed. The dua of Ibrahim (AS) was: "Show us our rituals." The pagans used to have certain rituals. The Muslims didn't know which of those rituals were legitimate from Ibrahim (AS) and which were added later. They had no idea. Even the companions from Makkah had performed Hajj before Islam, but that Hajj had weird practices mixed in with legitimate ones, and they didn't know how to distinguish between them.

So Allah ﷻ makes it clear: one of the main rites of Hajj that is legitimate and should be honored—"إِنَّ ٱلصَّفَا وَٱلْمَرْوَةَ—certainly the locations of Safa and Marwah مِن شَعَآئِرِ ٱللَّهِ—are from the sha'a'ir of Allah."

The word شَعِيرَة (plural شَعَآئِر) comes from a root that means something that makes you think of something, that brings about feelings. The Kaaba brings about a feeling when you look at it. When you haven't seen a relative for a long time and then you see them, it brings overwhelming emotion—you might start crying without being able to help yourself. That's sha'eerah.

Safa and Marwah should bring about feelings. When you see them, you should be overwhelmed with emotion. They should make you realize something profound. These are places that have emotional value, sentimental value to a Muslim. They're not just places for hiking.

When I was a kid and my parents took me to Umrah, I used to love Safa and Marwah only because you get to climb to the top. But the true spirit is that when you get there, you should remember the struggle of that amazing mother, Hajar (RA), and that little baby, Ismail (AS), running between these hills searching for water, trusting in Allah ﷻ completely.

"فَمَنْ حَجَّ ٱلْبَيْتَ أَوِ ٱعْتَمَرَ—so whoever makes Hajj of the house or makes Umrah—" Can they make Hajj and Umrah at this time? No. Why not? Because the Quraysh are in control of the sacred mosque. But Allah ﷻ is already saying very casually, "When you go make Umrah..." He's opening the door to go to Hajj.

Think about what's already been discussed. You're already praying towards the Kaaba, thinking about it all the time. Now Allah ﷻ is saying, "By the way, when you're going to make Hajj—" Wait, if we're going to make Hajj, the Quraysh probably won't like that very much. We might have to confront them if we're going to Hajj. What's the logical consequence of this discussion? Fighting. It's the logical consequence.

"فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِ أَن يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِمَا—there's no harm in that person who goes to make Hajj that he should make tawaf of them." Tawaf means going back and forth—he should go back and forth between Safa and Marwah also.

"وَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا—and whoever volunteered out of will, whoever went out of their way to earn more good deeds, to do extra—فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَاكِرٌ عَلِيمٌ—certainly Allah is grateful, truly appreciative, and He knows."

It's such a striking thing for Allah ﷻ to say He's grateful. It's almost embarrassing for the believer. If your mother said to you, "Can you call me? If you did, I would be really grateful. It would be a huge favor if you just called me once"—wouldn't that be humiliating? Your mother thinks she has to ask for a favor just to get a phone call from you?

Or imagine your mother saying, "Could you do me a huge favor and give me five dollars? I promise I'll pay you back." If your mother said that, it would be the most humiliating thing you've ever heard. She thinks you expect repayment from her?

Allah ﷻ says, "He will be grateful." Isn't that embarrassing to the believer? "Can you please make Hajj? I'll be very grateful." It should shake us. What would keep a believer from going to Hajj when Allah ﷻ says He'll be grateful? He's appreciative. He'll really appreciate it. He knows you made an effort.

Later, He'll say, "Can someone give Me a loan?" (2:245). Can someone give Me a loan—a beautiful loan? An open-ended loan where Allah ﷻ doesn't even specify when He'll pay you back. But He promises: "I'll multiply it for you." It should be embarrassing. We'll only do it if He multiplies it? The fact that He asked for a loan should be enough.

These are ways by which the conscience of a believer is rattled. Of course we should do it. But we haven't been doing it, so He puts it in these terms to wake us up.

translation: Indeed, those who conceal what We sent down of clear proofs and guidance after We made it clear for the people in the Scripture - those are cursed by Allah and cursed by those who curse
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَآ أَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلْهُدَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا بَيَّنَّـٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّـٰعِنُونَ

"Those who no doubt hide what We sent down from the clearest proofs and guidance." This refers to the Jewish scholars who were hiding the fact that the Kaaba is legitimate, that the Prophet ﷺ is truly the Messenger. But the ayah says something far broader: "Whoever hides what We have sent down from the clearest proofs and guidance, even after it's been made clear to people."

Hasn't the Quran been made clear to people? It has. Allah ﷻ made it clear to the Messenger ﷺ and to all of mankind. Now, whose responsibility is it to make sure the Quran is not hidden? Who's supposed to ensure it's clarified and shared? The Muslim Ummah—we are witnesses against humanity. This is our responsibility now.

Allah ﷻ says: "Those who fail to show it, who keep it hidden—أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ—those people, Allah curses them. وَيَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّـٰعِنُونَ—and the cursers curse them."

There are angels—certain angels whose only job is to curse. That's their sole function. لَعْن (la'n) means to be removed from Allah's mercy. There are angels specifically assigned to curse people who hide the book.

On one hand, there are people who hide the book. On the other hand—and we'll learn this in the next ayah—there are those who are the exception to this curse. What we're learning is you can either be one or the other. There's no in-between. Either you're hiding the book or you're clarifying it. There's no neutral position.

As an Ummah, either we are engaged in the task of clarifying Allah's book, and if we're not, then by definition we are hiding it. If one is hiding it, what do they get? Curses. And if you've been hiding it, you better make tawbah, fix yourself, and start clarifying.

translation: Except for those who repent and correct themselves and make evident [what they concealed]. Those - I will accept their repentance, and I am the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ وَبَيَّنُوا۟ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنَا ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟—except those who repented, وَأَصْلَحُوا۟—and corrected their behavior, reconciled, fixed themselves up, وَبَيَّنُوا۟—and clarified."

The question is: clarified what? The book. They clarified the Quran. Either you're clarifying the Quran or you're hiding it. Either we as an Ummah are engaged in the task of clarifying Allah's book, and if we're not, then we are hiding it.

These ayahs, personally for myself at least, continuously motivate me to just talk about the Quran—that maybe, maybe some of this will count as an effort to clarify it. We as an Ummah have this responsibility. The Prophet ﷺ told us: "Convey on my behalf even if it's one ayah." Spread it. Don't hide this book. This book is meant to be spread.

But how can we spread something we don't even know? We don't even appreciate. We don't even love. We have to become these people. You can lose sight of the fact that this is Allah's word. It can start to sound like just another speech—you hear speeches all the time. But we're going through Allah's words. Allah ﷻ is telling us this stuff Himself. This is His speech to us, not mine, not yours.

"فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ—those are the people whose repentance I will accept. وَأَنَا ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ—And I am the continually accepting of repentance, always merciful."

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve and die while they are disbelievers - upon them will be the curse of Allah and of the angels and the people, all together
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ

"إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟—no doubt about it, those who disbelieved." Kufr in the Quran is always contextual. You have to look at what was discussed before to understand what kind of disbelief is meant.

The ayahs before talked about what you have to do to have Allah's tawbah accepted—those who repented, corrected themselves, and clarified. So who are the disbelievers here? Those who denied that responsibility. Those who didn't care for it. They disbelieved—kufr in general, but in particular here, which kind of disbelief? Disbelief in that responsibility to clarify Allah's message.

"وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ—and they died while they were in that state of denial." They never changed.

"أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ—those are the ones that have the curse of Allah upon them."

Before, when Allah ﷻ spoke about the curse while they were alive, He used the present tense verb يَلْعَنُهُمُ: "Allah curses them." A verb in present tense indicates something temporary, ongoing. While they're alive, the curse is active, but it's temporary because they can make tawbah and escape it.

But if they died in that state, the curse becomes permanent. So now it's in the nominal form—the noun form. لَعْنَةُ—a curse, permanent and fixed. Upon them is the curse of Allah ﷻ, the angels, and all people altogether.

This is separate from the testimony the Messenger ﷺ will give against them—that's already covered. This curse is on top of that problem.

translation: Abiding eternally therein. The punishment will not be lightened for them, nor will they be reprieved
commentary:
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ

"خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا—they will remain in it." In what? In the curse, in the punishment that follows from that curse.

"لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ—the punishment will not be lightened for them." It might be lightened for others, but not for these. Why? Because they had the truth, they knew it, and they actively concealed it, preventing others from guidance. That's a compounded crime.

"وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ—and they won't even be looked at." The torturers won't even glance at them. And they won't even be given respite—no pause, no waiting. They'll beg: "Just look at me, just stop for one second, let me recover from the last blow." No. Too gruesome to even contemplate, but this is the reality Allah ﷻ describes.

Someone might say, "What kind of God would do that?" A sister once came up to me and said, "What kind of God has so much punishment?" I asked her, "Have you read the next ayah?"

translation: And your god is one God. There is no deity [worthy of worship] except Him, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful
commentary:
وَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"وَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ—your God is one single God. لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ—there is no one worthy of worship and obedience except He. ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ—the extremely, unimaginably merciful, the always merciful."

Right after mentioning severe punishment, Allah ﷻ declares His mercy. Why? Because His mercy was supposed to be delivered to all of humanity through His guidance. The people responsible to deliver Allah's mercy were the Muslim Ummah.

When the Muslim Ummah becomes the reason the mercy never got delivered, shouldn't they be punished? Isn't that a crime? Allah ﷻ says He's extremely merciful. The ultimate mercy of Allah ﷻ is what? The Quran. When the Quran is kept from humanity, who's to blame?

It's a serious question. The severe punishment explains itself in the context. Why should this message be delivered to people? So people can know who Allah ﷻ is: "وَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ." That's what you kept people from learning.

translation: Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and earth, and the alternation of the night and the day, and the [great] ships which sail through the sea with that which benefits people, and what Allah has sent down from the heavens of rain, giving life thereby to the earth after its lifelessness and dispersing therein every [kind of] moving creature, and [His] directing of the winds and the clouds controlled between the heaven and the earth are signs for a people who use reason
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلَّتِى تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن مَّآءٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ وَٱلسَّحَابِ ٱلْمُسَخَّرِ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ

This remarkable ayah shifts the discussion entirely. The previous ayahs were about hiding revelation—those are ayat of revelation. But people could argue, "We never came into contact with Allah's revelation. That's why we were never guided."

Allah ﷻ responds: Actually, there are other ayat too. If the ayat of revelation never came to you, the ayat of creation were all around you. They've been there since the beginning. So there's a deliberate switch from the ayat of revelation to the ayat of creation and existence.

"إِنَّ—certainly, فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—there truly are miraculous signs in the creation of the skies and the earth." Just looking at the heavens and the earth—there are signs.

"وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ—and in the alternation of the night and day." The conflict, the changing between them.

"وَٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلَّتِى تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ—and in the ships that sail in the midst of the ocean with what benefits people." The cargo they carry, the way they transport goods.

"وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن مَّآءٍ—and whatever Allah sent down from the sky from water." Rain—there's a sign in that also.

"فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا—then He gives life to the earth after it had died, after its death." Dead, barren land becomes green and lush.

"وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ—and He distributed on this earth all manner of creatures." Every type of animal, insect, living thing.

"وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ—and in the changing of the winds." Winds blow this way, then that way. They blow soft, they blow strong. They create hurricanes and soft breezes.

"وَٱلسَّحَابِ ٱلْمُسَخَّرِ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—and the clouds hanging between the sky and the earth, the suspended clouds."

"لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ—there truly are miraculous signs in all of these things. لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ—for a nation that tries to apply its intellect."

Notice the unexpected ending. You'd expect "for believers" or "for those who reflect." Instead: "for a people who reason." This is about using your mind.

How are these things miraculous signs? How are there signs in the creation of the skies and earth, in ships sailing, in all these phenomena? We're supposed to think about this. We're supposed to be deep thinkers. Everything around us should make us think.

The sky should make us think about harmony and order. The sun and moon—they follow a schedule and are never late, always on time. The One who made these must love discipline. You can tell the manufacturer by the style of His manufacturing, by His design. He loves harmony, discipline, order, balance.

Then He creates diversity. He loves beauty because He creates beautiful things. You're beautiful. He loves beautiful things. You can see this in the mountains He creates, the oceans, the animals. We take our kids to aquariums to look at these amazing creatures. We're appreciating the beauty that Allah ﷻ made. That's a sign.

Ships that sail in the middle of the ocean—how is that a sign? The little pearl at the bottom of the ocean and a giant tanker floating on top. Who's keeping it there?

Think about birds coasting without flapping their wings, just gliding. Who's holding them in the air?

Consider the clouds. When clouds don't show up, entire nations die of dehydration. People go to war over water shortages. All from clouds. And clouds don't move unless winds make them. The way wind moves can destroy a nation or build it. Winds carry pollen, carry all of life with them.

We're supposed to think about all of this. Everything around us works in such harmony. There are so many departments of creation, all working together perfectly. When human governments have many departments, things fail because they don't communicate. But the agricultural department of creation works with the weather department, works with the geological department—all these aspects working in harmony just so we can have a comfortable life.

"لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ—truly miraculous signs for people who really want to apply their intellect."

When you can put your emotions in check, when your mind isn't full of clutter and filth, you can actually think about real things.
