If your mind is full of distractions, the moment you have an extra second to think, you'll say, "I can't think. I need to play a game. I need to listen to a song." You can't stay still, can't think, can't have idle time to just reflect.

How many of us actually sit and just think? Look at the sky and think? When was the last time we did that? We're supposed to be people of thought. Using our intellect is a gift from Allah ﷻ that we neglect.

I have a friend—and I admire him because when I'm around him, I realize I'm not naturally a deep thinker like he is. We were traveling once in Manhattan, and he just grabbed my arm and said, "Stop. Look around you."

I looked at the sidewalk. There was a crack with a little plant growing through it. He said, "You know, this is the only thing here that doesn't fit. Everything is concrete—squares, glass, plastic, metal. Everything is artificial. The only thing out of place is this little plant that had to crack through concrete to make its way. It's out of place, and yet it's the only thing that's natural. Everything else is unnatural. That little plant is us—Islam. It seems so weird to everybody, but it's the only thing that's really supposed to be here."

What makes this friend amazing? Every time I see him, he has a copy of the Quran in his hand. Every conversation, he says, "Oh, that reminds me of an ayah," and we talk about it. His entire worldview is shaped by the Quran. That's what it means to be a thinker.

We need to become thinkers. It's a gift to be able to think—like putting on glasses that let you see everything through the Quran. Everything gets colored beautifully. You see guidance in everything. Everything is a lesson.

That's the kind of people Allah ﷻ wants us to be—people who don't lose sight of the Quran, where the Quran runs in their blood, is on their tongue, in their hearts and thoughts all the time.

translation: And [yet], among the people are those who take other than Allah as equals [to Him]. They love them as they [should] love Allah. But those who believe are stronger in love for Allah. And if only they who have wronged would consider [that] when they see the punishment, [they will be certain] that all power belongs to Allah and that Allah is severe in punishment
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَندَادًا يُحِبُّونَهُمْ كَحُبِّ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِّلَّهِ ۗ وَلَوْ يَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ إِذْ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْعَذَابَ أَنَّ ٱلْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعَذَابِ

The previous ayah was about people who use their intellect. But the intellect only goes where the heart takes it. This is an awesome transition. People who don't think—it's because their hearts are in the wrong place. If their hearts aren't grateful, they won't look for reasons to think.

"وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ—from among people مَن يَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَندَادًا—there are those who take other than Allah as competitors, as rivals."

"يُحِبُّونَهُمْ كَحُبِّ ٱللَّهِ—they love them as though they should be loving Allah, as they love Allah, in place of loving Allah."

Notice: if these competitors were just idols—statues—Allah ﷻ would say يَعْبُدُونَهَا (they worship them). But He says يُحِبُّونَهُمْ—they love them. The pronoun used is for people and conscious beings, not just objects. This means the rivals include both human and non-human competitors.

"وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِّلَّهِ—but those who really do believe are intense in their love for Allah."

We have to ask ourselves: besides Allah ﷻ, what are the competitors that we love like Allah ﷻ should be loved? There's an Arabic expression: "Whoever loves something talks about it all the time."

Ask yourself: what do you mention all the time? What are you talking about constantly? Sports? Games? Movies? Playoffs? Stats? Cars? Technology? Fashion? Clothing? Weddings? What are we obsessed with? What's running in our heads constantly? That's what you love most.

What has taken the place of Allah ﷻ? Idols can be in front of you like statues, but idols can be in your heart too. You have to fight those internal idols. Find them and crush them. This is the hidden shirk the Prophet ﷺ warned about—harder to detect than an ant crawling on a black stone in the dark of night.

These are things we love more than we should love Allah ﷻ. Things we're obsessed with that have taken over the rightful place of Allah ﷻ in our hearts. Allah ﷻ wants complete space. He doesn't like to share.

We can love other things—our family, playing sports, enjoying ourselves. But those loves have to submit to the love of Allah ﷻ. They have to be secondary.

"وَلَوْ يَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟—and if only those who did wrong could just see." The ayah switches from love to power.

"إِذْ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْعَذَابَ—when they'll be looking at the punishment أَنَّ ٱلْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا—that power belongs to Allah alone."

Had they realized that when they were showing up and not giving Allah ﷻ His right, the wrongdoing here is not showing Allah ﷻ His due love. That's the wrong in this ayah. But if you don't show Allah ﷻ love and you commit the act of shirk, then you taste His might.

"وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعَذَابِ—and that Allah is severe in giving punishment."

translation: [And they should consider that] when those who have been followed disassociate themselves from those who followed [them], and they [all] see the punishment, and cut off from them are the ties [of relationship]
commentary:
إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتُّبِعُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَسْبَابُ

"إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتُّبِعُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟—when those who used to be followed are going to be cutting themselves off from those who used to follow them."

This is about trendsetters and trend followers. This concept is incredibly relevant to our time on multiple levels.

There were Jews who wanted to become Muslim but didn't. Why? They realized that if they converted, they'd be cut off from their tight-knit community. Some were in positions of leadership. They were convinced of Islam intellectually but didn't embrace it publicly. Why not? Because they had status, influence. "If I say something publicly unpopular, everybody will hate me. I can't handle that pressure. I'll just stay where I am."

This happens within Islam too. Scholars are afraid to speak truth in their own communities. There's some problem happening, people have weird understandings, they're doing strange things in the mosque, but those same people are the biggest donors. The imam thinks, "I want to keep my job. I shouldn't say anything. I'll just talk about mercy. There are so many topics in Islam—why talk about the one that will get me in trouble? There's an endless list of safe topics. Why strike a nerve?"

Then there are other kinds of trend following. High school kids in America—high school is supposed to be about expressing freedom, but it's actually a classic example of modern slavery. You're forced to fit into a clique. You have to dress like them, talk like them, walk like them. If you don't have the same "swag," you're out. You're weird. You don't fit.

You have to buy certain brands of clothes, certain sneakers. They have to be untied a certain way—it goes that far. The laces have to be untied in a specific style, and it changes generation to generation. In the eighties it was Pumas with different colored laces. Fashion becomes almost a religion.

You have to talk a certain way, hang out with certain friends, listen to certain music. You do all of this because you need to fit in.

Then it goes further. People literally worship icons. Girls crying at concerts, putting up posters, covering their notebooks with stickers. Constant obsession. And it's not just celebrities—even within Muslim communities, there are circles where you have to keep up appearances.

If you're a physician, you have your circle. If you're a businessman, your circle. Each circle has norms. You have to keep up. If someone threw a lavish wedding and went into serious debt, then when your daughter gets married, you think, "We can't do it at the masjid. What will people say? What will our relatives think? They did theirs at that hotel—ours has to match or we'll look cheap."

Because of others, you put yourself in trouble, do things that might even be haram, all because of what people will say. This is trend following.

People live their entire lives for "what will people say?" And on Judgment Day, everyone cuts themselves off.

"وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ—and they'll both be looking at the punishment"—the followers and the followed, everyone.

"وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَسْبَابُ—and all their relationships will be cut off." All connections, all networks—gone. They used to have all these ties, and now every single one is severed.

translation: Those who followed will say, "If only we had another turn [at worldly life] so we could disassociate ourselves from them as they have disassociated themselves from us." Thus will Allah show them their deeds as regrets upon them. And they are never to emerge from the Fire
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَتَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُمْ كَمَا تَبَرَّءُوا۟ مِنَّا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُرِيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ حَسَرَٰتٍ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَمَا هُم بِخَـٰرِجِينَ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ

"وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟—and those who used to follow will say لَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً—if we had another chance, if we could go back."

"فَنَتَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُمْ كَمَا تَبَرَّءُوا۟ مِنَّا—we would cut ourselves off from them like they're cutting themselves off from us now."

If we had another chance, "I would not have been your fan. I wouldn't have been like you. I wouldn't have bought a house in your neighborhood just to be near you. I wouldn't have gotten into that business just because you did. I wouldn't have done any of it."

But do they get another chance? No.

"كَذَٰلِكَ يُرِيهِمُ

translation: O mankind, eat from whatever is on earth [that is] lawful and good and do not follow the footsteps of Satan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ كُلُوا۟ مِمَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ

"O humanity, eat from whatever is on earth that is halal and good, and do not follow the footsteps of shaytan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy."

This ayah addresses all of humanity with a fundamental command about consumption and boundaries. Allah ﷻ uses the comprehensive address يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O humanity) to establish a universal principle. The command كُلُوا۟ (eat) encompasses two essential qualities: حَلَـٰلًا (halal - permissible) and طَيِّبًا (tayyib - good and pure). The word طَيِّبًا combines both goodness and purity, establishing that what we consume must meet both legal and qualitative standards.

The placement of this ayah immediately after the discussion of false followers reveals a crucial connection. When people follow societal trends and cultural norms blindly, they inevitably cross the boundaries that Allah ﷻ has established. The fundamental problem with trend-following is that people stop caring about the lines drawn by Allah ﷻ and instead prioritize the norms of their culture or social circles.

What makes this address particularly powerful is that it's directed at all humanity, not just believers. The original context addresses those outside Islam who are considering entering it but hesitate because they don't want to abandon the haram they've grown accustomed to. The ayah establishes that you can participate in society and culture, but the line must be drawn where Allah's ﷻ boundaries lie.

The warning against following شَيْطَـٰنِ (shaytan's) footsteps reveals how spiritual corruption progresses gradually. The phrase خُطُوَٰتِ (footsteps) indicates that shaytan doesn't immediately control you completely. Rather, he begins with whispers and enticements, gradually gaining more authority as you give in to his suggestions. In the beginning, shaytan can only whisper and try to convince you. But over time, when you surrender to his footsteps, he gains increasing authority while you progressively lose control.

The declaration إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (he is certainly for you a clear enemy) uses emphatic language. The word مُّبِينٌ means an open, obvious enemy - there should be no doubt about his enmity toward you.

translation: He only orders you to evil and immorality and to say about Allah what you do not know
commentary:
إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلسُّوٓءِ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

This ayah exposes shaytan's strategy in three escalating stages. The word يَأْمُرُكُم doesn't only mean "command" - it represents a spectrum from suggesting to advising to encouraging, and finally to commanding. Shaytan begins by suggesting something wrong, but as you grant him authority, he eventually gains enough power to command you toward evil.

The first level is ٱلسُّوٓءِ (su') - things that are evil and ugly in themselves, like a filthy corpse on the street. These acts have no appeal or attraction, yet shaytan gets people to commit them anyway. Backbiting and foul language fall into this category - there's no real allure to them, but people engage in them due to shaytan's influence.

The second level is ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ (fahsha) - shameless and lewd behavior that does have appeal and attraction. These sins are more dangerous precisely because they entice and allure. If shaytan gets you to commit both su' and fahsha, he has gained significant authority over you.

The third and most dangerous stage comes when shaytan pushes you to وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (say about Allah ﷻ what you have no knowledge of). At this point, you begin justifying your misbehavior by making claims about religion without any actual knowledge. You might say, "Islam never really said you can't do this" or "What's so wrong about this anyway?" This represents shaytan's ultimate authority - when he gets you to twist religion itself to justify your desires.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Follow what Allah has revealed," they say, "Rather, we will follow that which we found our fathers doing." Even though their fathers understood nothing, nor were they guided
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ مَآ أَلْفَيْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۗ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ

When they are told to follow what Allah ﷻ has revealed, their response reveals the poverty of blind tradition: "Rather, we will follow what we found our forefathers upon." The word أَلْفَيْنَا (alfayna - we found) is particularly significant. This verb describes finding something the way an animal stumbles upon an object and starts digging at it - it doesn't require human intellect. In contrast, وجد (wajada) with jim represents intellectual discovery, a distinctly human activity.

Allah ﷻ is highlighting that their following is less intellectual, more animalistic. They simply stumbled upon their ancestors doing something and decided that was sufficient reason to continue it. There's no critical thinking, no examination of whether these traditions have rational validity. The mere fact that their ancestors did something becomes its own justification.

Then Allah ﷻ delivers a devastating critique: أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (even if their forefathers understood nothing and were not guided?). Not only are the followers not thinking, but their ancestors weren't thinking either. The forefathers themselves hadn't understood anything, nor were they committed to guidance. It's blind leading the blind across generations.

translation: The example of those who disbelieve is like that of one who shouts at what hears nothing but calls and cries cattle or sheep - deaf, dumb and blind, so they do not understand
commentary:
وَمَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِى يَنْعِقُ بِمَا لَا يَسْمَعُ إِلَّا دُعَآءً وَنِدَآءً ۚ صُمٌّۢ بُكْمٌ عُمْىٌ فَهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ

The example of those who chose to disbelieve mindlessly is compared to a shepherd calling to animals that don't understand words. No matter what the shepherd says, the sheep doesn't process the meaning. If a sheep is heading toward a cliff and the shepherd yells warnings, the sheep doesn't stop to consider, "Perhaps I shouldn't go this way." It simply keeps going until it falls.

The calls and cries of the shepherd are useless except when he uses his stick to physically redirect the animals. The sheep hears nothing except sounds - no meaning, no comprehension. The comparison extends to the Prophet ﷺ calling people to guidance. No matter what he says, it has no effect on them, like words falling on deaf ears.

What makes this comparison even more striking is that eventually, sheep do follow the shepherd despite not understanding him. But these people are even worse than cattle - they won't even follow despite the clear guidance being offered.

The ayah concludes with a devastating description: صُمٌّۢ بُكْمٌ عُمْىٌ (deaf, mute, and blind). They're deaf and cannot hear guidance. They're mute and cannot even ask for help. They're blind and cannot see the path. Every door to guidance is sealed shut. فَهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ - they will not understand. Their commitment to ancestral tradition has completely closed them off from reason and guidance.

translation: O you who have believed, eat from the good things which We have provided for you and be grateful to Allah if it is [indeed] Him that you worship
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِلَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ

Now the address shifts to believers with يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - a warning not to become like those who let blind tradition corrupt the pure teachings of religion. What happened to the followers of Musa (AS)? They received pure Islam, but over time it became mixed and corrupted. Eventually, those Muslims couldn't distinguish halal from haram anymore - everything became murky and confused.

The same danger exists for this Ummah. The Messenger ﷺ was given pure Islam with clear distinctions between halal and haram. But over time, things get mixed in, and the lines become blurred. Allah ﷻ warns believers against this trajectory.

The command كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ (eat from the good things We have provided you) echoes the earlier universal command to humanity, but with a critical addition: وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِلَّهِ (and be grateful to Allah ﷻ).

This statement about gratitude is profoundly important. The only reason believers would pursue haram is because they're not sufficiently grateful for the halal they already have. When you have permissible things in front of you - yes, perhaps less variety than what exists in the world, perhaps more restrictions - you should be grateful because what you have is good and pure.

The ayah ends with a powerful connection: إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (if it is truly Him alone that you worship). The mention of worship in the context of consumption isn't random. Some people worship Allah ﷻ abundantly - they pray, perform tahajjud, go to Umrah regularly - but they consume haram, earn haram income, and eat haram food. Allah ﷻ says no: you wouldn't be eating haram if you were truly engaged in worshiping Him. True worship encompasses all aspects of life, including what you put in your body and how you earn your sustenance.

translation: He has only forbidden to you dead animals, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been dedicated to other than Allah. But whoever is forced [by necessity], neither desiring [it] nor transgressing [its limit], there is no sin upon him. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةَ وَٱلدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ بِهِۦ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

The structure إِنَّمَا (innama - only, merely) emphasizes restriction: "He has only made forbidden to you..." The list is deliberately short, highlighting Allah's ﷻ mercy in not overburdening His servants.

For modern readers, it's easy to glance at this list and move on: dead animals, blood, swine, and what's been sacrificed to other than Allah ﷻ. Simple enough. But understanding the historical context reveals the magnitude of this command. The people of Medina weren't living next to a Walmart. Their food supply was severely limited in a desert climate. They didn't have abundant cattle - animals were scarce and precious resources.

When Allah ﷻ prohibited swine, that represented potentially six months of food supply eliminated instantly. Dead animals that perished from heat or dehydration - that was at least a month's worth of meals, now forbidden. Even blood, which they used in stews due to the scarcity of liquids in that dry climate, became impermissible. Allah ﷻ essentially cut their food supply by at least half, and they weren't a wealthy people to begin with.

This makes the earlier command to "be grateful" even more powerful. Despite having their options drastically reduced, they were expected to be grateful for what remained permissible.

The exceptions show Allah's ﷻ mercy alongside His law: فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ (whoever is forced by necessity). But notice the conditions: غَيْرَ بَاغٍ (without being rebellious). Baghi describes someone who, when told not to do something, responds with defiance: "Oh yeah? Now I'm definitely going to do it just because you told me not to." That attitude nullifies the exception.

وَلَا عَادٍ (and not transgressing) means not acting out of animosity or saying, "I'm tired of eating the same permissible foods. I can't take it anymore." The exception is purely for survival - when you're in the desert about to die and there's nothing halal available.

Even then, the early Muslims would have felt spiritually uncomfortable eating haram, even in that permitted state. They would consume only what was necessary to survive, not treating it as an opportunity to indulge.

The ayah concludes with إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - Allah ﷻ is Ghafur (extremely forgiving) and Rahim (constantly merciful). Understanding these attributes requires precision. When Allah ﷻ mentions His forgiveness, He uses the pattern of hyperbole (ghafur), emphasizing its extreme nature. When He mentions mercy, He uses the pattern of constancy (rahim), emphasizing its permanence.

This distinction maintains balance in our relationship with Allah ﷻ. If He said He was "always forgiving," we might become reckless, thinking we have a free pass to sin because forgiveness is guaranteed. But He says He is "extremely forgiving" - suggesting depth and magnitude without suggesting automatic or unlimited forgiveness. However, He is "constantly merciful" - His mercy is permanent and ongoing. This combination keeps us balanced between hope and fear, gratitude and caution.

translation: Indeed, they who conceal what Allah has sent down of the Book and exchange it for a small price - those consume not into their bellies except the Fire. And Allah will not speak to them on the Day of Resurrection, nor will He purify them. And they will have a painful punishment
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَشْتَرُونَ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارَ وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَلَا يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

This ayah addresses a dangerous phenomenon that occurs when religion becomes corrupt: religion transforms into an industry. The scenario described is of religious authorities - those who hold knowledge of scripture - who hide what Allah ﷻ has revealed and sell it for a small price.

When religion becomes commercialized, those who possess religious knowledge find themselves in a position of tremendous power. People come to them for religious verdicts, answers, and guidance. These authorities become the sole source of religious interpretation. But here's where corruption enters: if they give people answers people don't want to hear, their "client base" moves elsewhere. So to maintain their position, income, and influence, they start giving people the answers they want to hear rather than the truth.

This is selling the ayat of Allah ﷻ. You look at the sacred texts and interpret them in ways that justify what people want to do rather than what Allah ﷻ actually commands. In our times, we've developed sophisticated ways of doing this. The easiest cop-out is to say, "Well, there's a difference of opinion." You might have no idea what those different opinions actually are, who held them, or what their evidence was - but that phrase becomes a blanket justification for anything.

Allah ﷻ knows when people are playing games with religion. You might convince others, but you cannot deceive Him. When those with religious knowledge use that knowledge for personal advantage, strategic benefit, or financial gain, Allah ﷻ declares: مَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارَ (they consume nothing in their bellies except fire).

The consequences on Judgment Day are severe: وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (Allah ﷻ will not speak to them on the Day of Resurrection). Imagine the horror of standing before Allah ﷻ and receiving complete silence - no communication, no acknowledgment. وَلَا يُزَكِّيهِمْ (nor will He purify them) - they will remain in their corruption, unpurified and unforgiven. وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (and for them is a painful punishment).

The emphasis on "painful" punishment specifically for this sin highlights how seriously Allah ﷻ takes the corruption of religious knowledge. These are people who had both knowledge and responsibility, and they betrayed both.

translation: Those are the ones who have exchanged guidance for error and forgiveness for punishment. How patient they are in pursuit of the Fire
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْعَذَابَ بِٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ فَمَآ أَصْبَرَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ

These are the ones who purchased misguidance in exchange for guidance, and punishment in exchange for forgiveness. The transaction metaphor is powerful - they had access to the most valuable commodity imaginable (guidance and divine forgiveness) and they traded it for worthless goods (misguidance and punishment).

The final statement فَمَآ أَصْبَرَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ (how patient they are toward the Fire!) is dripping with sarcasm from Allah ﷻ. It's as if He's saying, "Wow, look how remarkably patient they are against the Fire. What thick skin they must have! How impressive their endurance!" But of course, this isn't praise - it's devastating irony. They're marching toward the Fire with such determination, such persistence, such "patience" in their wrong path that it's almost remarkable. Their stubbornness in clinging to corruption is so extreme that they're willing to face eternal fire for temporary worldly gain.

translation: That is [deserved by them] because Allah has sent down the Book in truth. And indeed, those who differ over the Book are in extreme dissension
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَفِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

The ayah explains why these scholars face such severe punishment: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ (that is because Allah ﷻ sent down the Book with truth, with purpose). The Book came with absolute truth and clear purpose - it wasn't ambiguous or unclear. The corruption that followed wasn't due to any deficiency in the revelation itself.

وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (and indeed those who differed concerning the Book) - they created disagreements and divisions in something that was clear. لَفِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (are in extreme dissension). The word شِقَاقٍ (shiqaq) implies schism, splitting, division. They kept creating division after division, each time moving further from the truth, driven by personal agendas rather than sincere pursuit of understanding.

This sets up the next passage perfectly. After discussing how religion becomes corrupted through false scholars creating divisions, Allah ﷻ will now present the comprehensive, balanced view of what it truly means to be good in Islam.

translation: Righteousness is not that you turn your faces toward the east or the west, but [true] righteousness is [in] one who believes in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets and gives wealth, in spite of love for it, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveler, those who ask [for help], and for freeing slaves; [and who] establishes prayer and gives zakah; [those who] fulfill their promise when they promise; and [those who] are patient in poverty and hardship and during battle. Those are the ones who have been true, and it is those who are the righteous
commentary:
۞ لَّيْسَ ٱلْبِرَّ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَءَاتَى ٱلْمَالَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِۦ ذَوِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَٱلسَّآئِلِينَ وَفِى ٱلرِّقَابِ وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ۖ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ فِى ٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ وَحِينَ ٱلْبَأْسِ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ

This remarkable ayah presents a comprehensive, balanced vision of righteousness in Islam. Before examining it, we must understand that Muslims tend to fall into two extreme definitions of goodness.

On one extreme, you have people who focus entirely on ritual worship. They pray on time, commit to proper dress codes, maintain strict dietary restrictions, and are clearly serious about religious practice. However, they show no consideration for ethics, courtesy to neighbors or coworkers, honesty in the workplace, or fair dealings with family. Religious rituals are flawless; morality and justice are absent. The person has a long beard, recites Quran beautifully, but is a terrible employer who doesn't pay workers on time and takes loans he never repays.

This creates a devastating perception problem. Most Muslims who aren't particularly religious look at outwardly religious people and conclude they're all hypocrites and con artists. They assume that women in hijab and men with beards are just putting on a show while actually being dishonest.

On the other extreme, you have the purely ethical definition: "I'm a nice person. I don't hurt anyone. I'm good to my family, honest at work, a kind neighbor. I pay my taxes and don't commit crimes. I don't need to pray or fast or go to Hajj to be good." This reduces goodness to social morality while abandoning religious obligations entirely.

This ayah bridges both extremes, combining the ritualistic and ethical dimensions into one balanced personality.

لَّيْسَ ٱلْبِرَّ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ (Righteousness is not that you turn your faces toward the east or west). Allah ﷻ has mentioned this multiple times - the Qiblah is important, but it's not everything. Don't reduce religion to one or two things. Muslims commonly take something they're good at or particularly interested in and make their entire religion revolve around that one thing.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنْ ءَامَنَ (but righteousness is [in] one who believes). The grammatical structure is striking - "righteousness is the one who believed" - suggesting that righteousness becomes materialized and manifest in a person with these qualities.

The foundation is belief in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day, followed by three components: angels, scripture, and prophets. This isn't random - it reflects the three core elements of Islamic faith. First, belief in Allah ﷻ. Second, belief in the Hereafter. Third, belief in revelation, which has three parts: the message (scripture), the means of delivery (angels), and the certified teacher (prophets).

Understanding why these are mentioned in this specific order reveals something profound about righteousness. The number one motivation for doing good should be pleasing Allah ﷻ - that's the highest possible reason. But it's not the only legitimate reason. You might do good deeds because you want Paradise or avoid bad deeds because you fear Hell. These are lesser motivations, but they're still valid and recognized by Allah ﷻ.

Once you have these motivations (pleasing Allah ﷻ and hoping for the Hereafter), the question arises: How do I know what good deeds are? The answer: by following what Allah ﷻ brings in the Book, delivered by angels, to the prophets.

Notice that the ayah mentions ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (the Book) in singular form. Later in the Surah, we'll find it in plural (الكتب - the books). Why singular here? Because the Jews are being addressed. The message is clear: now the only way to righteousness is through this Book, the Quran. That option of following previous scriptures is closed. By using the singular, Allah ﷻ eliminates any ambiguity - they can't say, "Our book is good too, so we'll just keep following ours."

Now comes the ethical dimension: وَءَاتَى ٱلْمَالَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِۦ (and gives wealth, despite love for it). Everyone loves their money - it takes hard work to earn. Spending it is difficult. But true righteousness means fighting against your natural attachment to wealth and giving anyway.

The categories of recipients reveal deep wisdom:

ذَوِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ (those who possess close relations) - this comes first and is perhaps the hardest. Your closest relationships are often the ones you fight with most. Family members, cousins, uncles - these relationships come with drama and conflict. Giving money is already hard because it's yours. But giving it to family members you might be feuding with? That's double difficulty. Yet Allah ﷻ has wisdom in making them your family. There's a reason He chose these specific people to be your relatives.

وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ (the orphans) - but how do you know who the orphans are unless you're connected with your community? Nobody wears a sign declaring they're an orphan. Only through genuine community engagement do we learn who needs help.

وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ (those in need) - again, we can only help them if we know who they are. Later in the Surah, Allah ﷻ will say about this group: لَا يَسْـَٔلُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ إِلْحَافًا (they don't beg from people persistently). They don't wrap themselves around people at airports or bus stops begging for money. Just because they don't beg doesn't mean they're not in desperate need. But we won't know they need help unless we're genuinely connected to our community.

وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (the traveler) - when you truly know your community, you can identify when someone is traveling through and needs help. We should compete with each other to host guests, recognizing that hospitality is a tremendous honor from Allah ﷻ. Instead, when a cousin says they're coming to visit, we immediately email them a list of local hotels. Before they can even ask to stay over, we've already directed them elsewhere because we don't want to be inconvenienced.

Modern Muslims have largely abandoned the Islamic culture of hospitality. We've made it weird and uncomfortable. People invite scholars to their homes not out of pure hospitality but to collect photos and stories to share with others. They'll invite guests over then call all their friends: "He's staying at our house - come over if you want to meet him!" This completely misses the point of dignified hospitality.

وَٱلسَّآئِلِينَ (those who ask) - of course, when we think of charity, we first think of those who ask. But Allah ﷻ mentions them toward the end, teaching us that righteousness means identifying and helping those who don't ask even before helping those who do.

وَفِى ٱلرِّقَابِ (and for freeing slaves/those in bondage) - those who are imprisoned or enslaved need to be helped toward freedom. In modern times, invisible chains include crushing debt - mortgages, student loans, business debt. People are literally drowning, choking under financial burden. If you know people in these situations, helping free them from this modern slavery is part of righteousness.

Notice the structure: Allah ﷻ mentions faith first as the foundation of the building - you don't even see a foundation when looking at a building. The first visible floor is charitable giving despite love of wealth. The second floor built on top of that is وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ (establishing prayer and giving zakah).

This is remarkable. When we think of a good Muslim, what comes to mind first? Prayer and zakah. But Allah ﷻ dedicates a long portion of this ayah to charitable giving and benevolence, then mentions zakah separately. The message: zakah itself is mandatory charity - paying it doesn't make you righteous, it makes you law-abiding. If you don't pay it, you're a criminal. True righteousness means going above and beyond the minimum requirement.

There's also a crucial distinction between صلى (he prayed) and أَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ (he established the prayer). The latter means prayer becomes an institution in your life. Your life revolves around it. Just as furniture in a house must be arranged around structural pillars, everything in the righteous person's life - schedules, appointments, work, sleep - must fit around Salah. Salah doesn't get squeezed into your schedule; your schedule is built around Salah.

What about zakah? By giving what purifies his wealth, the rest of his wealth - the portion he keeps and consumes - becomes pure.

Now Allah ﷻ shifts to grammatical forms indicating permanent qualities: وَٱلْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ (and those who fulfill their promise when they promise). These are people who always, consistently fulfill their commitments and agreements.

This includes our employment contracts. Students who come to programs with community or individual sponsorships have entered an agreement - a trust. You should sacrifice sleep and entertainment until you've understood the lesson because you've been entrusted with something. Our jobs are trusts. Our citizenship is a trust. Our neighbors trust us. When you're driving, you literally hold your life and others' lives in your hands - that's a trust. Your driver's license is an agreement to abide by certain laws.

Many Muslims pray abundantly but show no integrity in these daily trusts. They take a 9-to-5 job but work 9-to-3, spending the rest of the time watching videos or using the office copier for personal flyers. What kind of trust is that? This is a violation of Islamic ethics regardless of how much you pray.

Then comes the climax: وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (and the patient). The grammatical shift here (from المُوفُون to الصَّابِرِينَ) is significant - this special construction elevates this quality above everything else. It's like when my wife sends me to the grocery store: "Get milk, eggs, cereal, and... DIAPERS." The way she emphasizes diapers means without milk we'll be fine, without bread things will be okay, but don't forget the diapers!

Why does patience receive this special emphasis? Because without persistence and consistency, everything else becomes seasonal. You might have faith for a while, then lose it. You might establish prayer for months, then become lax. You might fulfill promises for years, then become unreliable. What holds all these qualities together? Sabr - persistence, consistency, constancy.

The ayah specifies three contexts for this patience: فِى ٱلْبَأْسَآءِ (in poverty and hardship), وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ (in sickness and adversity), وَحِينَ ٱلْبَأْسِ (and in times of battle/war). Even when times are tough - financially, physically, or in the midst of conflict - they maintain their commitment to faith. They don't let go.

The conclusion is powerful: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ (those are the ones who have been true). They've proven that they really are good. وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ (and it is those who are the people of taqwa).

By declaring that people of true righteousness are the people of taqwa, and remembering that this Surah began by saying the Quran is guidance لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (for those who have taqwa), the circle completes. This comprehensive definition of righteousness is what taqwa actually looks like in practice. The people of goodness are the people of taqwa - that's who they are.

translation: O you who have believed, prescribed for you is legal retribution for those murdered - the free for the free, the slave for the slave, and the female for the female. But whoever overlooks from his brother anything, then there should be a suitable follow-up and payment to him with good conduct. This is an alleviation from your Lord and a mercy. But whoever transgresses after that will have a painful punishment
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِصَاصُ فِى ٱلْقَتْلَى ۖ ٱلْحُرُّ بِٱلْحُرِّ وَٱلْعَبْدُ بِٱلْعَبْدِ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰ بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ ۚ فَمَنْ عُفِىَ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَخِيهِ شَىْءٌ فَٱتِّبَاعٌۢ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَأَدَآءٌ إِلَيْهِ بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ تَخْفِيفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ ۗ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

Now that righteousness has been comprehensively defined, Allah ﷻ can properly contextualize His injunctions. Everything has its proper place now. We have a sense of priority.

كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِصَاصُ فِى ٱلْقَتْلَى (Prescribed for you is legal retribution for the murdered). The phrase sounds straightforward, but the details reveal profound justice.

ٱلْحُرُّ بِٱلْحُرِّ وَٱلْعَبْدُ بِٱلْعَبْدِ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰ بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ - the free for the free, the slave for the slave, the female for the female. This seems confusing at first. Does it mean if one freeman was killed in that tribe, we kill any random freeman from your tribe? If one slave was killed here, we take any random slave from there?

No. Notice the structure: الحُرُّ بِالحُرِّ (the free with the free), العَبْدُ بِالعَبْدِ (the slave with the slave), الأُنثَىٰ بِالأُنثَىٰ (the female with the female). The construction means: the same free person, the same slave, the same female. It's establishing that the murderer themselves must face justice - they cannot substitute someone else.

A free man who commits murder cannot say, "Take my slave and execute him instead." He must answer for his own crime. A slave who commits murder - the master cannot say, "I like this slave, take a different one." That specific slave must face justice. A woman cannot say, "I'm just a girl, take one of my servants instead." She bears personal responsibility.

This principle is revolutionary: everyone is personally responsible for their crimes and cannot offset that responsibility to someone else.

When a murder occurs, Islamic law provides three options, with the family of the victim holding decision-making power:

1. **Qisas (Legal Retribution)**: The family can demand that the murderer be executed. One life was taken; they have the right to ask the court to take the murderer's life.

2. **Diyah (Blood Money)**: They can pursue financial compensation through a lawsuit. The murderer was perhaps the provider for his family, and they've suffered damages. They can seek financial restitution.

3. **'Afw (Pardon)**: The family has the right to forgive the criminal completely, asking for nothing.

The court makes the legal determination of guilt, but once guilt is established, the sentencing power rests with the victim's family. Compare this to Western legal systems without revealing its Islamic origin. Go to any family of a murder victim and ask: "Would you prefer a system where, after the guilty verdict, you have the right to decide whether the person should be executed, whether you want to sue them, or whether you want to forgive them completely? Or would you prefer the current system where the state decides and the criminal gets a prison sentence, maybe released early for good behavior?"

What would any human being prefer? Any family of any victim would choose the former system. They would embrace Shariah law - as long as you don't call it that. Families of murder victims often have no sense of closure or peace because the perpetrator is still living, eating free meals in prison. They protest and nothing comes of it. They hold press releases saying, "This isn't justice." There's no peace for them because the so-called justice system provides no actual justice.

Now you might think Islam emphasizes forgiveness, so perhaps Allah ﷻ would constantly encourage us to forgive. Let's see what He actually says.

فَمَنْ عُفِىَ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَخِيهِ شَىْءٌ (But whoever overlooks from his brother anything) - if something is pardoned, if the family forgives - فَٱتِّبَاعٌۢ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ (then there should be a following in a decent manner). Once the family has forgiven, others shouldn't interfere saying, "He shouldn't get away with murder, we should kill him anyway." No. The family had the right to decide, they've made their decision to forgive, now everyone else should respect that and follow it in a proper way.

وَأَدَآءٌ إِلَيْهِ بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ (and payment to them in excellence). If they requested blood money instead, it should be delivered in the best possible manner - not begrudgingly or with difficulty.

ذَٰلِكَ تَخْفِيفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ (That is a lightening from your Lord and a mercy). The verdict could have been strictly an eye for an eye, a person for a person, with no other option. But Allah ﷻ made financial compensation and forgiveness available as options - that's the lightening of the burden, that's the mercy.

فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ (But whoever transgresses after that) - whoever doesn't respect this law and still violates it - فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (will have a painful punishment).

translation: And there is for you in legal retribution [saving of] life, O you [people] of understanding, that you may become righteous
commentary:
وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْقِصَاصِ حَيَوٰةٌ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ

"And there is for you in legal retribution life, O you of understanding, that you may become righteous."

This statement seems paradoxical: in taking a life, there is life? How does killing preserve life?

The wisdom is societal. If society constantly, automatically forgave murderers, what would happen? Murderers would receive a free pass. If the norm became simply fining murderers - say $100,000 - then wealthy people could effectively purchase licenses to kill. "I hate that guy. What will it cost me? Oh, I can afford it."

When criminals feel they can get away with murder without risking their own lives, the license to kill opens up. Society becomes dangerous for everyone. But when the law maintains qisas as a real possibility - when a murderer knows his own life hangs in the balance - lives are protected. Potential murderers think twice. The preservation of innocent life comes through the possibility of taking the guilty murderer's life.

وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْقِصَاصِ حَيَوٰةٌ - for you, there is preservation of life in retribution.

Now here's the nuance: individually, we're supposed to be forgiving people. But as a society, we must sometimes execute punishment to keep society in order, to keep it in check. Society needs the deterrent of serious consequences.

يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (O people of understanding) - those with pure minds, those who think deeply, will grasp this. لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (so that you may protect yourselves). Here, taqwa isn't in the spiritual sense alone - it's physical and literal. So you may protect yourselves in society. You establish justice so people can be safe.

translation: Prescribed for you when death approaches [any] one of you if he leaves wealth [is that he should make] a bequest for the parents and near relatives according to what is acceptable - a duty upon the righteous
commentary:
كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا ٱلْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

"Prescribed for you when death approaches one of you - if he leaves wealth - is that he makes a bequest to parents and near relatives according to what is acceptable, a duty upon those who have taqwa."

Before discussing wealth distribution, there's something even more important. Remember when the Quran mentioned death presenting itself to Ya'qub (AS)? What did he leave for his family? A question: "What will you worship after I'm gone?"

Before you think about which child gets the business, which relative gets the car, who receives what asset - what advice are you leaving your family? When a person is on their deathbed, their final words become forever imprinted on people's minds. You never forget them. Ya'qub (AS) used that sacred moment to ask the most important question possible: What will you worship after I'm gone?

This is the first hint already given earlier in the Surah. Now comes the second instruction: إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا (if he leaves wealth) - leave something good. ٱلْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ (make a bequest to parents and near relatives). بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ (according to what is acceptable) - what is decent and appropriate by societal standards.

حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُتَّقُينَ (a duty upon those who have taqwa) - this is an expectation from people of taqwa. Notice that Allah ﷻ hasn't specified exact proportions yet - that will come later in Surah An-Nisa. For now, He's left it general: leave something good for parents and close relatives, in decent amounts, as is expected from people of taqwa.

translation: Then whoever alters the bequest after he has heard it - the sin is only upon those who have altered it. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
فَمَنۢ بَدَّلَهُۥ بَعْدَ مَا سَمِعَهُۥ فَإِنَّمَآ إِثْمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

Not everyone is present when a person makes their final pronouncements. Perhaps someone was there who heard the dying person say, "My assets should be distributed this way." Now that person becomes the ambassador for the deceased - the dead can't speak for themselves anymore. Maybe they didn't leave a written will; they just spoke it. This lone witness is the only person who knows what was said.

فَمَنۢ بَدَّلَهُۥ بَعْدَ مَا سَمِعَهُۥ (Then whoever alters it after he has heard it) - if this witness changes what they heard - فَإِنَّمَآ إِثْمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُۥٓ (the sin is only upon those who alter it). Those who change it will carry the sin. They might think they're the only ones who know, but they're forgetting something crucial.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Hearing and Knowing). The deceased might not be able to speak anymore, and other humans might not have heard, but Allah ﷻ certainly heard. He knows exactly what was said, and He knows what you're changing. The sin of that alteration will be yours to bear on Judgment Day.

translation: But if one fears from the bequeather [some] error or sin and corrects that which is between them, there is no sin upon him. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
فَمَنْ خَافَ مِن مُّوصٍ جَنَفًا أَوْ إِثْمًا فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

But there's an exception to this rule. فَمَنْ خَافَ مِن مُّوصٍ جَنَفًا (But if one fears from the bequeather injustice) - if you fear the person making the will is being partial or biased - أَوْ إِثْمًا (or sin) - or you fear they're about to commit a sin in their will.

Imagine someone on their deathbed saying, "The rest of my money should go to buy beer for everyone in town" or something equally absurd. Or they're being grossly unfair: "Give everything to this one son and nothing to the others" without legitimate reason.

فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ (and one puts things right between them) - if someone intervenes and makes reconciliation, divides things fairly, removes the injustice, corrects the sinful element - فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ (there is no sin upon him).

This is the one case where changing a corrupt or unjust will is not only permissible but praiseworthy. You're not carrying sin; you're preventing it. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful) - He understands the wisdom in correcting an unjust will, and there's mercy in allowing this intervention.

translation: O you who have believed, decreed upon you is fasting as it was decreed upon those before you that you may become righteous
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلصِّيَامُ كَمَا كُتِبَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ

"O you who have believed, fasting has been prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you, that you may become righteous."

These are the early days of fasting in Islamic history. كَمَا كُتِبَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ (as it was prescribed for those before you) - initially, Muslims fasted on the same days the Jews fasted. The days were identical. That's why Allah ﷻ says "just as it was prescribed for those who came before you" - because at this stage, it was exactly like theirs.

But notice what Allah ﷻ emphasizes as the purpose of fasting: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (so that you may attain taqwa). The purpose of fasting is to develop taqwa. Remember this, because we're about to see that the purpose of Ramadan is different from the purpose of fasting. For us, they've become mixed together - we think of them as the same thing. But Allah ﷻ distinguishes between them.

translation: [Fasting for] a limited number of days. So whoever among you is ill or on a journey [during them] - then an equal number of days [are to be made up]. And upon those who are able [to fast, but with hardship] - a ransom [as substitute] of feeding a poor person [each day]. And whoever volunteers excess - it is better for him. But to fast is best for you, if you only knew
commentary:
أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُطِيقُونَهُۥ فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ ۖ فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَن تَصُومُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ - just a handful of numbered days. The phrase أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ (ayyaman ma'dudat) uses the feminine plural form, which in Arabic grammar indicates a very small number - less than ten days. These were the three middle days of every lunar month that early Muslims fasted before Ramadan was instituted.

فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ (But whoever among you is ill or on a journey) - if you miss fasting those days due to illness or travel - فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ (then an equal number of other days). You should make them up later.

وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُطِيقُونَهُۥ فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ (And upon those who are able, a ransom of feeding a poor person). In these early laws of fasting, if you missed a fast, you had two options: make it up by fasting, or if you have the financial means, you can pay fidyah by feeding a poor person. Two ways to compensate for a missed fast.

فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ (And whoever volunteers good, it is better for him) - if you both make up the fast and pay to feed a poor person, congratulations, that's even better.

وَأَن تَصُومُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ (But to fast is better for you) - if you were to fast to make up the missed days instead of just paying fidyah, it would be better for you. إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (if you only knew) - if you truly understand what's good for you, you'll make it up by fasting.

This was the flexibility Allah ﷻ provided in the early laws. Now watch what happens in the next ayah with Ramadan.

translation: The month of Ramadhan [is that] in which was revealed the Qur'an, a guidance for the people and clear proofs of guidance and criterion. So whoever sights [the new moon of] the month, let him fast it; and whoever is ill or on a journey - then an equal number of other days. Allah intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship and [wants] for you to complete the period and to glorify Allah for that [to] which He has guided you; and perhaps you will be grateful
commentary:
شَهْرُ رَمَضَانَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ فِيهِ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْفُرْقَانِ ۚ فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ ٱلشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ مَرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۗ يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُمُ ٱلْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ ٱلْعُسْرَ وَلِتُكْمِلُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ وَلِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

شَهْرُ رَمَضَانَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ فِيهِ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ (The month of Ramadan in which the Quran was revealed). When we think of Ramadan, we think of the month of fasting. Allah ﷻ wants us to think of it as the month in which the Quran came down. It's the month of Quran, not primarily the month of fasting.

The previous ayah discussed the purpose of fasting - remember what it was? لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (so you may attain taqwa). The purpose of Ramadan, as we'll see, is different. For most of us, they're the same thing - when we think of Ramadan, we think of samosas, dates, and French fries. That's what dominates our thoughts. Most people don't even lose weight in Ramadan. But Allah ﷻ paints a different picture.

First, think of Ramadan as the month of Quran, not fasting. The month in which the Quran was sent down. هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ (as guidance for the people) - and here's something that would have deeply offended the Jews. They fasted on certain days, and they were already offended when Muslims changed the direction of prayer. Now they're even more offended because Muslims are fasting in a different month. Why are we fasting in a different month? Because we're celebrating a different revelation - we have the Quran, and Ramadan is when it was revealed.

Then Allah ﷻ goes further: this isn't just guidance for a select nation. This is هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ (guidance for all mankind). The Jews believed their guidance was exclusively for themselves. This is another challenge to their assumptions.

وَبَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْفُرْقَانِ (and clear proofs of guidance and criterion). The Quran contains clear proofs that it is guidance, and it is the furqan - the distinguisher between right and wrong. It has exposed the corruptions they committed.

Celebrate the Quran, because you are a new nation now. Celebrate it in your own month. Ramadan is a celebration of Muslim identity as people of the Quran. We don't typically think of it this way, but that's exactly what it is. The two things that made us distinctly different were the change of Qiblah and the change of our fasting month. These are markers of our unique identity.

فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ ٱلشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ (So whoever witnesses the month, let him fast it). Before, how many days were there? Three days each month - أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ (a few numbered days). Now, whoever witnesses the month must fast the entire month. How many days is that? The whole month - approximately thirty days.

Is this harder or easier? It's already harder because it's more days. That's step one - it got tougher.

Step two: وَمَن كَانَ مَرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ (But whoever is ill or on a journey, then an equal number of other days). Before, you had two options: make it up by fasting, or pay fidyah by feeding a poor person. Now there's only one option - you must make it up. You can't just pay someone.

Having two options is easier than having one. So this became harder in two ways: it's thirty days instead of a few, and you only have one option for making up missed fasts instead of two.

You'd think a believer listening to this would say, "Wow, this just got harder." And the very next words are: يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُمُ ٱلْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ ٱلْعُسْرَ (Allah ﷻ intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship).

How remarkable is that? First, it appears He made it harder, then immediately He says, "Actually, I did this because I want ease for you and I don't want difficulty for you."

How do we understand this? Remember the purpose of fasting: to build taqwa. Taqwa isn't something easily attained - it requires effort and discipline. But here's the connection: this Book is هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (guidance for those who have taqwa). The more you fast, the more taqwa you develop. The more taqwa you have, the more ready you are to receive guidance from the Quran.

Allah ﷻ wants guidance to come to you easily. For guidance to come easily, you need strong taqwa. To get strong taqwa, you need to fast more. So you fast during the day, building your taqwa. Then you listen to Quran at night, and it becomes easier for you to absorb because your taqwa has been elevated. Without proper taqwa, you won't be able to process this guidance effectively.

Allah ﷻ wants true ease for you - not just temporary comfort here, but eternal ease. He doesn't want difficulty for you anywhere, not here and not in the Hereafter. People who develop taqwa and receive guidance are promised a life of ease both in this world and the next. That's why He made it more rigorous - so you can truly become people of taqwa. Through that, He has made access to guidance easier. In the month of guidance, the month of the Quran, He's given you the tools to actually benefit from it.

وَلِتُكْمِلُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ (And so you may complete the period) - He gave you this so you can complete the count of days. وَلِت

translation: And when My servants ask you, [O Muhammad], concerning Me - indeed I am near. I respond to the invocation of the supplicant when he calls upon Me. So let them respond to Me [by obedience] and believe in Me that they may be [rightly] guided
commentary:
وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِى عَنِّى فَإِنِّى قَرِيبٌ ۖ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ ٱلدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ ۖ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِى وَلْيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِى لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ

Before we examine this ayah, we need to understand how it connects with what came before. If you go back to ayah 177, Allah ﷻ presented us with a comprehensive concept of goodness. At the end of that discussion, He ﷻ said: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ – "Those are the ones who have confirmed the truth, and those are indeed the people of Taqwa." This same term was used at the beginning of the Surah, where the Quran is described as guidance for the people who possess Taqwa, those who actually protect themselves.

That ayah began at the individual level with Iman, which is a very individual concern. Then it moved to giving your wealth against your love of money – again, an individual matter. After that came establishing Salah and giving Zakah, which are collective affairs but remain essentially individual responsibilities. Then fulfilling promises when they are made – and this is one of those very comprehensive statements that captures the essence of the entire religion. Islam is essentially a promise, an agreement between us and Allah ﷻ, between us and the Messenger ﷺ, between ourselves and the Book.

The section then moved to patience during times of hardship and battle. Sabr is a very individual test. You could be standing in a battlefield with a thousand other soldiers, but your patience is being tested individually. Where your heart stands may not be where the person next to you stands.

Overall, this concept of goodness and finally attaining Taqwa is an individual matter. But the Taqwa of an individual is different from the Taqwa of a society. The way a society protects itself and preserves its peace is something else. Allah ﷻ told us about the individual preserving their harmony, then immediately switched to the farthest extreme of the collective – society as a whole. The thing that can create the worst chaos in a society is murder, so Allah ﷻ addressed the laws of retribution.

After discussing what can disrupt societal harmony, the conversation moved to what can disrupt family harmony – inheritance disputes, especially after the death of someone holding the family together. Then finally, we come back to the individual with the discussion of fasting. Through all of this, the conversation of Taqwa never left – it remained at the individual level, the collective level, the family level, and back again to the individual.

Now we reach this remarkable ayah about dua. The word إِذَا when it comes in the past tense still means future tense, and it necessarily pushes the meaning to the future. So this means: "When my slaves ask you about Me" – whenever this happens in the future. Notice that Allah ﷻ didn't say "if they ask" but "when they ask," indicating this is certain to happen.

The slaves can ask about Allah ﷻ in all kinds of ways. They ask about what is halal and haram, what pleases Him ﷻ, what the best deeds are. And the comprehensive response to all of those questions is: فَإِنِّى قَرِيبٌ – "Tell them I am definitely near, I am definitely close."

Why is this the answer to all their questions? Because the one thing a believer needs to hold onto the lesson of Taqwa that has been taught in the previous ayat is to remember that Allah ﷻ is near. Think about it with a simple example: if you're about to do something wrong, about to say something inappropriate, and someone says "The principal can hear you, he's right behind the door" – what happens? You immediately stop. Your tone changes. This is a form of Taqwa: knowing that the authority is near.

When you think the authority is far, you feel free to do as you please. Allah ﷻ is teaching about Himself: the thing that will make you remember and implement Taqwa is constantly remembering that, without a doubt, Allah ﷻ is near.

On one hand, this makes us conscious of Allah ﷻ. But on the other hand, His ﷻ being close is an opportunity, because when someone is close, you're in a better position to communicate with them. This changes that first factor of awareness from something scary to something filled with opportunity.

أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ ٱلدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ – "I respond to the call of the caller when he calls Me." The word أُجِيبُ comes from إِجَابَة, which means to respond immediately. This is powerful – Allah ﷻ responds right away.

فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِى – "Then they should try to respond to Me." Allah ﷻ asks for their response in return. They should at least make an attempt to answer His ﷻ demands.

وَلْيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِى – "And they should believe in Me." This phrase also implies they should trust Allah ﷻ. When they make dua, they should actually believe that He ﷻ is listening. They should actually believe that He ﷻ received it and understands what they're going through.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ – "So that they may be set straight." This is the ultimate purpose – that through this relationship of nearness, calling, and responding, they become rightly guided.

translation: It has been made permissible for you the night preceding fasting to go to your wives [for sexual relations]. They are clothing for you and you are clothing for them. Allah knows that you used to deceive yourselves, so He accepted your repentance and forgave you. So now, have relations with them and seek that which Allah has decreed for you. And eat and drink until the white thread of dawn becomes distinct to you from the black thread [of night]. Then complete the fast until the sunset. And do not have relations with them as long as you are staying for worship in the mosques. These are the limits [set by] Allah, so do not approach them. Thus does Allah make clear His ordinances to the people that they may become righteous
commentary:
أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ لَيْلَةَ ٱلصِّيَامِ ٱلرَّفَثُ إِلَىٰ نِسَآئِكُمْ ۚ هُنَّ لِبَاسٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لِبَاسٌ لَّهُنَّ ۗ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَعَفَا عَنكُمْ ۖ فَٱلْـَٔـٰنَ بَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۚ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمُ ٱلْخَيْطُ ٱلْأَبْيَضُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْطِ ٱلْأَسْوَدِ مِنَ ٱلْفَجْرِ ۖ ثُمَّ أَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلصِّيَامَ إِلَى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُبَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَأَنتُمْ عَـٰكِفُونَ فِى ٱلْمَسَـٰجِدِ ۗ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَقْرَبُوهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ

We're still in the discussion of fasting, but we need some important context. Before diving into the ayah, let me share something about the Arabic word صَوْم itself. There are other words used in Arabic for fasting, and later in the Quran we'll encounter them. But this particular word was used by the Arabs before Islam for a very specific purpose: training horses for battle.

The camel is created for the desert environment and can survive a long time without water. But you can't take a camel into battle – it's too slow, can't attack quickly, can't escape fast enough. Horses were the preferred vehicle in battles, but they don't last in the heat and can dehydrate quickly. So the Arabs had a way of training their horses to survive the heat, and they called that training صَوْم.

When the old Arabic listener heard the word صَوْم, the first thought that crossed their mind was: getting ready for battle, preparing the horses. Now Allah ﷻ says He has mandated صِيَام upon you. The first thought that crosses the mind is: "Now I'm the horse. I'm the owned property that is under training, under Allah's command, preparing for battle." This is significant because these ayat are precursors to the Battle of Badr, and the believers are actually being mentally prepared for battle and tough times through the ayat on fasting.

Now to the specific rulings. At the time of revelation, the Jews – the followers of Musa (AS) – also had fasting, but their rulings were different. We don't know if this was originally from Musa (AS) or their own altered form, but their established practice was that fasting began at sunset and continued the entire night until the next day – basically one meal and then almost 24 hours of fasting. During that state, they couldn't have any relations with their wives throughout the night.

Allah ﷻ made the Islamic fasting distinct not just in the days we fast, but also in its form and laws. He ﷻ says: أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ لَيْلَةَ ٱلصِّيَامِ ٱلرَّفَثُ إِلَىٰ نِسَآئِكُمْ – "It has been made halal for you during the nights of the fast to have intimacy with your women."

Then comes a beautiful, comprehensive expression: هُنَّ لِبَاسٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لِبَاسٌ لَّهُنَّ – "They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them." This phrase captures the essence of marriage in the most profound way.

What does it mean that your spouse is like clothing for you? Just as clothing makes you presentable to the outside world, your spouse makes your life beautiful and complete. Just as we don't go into the world without our clothing, our clothing is an essential part of our dignity – so too is our spouse an essential part of our dignity. They become a part of us, we become a part of them. You were part of a whole.

Clothing beautifies you, and your spouse is supposed to make you a better person, to beautify your personality. Clothing protects you from the elements of weather, and a healthy marriage protects you from fitnah, from temptation from the outside, from all kinds of problems. It gives you peace and tranquility that you cannot experience otherwise.

عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ – "Allah knew that you were cheating yourselves." Some new Muslims among the Sahaba (RA) had been Jews or Christians before. In their tradition, fasting went on the entire night, so they assumed Islamic fasting must be the same. They thought they weren't supposed to be close to their wives in the evening. But knowing they shouldn't – or thinking they shouldn't – they still went ahead and had relations with their wives.

Allah ﷻ says: "I already knew you were cheating yourselves." But here's the question: it wasn't actually a sin because Allah ﷻ says it's halal. So why would this be considered something requiring forgiveness? The answer: to the best of their knowledge, they shouldn't have done it. To the best of their knowledge, they were cheating. Allah ﷻ judges not just what we do, but what's in our hearts. In their hearts, they were actually being unfair to themselves, circumventing what they thought was the law.

فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَعَفَا عَنكُمْ – "So He turned to you in repentance and pardoned you." Not only did He ﷻ accept their repentance, but He ﷻ pardoned them lovingly. عَفَا is a loving kind of pardon – "Don't worry about it, it's no big deal. Clean slate. Start over."

فَٱلْـَٔـٰنَ بَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ – "Now you can enjoy their company." وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ – "And pursue what Allah has written for you," meaning what Allah ﷻ has made lawful for you. There's no harm in it anymore.

Someone might try to be more spiritual than the injunctions of Allah's Book and the Sunnah of His Messenger ﷺ and say, "Ramadan is a spiritual month and this seems like such a worldly thing." But that's not the Islamic perspective. Allah ﷻ always keeps the balance between deen and dunya.

وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ – "Go and eat and drink." The original Sahaba (RA) were so zealous that they would go overboard in their worship. Allah ﷻ had to tell them: take it easy. You need to eat and drink.

حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمُ ٱلْخَيْطُ ٱلْأَبْيَضُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْطِ ٱلْأَسْوَدِ مِنَ ٱلْفَجْرِ – "Until the white thread becomes distinct from the black thread of dawn." Eat until the horizon becomes clear, meaning the Fajr time is clearly in.

ثُمَّ أَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلصِّيَامَ إِلَى ٱلَّيْلِ – "Then complete the fasting until the night." For Ahlus Sunnah, we understand this to mean Maghrib. وَلَا تُبَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَأَنتُمْ عَـٰكِفُونَ فِى ٱلْمَسَـٰجِدِ – "And do not have intimacy with them while you are in I'tikaf in the masajid." Yes, in the evenings certain things have been made available, except if you're in I'tikaf – then it's completely forbidden.

تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَقْرَبُوهَا – "Those are the limits of Allah, so do not approach them." These are the boundaries prescribed by Allah ﷻ, revealed from on high. Don't go near them.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ – "That is how Allah clarifies His miraculous signs for people." Notice Allah ﷻ didn't say الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (those who believe) here – He ﷻ said النَّاسِ (the people). Even though the previous ayah was about the believers, at the end Allah ﷻ says He's clarifying these ayat for all people. This is how you're supposed to fast. He's ﷻ even clarifying it for the People of the Book.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ – "So that they can become people of Taqwa." So they can become cautious and protective of themselves from disappointing Allah ﷻ.

translation: And do not consume one another's wealth unjustly or send it [in bribery] to the rulers in order that [they might aid] you [to] consume a portion of the wealth of the people in sin, while you know [it is unlawful]
commentary:
وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتُدْلُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَى ٱلْحُكَّامِ لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنْ أَمْوَٰلِ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

This next ayah seems like a departure from the subject. Between discussions of fasting, suddenly Allah ﷻ starts talking about business practices and justice in business dealings. Why?

Because this is the first and immediate litmus test of Taqwa. If you really have Taqwa, if you've gained it, where should it manifest? In how you deal with people. It's easy when you're inside the masjid – what are you going to do there? Pray. That's what you do. Taqwa is tested when you're away from the remembrance of Allah ﷻ, when you're dealing with people, when temptation is in front of you.

There's no temptation in front of you when you're in I'tikaf. You're in an environment where you're not tempted. Temptation comes when the cash is right there in your cashier and there's an opportunity for you to skim some of it, knowing the boss will never find out because the security camera isn't working. Temptation is when you can go late to work because the manager is away on call somewhere and your friend can punch in for you. That's where Taqwa is tested. That's where the real battle is.

وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ – "Do not consume your wealth among yourselves using falsehood." Don't con each other, scam each other, cheat each other, steal from each other, or shortchange each other.

This also means: don't overprice things. When someone comes to you for an estimate – you're a painter, carpenter, mechanic – and business has been slow, so you think, "I'll give them a great price." It should cost $300, but you say $3,000 because the person doesn't know any better and you can scam them for it. They're desperate, so you can make extra money. Don't do that.

Then Allah ﷻ goes further: وَتُدْلُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَى ٱلْحُكَّامِ – "And dangle it towards the rulers." The word تُدْلُوا comes from دَلْو, which means to lower a bucket. This expression is like tying food or water in a bucket to a rope, leaving it there, and when an animal comes, you pull the bucket and the animal follows.

Don't dangle money in front of rulers to pull them toward yourself, to get closer to them. Don't pay those extra sums to go to the fundraising dinner for the senator so you can push your legislation for your company trying to set up a polluting factory in the state.

لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنْ أَمْوَٰلِ ٱلنَّاسِ – "So that you may consume a portion – meaning a huge chunk – from the wealth of the people." Why do you want to get close to the rulers? Because the rulers are responsible for maintaining justice among people. But you want to engage in business practices that are clearly going to violate people's rights and shortchange them. To do that under the ruler's nose, you need to grease some palms. You need to make sure he's happy with you and looks the other way.

بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ – "Using sin, while you know exactly what you're doing." Often when executives get caught, what do they claim? "I had no knowledge of what was going on in my company." They'll stand in front of investigations for hours and say, "I wasn't aware of this meeting. I don't recall. I don't know."

Allah ﷻ says: وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ – "But you are the ones who in fact know." You know exactly what you're doing.

This corruption in business practices existed back then and continues until now. These are manifestations of a lack of Taqwa. When you think nobody's watching, nobody's near, and the ones who are near you've already paid off, then you feel safe to spread all kinds of corruption in the earth.

It's powerful that Allah ﷻ put this right after the concept of fasting. We've taken such a powerful thing – Ramadan, something that identifies Muslims, something that's supposed to empower them to become people of justice according to the spirit of these ayat – and turned it into just a festival. We celebrate it and move on. We don't see ourselves as a people with a higher calling.

The Quran instills a certain kind of vision, a way of looking at the world, an attitude that becomes hard to get away from when you become a direct student of the Quran. It becomes hard for you to sell out, to look at the religion in a compartmentalized way.
