translation: They ask you, [O Muhammad], about the new moons. Say, "They are measurements of time for the people and for Hajj." And it is not righteousness to enter houses from the back, but righteousness is [in] one who fears Allah. And enter houses from their doors. And fear Allah that you may succeed
commentary:
۞ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَهِلَّةِ ۖ قُلْ هِىَ مَوَٰقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَٱلْحَجِّ ۗ وَلَيْسَ ٱلْبِرُّ بِأَن تَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِن ظُهُورِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ ۗ وَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبِهَا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَهِلَّةِ – "They ask you about the changing phases of the moon." When fasting was revealed, people had to pay attention to the moon, so they started asking about it. What about these phases? What's the benefit in them? How should we watch for them?

قُلْ هِىَ مَوَٰقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ – "Tell them: these phases of the moon are a means of people keeping track of time." The moon is essentially a calendar. Just as you hang a calendar on the wall, Allah ﷻ hung a calendar in the sky. It's a schedule for people – not just for religious purposes, but for business, travel, keeping dates in mind. Now we have printed calendars and mostly follow the solar calendar, but the principle is the same: we keep track of all our affairs this way. Classes start on this day, you're off that day, you have a reservation, an appointment.

But of all our schedules, Allah ﷻ mentioned one thing that should definitely be marked on your calendar: وَٱلْحَجِّ – "And particularly for Hajj."

This is significant. The conversation right before was about fasting – you'd expect Allah ﷻ to continue with that. But instead He ﷻ immediately switches to Hajj. This is early Madinan Quran. No battles between Muslims and disbelievers have yet taken place. And Allah ﷻ says: Hajj.

The Sahaba (RA) heard "Hajj" and thought: "Really? We're going to go to Hajj now? We need to look out for when Hajj season is, but how are we going to go? The enemy is there!" Allah ﷻ has already dropped a hint to the believers that their mission will be to make Hajj, and being able to make Hajj will be the same as Islam having victory. Because when you make Hajj, the idols will be destroyed. When the idols are destroyed, the mission is complete. The house of Ibrahim (AS) will be clean once again.

Now every time they look at the moon, they remember: you're on a mission. Time is ticking. The Sahaba (RA) started preparing, thinking maybe they'd go this very year because the ayah came.

Some Sahaba (RA) who were from different backgrounds before Islam – maybe they were mushrikeen, maybe nominal Christians – had certain practices mixed into their tradition. This is an interesting phenomenon: when different religions live together, they start borrowing from each other. You find a certain flavor of Christianity in Africa, a different one in Asia. Certain practices get mixed in.

One such practice existed in Madinah: people had reverence for Makkah, as all Arab tribes did. They had a tradition of Hajj with its own customs. One practice was: if you're going to Hajj from out of town and you've already left, it's bad luck to come back home if you forgot something. If you must come back, don't use the front door – that's a bad omen. Come through the window or back door to protect yourself from being cursed.

Allah ﷻ shatters this myth: وَلَيْسَ ٱلْبِرُّ بِأَن تَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِن ظُهُورِهَا – "There is no righteousness in coming into homes from their backs."

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ – "However, righteousness is obtained by whoever possesses Taqwa." Look at this summary. Allah ﷻ has already taught the lesson of Taqwa. That long ayah 177 began defining birr and ended with Taqwa. Here, in just two phrases, He ﷻ says: go back and refer to that. I've already told you where birr starts and ends. You don't need to find other things to include in goodness.

وَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبِهَا – "Approach the homes from their doors." It's beautiful that Allah ﷻ used this expression, because Arab poets used to say things like "I approach my goal from the front door" to mean doing things the right way. Doing things properly is called "coming to it from the front door."

So Allah ﷻ is actually teaching: from now on, whatever you do, follow proper procedure. Follow proper protocol. Look at what the Messenger ﷺ himself is doing, what Allah ﷻ has established as good and acceptable – that is good and acceptable. Don't get creative outside of that.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ – "And remain cautious of Allah so that all of you may attain success." Real success is not whether you escaped a bad omen. Real success is going to Hajj for the purpose of gaining Taqwa. Just as the purpose of fasting is Taqwa, the purpose of Hajj is also Taqwa.

translation: Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you but do not transgress. Indeed. Allah does not like transgressors
commentary:
وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ

وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ – "And fight in the path of Allah." Suddenly we switch again. We were just talking about the moon, Hajj, whether to come home from the front or back door, and now: fight in the path of Allah.

Why? Because Hajj is being held hostage. You need to free it first. You can't just go make Hajj – the Quraysh are holding the House of Allah ﷻ, the House built by Ibrahim (AS), hostage with their filth and idols. It needs to be cleansed. They're not going to walk over and say, "Okay, you can break the idols and make your Hajj." You're going to have to fight for it.

Let me provide some historical context. The Prophet ﷺ moves to Madinah, and for six months – which is no time at all – he works to stabilize Madinah as a society and community. Brotherhood between the Muhajirun and Ansar has been established. A communal structure has been established. The masjid is not just a place of prayer; it's the Senate, the house of governance.

Within those six months, the Prophet ﷺ starts sending scouts to Makkah to observe what the Quraysh are doing. They're not supposed to engage the enemy, just watch. Some scouts went out, and during their observations one group got discovered on the outskirts of Makkah. A fight broke out. The Muslims killed those who attacked them and returned.

When the Muslims came back, the Prophet ﷺ wasn't pleased with what happened, but it had happened. He ﷺ started preparing the believers that there would be a reaction. This news reached Makkah: one of our caravans was attacked, people were killed.

Within Makkah, there were two camps of disbelievers. On one hand were people saying, "Look, if Muhammad gains influence and becomes the dominant power in the region, what's so bad about that? He's Qurayshi after all." On the other hand were those saying, "No, he's against all our traditions. He's a rebel. We need to fight and annihilate this disease infecting our society."

Abu Jahl (RA) was on the side of "we need to get rid of Muslims." Abu Sufyan and others were saying, "Maybe we should watch this play out." When news of the caravan attack reached Makkah, who won the debate? Those who said, "See? We need to kill them. They're trying to kill us." This fired up the mushrikeen to amass an army of 1,000, beat the drums of war, and march toward Badr.

But let's analyze this from an outsider's perspective. There are people who lived in their homes, and for no other reason than what they believe, they have been tortured, beaten, and kicked out. Attempted murder has been committed against them. If they stayed, they would have been executed. So they escaped to another city. And now those same people are responding against the very people who expelled and tried to kill them.

Is this justifiable war? Absolutely. And even when it was justifiable, the Muslims didn't engage until they were discovered. The first violent acts were committed by those who expelled citizens from their own land.

These ayat are the precursor to Badr. The Sahaba (RA) would know: it's only been six months since the Prophet ﷺ arrived, and already there's talk of battles. The Prophet ﷺ is preparing an army. They need to know what they've walked into. This Islam is going to lead to conflict with the opposing forces. This is no walk in the park.

وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ – "Fight in the path of Allah those who are fighting you." Allah ﷻ establishes here that they're the ones engaged in violence first. The Muslims didn't draw first blood.

وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ – "And don't go beyond limits. Don't go overboard." When you see the enemy who's done wrong to you and your family, it's easy to lose your temper. But Allah ﷻ says: don't transgress. That's a natural thing – when war happens, all kinds of other crimes happen. Soldiers making their way into civilian territory commit heinous acts. This is going beyond limits. Don't do that.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ – "No doubt about it, Allah does not love those who transgress, who violate limits."

translation: And kill them wherever you overtake them and expel them from wherever they have expelled you, and fitnah is worse than killing. And do not fight them at al-Masjid al- Haram until they fight you there. But if they fight you, then kill them. Such is the recompense of the disbelievers
commentary:
وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَخْرِجُوهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ أَخْرَجُوكُمْ ۚ وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَشَدُّ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ حَتَّىٰ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِيهِ ۖ فَإِن قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ – "And kill them wherever you find them." This is very interesting because the language makes it seem like the small entity is the big one and the big one is small. Who's smaller in number? The believers. So you'd expect to say about the larger force "kill them wherever you find them," because you'll have to search for them. But the Muslims would have no trouble finding 1,000 people on the battlefield.

This is teaching: don't underestimate the little guy in battle. The fly can make the eye of the lion bleed and get away with it. Allah ﷻ is telling the Muslims: even though you're a huge number in comparison, hunt them out. Annihilate them.

And remember, the Muslims barely have weapons. They don't have enough swords for everyone. They have maybe eight horses, thirteen camels – small numbers. These aren't even people used to battle. The Makkans and Madinans are largely agricultural people.

When the call was made for Badr, the Prophet ﷺ asked, "What do you think? Should we go?" Abu Bakr (RA) stood up, Umar (RA) stood up, Uthman (RA) stood up. The Muhajirun stood up, and the Prophet ﷺ listened, pleased but quiet. The Ansar realized: "Oh, he can't order us to fight. He's checking to see if we volunteer." So the Ansar stood up and said, "We're not like the followers of Musa (AS). We're coming with you. We're with you all the way." This homogeneous spirit was created among the Muslims. Then Allah ﷻ gave them this pep talk on top of it: get them wherever you find them.

These are the ayat that are often quoted out of context. But we shouldn't waste our breath contextualizing for people who have no interest in context. We should be clear for ourselves and for those who sincerely ask.

وَأَخْرِجُوهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ أَخْرَجُوكُمْ – "And expel them from where they expelled you." Where did they kick you out from? Makkah. You're going to go get them in Makkah. It didn't look likely at the time, but Allah ﷻ said: you're going to do this.

وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَشَدُّ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ – "And fitnah is more intense, worse, more terrible than even murder." You think fighting them is terrible? Allah ﷻ is saying: what they're doing is more terrible.

Fitnah, religiously defined, is a situation where disobedience to Allah ﷻ becomes easier and obedience becomes harder. The kuffar have created a situation of fitnah with what they've done to the House of Allah ﷻ. Shirk has become easy and remaining on Tawheed has been made extremely hard. Even consuming an animal the way they slaughter – not slaughtered properly – has become difficult. Finding the haram has become easy, finding the halal difficult. That's fitnah by definition.

Allah ﷻ says fitnah is worse than fighting. Fighting in itself is not a good thing – Allah ﷻ acknowledges in this ayah that it is a bad thing. But it's the lesser of two evils. If you don't fight, there will be fitnah, and fitnah is worse.

وَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ حَتَّىٰ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِيهِ – "And don't fight them when you get to Al-Masjid Al-Haram until they fight you in it." Allah ﷻ is saying: you're going to make it all the way to the Haram. When you get there, don't fight them.

فَإِن قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ – "And if they were to fight you there, then kill them." Notice Allah ﷻ didn't say فَإِن قَاتَلُوكُمْ فَقَاتِلُوهُمْ (if they fight you, then fight them). He ﷻ said فَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ (then kill them). Fighting someone means you might win or might lose. Killing someone necessarily means you win. If you make it all the way to the Haram, it means they've already lost. All that's left is their destruction. This is very eloquent language from a strategic point of view.

كَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ – "That is how the compensation of the disbelievers works."

translation: And if they cease, then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ – "And if they were to stop." If they fought you all the way from the outskirts, and the battle continued, and they kept getting pushed back all the way inside Makkah, all the way inside Al-Masjid Al-Haram, and you were fighting, and suddenly they drop their arms and say "We give up" – then you stop too.

Even at that point, you can't say "No, we're not stopping. You did wrong, so we're not stopping." No. At that point, you better stop too. This is the height of discipline for an army. These Arabs only knew violence before. They didn't have disciplined armies. In an army, discipline is everything. Telling soldiers to start fighting is the easier part. Telling soldiers to stop fighting is the harder part. When the commander says "cease fire," it takes multiple commands before they stop, because in the heat of battle you can't stop yourself.

But Allah ﷻ created this discipline in the Muslim army: if they stop, that's it.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ – "Then certainly Allah is Extremely Forgiving, Always Merciful." Allah ﷻ is forgiving them. Are you going to come after someone that Allah ﷻ has forgiven?

And by the way, them stopping also means they've given up, they've surrendered. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ – Allah ﷻ is forgiving, so you should be forgiving too.

translation: Fight them until there is no [more] fitnah and [until] worship is [acknowledged to be] for Allah. But if they cease, then there is to be no aggression except against the oppressors
commentary:
وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ لِلَّهِ ۖ فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ – "And continue to fight them until there's no more fitnah." Once again, fitnah being a situation where disobedience is easier and obedience to Allah ﷻ is harder, where kufr is easier and Iman is harder.

وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ لِلَّهِ – "And the religion belongs to Allah." The way life runs belongs to Allah ﷻ. There should be no other deen left in the region, especially in Makkah.

فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ – "Then if they stop, there is no animosity at all except upon those who do wrong continually."

In other words: you cease fire, they say "we don't fight anymore" and drop their arms. You turn your back, and they pick up their weapons again. Those are zalimeen – they're treacherous. Yes, you can have animosity against them. Otherwise, there is no animosity left.

translation: [Fighting in] the sacred month is for [aggression committed in] the sacred month, and for [all] violations is legal retribution. So whoever has assaulted you, then assault him in the same way that he has assaulted you. And fear Allah and know that Allah is with those who fear Him
commentary:
ٱلشَّهْرُ ٱلْحَرَامُ بِٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَٱلْحُرُمَـٰتُ قِصَاصٌ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱعْتَدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

ٱلشَّهْرُ ٱلْحَرَامُ بِٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ – "The sacred month for the sacred month." The Arabs had four months in which fighting was forbidden – months dedicated for Hajj and Umrah. The mutual agreement among all Arabs was: we will not fight in these months.

Allah ﷻ is saying: if they violate that and come after you in one of those sacred months, then you can break that rule and respond. In Arabic they have an expression: هٰذَا بِذَاكَ – "This is for that," tit for tat. I'm doing this because you did that. But the one who started it is the real wrongdoer.

وَٱلْحُرُمَـٰتُ قِصَاصٌ – "And all sacred things are executed the same way." It's not just certain months that are haram. Certain places are haram too. You can't fight at Al-Masjid Al-Haram – that's from the hurumat. But if they fight there, then you can respond in that place. It becomes legitimate.

فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱعْتَدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ – "Then whoever violated against you, crossed limits against you, then advance against him, cross limits against him, in the same way that he crossed lines against you."

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ – "And even when you cross those lines and fight back, remain cautious of Allah ﷻ."

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ – "And you had better know that Allah is with the people who have Taqwa."

The thing that will give you victory in battle is that Allah ﷻ is with you. Allah ﷻ will no longer be with you if you lose your Taqwa. If your Taqwa is gone, so is your victory. Your victory depends on whether or not Allah ﷻ is with you. If your Taqwa gets lost in the heat of battle, you no longer have divine support.

translation: And spend in the way of Allah and do not throw [yourselves] with your [own] hands into destruction [by refraining]. And do good; indeed, Allah loves the doers of good
commentary:
وَأَنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تُلْقُوا۟ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى ٱلتَّهْلُكَةِ ۛ وَأَحْسِنُوٓا۟ ۛ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

وَأَنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ – "And spend in the path of Allah ﷻ." Now that battle is being discussed, what happens to a country going into war financially? It goes into financial deficit because you need money to prepare an army. It's the biggest chunk of any nation's budget, especially an army about to go into battle.

Now that Allah ﷻ has talked about the army, the next logical conversation is: what is the most immediate practical need to prepare that army? Spending in the path of Allah ﷻ.

وَلَا تُلْقُوا۟ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى ٱلتَّهْلُكَةِ – "And do not with your own hands consign yourselves to ruin." Some mufassirun explain this is imagery of a person standing at the brink of a cliff who extends his arms out and leans forward, about to fall into the river and drown. Don't be like that.

What Allah ﷻ is saying: spend in the path of Allah ﷻ and don't waste money anywhere else. Don't, by your stinginess, lead to your own destruction. If you become cheap now, it will show that you love dunya more than akhirah. The support of Allah ﷻ will be gone, and that will be the reason for your destruction. Don't get stingy at this time.

If you've internalized the previous lessons of Taqwa, of being ready to fight for the sake of Allah ﷻ – when you're ready to fight, you're ready to give your life up. Your money is an extension of yourself. You and I give up a part of our life to earn money. We don't play, we don't sleep – we give those things up to earn a living. People are naturally attached to their money because they gave up a part of their life for it.

Many people are in specialized professions, meaning they went into studies for many years. Not only are you giving time to your income now when you're working, you've been giving the best years of your youth. You want to hold on to that paycheck that came after so much sacrifice.

But Allah ﷻ is saying: you need to learn to let go. Just as you've learned to let go of your life for Allah's sake, letting go of money should be an easier step. How can it be that you're ready to fight for Allah's sake but you're still cheap? You're saying you yourself belong to Allah ﷻ, but your money is your own? How can that be?

وَأَحْسِنُوٓا۟ – "And when it comes to spending, excel. Do your best. Go out of your way to spend for the sake of Allah ﷻ."

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ – "Certainly Allah loves those who truly excel, who in fact go out of their way."

I'll tie this idea of ihsan to what Allah ﷻ said before in ayah 186: when the slave asks about Me, tell them I'm very near. The definition of ihsan is أَن تَعْبُدَ اللَّهَ كَأَنَّكَ تَرَاهُ – "That you worship Allah as if you can see Him." The idea of seeing someone is that they're close. فَإِن لَّمْ تَكُن تَرَاهُ فَإِنَّهُ يَرَاكَ – "And if you can't see Him, know that He sees you." And again, if He ﷻ can see you, that means He's ﷻ close.

If you really internalize the closeness of Allah ﷻ, you will become a muhsin. You'll know: Allah ﷻ is watching right now, He's ﷻ close. When I'm spending for His sake, it's going toward a worthy cause. He's ﷻ recorded it for me Himself.

translation: And complete the Hajj and 'umrah for Allah. But if you are prevented, then [offer] what can be obtained with ease of sacrificial animals. And do not shave your heads until the sacrificial animal has reached its place of slaughter. And whoever among you is ill or has an ailment of the head [making shaving necessary must offer] a ransom of fasting [three days] or charity or sacrifice. And when you are secure, then whoever performs 'umrah [during the Hajj months] followed by Hajj [offers] what can be obtained with ease of sacrificial animals. And whoever cannot find [or afford such an animal] - then a fast of three days during Hajj and of seven when you have returned [home]. Those are ten complete [days]. This is for those whose family is not in the area of al-Masjid al-Haram. And fear Allah and know that Allah is severe in penalty
commentary:
وَأَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلْحَجَّ وَٱلْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ ۚ فَإِنْ أُحْصِرْتُمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ ۖ وَلَا تَحْلِقُوا۟ رُءُوسَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ ٱلْهَدْىُ مَحِلَّهُۥ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ بِهِۦٓ أَذًى مِّن رَّأْسِهِۦ فَفِدْيَةٌ مِّن صِيَامٍ أَوْ صَدَقَةٍ أَوْ نُسُكٍ ۚ فَإِذَآ أَمِنتُمْ فَمَن تَمَتَّعَ بِٱلْعُمْرَةِ إِلَى ٱلْحَجِّ فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ ۚ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ وَسَبْعَةٍ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ ۗ تِلْكَ عَشَرَةٌ كَامِلَةٌ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن لَّمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُۥ حَاضِرِى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

Before exploring this ayah, it's important to understand the proper approach to studying Islamic law. Matters of fiqh and Sharia should be discussed by those who are truly qualified in these areas. Those of us with casual or informal knowledge should direct people to qualified scholars rather than offer our own interpretations. A little knowledge in this realm can quickly become problematic when it transforms into the only "right" opinion, especially when scholars far more knowledgeable see these matters with greater nuance than we do.

These lectures go through ayahs containing detailed legal rulings because they're part of the text we're studying, but they should never substitute for proper consultation with qualified scholars on matters of hajj, divorce, inheritance or other areas of Islamic law. Qualified scholars will be invited to provide dedicated sessions on these topics to supplement our study.

Allah ﷻ commands us: وَأَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلْحَجَّ وَٱلْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ - "Complete the hajj and Umrah for the sake of Allah." Once you've set out for hajj, you must complete it entirely for His sake. Understanding what this meant in the early Muslim context is crucial. For the Companions (RA), deciding "I'm going to hajj this year" wasn't about booking flights and hotel reservations nine months in advance. It meant beginning a life journey, often on foot, from places like Iraq. The journey itself could take nine months or longer, and there was no guarantee of return.

There's a powerful story about Imam Shafi'i (may Allah have mercy on him) who once encountered a man without legs dragging himself along the road. When asked where he was going, the man replied he was traveling to hajj. The Imam told him this wasn't even mandatory upon him due to his condition and the impossible distance. The man responded with poetry, one line of which captures everything: "As for the distance, my passion for hajj will make it closer." His love and yearning would bridge the gap.

Allah ﷻ then addresses potential obstacles: فَإِنْ أُحْصِرْتُمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ - "If you are prevented, then sacrifice whatever is easy to obtain from the sacrificial animal." In the context of early Islam, Muslims attempting hajj faced the real possibility of being surrounded and stopped by the Quraysh tribes who refused to let them near Makkah. The mushrikeen wouldn't necessarily kill them since even they respected the sacred months, but they would block their entry. If Muslims were prevented from completing hajj, they should sacrifice whatever animal they could easily find, even though ideally the sacrifice should occur within Makkah. An educated assumption could be made about when the animal would have reached its proper place, and then they could complete the rituals of exiting ihram.

The ayah continues with accommodations: فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ بِهِۦٓ أَذًى مِّن رَّأْسِهِۦ - "Whoever among you is sick or has an injury on his head." The word أَذًى here doesn't refer to a simple headache. It means a serious injury requiring treatment. Someone can't claim they turned back from hajj because they had a migraine and couldn't find extra-strength pain medication. This refers to a legitimate head wound that requires shaving the hair for medical treatment. If someone must shave early due to such an injury, the compensation (fidyah) can be fasting three days, feeding six poor people, or sacrificing an animal later.

Then Allah ﷻ addresses those who reach safety: فَإِذَآ أَمِنتُمْ فَمَن تَمَتَّعَ بِٱلْعُمْرَةِ إِلَى ٱلْحَجِّ - "When you are secure, whoever performs Umrah followed by hajj..." The word تَمَتَّعَ - "enjoys" or "takes benefit from" - is significant. Before Islam, performing Umrah and hajj in the same journey was forbidden. The Arabs had designated Rajab specifically for Umrah, while Dhul-Qa'dah, Dhul-Hijjah and Muharram were the months of hajj. Combining them was considered disrespectful.

Islam changed this, giving believers the gift of being able to perform both in one journey. Our modern mindset might be: "I'm already exhausted from hajj, and now I have to do Umrah too? That's me doing extra for Islam." But Allah ﷻ frames it completely differently - this is Him giving us an extra opportunity, an additional gift. He's turning our attitude around. The early Arabs understood: "You actually get to do Umrah with your hajj now, when before it was forbidden."

There are three types of hajj: Hajj al-Ifrad (singular hajj with one ihram only for hajj), Hajj al-Qiran (one ihram for both Umrah and hajj without removing it in between), and Hajj al-Tamattu (performing Umrah first, exiting ihram, then entering ihram again for hajj). 

For those who combine Umrah with hajj, فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ - "sacrifice whatever is easy from the animals." This sacrifice isn't expiation for a shortcoming; it's an expression of gratitude to Allah ﷻ for the opportunity to perform both rituals. 

فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ وَسَبْعَةٍ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ - "Whoever cannot find an animal should fast three days during hajj and seven when you return." These would be the 7th, 8th and 9th days of Dhul-Hijjah (not the 10th, which is Eid), and seven more days upon returning home. تِلْكَ عَشَرَةٌ كَامِلَةٌ - "That is ten complete days." 

These provisions apply to ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن لَّمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُۥ حَاضِرِى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ - "those whose family are not residents near the Sacred Mosque." The locals of Makkah must show additional respect by making separate journeys and don't receive the same dispensations as travelers.

The ayah concludes powerfully: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - "Have taqwa of Allah and know that Allah is severe in retribution." The word عِقَاب means taking revenge or retribution. Allah ﷻ is telling us to respect the rituals of hajj with absolute precision, because otherwise we won't just anger Him - we'll anger Him to the point where He will take serious retribution. This is His standard for Muslims trying their best to perform hajj correctly.

Now consider: if Allah ﷻ has such high expectations for believers striving to worship Him properly at His House, what level of rage must He have against those who placed idols in His House and committed shirk at His sacred sanctuary? This makes us realize the immense value Allah ﷻ places on His Sacred House.

translation: Hajj is [during] well-known months, so whoever has made Hajj obligatory upon himself therein [by entering the state of ihram], there is [to be for him] no sexual relations and no disobedience and no disputing during Hajj. And whatever good you do - Allah knows it. And take provisions, but indeed, the best provision is fear of Allah. And fear Me, O you of understanding
commentary:
ٱلْحَجُّ أَشْهُرٌ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٌ ۚ فَمَن فَرَضَ فِيهِنَّ ٱلْحَجَّ فَلَا رَفَثَ وَلَا فُسُوقَ وَلَا جِدَالَ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ ۗ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَتَزَوَّدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ ٱلزَّادِ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ وَٱتَّقُونِ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

Allah ﷻ establishes: ٱلْحَجُّ أَشْهُرٌ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٌ - "Hajj is in well-known months." This validates what the Arabs had understood from the tradition of Ibrahim (AS) all along - that there are sacred months designated for hajj. These aren't months when hajj is performed throughout, but rather months when the intention is made and the journey begins. The actual rites occur at very specific times, but the preparation and travel begin much earlier. This wasn't something the Arabs invented; it traces back to the legacy of Ibrahim (AS).

فَمَن فَرَضَ فِيهِنَّ ٱلْحَجَّ - "Whoever undertakes hajj in these months..." The word فَرَضَ means to mandate or make obligatory upon oneself. Once you've committed to hajj and begun the journey, certain prohibitions become absolute.

فَلَا رَفَثَ وَلَا فُسُوقَ وَلَا جِدَالَ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ - "There is no sexual relations, no wickedness, and no arguing during hajj." 

رَفَث refers to intimate relations with one's spouse. فُسُوق encompasses all forms of corruption and sin - backbiting, stealing, complaining, any violation of Allah's commands. جِدَال means arguing and disputing.

Allah ﷻ specifically mentions the prohibition on arguing because hajj involves massive crowds. People will inevitably get on your nerves. You'll be pushed around constantly. You'll stand in line and people will cut in front of you. Countless situations will test your patience. Allah ﷻ acknowledges this reality and says: I already know all of this will happen, and still, you cannot engage in any of these behaviors while in hajj.

وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ - "Whatever good you do, Allah knows it." The phrase مِنْ خَيْرٍ - "any good at all" - also encompasses restraint from evil. Every time you have the urge to argue and hold yourself back, Allah ﷻ knows. When someone takes your slippers and you have to walk barefoot but you refrain from retaliating, Allah ﷻ knows. When you stand in line and people say obnoxious things but you remain patient and silent, Allah ﷻ knows.

Some people go to hajj and want to make sure everyone around them knows about their good deeds. They announce, "I'm going to make some extra nawafil now because I really want to do something special. I'll be back in 40 minutes because I usually pray very long prayers." The message here is: don't tell anyone. Whatever you do between you and Allah ﷻ, keep it between you and Allah ﷻ. Don't go around sharing, "Last night I recited so much Quran, it was so awesome!" If you do something for Allah's sake, keep it special between you and Him. When you share it with people, you dilute its value.

وَتَزَوَّدُوا۟ - "Take provisions." The word تَزَوَّد is deeper than simply packing for a journey. زَاد refers to provisions you need, but زَادَ means to pack extra - more than the bare minimum. Like taking extra cash for emergencies, having backup funds. It means preparing well with precautionary measures.

فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ ٱلزَّادِ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ - "The best provision is taqwa." This transforms everything. Yes, pack your luggage, make your hotel reservations, arrange your flights, prepare your documents. But in the middle of all these material preparations, don't forget to pack the most important thing: consciousness of Allah ﷻ. Just as you keep your passport with you at all times, keep your taqwa with you at all times during this journey.

This ayah also has a practical dimension. Protect yourself by packing well. If you don't take adequate resources, you might end up begging there. Don't put yourself in vulnerable positions. Proper preparation protects you from difficulties and prevents you from becoming a burden on others.

وَٱتَّقُونِ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ - "And be conscious of Me, O people of understanding." The mention of أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (people of pure intellect) at this juncture is profound. Hajj should make you people of pure minds. Why? Because at hajj, nothing should distract you. You can finally have a completely focused mind, thinking of nothing but Allah ﷻ and your ultimate destination.

Hajj makes you forget about dunya and turns your attention entirely toward the akhirah. You're wearing the clothes of your death, simulating Judgment Day. You're standing before Allah ﷻ in huge crowds, begging for forgiveness - this is a rehearsal for Judgment Day. And where will all humanity be gathered on that Day? In the plains of Arafat. It's remarkable that we go through this simulation exercise of Judgment Day. It should cleanse you of all distracting thoughts and make you people of pure, clear, precise thought.

translation: There is no blame upon you for seeking bounty from your Lord [during Hajj]. But when you depart from 'Arafat, remember Allah at al- Mash'ar al-Haram. And remember Him, as He has guided you, for indeed, you were before that among those astray
commentary:
لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۚ فَإِذَآ أَفَضْتُم مِّنْ عَرَفَـٰتٍ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عِندَ ٱلْمَشْعَرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ وَٱذْكُرُوهُ كَمَا هَدَىٰكُمْ وَإِن كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ

لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ - "There is no blame upon you for seeking the bounty of your Lord." This addresses a practical reality: people are traveling from Pakistan, Bangladesh, the United States, Canada, Australia - from all over the world to make hajj. You're making this journey primarily for worship, but if you also have some business to conduct, some trade to engage in, there's no harm in that whatsoever. Makkah has always been a marketplace. You can go there for your deen and also engage in permissible business transactions. There's no contradiction.

فَإِذَآ أَفَضْتُم مِّنْ عَرَفَـٰتٍ - "When you depart from Arafat." The word أَفَضْتُم is often translated as "return from" or "depart from," but the Arabic carries a more vivid image. أَفَاضَ literally means to overflow, like water overfilling a container and spilling out. Allah ﷻ is describing the massive population pouring out from Arafat like liquid being poured - a flood of humanity. This is the aerial view, the divine perspective of people flowing quickly down from Arafat like rushing water.

Before continuing, it's crucial to understand that the essence of hajj is Arafat. Everything else can potentially be made up for in some way, but you cannot make up for missing the Day of Arafat. If you miss standing at Arafat, you've missed hajj entirely. On the 8th of Dhul-Hijjah, people begin leaving toward Arafat in caravans, trying to arrive before the time for Dhuhr. Just before reaching Arafat, you stop at Muzdalifah to pray Dhuhr and Asr combined - you don't pray inside Arafat itself. Then you enter Arafat and spend the entire day there.

فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عِندَ ٱلْمَشْعَرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ - "Remember Allah at the Sacred Mash'ar (Muzdalifah)." المَشْعَر comes from a root meaning something that evokes powerful feelings. Muzdalifah is the sacred place that should fill you with intense spiritual emotion. Even at Arafat, we don't pray Maghrib. We eventually join Maghrib and Isha together once we reach Muzdalifah.

وَٱذْكُرُوهُ كَمَا هَدَىٰكُمْ - "Remember Him as He has guided you." This means you shouldn't even be making the regular obligatory prayers at Arafat - those are combined elsewhere. At Arafat itself, engage in every form of dhikr except the structured prayers. Find other ways of remembering Allah ﷻ. You're sitting there for the entirety of the day, and what you do with that time reveals everything.

For people who have developed an appetite for talking to Allah ﷻ throughout hajj, Arafat becomes the most beautiful day of their lives. For those who merely went through the motions of hajj, who never truly connected, Arafat becomes the most miserable day. They sit there thinking, "What am I supposed to do here? I'm so bored. That person reading Quran is making me sleepy. I only know a few duas."

But you don't have to know everything in Arabic. You can make dua to Allah ﷻ in English. If there's one place you should become completely comfortable talking to Allah ﷻ, it's hajj. And it all culminates at Arafat, where you spend the day in conversation with Him. Don't engage in small talk with people. Don't waste time in chitchat. Just talk to Allah ﷻ for that one day.

وَإِن كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ - "Even though before this you were among those astray." Before Allah ﷻ guided you, you had no idea how to pray to Him, where to pray, what rituals to perform, where to stop, how long to remain. You were completely lost. Now He's guiding you through every detail of this sacred journey.

translation: Then depart from the place from where [all] the people depart and ask forgiveness of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَفِيضُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَفَاضَ ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

ثُمَّ أَفِيضُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَفَاضَ ٱلنَّاسُ - "Then depart from where the people depart." Before Islam, some Quraysh families had developed a sense of superiority. They believed they were special custodians of Makkah and didn't need to go to Arafat like everyone else. They would stay in Mina while everyone else traveled to Arafat. 

Allah ﷻ eliminates this arrogance entirely: pour out from where all the other people pour out. Go to Arafat just like everyone else. This applies to everybody without exception. Nobody gets special treatment. Nobody gets to claim religious privilege or geographic proximity as an excuse to avoid this essential ritual.

وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - "And seek forgiveness from Allah." This command comes immediately after Arafat. This is the supreme moment to seek Allah's forgiveness, and the instruction applies to everyone - even those who might think they're above needing forgiveness. Even those who considered themselves special because of their lineage or proximity to the Haram must humble themselves and seek Allah's mercy.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - "Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." The pairing of these two attributes is significant. غَفُور - Extremely Forgiving, and رَّحِيم - Always Merciful. Allah ﷻ doesn't just forgive; He showers His mercy continuously. This should give us immense hope as we stand at Arafat seeking His forgiveness.

translation: And when you have completed your rites, remember Allah like your [previous] remembrance of your fathers or with [much] greater remembrance. And among the people is he who says, "Our Lord, give us in this world," and he will have in the Hereafter no share
commentary:
فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُم مَّنَـٰسِكَكُمْ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَذِكْرِكُمْ ءَابَآءَكُمْ أَوْ أَشَدَّ ذِكْرًا ۗ فَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ

فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُم مَّنَـٰسِكَكُمْ - "When you have completed your rites..." This refers to finishing the core rituals of hajj, including the Day of Sacrifice (the 10th of Dhul-Hijjah).

فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَذِكْرِكُمْ ءَابَآءَكُمْ أَوْ أَشَدَّ ذِكْرًا - "Then remember Allah as you remember your forefathers, or with even greater remembrance." 

The mention of آبَاء ( forefathers/ancestors) here is culturally significant. For the Arabs, their favorite pastime was recounting the stories of their ancestors - just as people today might obsess over movies, sports, cars or technology. The Arabs would sit together and share tales: "My grandfather was this great warrior who did such-and-such..." These ancestral stories were their entertainment, their action movies, their prime conversation topic.

When they gathered to reminisce about these stories, time would fly. They'd move from one story to another, and five hours would pass without them noticing. They'd suddenly realize it was 2 AM. Allah ﷻ is saying: remember Me with that same enthusiasm, that same passion, that same absorption where you lose track of time. Or remember Me even more intensely than that.

Now comes a critical observation. Hajj is the one place that should simulate Judgment Day for us. It should make us forget about dunya entirely and focus completely on akhirah. Yet even there, Allah ﷻ observes different types of people and their duas.

فَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا - "Among the people is he who says, 'Our Lord, give us in this world...'" 

وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ - "And he will have no share in the Hereafter." The word خَلَاق means portion or share.

Sometimes people go to hajj for the wrong reasons entirely. In some Muslim cultures, people go to gain the honorific title "Al-Hajj" so others will call them "Hajji Sahab" and view them as pure and sacred. Some go because of guilty consciences - they're running completely haram businesses, oppressing their employees, taking their daughters' rightful inheritance, engaging in all kinds of injustice. They have this guilty weight, so they'll go make hajj. But the dunya is still deeply rooted in their hearts.

At hajj - the one place that should have purged this attachment to dunya - their duas still revolve entirely around worldly matters. They come to this spiritual treasure, this premium opportunity to ask for the greatest treasure (guidance), yet they have no concern for it at all. Their clear preference for this world over the next becomes an insult to this deen.

There are many materialistic Muslims living lives completely consumed by dunya, with virtually no concern for akhirah. Then Ramadan comes and they'll pray a lot. They'll go to hajj too. But all their duas are about worldly success: "Allah, give me a promotion. The economy is bad - make sure I keep my job. Make sure the house gets paid off." That's it. Nothing else.

I'm not saying you shouldn't make dua for those things. But if you miss the entire point of why you exist, if your biggest concerns are exclusively worldly, then you've forgotten the essence of this deen. And the one exercise that should have reminded you of this was hajj.

The Prophet ﷺ taught us to ask Allah ﷻ for everything, even for a shoelace. Yet here Allah ﷻ is criticizing someone who asks for dunya. How do we reconcile this? We ask Allah ﷻ with proper priorities. Ask Him first and foremost for guidance - اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ (Guide us to the straight path). That's the first complete dua in the Quran. Ask for guidance first.

Everything else you enjoy in this world exists so you can sustain yourself for your real purpose. But when people come to this spiritual treasure, this prime position to ask for the greatest treasure - guidance - and they demonstrate no care for it whatsoever, showing their clear preference for this world over the next, that becomes a profound insult to the deen. Allah ﷻ responds: Fine, I'll just give you what you want.

There are many materialistic Muslims - may Allah ﷻ not make us from among them - living completely worldly lives with no concern for akhirah. They'll pray a lot in Ramadan and even go to hajj, but all their duas are: "Let the economy improve. Give me that promotion. Let my house get paid off." If your existence revolves around worldly concerns and you miss the purpose of why you were even created, with your biggest priorities being material, then you've forgotten what this deen is about.

translation: But among them is he who says, "Our Lord, give us in this world [that which is] good and in the Hereafter [that which is] good and protect us from the punishment of the Fire
commentary:
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حَسَنَةً وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حَسَنَةً وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ - "And among them is he who says, 'Our Lord, give us good in this world and good in the Hereafter, and protect us from the punishment of the Fire.'"

This is one of the most well-known duas, yet it's deeply misunderstood. Many people think it simply means: "Give us really good stuff here and super hook us up over there." But that's not what this dua means at all.

رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً - "Our Lord, give us حَسَنَة (the most beautiful thing) in this dunya." What is the most beautiful thing you can possibly have in this world? 

We have a role model in this dunya - the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Is he a role model for us? Absolutely. And we have role models for the akhirah as well. Allah ﷻ establishes that the best thing you can have in this world is to be a follower of the best role model, to live according to the example of the one who lived the best possible life in this world - the Prophet ﷺ.

When you ask Allah ﷻ for the best in this world, you're asking to follow the Messenger ﷺ in the best possible way. You're asking for the ability to live your dunya according to his guidance, his character, his example. And if you truly do that, if you genuinely pursue the best this world has to offer (which is following the Prophet ﷺ), then it's only logical that you'll get the best in the akhirah as well.

This dua isn't about material wealth or worldly success. It's about seeking the best way to live in dunya - which is the way of the Prophet ﷺ - which will naturally lead to the best outcome in akhirah. You can have nice cars, a good house, success in your career - none of those things are problematic in themselves. But if you forget the purpose for which you were created, if you lose sight of why you exist, that's the real tragedy.

وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ - "And protect us from the punishment of the Fire." After asking for the best path in both worlds, we acknowledge our desperate need for Allah's protection from Hellfire.

translation: Those will have a share of what they have earned, and Allah is swift in account
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ - "Those will have a portion of what they have earned."

There's something profound and even frightening in how Allah ﷻ words this. He doesn't say نَصِيبٌ مَا كَسَبُوا (a portion of what they earned), but rather نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ - "a portion from what they earned." The word مِّمَّا - "from what" - changes everything.

What we earn includes both good deeds and bad deeds, sincere deeds and insincere deeds, quality prayers and broken, distracted prayers where we weren't really paying attention. Allah ﷻ is telling us: you'll get a portion from your deeds. Not everything you earned is worth compensation. Not everything you worked on was good enough.

Some of what you and I did was no good. Some of our efforts need serious improvement. We ask Allah ﷻ to overlook all our shortcomings and give us more than we deserve - because without His mercy, we cannot survive on Judgment Day. But He wants us to understand: you have to genuinely earn, and there are some things you've done that you really don't want to get paid for. There are things you'd rather Allah ﷻ not even count.

وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ - "And Allah is swift in account." Allah ﷻ will take that account very, very quickly. This should instill both urgency and caution in how we live our lives.

translation: And remember Allah during [specific] numbered days. Then whoever hastens [his departure] in two days - there is no sin upon him; and whoever delays [until the third] - there is no sin upon him - for him who fears Allah. And fear Allah and know that unto Him you will be gathered
commentary:
۞ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۚ فَمَن تَعَجَّلَ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ وَمَن تَأَخَّرَ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ لِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ

وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ - "And remember Allah during numbered days." These are the limited days after Eid al-Adha - specifically the 11th, 12th and 13th of Dhul-Hijjah, known as Ayyam al-Tashriq.

After the Day of Sacrifice (the 10th), there are three days when you voluntarily stay to remember Allah ﷻ. The minimum is two days, with the option to stay a third. These are days meant for your heart to remain attached to Allah ﷻ after visiting His House. It's as if you don't want to leave, so you stay a couple extra days just to continue remembering Him. That's the spirit behind these days.

Before continuing, it's worth noting that the phrase أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ("numbered days") appears elsewhere in the Quran. When fasting was first revealed, Allah ﷻ said the fasts are أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ - also three days per month. When there are exactly three of something, Arabic uses this phrase. When there are more than three, you would say أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَةٍ instead.

فَمَن تَعَجَّلَ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ - "Whoever hastens in two days, there is no sin upon him." If someone makes dhikr, makes istighfar, makes dua for two days and then needs to leave - perhaps they can't afford to stay a third day, or they have urgent matters to attend to - there is absolutely no sin on them whatsoever.

وَمَن تَأَخَّرَ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ - "And whoever delays, there is no sin upon him." Those who stay behind for the third day also have no sin. Both choices are completely acceptable.

However, there's a condition: لِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ - "for whoever has taqwa." By the third day, some people start getting agitated and bored. They become irritated and figure the only way to pass time is to annoy others around them. They become a disturbance to those trying to remember Allah ﷻ, who are now trying to remember Allah ﷻ and simultaneously trying to forget the annoying person next to them.

If that's going to be your state, it's better you leave after two days. If you're going to stay the third day, maintain your taqwa because it will be tested.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - "Maintain taqwa of Allah."

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ - "And know that to Him you will be gathered." 

You're leaving this gathering now, but Allah ﷻ reminds you: you're going to be back here. When? Maybe next year's hajj. Maybe on Judgment Day. The word تُحْشَرُونَ is significant. حَشْر in Arabic is different from جَمْع. Both mean gathering, but hashr specifically means to herd animals. You'll be herded like animals on Judgment Day. Everyone will be stampeding toward the gathering grounds - a powerful and humbling image.

translation: And of the people is he whose speech pleases you in worldly life, and he calls Allah to witness as to what is in his heart, yet he is the fiercest of opponents
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُعْجِبُكَ قَوْلُهُۥ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا فِى قَلْبِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَلَدُّ ٱلْخِصَامِ

Having just discussed people at hajj - the place that should humble everyone and eliminate all arrogance - Allah ﷻ now describes a particular type of person. This isn't a random transition; it's deeply connected to what came before.

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُعْجِبُكَ قَوْلُهُۥ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - "Among people is he whose speech impresses you in worldly life."

This person has the gift of eloquence. He can really talk, always trying to impress. He speaks beautifully, saying things like: "How blessed we are to be sincere believers. May Allah ﷻ increase us all in faith." He goes out of his way to prove his loyalty and demonstrate his commitment.

Allah ﷻ describes someone whose speech impresses you فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - "in worldly life." This phrase is critical. His speech impresses in dunya, alluding to the fact that a time will come - in akhirah - when he'll speak and it won't be impressive at all. You'll see what he really is, and it will be anything but impressive.

وَيُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا فِى قَلْبِهِۦ - "And he calls Allah to witness as to what is in his heart." Whenever people doubt what he's saying, he makes Allah ﷻ his witness to validate his claims. "I swear by Allah ﷻ, I love you so much!" "Wallahi, no one will pay you more than I will." "I swear by Allah ﷻ, no one has done more charity than I have." "No one wishes better for you than I do." 

He's constantly making Allah ﷻ witness to justify or validate what he's saying. This is one characteristic of certain hypocrites - excessive oath-taking to establish credibility.

وَهُوَ أَلَدُّ ٱلْخِصَامِ - "Yet he is the fiercest of opponents." 

أَلَدّ comes from لَدَد, which means the most intense, nasty kind of argumentation and animosity. It's worse than جِدَال. While jidal is regular arguing, ladd is the worst and most intense form of contentious behavior.

Some scholars describe al-aladd al-khisam as someone who, when he argues, all he does is talk. He refuses to listen. Even when he appears to be listening to you, he's not actually processing anything you're saying. You can always tell people like this - they're extremely loud when they talk, and when you try to speak, they just drown you out by raising their voices even more.

This is his true nature. He's soft-spoken and eloquent in public, speaking very nicely. But occasionally you see him on his angry side and you think, "Whoa, I had no idea this person was capable of that." When they reach that point, everyone backs off and nobody says anything to them. People become afraid of confronting them.

translation: And when he goes away, he strives throughout the land to cause corruption therein and destroy crops and animals. And Allah does not like corruption
commentary:
وَإِذَا تَوَلَّىٰ سَعَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُفْسِدَ فِيهَا وَيُهْلِكَ ٱلْحَرْثَ وَٱلنَّسْلَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَسَادَ

وَإِذَا تَوَلَّىٰ - "And when he turns away..." When he turns his back, when he leaves, when he gains some authority or distance...

سَعَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُفْسِدَ فِيهَا - "He strives throughout the land to cause corruption therein."

وَيُهْلِكَ ٱلْحَرْثَ وَٱلنَّسْلَ - "And destroys crops and livestock." 

The words الْحَرْث (crops/cultivation) and النَّسْل (offspring/livestock) represent people's livelihoods and sustenance. This person actively works to harm others' businesses, intimidate people, threaten them, bully them. He destroys what people have worked to build.

These are dangerous people in society who feed off threatening, scaring and intimidating others. People become afraid of them and don't speak up because they know if they try to confront them, they'll explode and become even worse.

Don't think these are just street criminals with guns. These exist in all forms and manners. Allah ﷻ is specifically describing munafiqun (hypocrites) within the ranks of Muslims - people who will try to threaten you, ruin your reputation, spread lies about you, send emails to the masjid board about you. They won't leave any stone unturned in trying to cause you harm.

They might spread rumors that you don't really sell halal meat, that you mix pork into your sandwiches - anything to hurt your business, to damage you. That's their only goal. But on the surface, they're very nice, very smiley. When you meet them, you think, "This person is so pleasant." You don't know their dark side.

وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَسَادَ - "And Allah does not love corruption." This is a severe statement. When Allah ﷻ says He doesn't love something, it's an indication of how displeasing that behavior is to Him.

translation: And when it is said to him, "Fear Allah," pride in the sin takes hold of him. Sufficient for him is Hellfire, and how wretched is the resting place
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ ٱلْعِزَّةُ بِٱلْإِثْمِ ۚ فَحَسْبُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ - "And when it is said to him, 'Fear Allah...'"

When somebody finally gets the courage to approach this person and say, "You know what? You need to fear Allah ﷻ. Come on, be conscious of Allah ﷻ. Protect yourself from His wrath..."

أَخَذَتْهُ ٱلْعِزَّةُ بِٱلْإِثْمِ - "Pride in sin seizes him."

His ego, his pride, his sense of authority completely takes hold of him. "How dare you tell me to have taqwa? Who do you think you are? How dare you speak to me like this?" He explodes in indignation.

The Prophet ﷺ said that من علامات المنافق (from the signs of the hypocrite): إذا جادل فجر - "When he argues, he becomes obscene/explosive." He can't handle correction. Someone tries to advise him sincerely, and he erupts: "Who do you think you're talking to? Don't you know I'm the president of the masjid? I'm the secretary of the organization! How dare you?"

It's tragic how these positions and titles get into people's heads. Allah ﷻ is describing this exact kind of arrogance. This comes right after discussing hajj - the one thing that should humble you completely. At hajj, you realize you're nothing special. You're dressed like everyone else. You have no class distinction. You didn't park a nicer car outside. You get pushed around like everyone else. Nobody comes and asks if you're okay. Nobody cares about your status. You're going to get jostled and kicked around like everybody else.

Yet at the end of all that, some people still haven't let go of their ego.

فَحَسْبُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ - "Sufficient for him is Hellfire." The only thing enough for this person is Jahannam. جَهَنَّمُ will embrace him, squeeze him.

وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ - "And what a wretched resting place." المِهَاد comes from the same root as مَهْد - a cradle, how a mother cradles and holds her child. Jahannam will cradle this person, but what a horrible cradle it is.

translation: And of the people is he who sells himself, seeking means to the approval of Allah. And Allah is kind to [His] servants
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِى نَفْسَهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ

After describing that terrible personality type, Allah ﷻ presents the complete opposite:

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِى نَفْسَهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ - "And among people is he who sells himself seeking the pleasure of Allah."

The word يَشْرِى means to sell. But notice: he doesn't just seek to please Allah ﷻ - that would be one level (making Allah ﷻ pleased). There's another level: trying not to make Allah ﷻ angry. But this person has sold himself entirely in pursuit of مَرْضَاة - making Allah ﷻ extremely pleased, maximally happy.

He's given himself up completely. His entire existence is dedicated to this pursuit of Allah's ultimate pleasure. These are role models we should learn from - those who sold themselves entirely for Allah's sake.

May Allah ﷻ make us from among them.

وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ - "And Allah is compassionate with His slaves."

رَءُوف is a beautiful attribute. It describes someone who truly knows what you're going through, who understands your struggle. Allah ﷻ knows what it's like - what the slaves are going through when someone sells themselves in this way. Allah ﷻ knows the immense trouble they had to go through.

Allah ﷻ knew the trouble the Sahaba (RA) had to go through when they sold themselves in pursuit of making Him truly happy. Those who traveled from Makkah, who gave up everything, who sacrificed their families, their wealth, their comfort, their safety - Allah ﷻ knew every hardship they endured.

May Allah ﷻ make us from those who learn from these noble role models.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - The very next ayah begins with "O you who believe..." You should learn from those role models - the ones who gave themselves up entirely to please Allah ﷻ. Those are at the highest level. But before reaching that level, at least get started.

What's the first step? ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِى ٱلسِّلْمِ كَآفَّةً - "Enter into Islam entirely." 

This transitions into the next section, but the message is clear: we have before us two paths. One leads to the person who speaks beautifully but whose heart is filled with arrogance, who causes corruption, who can't accept advice, whose destination is Jahannam. The other path is the person who sells themselves entirely for Allah's pleasure, who Allah ﷻ treats with compassion and mercy.

The question for each of us is: which path will we choose?

translation: O you who have believed, enter into Islam completely [and perfectly] and do not follow the footsteps of Satan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِى ٱلسِّلْمِ كَآفَّةً وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ

This ayah continues a conversation that began with the discussion of Hajj. Allah ﷻ first described a particular hypocritical character who cannot benefit from even the extraordinary opportunity of Hajj. He then contrasted this with someone who doesn't just sell himself to please Allah ﷻ during Hajj, but dedicates his entire life to this purpose. After identifying who these true slaves of Allah ﷻ are, He invites all of us to adopt this same complete submission, to give ourselves up entirely into the fold of Islam.

The call here is comprehensive. Allah ﷻ commands believers to enter into Islam كَآفَّةً (entirely, completely). This isn't a partial commitment or selective practice. It's an invitation to wholehearted devotion where every aspect of life comes under the banner of submission to Allah ﷻ.

translation: But if you deviate after clear proofs have come to you, then know that Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
فَإِن زَلَلْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْكُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

The expression فَإِن زَلَلْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْكُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ (if you slip even after the clearest proofs have come to you) carries profound weight. This exact phrase was used when Shaytan made Adam (AS) slip. It was also used for the People of the Book who refused to believe even after clear evidence came to them. Now Allah ﷻ addresses Muslims with the same language.

The message is stark: I have asked you to give yourselves up entirely. Even after the clearest proofs have come showing you need to be this way, if you slip from this attitude, this instruction, this intention, then you had better know that I have all authority. The fact that Allah ﷻ isn't punishing immediately doesn't reflect inability—it reflects wisdom. There is a time appointed, and when that time comes, His authority will be executed.

The two divine names at the end—عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (the Ultimate Authority, the All-Wise)—work together. My authority will be exacted against you, but not yet. There's a wise plan in place. As Allah ﷻ says later in Surah Ash-Shura, had it not been for a particular time He declared in His wisdom when punishments will be executed, their matter would have been decided immediately.

translation: Do they await but that Allah should come to them in covers of clouds and the angels [as well] and the matter is [then] decided? And to Allah [all] matters are returned
commentary:
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ظُلَلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْغَمَامِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ۚ وَإِلَىٰ ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ

This addresses the attitude of procrastination that Allah ﷻ has already warned against. It's what Arabs call تَدَابُّ—putting things off. Someone recognizes what is right, knows they should give up the haram and pursue only the halal, understands they need to make serious changes to show Allah ﷻ they are a true, sincere, submissive believer. But always, there's "later." After I finish this project, after I get married, after the wedding celebrations, after I secure my promotion—then I'll be a better Muslim.

That "tomorrow" never comes. Allah ﷻ asks: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ (Are they waiting?) The verb يَنظُرُونَ suggests looking forward to something, procrastinating until something happens. What are they waiting for? إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ظُلَلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْغَمَامِ (Until Allah comes at them from the shades of the clouds). The word يَأْتِي doesn't just mean "to come"—it also means "to attack." Are they waiting until Allah ﷻ Himself attacks them from the shades of clouds, until the angels descend, until the matter is decided?

وَإِلَىٰ ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (And to Allah all matters are being returned). Every decision you and I make, in public or private, is being taken back to Allah ﷻ and recorded. On that Day, these decisions will receive their verdict. So what are you waiting for? Get your act together now.

translation: Ask the Children of Israel how many a sign of evidence We have given them. And whoever exchanges the favor of Allah [for disbelief] after it has come to him - then indeed, Allah is severe in penalty
commentary:
سَلْ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ كَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍۭ بَيِّنَةٍ ۗ وَمَن يُبَدِّلْ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

Allah ﷻ tells us to ask the Children of Israel: How many clear, miraculous signs did We give them? The Quran has already informed us thoroughly about how they procrastinated, how they didn't get their act together despite overwhelming evidence.

وَمَن يُبَدِّلْ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ (Whoever replaces the favor of Allah)—and what is this favor? Revelation. Whoever replaces the revelation of Allah ﷻ even after it came to him has made a terrible choice. Replacement doesn't necessarily mean believing something else entirely. It means prioritizing something else. Something else becomes more immediately important. Revelation and its demands can wait—it's not your top priority right now.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (Then certainly Allah is severe in taking retribution). This is the consequence of treating Allah's guidance as secondary.

translation: Beautified for those who disbelieve is the life of this world, and they ridicule those who believe. But those who fear Allah are above them on the Day of Resurrection. And Allah gives provision to whom He wills without account
commentary:
زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۘ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ

This is a beautiful and powerful reminder for the believer. Allah ﷻ says: زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا (For the disbelievers, worldly life was beautified). The passive construction and word order are significant—it's been beautified exclusively for those who disbelieve.

We need to distinguish between الْعَالَم (the world—mountains, trees, sky, nature) and الدُّنْيَا (worldly life). Every human lives in the same عَالَم, but each has their own دُنْيَا—their stuff, their issues, their life. Your دُنْيَا includes your family, career, savings, friends, relationships. My دُنْيَا is different from yours. We share this world, but each of us has our own worldly life.

Allah ﷻ beautified the عَالَم for all creation, but He's saying the حَيَاة الدُّنْيَا—this worldly existence—who has it been particularly beautified for? The disbelievers. Even though we as believers find beauty in family, homes, travel, friendships, Allah ﷻ is saying: This beauty I made for disbelievers? You think this is beautiful? Wait until you see what I have for you. For disbelievers, this is all they get. There's no Jannah waiting for them. As the hadith states, the dunya is the prison of the believer and the paradise of the disbeliever.

For the disbeliever, everything is permissible—no restrictions, no rules, no limitations. They look at believers and see people who can't dress however they want, can't eat whatever they want, have time restrictions for prayer, can't say what they want, can't watch what they want. They see believers as enslaved, oppressed, primitive.

وَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (And they make fun of those who believe). They mock everything we stand for. They say, "Live a little! What kind of life are you living?" They can't understand commitment to anything beyond immediate pleasure.

Tragically, this attitude—which is supposed to characterize disbelievers—has infected Muslims today. Young people make fun of other Muslims who take Islam seriously. Family members mock those who commit to the faith. "You need to loosen up. Don't worry about it so much. Just be like us."

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (And those who had taqwa will be above them on the Day of Resurrection). Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't say "those who have iman" here—He says those who have taqwa. Having iman doesn't automatically mean having taqwa. Many enter the faith but don't actively protect themselves from worldly temptation. Those who actually developed taqwa, who protected themselves consistently—they will be فَوْقَهُمْ, above them, literally and figuratively. They'll be in elevated stations while looking down at those in the Fire.

وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (And Allah provides for whoever He wills without limitation). Why mention provisions here? Because disbelievers have the entire world before them with no restrictions on how they earn. What we can earn, how we earn, where we earn—it's all limited by halal and haram. But Allah ﷻ promises: those who have taqwa, who follow these limitations, will find doors opened for them without any restriction, in this world and the next. وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مَخْرَجًا (Whoever has taqwa of Allah, He will make a way out for them). He'll provide from where they couldn't imagine—legitimate jobs, halal business opportunities. Live by taqwa and watch what doors Allah ﷻ opens.

translation: Mankind was [of] one religion [before their deviation]; then Allah sent the prophets as bringers of good tidings and warners and sent down with them the Scripture in truth to judge between the people concerning that in which they differed. And none differed over the Scripture except those who were given it - after the clear proofs came to them - out of jealous animosity among themselves. And Allah guided those who believed to the truth concerning that over which they had differed, by His permission. And Allah guides whom He wills to a straight path
commentary:
كَانَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ فِيمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ فَهَدَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

How did this division between believers and disbelievers happen? History wasn't always like this. كَانَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً (Mankind was one nation). The implication is that they were united, then they fell apart. To reunite them, Allah ﷻ appointed prophets—فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ (giving good news and giving warning). The order is significant: first good news, then warning. Listen to this message and you'll have the best outcome. But when people mocked the message, the prophets warned them: you'd better take this seriously.

وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ (And He sent with them the Book with truth and justice) لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ فِيمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ (so that it could make decisions between them in whatever they disagreed about). The Book was meant to unify, to settle disputes.

But then comes the tragedy: وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ (No one disagreed about the Book except those who were given it). The very people entrusted with the revelation became the source of disagreement. مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ (Even after the clearest proofs came to them)—they were people of knowledge, those who carried and represented the teachings of religion. They were supposed to be the means by which the world unified, but they became the means by which the world divided further.

Why? بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ (Out of transgression among themselves). The word بَغْي is powerful—it means the urge to dominate another. They saw their religion as a means of expanding their power and influence. Scholars viewed their knowledge as market share. If another scholar's following grows, mine shrinks. So they disagreed not over truth, but over territory, influence, control.

Allah ﷻ is warning us about religious leaders who are more concerned with refuting others than teaching truth. If someone's entire work consists of writing books about why other scholars are wrong, why other religions are misguided, they've missed the point entirely. People with nothing solid to stand on spend all their time pointing at others' flaws. It's a deflection, a sign of insecurity.

فَهَدَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِۦ (And Allah guided those who believed to what they disagreed about of the truth, by His permission). Muslims sometimes ask: with so many groups, scholars, sects, and interpretations, how do I know which is right? Allah ﷻ answers: whoever truly has iman, who sincerely seeks, He will guide them. وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (And Allah guides whoever He wills to a straight path).

It's not a scholar that protects your religion, not a teacher, not a book alone—it's the Quran and your sincerity. When those are in place, Allah ﷻ will guide you to the right people, expose you to the right teachings, develop the right mindset in you.

Our deen is 90% attitude and 10% knowledge. Knowledge is only there to fortify our attitudes. The fundamental attitudes are straightforward: I'm going to submit to Allah ﷻ no matter what. I'm going to earn my Akhirah. I'm going to save myself and my family from Hell. These aren't complicated—you don't need to be a scholar to have them. When these attitudes are in place and you learn more, that learning helps you submit better, earn Akhirah more efficiently, avoid Hell more effectively.

The problem today is people obsess over knowledge while lacking proper attitudes. Why are you learning? For what purpose? With the right attitude, when you encounter something under the label of "Islamic knowledge" that takes you in wrong directions, an internal alarm goes off. You don't have to be a scholar—your sincerity, your intention will tell you, "That doesn't sound right."

This is the insight Umar (RA) had. He would just see things and know. When people said women shouldn't cover, he knew it didn't jive with Islam. When proposals about alcohol came up, he recognized they contradicted the faith. He had the right attitude—a certain spiritual perception that comes from proper intention. Developing this attitude, this thought process, is more important than accumulating facts.

We need to be more confident in the Quran. Anyone who comes sincerely to Allah's Book—do you really think Allah ﷻ will let them be misguided? The only people Allah ﷻ allows to be misguided by the Quran are those with corrupt intentions—وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (He doesn't mislead anyone with it except the inherently corrupt). Anyone who comes to the Quran sincerely, there's no way Allah ﷻ will let them go astray. We must have more confidence in our Book.

translation: Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while such [trial] has not yet come to you as came to those who passed on before you? They were touched by poverty and hardship and were shaken until [even their] messenger and those who believed with him said, "When is the help of Allah?" Unquestionably, the help of Allah is near
commentary:
أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُم ۖ مَّسَّتْهُمُ ٱلْبَأْسَآءُ وَٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ مَتَىٰ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ نَصْرَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ

After discussing fasting, Hajj, and various acts that earn forgiveness, a Muslim might think, "I think I've got this. I'll be alright on Judgment Day." Then comes this sobering ayah.

أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ (Do you really think you're just going to enter Jannah like that?) وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُم (And the things that happened to those who came before you haven't happened to you yet?) None of those troubles have reached you yet.

مَّسَّتْهُمُ ٱلْبَأْسَآءُ وَٱلضَّرَّآءُ (They were touched by hardship and adversity). وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ (And they were shaken)—the earth beneath their feet shook so violently they couldn't stand. That's how rattled they were while holding on to Islam.

حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ مَتَىٰ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ (Until even the messenger would say, along with those who believed with him, "When is Allah's help coming?"). They were so shaken, even a prophet would ask: When is relief coming? This is too much. We can't take anymore.

أَلَآ إِنَّ نَصْرَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ (Indeed, the help of Allah is near). How can Allah's help be far when Allah ﷻ Himself is near? He already established in this surah: وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِى عَنِّى فَإِنِّىٓ قَرِيبٌ (And when My servants ask you about Me, indeed I am near). If He is near, His help is near.

This ayah makes clear that Muslims have been handed a legacy that lands us in trouble. The tradition of calling to Allah's way—the tradition of the prophets—got them into serious difficulties.
