Whether Nuh (AS), Hud (AS), Salih (AS), Shu'ayb (AS), Ibrahim (AS), or Muhammad ﷺ—these were the kindest people in the world, yet people wanted to throw them in fire, kick them out of their homes, kill them. People would call them truthful and honest while simultaneously plotting their murder.

Allah ﷻ stationed legions of devils from jinn and humans specifically to be enemies of the prophets. When we say we'll carry on the mission of the prophets, when we commit to being part of this ummah, we must be mentally prepared. This won't be an easy journey. There will be people who hate us, who try to shake the ground beneath our feet, until we beg for Allah's help. And it is then that help will come.

Muslims suffer oppression for one of two reasons. Either they're doing their job so well that Allah ﷻ is testing them to raise their ranks—the harder the trials, the greater the help and reward in this world and the next. Or Muslims aren't doing their job, and Allah ﷻ is literally whipping them at the hands of disbelievers to wake them up. Muslims are asleep, experiencing oppression as an alarm clock.

We have to ask ourselves: which situation describes us?

translation: They ask you, [O Muhammad], what they should spend. Say, "Whatever you spend of good is [to be] for parents and relatives and orphans and the needy and the traveler. And whatever you do of good - indeed, Allah is Knowing of it
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ ۖ قُلْ مَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۗ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ

They ask about spending: يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ (They ask you what they should spend). This question had been addressed before—Allah ﷻ had already explained spending in the path of Allah without specifying exact amounts or recipients. Now they're asking again, perhaps hoping for an easier answer.

قُلْ مَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ (Tell them: whatever you spend from good). The word خَيْر here encompasses both good intention and any good cause—intention and purpose are both implied.

فَلِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (It should be for parents, close relatives, orphans, those in need, and travelers). These categories were mentioned before, but now when they ask again, something happens.

We learned from the Children of Israel that when you ask too many questions about religious obligations, do the requirements get easier or harder? They get harder. So when Muslims ask a second time, Allah ﷻ responds: وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (And whatever you do of good, indeed Allah is knowing of it).

Notice the shift—from "whatever you spend" to "whatever you do." It's no longer just about spending money; it's about any good deed. And the emphasis shifts to Allah's knowledge: He is fully aware of it. The instruction becomes broader and the accountability becomes more direct.

translation: Fighting has been enjoined upon you while it is hateful to you. But perhaps you hate a thing and it is good for you; and perhaps you love a thing and it is bad for you. And Allah Knows, while you know not
commentary:
كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ وَهُوَ كُرْهٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تُحِبُّوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ شَرٌّ لَّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

When spending is mentioned, you should remember: yes, you can spend on all these good causes, but what's the most pressing reason right now? Fighting has been made mandatory—كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ (Fighting has been prescribed upon you).

وَهُوَ كُرْهٌ لَّكُمْ (And you find it disgusting). There was a group of hypocrites who hated the idea of fighting. "We have to fight? Why? Islam is about peace and love. Why should we fight? We should just make peace with them."

Allah ﷻ responds with a principle that extends far beyond fighting: وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ (It may be that you hate something and it is good for you). وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تُحِبُّوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ شَرٌّ لَّكُمْ (And it may be that you love something and it is bad for you).

Think of a sick child whose mother forces medicine down his throat. To the child, it seems like oppression, like cruelty. But is it oppression or mercy? It's mercy. The child doesn't understand that this bitter medicine is good for him.

Fighting is painful. Who prescribes it? Someone who loves you and me more than our mothers love us. It is mercy, whether we understand it or not. Just as the child doesn't comprehend that medicine is beneficial, when Allah ﷻ commanded Muslims to fight, they may not have fully grasped why it was good for them—but it was.

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (Allah knows and you do not know). Who are you to like and dislike? I reveal, you follow. That's it. Allah ﷻ is literally laying down the law.

translation: They ask you about the sacred month - about fighting therein. Say, "Fighting therein is great [sin], but averting [people] from the way of Allah and disbelief in Him and [preventing access to] al-Masjid al-Haram and the expulsion of its people therefrom are greater [evil] in the sight of Allah. And fitnah is greater than killing." And they will continue to fight you until they turn you back from your religion if they are able. And whoever of you reverts from his religion [to disbelief] and dies while he is a disbeliever - for those, their deeds have become worthless in this world and the Hereafter, and those are the companions of the Fire, they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ ۖ قُلْ قِتَالٌ فِيهِ كَبِيرٌ ۖ وَصَدٌّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَكُفْرٌۢ بِهِۦ وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَإِخْرَاجُ أَهْلِهِۦ مِنْهُ أَكْبَرُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَكْبَرُ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ ۗ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَن دِينِكُمْ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يَرْتَدِدْ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَيَمُتْ وَهُوَ كَافِرٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

The hypocrites raised another objection: يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ (They ask you about the sacred month, fighting in it). "Are you going to make us fight in the sacred months when fighting is prohibited?"

قُلْ قِتَالٌ فِيهِ كَبِيرٌ (Tell them: Fighting in it is a great matter). It's no small violation to fight in the sacred months—Allah ﷻ acknowledges this is كَبِير (big, serious).

But then He provides context: وَصَدٌّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَكُفْرٌۢ بِهِۦ وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَإِخْرَاجُ أَهْلِهِۦ مِنْهُ أَكْبَرُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ (Yet obstructing from the path of Allah, disbelieving in Him and the Sacred Mosque, and expelling its people from it—these are أَكْبَرُ, greater in Allah's sight).

The crimes of the Quraysh—blocking people from Allah's path, disbelieving in Him, obstructing access to the Sacred Mosque, expelling those worthy of being there—these crimes together are أَكْبَرُ, bigger than the violation of fighting in sacred months.

وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَكْبَرُ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ (And fitnah is greater than killing). Earlier Allah ﷻ said fighting is أَشَدّ (more intense) than killing. Now He says fitnah is أَكْبَر (bigger, greater) than killing. What is fitnah? It's when halal becomes difficult to access and haram becomes easy, when the road to shirk is wide open and the road to tawhid is blocked. That fitnah is far more serious than fighting. You think fighting is a big deal? Fitnah is way bigger.

وَلَا يَزَالُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَن دِينِكُمْ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ (And they will never stop fighting you until they turn you back from your religion, if they can). You're worried about fighting them? You want to make peace? Let Me tell you something about them: they're not interested in peace. They will continue to fight you until they make you abandon your faith, if they possibly can.

وَمَن يَرْتَدِدْ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَيَمُتْ وَهُوَ كَافِرٌ (And whoever turns back from his religion and dies as a disbeliever)—meaning whoever abandons Islam and returns to the ways of Quraysh and dies in that state—فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ (then those are the ones whose deeds are nullified in this world and the Hereafter). Anyone who leaves Islam loses all the good they did while they were Muslim.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (And those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally). There is no hope for someone who dies as a disbeliever after having been Muslim. Their fate is sealed.

translation: Indeed, those who have believed and those who have emigrated and fought in the cause of Allah - those expect the mercy of Allah. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

On one hand, there are people with no hope—those who died as disbelievers after leaving Islam. But who should have hope for Allah's mercy?

This ayah is a crucial wake-up call for the ummah today. Every single Muslim wants mercy from Allah ﷻ. There isn't a Muslim who says, "I don't want Allah's mercy." But this ayah identifies who actually has the right to hope for it.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (Indeed those who believed)—first, genuine faith. وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ (And those who migrated). This applied at the time of the Prophet ﷺ when people came from Makkah to Madinah, but it still applies today. The Prophet ﷺ was asked what hijrah means now, and he responded: "That you abandon everything Allah ﷻ doesn't like."

That's hijrah—migration from disobedience to obedience, from darkness to light, from laziness to activity, from strolling into salah to rushing to it, from consuming haram to consuming halal, from the questionable job to the legitimate one. These are all forms of hijrah.

وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (And struggled in the path of Allah). Notice the phrasing: جَاهَدُوا فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ is different from جَاهَدُوا لِلَّٰهِ. The latter would mean "struggled for Allah's sake"—I'm making struggles to please Allah ﷻ. The former, فِى سَبِيلِ, means "in the path of"—not just for whom am I struggling, but in what direction? Allah ﷻ has laid out a path, and treading that path requires struggle.

There's a goal Allah ﷻ has put before us individually: earn Jannah. There's also a goal He's put before us collectively as an ummah: give His deen victory, spread it, empower it, make it a reality on earth. That is the mission Allah ﷻ has given this ummah. Whether it will be accepted or completed is another question, but the mission exists.

At the individual level, we struggle for Jannah through good deeds, seeking forgiveness, maintaining taqwa. At the collective level, we need to make progress toward that larger goal. Just as taqwa operates at individual and collective levels, so does jihad. Jihad at the individual level is one thing; jihad as a larger communal struggle is something else.

Don't be fooled into thinking jihad equals only fighting. That's a shallow, often malicious misreading. The Quran talks about jihad in Makkah when there was no fighting at all. All the Sahaba in Makkah were engaged in jihad without lifting a sword—they were struggling to fulfill the mission Allah ﷻ gave them.

Most religions exist primarily to make you feel good about yourself. People turn to religion when sad, lonely, or depressed, and our deen does offer that—it gives happiness, tranquility, peace. But our deen is so much more. It's not just something that makes you feel good. You have to make Allah ﷻ happy with it. It's not about you—it's about Him. It makes you selfless, not selfish.

There's a modern vision of religion that sees it as something meant to serve you. No—we're here to serve it. We're supposed to serve this cause. The reason Islam gives us peace and tranquility is so we're spiritually healthy enough to serve it. All those blessings exist so we can serve Allah's deen.

Consider the concept of tazkiyah—purifying yourself from jealousy, materialism, laziness, shamelessness. It's a constant struggle in a Muslim's life. None of us are pure—لَا تُزَكُّوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ (Don't declare yourselves pure). We constantly struggle. But why? Even purification isn't the final goal—it's a means to an end.

Think of a farmer caring for soil: watering it, giving it sun, removing weeds. Why? Because there's a seed inside. The soil gets attention because of what's growing within it. Now imagine there's no seed—just soil being cared for with no plant to grow. What's the point?

Tazkiyah exists because the seed of struggle for Allah's sake needs healthy soil to grow. If purification itself becomes the goal, you've missed the point. There's so much more work to be done. Some people worry so much about tazkiyah they say, "We shouldn't do dawah yet—we're not pure enough." You're never going to be pure enough in this dunya! Are you going to wait until angels come? That's not happening.

Others say, "If you're concerned with calling people to Allah ﷻ, you're being arrogant—how can you tell others to do good when you're not perfect?" But Allah ﷻ addresses this: أَتَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبِرِّ وَتَنسَوْنَ أَنفُسَكُمْ (Do you order people to righteousness and forget yourselves?). Yes, we can't forget ourselves. So we say, "What I'm telling you applies to me too. If I'm saying we're falling short, I'm included in that problem. I'm not claiming to be the solution—I'm part of the problem too. We're all in this together."

That doesn't stop us from reminding each other. It's not self-righteousness if done with proper intention. On the other extreme, you have activism without tazkiyah—and that's hollow. It doesn't go anywhere. If the soil isn't clean, the plant won't be healthy. These two sides of the coin must go hand in hand.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ (Those are the ones who hope for Allah's mercy)—those who believe, who make hijrah from wrong to right, who struggle in Allah's path. Those are the ones with legitimate hope.

وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful). His forgiveness and mercy are available, but they're for those actively engaged in this mission.

translation: They ask you about wine and gambling. Say, "In them is great sin and [yet, some] benefit for people. But their sin is greater than their benefit." And they ask you what they should spend. Say, "The excess [beyond needs]." Thus Allah makes clear to you the verses [of revelation] that you might give thought
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ ۖ قُلْ فِيهِمَآ إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُمَآ أَكْبَرُ مِن نَّفْعِهِمَا ۗ وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ قُلِ ٱلْعَفْوَ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ

They ask about الْخَمْر (alcohol) and الْمَيْسِر (gambling). قُلْ فِيهِمَآ إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ (Say: In them is great sin and benefits for people).

Many translations unfortunately render this as "In them is harm and benefit, and their harm is greater than their benefit." But the Arabic doesn't say ضَرَر (harm)—it says إِثْم (sin). This distinction is crucial. If you mistranslate it as "harm," the conversation changes entirely. You'll have people sitting around saying, "Allah ﷻ said the harm is greater than benefit, but there's still benefit. He didn't say it's completely haram. And personally, the harm doesn't affect me—I don't get drunk. Actually, it's good for my liver..."

Allah ﷻ isn't comparing harms and benefits. He's comparing sin and benefits. وَإِثْمُهُمَآ أَكْبَرُ مِن نَّفْعِهِمَا (And their sin is greater than their benefit). Even if there are physiological or economic benefits, even if medical studies show some advantages, it doesn't matter. The sin is still there, and it outweighs any benefit.

One benefit that comes from alcohol and gambling? Look at the gambling industry: college tuitions paid for by casino revenue, police forces funded during budget crises, struggling economies propped up by gambling taxes. States facing budget shortfalls say, "Our state law prohibits casinos on land, so we'll set up gambling yachts on the waterfront." They generate tax revenue at the expense of making thousands bankrupt and exponentially increasing crime, driving people to desperation and suicide.

Their sin is greater than their benefit. This was early revelation, before the final prohibition of alcohol. But for some Muslims, even this early criticism from Allah ﷻ was enough. They dropped alcohol immediately, even though this wasn't yet the final verdict.

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ (And they ask you what they should spend). They've asked this before. Now they're asking again. قُلِ ٱلْعَفْوَ (Tell them: al-'afw). This is the harshest possible answer. العَفْو means what you can afford—in other words, take enough to survive as a human being and give Me the rest. Live at bare minimum sustenance level and give the rest.

You wanted to ask again? Here's the raised bar. Allah ﷻ is saying: you really want to know what to spend? This is what I'll say.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (Thus Allah makes clear to you the verses that you may think deeply). لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ—so that you think deeply, contemplate, reflect.

translation: To this world and the Hereafter. And they ask you about orphans. Say, "Improvement for them is best. And if you mix your affairs with theirs - they are your brothers. And Allah knows the corrupter from the amender. And if Allah had willed, He could have put you in difficulty. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۗ وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ ۖ قُلْ إِصْلَاحٌ لَّهُمْ خَيْرٌ ۖ وَإِن تُخَالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَعْنَتَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ (In the dunya and the Akhirah)—think deeply about your spending, about your financial decisions in this world and how they connect to the next. The previous ayah was about spending, and Allah ﷻ says you need budget planning not just for dunya but for Akhirah. Just as you look at your bank account and decide whether you can afford rent, car payment, new computer, whatever—you make financial plans big and small—you need to have budget planning for Jannah.

How much did I deposit into my Jannah account this week? How much am I investing in my Akhirah? Think deeply about your investment in the eternal life. When we spend in Allah's path, we're not really spending—we're depositing. Allah ﷻ promises: لَن تَنَالُوا۟ ٱلْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ (You will never attain righteousness until you spend from what you love). But then He promises: وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍۢ فَهُوَ يُخْلِفُهُۥ (Whatever you spend of anything, He will replace it). You'll find it. You'll be paid back in full. Trust Me. You won't be wronged.

You're just transferring from dunya funds to Akhirah funds. It's still your account, still has your name. You're the only one who can access it. So deposit in that account. The account you never deposit into is the account you never think about. When you have an account you opened but never put money in, you don't worry about it, don't check it. But we have an Akhirah account—when you never put anything in it, you never think about Jannah.

Keep putting deposits in, and what happens? You're forced to keep thinking about Jannah. As Isa (AS) reportedly said: When you save in this world, you fear the robber, the termite, the moth eating your stored goods. But save with Allah ﷻ and you have no fears. It's guaranteed. You don't need insurance—it's taken care of.

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ (And they ask you about orphans). Allah ﷻ has emphasized giving orphans their rights. If an orphan is living with you, some Companions worried: "This is the orphan's money, which should be kept separate from our money. Should we feed them separately? Cook for them separately?" Some Sahaba started setting up two kitchens—one for the orphan, one for themselves—because they were so careful not to consume even one bite from the orphan's portion.

قُلْ إِصْلَاحٌ لَّهُمْ خَيْرٌ (Say: Setting their affairs right is best). وَإِن تُخَالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ (And if you mix with them, then they are your brothers). Allah ﷻ says: Listen, you don't have to go that far. If you mix them in with yourselves, they're your brothers. They're family. It's fine.

An orphan is completely at your disposal, especially in societies where the father has died and assets are taken away, leaving the child treated poorly while living with an uncle or other relatives. This is still common in many parts of the Muslim world.

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِ (Allah knows the corrupt one from the one who seeks reconciliation). Allah ﷻ knows those with corrupt intentions versus those with good intentions.

We'll learn later in the Quran that it's permissible to hold money back from orphans when they're young. If the father left significant wealth, hold it back, give them a small allowance, keep them with you until they're mature enough to manage finances. Then give them their money back—don't keep it.

وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَعْنَتَكُمْ (And if Allah had willed, He would have put you in difficulty). He could have forced you to keep separate finances, separate kitchens, making it much harder. But He didn't.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (Indeed Allah is the Ultimate Authority, All-Wise). He has the authority to make things as difficult as He wants, but in His wisdom, He makes things manageable for those who sincerely seek to do right by the orphans.

translation: And do not marry polytheistic women until they believe. And a believing slave woman is better than a polytheist, even though she might please you. And do not marry polytheistic men [to your women] until they believe. And a believing slave is better than a polytheist, even though he might please you. Those invite [you] to the Fire, but Allah invites to Paradise and to forgiveness, by His permission. And He makes clear His verses to the people that perhaps they may remember
commentary:
وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ حَتَّىٰ يُؤْمِنَّ ۚ وَلَأَمَةٌ مُّؤْمِنَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن مُّشْرِكَةٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ ۗ وَلَا تُنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَتَّىٰ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ وَلَعَبْدٌ مُّؤْمِنٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن مُّشْرِكٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكُمْ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۖ وَيُبَيِّنُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ

As the new Muslim community in Medina continued to develop, Allah ﷻ began addressing the fundamental structures that would form the foundation of this society. While the broader purpose and drive for the community had already been established through the concept of taqwa, the practical realities of daily life still needed guidance. At the heart of every family stands a marriage, and humanity itself began with this institution – with Adam and Hawwa (AS). The health of society depends entirely on the health of its marriages, because healthy marriages produce good parents, good parents raise good children, and good children eventually become good citizens.

Allah ﷻ commands in this ayah: "Do not marry mushrik women until they believe." The classical understanding, held by the majority of scholars, is that المشركات (al-mushrikat) refers predominantly to idolatrous women and does not include the People of the Book. The Quran distinguishes between mushrikeen and the People of the Book on multiple occasions, using different terminology for Christians and Jews. While some Sahaba held the opinion that Christians should also be included within this prohibition due to elements of shirk in their belief, the Quranic text maintains this distinction.

The ayah continues with a powerful statement about values: "And a believing slave woman is better than a mushrik woman, even if she impresses you." At that time, slavery was a social reality, and Allah ﷻ uses this stark comparison to emphasize that faith outweighs all worldly considerations – social status, beauty, wealth, or any other attractive quality. The essence of a person's worth lies in their belief, not in what impresses the eye.

This principle applies equally to women: "And do not give your women in marriage to mushrik men until they believe." Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't directly address the women here, but rather addresses their guardians (wali). This reflects the structure of Islamic marriage, where the authority figures in a woman's life have a responsibility in facilitating and approving her marriage. The command لَا تُنكِحُوا (la tunkihu) – "do not give in marriage" – is directed at these guardians, emphasizing their role in protecting the women under their care.

The contrast continues: "And a believing slave is better than a mushrik man, even if he impresses you." Again, the message is crystal clear – faith supersedes all other considerations.

But why these restrictions? Allah ﷻ provides the reasoning: "Those invite to the Fire, while Allah invites to Paradise and forgiveness by His permission." This is the fundamental difference between these marriages. When a believer marries a mushrik, they're entering into a constant spiritual conflict. The mushrik spouse, whether consciously or unconsciously, becomes a persistent invitation away from Allah ﷻ and toward the Fire. The difference in worldview, values, and ultimate purpose creates an environment where one way of life will inevitably try to dominate the other.

This reality becomes especially apparent when people convert to Islam while already married to non-Muslims. The journey of faith creates tremendous changes – not just in dietary habits or clothing, but in one's entire outlook on life, priorities, and daily practices. In societies where certain behaviors (like alcohol consumption) are normalized, suddenly abstaining creates friction. The new Muslim often faces pressure: "Why can't you be like you were before? You were so normal. Don't you love me anymore?" This manipulation, this constant pull away from the straight path, is what Allah ﷻ warns against.

Now, regarding converts who embrace Islam for marriage – this is a delicate matter that requires wisdom. In our communities today, particularly in Western countries, we encounter situations where someone takes shahada in circumstances that seem questionable. Perhaps a young Muslim man developed a relationship with a non-Muslim woman, and now, to marry Islamically, she takes shahada. People naturally become suspicious: "What kind of shahada is that? She's only doing it for him."

But here we must remember the decisive lesson from the incident of Usamah ibn Zayd (RA). During a military encounter, Usamah (RA) was about to kill an enemy combatant when the man suddenly declared, "La ilaha illallah, Muhammad Rasulullah." Usamah (RA), convinced this was merely a ploy to save his life, killed him anyway. When the Prophet ﷺ heard what happened, his response was severe. In one narration from Bukhari, he asked, "Did you open his chest and see for yourself?" In another narration from Muslim, he asked, "What will you do, Usamah, when that 'La ilaha illallah' comes testifying against you on the Day of Judgment?"

This was the clearest case imaginable of someone saying the shahada for the "wrong" reasons – to save his physical life. Yet the Prophet ﷺ made it absolutely clear that no human being has the authority to judge the quality or sincerity of another person's shahada. If this applies in such an obvious case, how much more does it apply in marriage situations where we cannot truly know what's in someone's heart?

Moreover, we're dealing with human beings whose hearts can change. I recently met a woman who had been in a relationship with a Muslim man. To marry him, she converted to Islam because his parents insisted. Initially, her conversion was superficial – she took shahada, got married, had children. But after five or six years, something shifted within her. She became genuinely interested in Islam, started studying seriously, committed to wearing hijab properly, began memorizing Quran, attended study circles, and started teaching her children. Her spiritual awakening was real and deep.

Ironically, her husband started freaking out. He would say, "I wanted you to be Muslim, but not *that* Muslim." She came to me confused: "I came to Islam for him, and now he wants me not to hold onto Islam for him. What should I do?" My response was that the real question is what he should do when he meets Allah ﷻ. These situations are rarely black and white, and only Allah ﷻ knows what's truly inside people's hearts.

The ayah concludes with a profound reminder: وَيُبَيِّنُ آيَاتِهِ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ – "And He clarifies His verses for people so that they may remember." The word يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (yatadhakkarun) is significant – it implies making an effort to remember, specifically when the situation arises. Studying these ayat in a comfortable setting is one thing. Actually remembering and applying them when you're in the heat of the moment, when emotions are running high, when you're making life-changing decisions – that's something entirely different.

How many people actually turn to the Quran for real answers when they're facing these situations? Too often, people get caught up in the moment. When someone tries to remind them of what Allah ﷻ says, the response is, "I'm not in the mood for a lecture right now. I need a real solution" – as if the guidance of Allah ﷻ isn't the most real solution available. Or they say, "Yes, I know what the ayah says, but my situation is special." Everyone thinks their situation is the exception. But Allah ﷻ revealed this final revelation knowing exactly what His slaves go through. This guidance is for everyone, for all times, because He is العليم بكل شيء – the All-Knowing of everything.

translation: And they ask you about menstruation. Say, "It is harm, so keep away from wives during menstruation. And do not approach them until they are pure. And when they have purified themselves, then come to them from where Allah has ordained for you. Indeed, Allah loves those who are constantly repentant and loves those who purify themselves
commentary:
وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْمَحِيضِ ۖ قُلْ هُوَ أَذًى فَٱعْتَزِلُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فِى ٱلْمَحِيضِ ۖ وَلَا تَقْرَبُوهُنَّ حَتَّىٰ يَطْهُرْنَ ۖ فَإِذَا تَطَهَّرْنَ فَأْتُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلتَّوَّٰبِينَ وَيُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ

Having established the fundamental principle that marriages with believers are based on purity, Allah ﷻ now transitions to specific guidance about maintaining that purity within marriage. The connection between these two ayat is significant: mushrik marriages are described as fundamentally impure (نَجَس – najis, as stated later in Surah At-Tawbah 9:28), while believing marriages are built entirely on the foundation of purity (tahara).

"And they ask you about menstruation." The Companions would bring their questions to the Prophet ﷺ, and through these questions, Allah ﷻ would reveal guidance for the entire Ummah. The response comes immediately: "Say: It is a time of pain and difficulty." The word أَذًى (adha) refers to harm, discomfort, and pain. This isn't just describing the physical state but acknowledging the discomfort women experience during this time.

The command follows: فَاعْتَزِلُوا النِّسَاءَ فِي الْمَحِيضِ – "So keep away from women during menstruation." The word اعتزلوا (i'tazilu) means to separate yourself from something. However, this requires proper understanding. This doesn't mean emotional or physical distance in the sense of avoiding your wife altogether or sending her away. It specifically refers to avoiding intimate relations during this time. The woman remains your wife, remains part of the household, remains deserving of love and companionship. What's being restricted is sexual intimacy specifically.

"And do not approach them until they are pure." The verb يَطْهُرْنَ (yathurna) is in a passive-like form, indicating a natural state of purification – when the period has ended and that time of difficulty has passed. But Allah ﷻ doesn't stop there. He adds: فَإِذَا تَطَهَّرْنَ – "But when they have purified themselves" – this refers to the active purification through ghusl (ritual bathing). Only after both conditions are met – the natural end of menstruation and the active purification through bathing – does the restriction lift.

"Then approach them from where Allah has commanded you." This is permission and guidance combined, directing believers back to what is permissible and proper within the marital relationship.

Then comes a beautiful conclusion to this ayah: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ وَيُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ – "Indeed, Allah loves those who constantly repent, and He loves those who purify themselves." Notice the word التَّوَّابِينَ (at-tawwabin) – this isn't just "those who repent" but those who *constantly* repent, who turn to Allah ﷻ over and over again. The pattern of tawwab (with the emphasis on repetition) indicates someone who makes tawbah repeatedly throughout their life.

Why does Allah ﷻ mention His love for those who constantly make tawbah right here? Because the one who makes tawbah is constantly thinking about their shortcomings. This keeps them from arrogance, keeps them grounded, keeps them humble. Tawbah is a road to humility. When you're constantly aware of your need for Allah's forgiveness, you can never become full of yourself. This consciousness – "I always have something to ask Allah's forgiveness for" – prevents spiritual arrogance.

And يُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ – He loves those who purify themselves. This refers to both spiritual purification (through tawbah) and physical purification (through maintaining wudu, ghusl, and cleanliness). These two forms of purification are meant to work together. The external purification reminds you of the internal purification that's needed. When you make wudu before salah, it's not just about physical cleanliness – it's a reminder that you're preparing to stand before Allah ﷻ, and that requires spiritual preparation as well.

This ayah teaches us that even in the intimate relationship between husband and wife, there must be consciousness of Allah ﷻ. Even here, in the most private aspects of marriage, His guidance applies. The boundaries He sets are for wisdom and mercy. They honor the woman's physical and emotional state during a difficult time, they maintain the purity of the relationship, and they keep both spouses conscious that this relationship exists within the framework of divine guidance.

translation: Your wives are a place of sowing of seed for you, so come to your place of cultivation however you wish and put forth [righteousness] for yourselves. And fear Allah and know that you will meet Him. And give good tidings to the believers
commentary:
نِسَآؤُكُمْ حَرْثٌ لَّكُمْ فَأْتُوا۟ حَرْثَكُمْ أَنَّىٰ شِئْتُمْ ۖ وَقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُم مُّلَـٰقُوهُ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

This ayah continues the discussion of the intimate relationship between husband and wife, but now shifts to a broader perspective about the purpose of marriage and intimacy. Allah ﷻ uses a powerful metaphor: "Your wives are a tilth for you" – حَرْثٌ (harthun), which refers to a cultivated field, a crop, a place where seeds are planted to produce.

This metaphor is rich with meaning. A farmer tends to his field with care, waters it, protects it, and waits patiently for it to produce. He doesn't exploit the field; he nurtures it. Similarly, the marital relationship isn't just about satisfying desires – it's about cultivation, about producing something meaningful together. The primary product of this "tilth" is children, the next generation who will carry on after you.

فَأْتُوا حَرْثَكُمْ أَنَّىٰ شِئْتُمْ – "So come to your tilth however you wish." This phrase gives flexibility within the marriage relationship regarding the physical aspect of intimacy, while always maintaining the boundaries Allah ﷻ has set. The emphasis is on mutual consent and maintaining what's permissible.

But then Allah ﷻ immediately shifts the focus from the physical to the spiritual: وَقَدِّمُوا لِأَنفُسِكُمْ – "And send forth for yourselves." This is a profound transition. Yes, you have this physical relationship, but remember – send something forward for yourselves. What does this mean? Have children, yes, but more than that, raise them properly. Build a family that will be a source of ongoing reward for you. The children you raise in faith, who pray for you after you're gone, who carry forward the values you instilled in them – these are what you're "sending forward" for your own souls.

This puts marriage in its proper Islamic perspective. The purpose isn't merely fulfilling desires or enjoying companionship, though both are legitimate. The deeper purpose is creating a family structure that pleases Allah ﷻ, raising righteous children, and building something that extends beyond this temporary life.

Too often in our communities, young people approach marriage with the wrong mindset. They think, "There's too much fitna in society, I need to get married" or "My desires are overwhelming, I need to get married." These aren't sufficient reasons from an Islamic perspective. The Quran describes believing men as مُحْصِنِين (muhsinin) – those who intend to bring women into their fortress, to honor them, protect them, raise a family with them, take responsibility for them. The word muhsin comes from hisn (fortress), indicating protection, honor, and commitment. Marriage isn't just about satisfying hormones or escaping fitna – it's about building something sacred and enduring.

وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ – "And have taqwa of Allah." Even in this most intimate relationship, even in your own bedroom with your spouse, you must maintain consciousness of Allah ﷻ. This isn't about paranoia or removing joy from marriage – it's about sanctifying the relationship, keeping it pure, ensuring it remains within the bounds Allah ﷻ has set.

وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُم مُّلَاقُوهُ – "And know that you will meet Him." This is a powerful reminder. You will stand before Allah ﷻ and answer for how you conducted yourself in this relationship. Were you just, kind, and respectful? Did you honor the trust placed in you? Did you treat your spouse with excellence? These questions will matter on that Day.

وَبَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ – "And give good news to the believers." After discussing the laws and boundaries of marriage, Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ to give glad tidings to the believers. There's good news in all of this. When you approach marriage with the right intention, when you maintain taqwa even in intimate matters, when you remember you'll meet Allah ﷻ – there's tremendous reward waiting for you. Marriage itself becomes an act of worship, a means of drawing closer to Allah ﷻ, and a source of blessing in this life and the next.

translation: And do not make [your oath by] Allah an excuse against being righteous and fearing Allah and making peace among people. And Allah is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عُرْضَةً لِّأَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ وَتُصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

Now Allah ﷻ shifts from marriage to the topic of oaths, but there's a logical connection. Marriage is one of the strongest oaths a person can take. Allah ﷻ describes it as مِيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا – "a strong covenant" (4:21). Having discussed this most serious of oaths, the Quran now addresses how believers should handle all other oaths they take.

The Arabs had a cultural tendency to throw oaths around casually. They would swear by Allah ﷻ for minor things, sometimes without even thinking about it. This habit continues in many Muslim cultures today. Someone might say, "Wallahi, I'm so hungry I could die!" or "Wallahi, if I don't get ice cream, I'm going to lose it!" These casual invocations of Allah's name in exaggerated statements are what this ayah addresses.

"And do not make Allah an obstacle through your oaths" – the word عُرْضَة (urdah) means something that's constantly exposed or used as a barrier. Don't use Allah's name as a barrier that prevents you from doing good. Don't make oaths that keep you from righteous deeds.

The ayah specifies three particular good deeds: أَن تَبَرُّوا وَتَتَّقُوا وَتُصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ – "that you do good, maintain taqwa, and make reconciliation between people."

Here's how this happens: You try to reconcile two people who are fighting. They both curse you out for interfering. In your anger and frustration, you declare, "Wallahi, I will never try to make peace between people again! Wallahi, I'm done with reconciliation!" Now you've used Allah's name to swear away from doing a good deed. This is exactly what Allah ﷻ forbids here.

Or someone asks you to help with a charitable cause, and you say, "Wallahi, I'll never give to that organization again after what happened last time." You've now made an oath that prevents you from potential good. Or you have a falling out with a family member and swear, "Wallahi, I will never speak to my uncle again." These are the kinds of foolish oaths this ayah warns against.

Allah ﷻ is telling us: Don't be stupid with your oaths. Don't use My name to lock yourself into avoiding good deeds. If you made such an oath in anger or haste, break it and make kaffarah (expiation), but don't let a foolish oath prevent you from doing what's right.

وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ – "And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing." This ending is significant. He hears when you invoke His name carelessly. He hears when you use His name to justify staying away from good deeds. He knows your intentions behind these oaths. He knows when you're using religion as an excuse for your grudges and ego. This is both a warning and a reminder: you can't hide behind religious language to justify bad behavior. Allah ﷻ knows exactly what you're doing.

translation: Allah does not impose blame upon you for what is unintentional in your oaths, but He imposes blame upon you for what your hearts have earned. And Allah is Forgiving and Forbearing
commentary:
لَّا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ

This ayah provides relief after the warning of the previous verse. Allah ﷻ distinguishes between two types of oaths: those that are mere لَغْو (laghw – meaningless talk, idle chatter) and those that are actual commitments from the heart.

"Allah will not hold you accountable for laghw in your oaths" – what is laghw? It's when something just rolls off your tongue without real intention. You're talking with friends and you say, "Man, if the team doesn't win tonight, I'm done with sports forever!" or "Wallahi, that was the best meal I've ever had!" These exaggerated expressions that people use in conversation, where Allah's name is mentioned but there's no actual oath being made, no real commitment intended – Allah ﷻ says He won't grab you for these.

You might say, "I'm just saying," and Allah ﷻ knows when you're "just saying." He knows the difference between casual speech and actual oaths. This is from His mercy and His understanding of human nature.

وَلَٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ – "But He will hold you accountable for what your hearts have earned." This is the key distinction. There are two types of oaths: what's on your tongue and what's in your heart. When you meant it – when your heart was committed to what you said – that's when it becomes a real oath. When you didn't mean it, when it was just loose talk, then it's only on your tongue, not in your heart.

This teaches us something profound about accountability before Allah ﷻ. He doesn't judge by appearances or by the words themselves. He judges by what's in the heart. Two people might say the exact same words, but one is joking and one is serious – Allah ﷻ knows the difference. He knows the intention behind every word that comes out of your mouth.

وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ – "And Allah is Forgiving, Forbearing." After explaining the serious nature of intentional oaths, Allah ﷻ reminds us of His forgiveness and forbearance. The word حَلِيم (halim) is particularly beautiful here. Hilm refers to the kind of patience and tolerance a mother has for her child. The child might misbehave, the mother might scold him, but even while she's upset, there's still love there. Right after disciplining him, she'll pat his head, give him extra food, show him affection – because she has hilm.

Allah ﷻ has this quality of hilm toward us. Even when we mess up with our oaths, when we speak carelessly, when we make mistakes – He's willing to forgive, He has tolerance for our weaknesses, He has this loving forbearance. This should give us comfort, but it shouldn't make us careless. We should still strive to be mindful of how we use Allah's name, while also taking comfort in knowing that He understands our human nature and is ready to forgive.

translation: For those who swear not to have sexual relations with their wives is a waiting time of four months, but if they return [to normal relations] - then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
لِّلَّذِينَ يُؤْلُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ تَرَبُّصُ أَرْبَعَةِ أَشْهُرٍ ۖ فَإِن فَآءُو فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Having discussed oaths in general, Allah ﷻ now addresses a specific type of oath that was practiced in pre-Islamic Arabia and unfortunately continued into Muslim society: the oath of ila (إيلاء). This is when a husband, in a moment of anger, swears that he will have nothing to do with his wife – sometimes saying, "I swear I won't touch you until the Day of Judgment" or making some other exaggerated, eternal oath to stay away from his wife.

This practice was a form of abuse. A man would make this oath, effectively suspending the marriage without actually divorcing his wife. She would remain married – unable to marry anyone else – but denied her rights as a wife. She'd be stuck in limbo, neither properly married nor divorced. It was a way of punishing and controlling women.

"For those who take an oath of abstention from their wives" – this is ila, swearing to stay away from intimacy with one's wife – "there is a waiting period of four months." Allah ﷻ puts a deadline on this ridiculous practice. Even if the husband said "forever" or "until Judgment Day," Allah ﷻ says the maximum time this can last is four months. This protects the woman from indefinite abuse.

فَإِن فَاءُوا – "If they return" – if within those four months the husband comes to his senses, recognizes his foolishness, and reconciles with his wife – فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ – "then indeed Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." Allah ﷻ will forgive this foolish behavior if he corrects it, if he returns to treating his wife properly.

This ayah reveals Allah's mercy toward both spouses. For the husband who made a foolish oath in anger, there's a way back. He has four months to cool down, to think clearly, to recognize his mistake and return to his wife with dignity and reconciliation. For the wife being mistreated, there's protection – she won't be left in limbo forever. Either the husband reconciles within four months, or the marriage must end.

This Islamic ruling completely abolished the pre-Islamic practice of using ila as a form of ongoing punishment. Islam doesn't allow a man to keep his wife suspended indefinitely. Either maintain the marriage properly or end it decisively. The woman's rights and dignity must be respected.

translation: And if they decide on divorce - then indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
وَإِنْ عَزَمُوا۟ ٱلطَّلَـٰقَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah continues from the previous one. If the four months pass and the husband has not reconciled, or if at any point he makes a firm decision: "And if they have resolved on divorce" – the word عَزَمُوا (azamu) is significant. It means to make a firm, determined decision. This isn't "maybe I should divorce" or "I'm thinking about it." عَزْم (azm) is decisive resolve – there's no doubt left, the decision has been made firmly.

"Then indeed Allah is Hearing, Knowing" – سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ. This might seem like a simple reminder, but its placement here is powerful. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't mention His forgiveness and mercy here like He did in the previous ayah. When the husband reconciles, Allah ﷻ says He's Forgiving and Merciful. But when the firm decision for divorce is made, Allah ﷻ says He's Hearing and Knowing.

Why this difference? Because Allah ﷻ heard the resolve in the husband's heart even before it was spoken. He knows what led to this decision. He knows whether it's being done justly or unjustly. He knows if the husband exhausted all efforts to save the marriage or if he's being hasty. He knows if the reasons are valid or based on ego and stubbornness.

This is both a warning and a witness. The husband might think his resolve is private, that his determination to divorce is just in his own heart. But Allah ﷻ is saying: I heard it. I know about it. And you'll answer to Me for this decision. Were you just? Were you patient? Did you try everything? Or are you being hasty and unfair?

The placement of these divine attributes – Hearing and Knowing – serves as a reminder that even the most private decisions, the thoughts that never leave your heart, are fully known to Allah ﷻ. This should make every person considering divorce pause and reflect: Is this really the right decision? Have I exhausted every option? Am I being fair? Because Allah ﷻ knows, and I'll have to answer to Him.

translation: Divorced women remain in waiting for three periods, and it is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs if they believe in Allah and the Last Day. And their husbands have more right to take them back in this [period] if they want reconciliation. And due to the wives is similar to what is expected of them, according to what is reasonable. But the men have a degree over them [in responsibility and authority]. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
وَٱلْمُطَلَّقَـٰتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ ثَلَـٰثَةَ قُرُوٓءٍ ۚ وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَهُنَّ أَن يَكْتُمْنَ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَرْحَامِهِنَّ إِن كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ وَبُعُولَتُهُنَّ أَحَقُّ بِرَدِّهِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ إِنْ أَرَادُوٓا۟ إِصْلَـٰحًا ۚ وَلَهُنَّ مِثْلُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ وَلِلرِّجَالِ عَلَيْهِنَّ دَرَجَةٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the situation of women who have been divorced – specifically, revocable divorce (talaq raj'i), where there's still possibility of reconciliation. "And divorced women" – الْمُطَلَّقَاتُ (al-mutallaqat) – "shall wait concerning themselves for three periods" – ثَلَاثَةَ قُرُوءٍ (thalathata quru), which refers to three menstrual cycles.

The word يَتَرَبَّصْنَ (yatarabbasna) means to wait, to observe a waiting period. This is the iddah period, and it serves multiple purposes: it allows time to confirm whether the woman is pregnant, it provides a cooling-off period where reconciliation might happen, and it maintains the dignity and clarity of family lineages.

Many scholars emphasized that during this waiting period, the woman should ideally remain in the husband's house. Why? Because physical proximity makes reconciliation easier. When couples are separated during their iddah, the distance can make the rift grow wider. They stew in their anger separately, they vent to their friends who fuel the fire, and reconciliation becomes increasingly difficult. But when they're still under the same roof – not talking perhaps, walking around each other, dealing with the awkwardness – eventually the silence breaks. Someone asks, "Want some ice cream?" and the other says "Okay," and slowly, gradually, they remember what they once had together. Alhamdulillah, this is wisdom from Allah ﷻ.

وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَهُنَّ أَن يَكْتُمْنَ مَا خَلَقَ اللَّهُ فِي أَرْحَامِهِنَّ – "And it is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs." This addresses a specific scenario: the divorced woman discovers she's pregnant, but she's so angry at her husband that she doesn't want to tell him. Maybe she thinks, "He doesn't deserve to know" or "I'll raise this child myself" or "This is my way of punishing him."

Allah ﷻ says no – it's not permissible for her to hide this pregnancy. إِن كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ – "If they believe in Allah and the Last Day." This phrase is crucial. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "if he deserves to know" or "if he was a good husband." The condition is belief in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day. If she truly believes, she'll tell him – not because of him, but because Allah ﷻ commanded it.

This is a profound lesson about obedience to Allah ﷻ. We can always find reasons to justify not doing what's right: "But he wronged me," "But she hurt me," "But they don't deserve it." Allah ﷻ cuts through all of that. Your reasons for obedience should never be based on whether someone deserves something. Your reason is simply: Allah commanded it, and you believe in Him and the Day you'll meet Him.

وَبُعُولَتُهُنَّ أَحَقُّ بِرَدِّهِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ إِنْ أَرَادُوا إِصْلَاحًا – "And their husbands have more right to take them back during this period, if they wish for reconciliation." The word بُعُولَة (bu'ulah) specifically means husbands. During the iddah period, if the husband says, "I'm sorry, I want you back," and sincerely wants to reconcile, he has the right to nullify that divorce and restore the marriage.

But notice the condition: إِنْ أَرَادُوا إِصْلَاحًا – "if they wish for reconciliation" – literally, "if they intend islah (repair, correction, improvement)." The husband can't just take her back to continue the same problems, or worse, to take revenge and punish her more. The intention must be genuine reconciliation, genuine repair of what was broken. This is the spirit in which he can exercise this right.

Now, this might seem like an imbalance. The husband can nullify the divorce even if the wife initially wanted to stay separated? He has more say in this decision? This could appear to disadvantage women. But Allah ﷻ immediately addresses this concern:

وَلَهُنَّ مِثْلُ الَّذِي عَلَيْهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ – "And they (women) have rights similar to those against them, according to what is recognized as fair." This is a beautiful, comprehensive principle. Whatever rights men have over women, women have similar rights over men. Whatever responsibilities women have toward men, men have similar responsibilities toward women. The word مِثْل (mithl) means "similar to" or "like" – the equation is balanced.

Yes, in this specific case of divorce and reconciliation, men have a particular advantage. But in other areas of Islamic law, women have advantages that men don't have. For example, a woman's wealth is entirely her own – she has no obligation to spend on the household. A man's wealth includes responsibility for his wife and children. What's his is shared; what's hers is purely hers. This is just one example of how the "equation" is balanced bil ma'ruf (in a recognized, fair way) across the entire system of Islamic law.

وَلِلرِّجَالِ عَلَيْهِنَّ دَرَجَةٌ – "And men have a degree over them." This phrase requires careful understanding. It's crucial to note that this statement is embedded within a specific discussion about divorce and reconciliation. It's not a universal declaration about male superiority over women in all matters at all times. The context matters enormously. In this particular case – the case of taking back a divorced wife during iddah – men have been given a degree of advantage, a degree more of authority in that specific decision.

Some people extract this phrase from its context and use it to justify all kinds of inappropriate behavior, but that's a misuse of the Quran. The phrase is specifically about the issue being discussed: the right to reconcile during the iddah period.

وَاللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ – "And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise." After establishing these laws about marriage, divorce, and reconciliation, Allah ﷻ reminds us of two of His attributes. He is عَزِيز (Aziz) – the Almighty, the Ultimate Authority. Yes, men might have a degree of authority over women in certain matters, but they are not the ultimate authority. Allah ﷻ is. Men will answer to Him for how they used whatever authority they were given.

And He is حَكِيم (Hakim) – the All-Wise. These laws aren't arbitrary. There's wisdom in every detail, in every right given, in every restriction placed. The system He designed balances justice, mercy, practicality, and spiritual growth. Even if we don't immediately understand every wisdom behind every ruling, we trust that the All-Wise has designed a system that serves the best interests of humanity.

translation: Divorce is twice. Then, either keep [her] in an acceptable manner or release [her] with good treatment. And it is not lawful for you to take anything of what you have given them unless both fear that they will not be able to keep [within] the limits of Allah. But if you fear that they will not keep [within] the limits of Allah, then there is no blame upon either of them concerning that by which she ransoms herself. These are the limits of Allah, so do not transgress them. And whoever transgresses the limits of Allah - it is those who are the wrongdoers
commentary:
ٱلطَّلَـٰقُ مَرَّتَانِ ۖ فَإِمْسَاكٌۢ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌۢ بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ ۗ وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِمَّآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّآ أَن يَخَافَآ أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا فِيمَا ٱفْتَدَتْ بِهِۦ ۗ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَعْتَدُوهَا ۚ وَمَن يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

"Divorce is twice" – الطَّلَاقُ مَرَّتَانِ. This establishes a crucial principle: revocable divorce can happen twice. These are the two times you can take your wife back during the iddah period without any special conditions. After saying talaq once, you can reconcile. After saying it a second time, you can still reconcile. But this isn't unlimited. The system isn't designed for people to play games, divorcing and reconciling repeatedly.

فَإِمْسَاكٌ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌ بِإِحْسَانٍ – "Then either keep them in a good manner or release them with excellence." This is the choice after those two instances: either you hold onto the marriage بِمَعْرُوفٍ – in a decent, honorable, respectful way – or you let her go بِإِحْسَانٍ – with excellence, with kindness, with dignity.

Notice the beautiful word choice. When it comes to keeping the marriage, Allah ﷻ says bil ma'ruf – according to what's known and recognized as decent. Everyone knows what it means to be a good husband or a good wife. You don't need to be Muslim to understand basic human decency in a relationship. It's universal – treat each other with respect, kindness, and honor. That's ma'ruf.

But when it comes to ending the marriage, Allah ﷻ uses an even higher word: بِإِحْسَانٍ (bi-ihsan) – with excellence, with going above and beyond. Even when letting someone go, even when the relationship is ending, do it in the most beautiful way possible. Don't just meet the minimum standard of decency – exceed it. Be generous, be kind, be respectful. This is the character of a believer even in the most difficult situations.

وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَأْخُذُوا مِمَّا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْئًا – "And it is not lawful for you to take back anything of what you have given them." This addresses a common practice where a husband, upon divorcing his wife, demands back the gifts he gave her during the marriage. He might say, "I gave you that jewelry when I loved you. I don't love you anymore, so I want it back" or "That money I gave you for your business – I want a refund."

Allah ﷻ says absolutely not. What you've given is given. It's hers. You can't take it back. You can't even ask for it back. This protects the woman's dignity and her property rights. Just because the relationship is ending doesn't mean you can reclaim everything you ever gave her.

إِلَّا أَن يَخَافَا أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ اللَّهِ – "Unless they both fear that they will not be able to keep within the limits ordained by Allah." This is the exception. If both the husband and wife recognize that they've crossed serious lines, that they've wronged each other, that there are legitimate grievances on both sides, then they can make some kind of mutual compensation. She might say, "Keep the mahr, I don't want to owe you anything on Judgment Day" or he might say, "Take extra, I wronged you and I want to make it right."

This is called khul' – when there's mutual agreement to end the marriage with some kind of financial settlement that brings closure and removes grudges. The key is mutual agreement: فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا فِيمَا افْتَدَتْ بِهِ – "Then there is no blame upon either of them concerning what she gives to secure her release."

تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ فَلَا تَعْتَدُوهَا – "These are the limits set by Allah, so do not transgress them." The word حُدُود (hudud) appears repeatedly in these ayat about marriage and divorce. Hudud are boundaries, limits, lines you don't cross. Allah ﷻ has set these boundaries for our protection, for justice, for maintaining the social fabric. They're not arbitrary restrictions meant to make life difficult – they're protective boundaries that ensure fairness and dignity for everyone involved.

وَمَن يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ اللَّهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ – "And whoever transgresses the limits of Allah – then those are the wrongdoers." The word الظَّالِمُونَ (adh-dhalimun) is powerful. These aren't just people who "made a mistake" – they are the wrongdoers, the oppressors. When you cross Allah's boundaries in divorce, when you take what doesn't belong to you, when you abuse the system He designed for justice – you become an oppressor. You're not just wronging your spouse; you're wronging your own soul, and ultimately, you're transgressing against Allah ﷻ Himself.

translation: And if he has divorced her [for the third time], then she is not lawful to him afterward until [after] she marries a husband other than him. And if the latter husband divorces her [or dies], there is no blame upon the woman and her former husband for returning to each other if they think that they can keep [within] the limits of Allah. These are the limits of Allah, which He makes clear to a people who know
commentary:
فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا تَحِلُّ لَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدُ حَتَّىٰ تَنكِحَ زَوْجًا غَيْرَهُۥ ۗ فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَآ أَن يَتَرَاجَعَآ إِن ظَنَّآ أَن يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ يُبَيِّنُهَا لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ

"Then if he divorces her" – this is the third and final divorce. Remember, the previous ayah established that revocable divorce is twice. This is what comes after that. فَلَا تَحِلُّ لَهُ مِن بَعْدُ – "Then she is not lawful for him afterward" – this is it, the marriage is completely finished.

حَتَّىٰ تَنكِحَ زَوْجًا غَيْرَهُ – "Until she marries another husband." If this woman, after the finality of this divorce, meets someone else, falls in love, gets married, and then that marriage also ends in divorce, only then can the original husband and wife consider remarrying each other.

فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا أَن يَتَرَاجَعَا – "Then if he (the new husband) divorces her, there is no blame upon either of them if they return to each other." At that point, the original couple can remarry, but with a crucial condition: إِن ظَنَّا أَن يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ اللَّهِ – "if they think they can keep the limits of Allah."

The word ظَنَّا (dhanna) here means to think, to believe, to have reasonable confidence. They both need to genuinely believe that this time will be different, that they'll respect Allah's boundaries, that they've matured and learned and grown enough to make the marriage work.

This law serves multiple profound purposes. First, it's a deterrent against playing games with divorce. In some cultures, people have so little taqwa that a man will say "talaq, talaq, talaq" in one breath during an argument – three divorces in one shot. Then later, when he cools down, he'll call a scholar: "Does that count as one or three?" The question itself reveals the problem. How dare you speak like that knowing Allah ﷻ is listening? How dare you play games with His law?

In Pakistan and other places, when this happens and people realize the marriage is actually over, some try to game the system further. They'll arrange a sham marriage – pay some man to "marry" the woman for a day, then divorce her, so the original couple can remarry. This is called tahlil marriage, and it's a complete mockery of Allah's Sharia. It's disgusting. As I've said, if the followers of Musa (AS) were still around from the time when they tried to circumvent the Sabbath fishing laws, they'd want these people's autograph – because this level of legal manipulation is even more creative in its shamelessness.

Allah ﷻ is teaching us that actions have consequences. If you divorce your wife three times, that's it – you've destroyed that relationship through your own foolishness. You can't just arrange a fake marriage to get around the law. This isn't a loophole to be exploited; it's a boundary set by Allah ﷻ that's meant to make you think very, very carefully before you divorce your wife even once, let alone three times.

وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ يُبَيِّنُهَا لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ – "And these are the limits of Allah which He makes clear for people who know." Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't say "for people who believe" – He says لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (li-qawmin ya'lamun) – "for people who want to know," people who actually seek knowledge.

These laws are crystal clear, but only for those who want to understand them, who approach them seeking guidance. For people who don't want to know, who've already made up their minds about what they're going to do regardless of what Allah ﷻ says – for them, no amount of clarification will help. You can give them a hundred lectures, sit them in countless counseling sessions, but if they don't want to hear it, they won't benefit.

This is both empowering and sobering. Allah's guidance is available, clear, and accessible – but only for those who sincerely seek it. When Muslims are going through divorce situations, the tragedy is that so few actually turn to the Book of Allah ﷻ to learn how to handle it properly. They turn to lawyers, they turn to their families, they turn to their friends – but they don't turn to Allah's guidance. These ayat are only beneficial لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ – for people who genuinely want to know and apply what Allah ﷻ has taught.

translation: And when you divorce women and they have [nearly] fulfilled their term, either retain them according to acceptable terms or release them according to acceptable terms, and do not keep them, intending harm, to transgress [against them]. And whoever does that has certainly wronged himself. And do not take the verses of Allah in jest. And remember the favor of Allah upon you and what has been revealed to you of the Book and wisdom by which He instructs you. And fear Allah and know that Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ سَرِّحُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ ۚ وَلَا تُمْسِكُوهُنَّ ضِرَارًا لِّتَعْتَدُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلْحِكْمَةِ يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

"And when you divorce women and they have reached their term" – when the iddah period is approaching its end – فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ سَرِّحُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ – "then keep them in an acceptable manner or release them in an acceptable manner."

This is the critical moment – the end of the iddah period. At this point, the husband must make a clear decision: either reconcile properly (بِمَعْرُوفٍ – with decency and honor) or let her go completely (بِمَعْرُوفٍ – also with decency and honor). Notice that ma'ruf is used for both options. Whether keeping or releasing, it must be done in a way that everyone recognizes as decent and fair.

وَلَا تُمْسِكُوهُنَّ ضِرَارًا لِّتَعْتَدُوا – "And do not keep them in order to harm them or to transgress." This addresses a specific type of abuse that Allah ﷻ explicitly forbids. Here's the scenario: a man has divorced his wife, and he's already decided in his heart that he's going to let her go. But he wants to torture her some more before he does. So he waits until the very end of the iddah period, and just before it expires, he takes her back – not because he wants to reconcile, but just so he can harm her more, prolong her suffering, get his revenge.

The word ضِرَارًا (diraran) means specifically to cause harm, to hurt someone intentionally. Allah ﷻ is saying: Don't hold them back just to harm them, just to get your grudges out, just to cross boundaries and limits. This is pure abuse, using the law as a weapon to hurt someone.

وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُ – "And whoever does that has certainly wronged himself." This is the first consequence Allah ﷻ mentions. Notice He doesn't say "has wronged his wife" – though that's obviously true. He says "has wronged himself." When you abuse your wife like this, when you use Allah's laws as a tool for revenge, you're not primarily hurting her – you're destroying your own soul. You're corrupting your own character. You're setting yourself up for Allah's punishment. The victim of your abuse will heal and move on eventually, but you? You've damaged your own akhirah.

وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا آيَاتِ اللَّهِ هُزُوًا – "And do not take the verses of Allah in mockery." This man who's playing games with divorce and reconciliation, who's using the iddah period as a tool to torture his wife – he's making a mockery of Allah's ayat. He's saying with his actions, "I can use these laws however I want. I'll take advantage of every loophole. I'll manipulate every rule to serve my ego and my revenge." This is treating Allah's guidance as a joke, as something to be gamed and exploited.

This portion of the ayah – from here to the end – is entirely dedicated to calling out this type of scumbag behavior. It's a man who uses Islam as a shield while abusing his wife, who says "Sharia allows me to take her back" while his intention is only to hurt her more.

وَاذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ – "And remember the favor of Allah upon you." Allah ﷻ has blessed you with Islam, guided you to faith, given you a Muslim wife, established laws to protect marriages and families. And you're going to repay these favors by abusing His ayat? By tormenting one of His female servants? Remember Allah's favor upon you and have shame.

وَمَا أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ الْكِتَابِ وَالْحِكْمَةِ يَعِظُكُم بِهِ – "And what He has sent down to you of the Book and wisdom by which He instructs you." Allah ﷻ sent you the Quran and the wisdom of the Prophet's teachings ﷺ – and part of that wisdom is marriage counseling. Real, divine marriage counseling. Guidance on how to treat your wife, how to handle conflicts, how to end a relationship if it must end. This guidance is your wa'dh (counsel, admonition) from Allah ﷻ. Will you take it?

وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ – "And have taqwa of Allah." Be mindful of Him. Protect yourself from His punishment. Remember that He's watching. You think you're getting away with this abuse because she can't defend herself, because maybe her family is weak, because society looks the other way? Allah ﷻ sees everything you're doing. You're not getting away with anything. Have taqwa.

وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ – "And know that Allah is Knowing of all things." He knows everything you're plotting. The psychological torture you're planning, the revenge you're scheming, the ways you're finding to hurt her within "legal" bounds – He knows it all. Even the things she can't articulate to others, the pain she keeps inside because it's too difficult to explain – Allah ﷻ knows those things too. He is بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ – All-Knowing of everything.

This ending is both a warning and a promise. For the abuser, it's a severe warning: you will be held accountable for every bit of harm you cause. For the abused, it's a promise: Allah ﷻ knows what you're going through even when no one else does, and He will bring justice.

translation: And when you divorce women and they have fulfilled their term, do not prevent them from remarrying their [former] husbands if they agree among themselves on an acceptable basis. That is instructed to whoever of you believes in Allah and the Last Day. That is better for you and purer, and Allah knows and you know not
commentary:
وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ أَن يَنكِحْنَ أَزْوَٰجَهُنَّ إِذَا تَرَٰضَوْا۟ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ يُوعَظُ بِهِۦ مَن كَانَ مِنكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۗ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses a different scenario. The previous ayah dealt with an abusive husband. This ayah deals with the opposite situation: a husband and wife who are both good people, both Muslims trying to do the right thing, but they went through a divorce. Now the iddah period has ended or is ending, and here's what's happening: the wife is considering reconciliation. The husband wants her back. He's asking for another chance. The thought is crossing her mind that maybe, just maybe, they could make it work.

But there's interference. Maybe it's her family: "Don't you dare go back to him!" Maybe it's her friends: "How stupid can you be? He divorced you! You're free now – don't go back!" Maybe it's her parents: "We told you from the beginning he was no good. Don't bring shame on this family by going back to him."

This external pressure, this constant bombardment of "Don't remarry him, don't be foolish, stay divorced" – this is what Allah ﷻ addresses.

فَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ أَن يَنكِحْنَ أَزْوَاجَهُنَّ – "Do not prevent them from remarrying their husbands." The word تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ (ta'dulu-hunna) is from عَضْل (adl), which means to strictly forbid, to create barriers, to prevent something from happening through strong opposition. This is more than just advising – it's forcefully blocking, creating obstacles, making it practically impossible for her to remarry her former husband even though she might want to.

إِذَا تَرَاضَوْا بَيْنَهُم بِالْمَعْرُوفِ – "When they have reached a mutual agreement between themselves in a proper manner." If the husband and wife have talked, worked things out, come to an understanding, and they both want to try again – and they're doing this بِالْمَعْرُوفٍ (in a way that's recognized as decent and proper) – then the family and friends have no right to interfere. The decision is between the husband and wife.

ذَٰلِكَ يُوعَظُ بِهِ مَن كَانَ مِنكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ – "That is instructed to whoever among you believes in Allah and the Last Day." Notice the passive voice: يُوعَظُ (yu'adhu) – "is being advised," "is being instructed." Allah ﷻ doesn't say "this is the advice I'm giving you." He uses the passive form. Why?

Because whoever interferes in this situation, whoever prevents a legitimate reconciliation between husband and wife, Allah ﷻ is displeased with them. He doesn't even want to mention Himself in connection with them. The message is: if you truly believe in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day, this instruction is for you. If you claim to be a believer but you still interfere, you'll answer for it. Don't say you believe in the Last Day and then act like you won't be questioned about preventing someone's lawful marriage.

ذَٰلِكُمْ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ – "That is purer for you and more purifying." The word أَزْكَىٰ (azka) refers to spiritual purity and growth. أَطْهَرُ (athar) refers to physical and material purity. Allah ﷻ is saying: not interfering, letting them make their own decision, supporting their reconciliation if they choose it – this is spiritually better for you and materially better for you.

How is it materially better?

translation: Mothers may breastfeed their children two complete years for whoever wishes to complete the nursing [period]. Upon the father is the mothers' provision and their clothing according to what is acceptable. No person is charged with more than his capacity. No mother should be harmed through her child, and no father through his child. And upon the [father's] heir is [a duty] like that [of the father]. And if they both desire weaning through mutual consent from both of them and consultation, there is no blame upon either of them. And if you wish to have your children nursed by a substitute, there is no blame upon you as long as you give payment according to what is acceptable. And fear Allah and know that Allah is Seeing of what you do

And those who are taken in death among you and leave wives behind - they, [the wives, shall] wait four months and ten [days]. And when they have fulfilled their term, then there is no blame upon you for what they do with themselves in an acceptable manner. And Allah is [fully] Acquainted with what you do

There is no blame upon you for that to which you [indirectly] allude concerning a proposal to women or for what you conceal within yourselves. Allah knows that you will have them in mind. But do not promise them secretly except for saying a proper saying. And do not determine to undertake a marriage contract until the decreed period reaches its end. And know that Allah knows what is within yourselves, so beware of Him. And know that Allah is Forgiving and Forbearing

There is no blame upon you if you divorce women you have not touched nor specified for them an obligation. But give them [a gift of] compensation - the wealthy according to his capability and the poor according to his capability - a provision according to what is acceptable, a duty upon the doers of good

And if you divorce them before you have touched them and you have already specified for them an obligation, then [give] half of what you specified - unless they forego the right or the one in whose hand is the marriage contract foregoes it. And to forego it is nearer to righteousness. And do not forget graciousness between you. Indeed Allah, of whatever you do, is Seeing
Maintain with care the [obligatory] prayers and [in particular] the middle prayer and stand before Allah, devoutly obedient
And if you fear [an enemy, then pray] on foot or riding. But when you are secure, then remember Allah [in prayer], as He has taught you that which you did not [previously] know
