The imagery continues from earlier: when you hold onto an anchor, your concern is whether the chain will hold. What if there's a weak link? What if it snaps? Allah ﷻ removes that fear: This chain has no breaking point whatsoever.

### The Closing

وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ - "And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing."

Why these two names here? Because سَمِيعٌ - He hears everything. He hears what the tongue says, but more importantly, He hears what the heart says. Some people declare faith outwardly but haven't truly believed inside. Some people have believed inside but can't yet declare it outside because of fear or circumstances.

Allah ﷻ knows exactly who has genuinely grabbed hold of this anchor and who hasn't, regardless of what their mouth says or what their external situation looks like.

عَلِيمٌ - He is All-Knowing. He knows the struggles of those holding on. He knows the strength it takes. He knows when someone is about to slip, and He knows when someone needs His help to keep holding.

This isn't just knowledge - it's the knowledge that leads to action. Because He knows, He helps. Because He hears, He responds. The believer isn't holding on alone in isolation. Allah ﷻ is سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ - actively aware of their struggle and actively supporting them.

These four ayahs together form a powerful sequence: the rankings among prophets and human disagreement despite clear guidance, the urgent call to spend before a Day of no bargaining, the comprehensive declaration of Allah's attributes in Ayatul Kursi, and finally the principle that with such clarity, there can be no compulsion - only an invitation to grab hold of the unbreakable lifeline that Allah ﷻ has extended to all of humanity.

translation: Allah is the ally of those who believe. He brings them out from darknesses into the light. And those who disbelieve - their allies are Taghut. They take them out of the light into darknesses. Those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide eternally therein
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُمُ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُم مِّنَ ٱلنُّورِ إِلَى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

This ayah builds upon the previous conversation about holding onto the strongest anchor. Someone who grasps onto Allah ﷻ doesn't merely receive protection during the storm—they establish a permanent relationship of protection with Him. Allah ﷻ has now become their protective friend, their Wali.

The Quran uses two distinct terms to describe this mutual relationship between us and Allah ﷻ: Wali and Mawla. Both appear in this Surah, with Mawla coming at the end: "That is because Allah is the Mawla of those who believe, while the disbelievers have no Mawla" (47:11). Understanding the difference between these terms reveals something beautiful about our relationship with Allah ﷻ.

Some of Allah's names simply describe His attributes. Others depict a relationship, necessarily implying someone on the other end. These are names like this one. Consider the difference between being protective of someone versus actually protecting them. A protective attitude indicates desire—your older brother might be protective of you, your parents protective of you, your spouse protective of you. But even with that protective sentiment, they may not always be able to actually protect you. These are two different things.

Wali refers to a protective friend—someone who has the attitude and desire to protect you. Mawla refers to a protecting friend—someone who actively engages in protecting you. One depicts sentiment, the other action. This distinction becomes profound when we examine how the Quran uses these terms.

Allah ﷻ is our Wali. This ayah establishes that believers have Someone who wants to protect them. Disbelievers also have a wali—taghut, entities that rebel against Allah ﷻ and call others to rebellion. So both groups have someone protective of them. But here's what's remarkable: in this Surah, Allah ﷻ is our Wali. In Surah Muhammad, He's our Mawla. We have both—Someone who is protective of us and Someone who actually protects us. But when it comes to disbelievers having a Mawla, the Quran explicitly states: "That is because Allah is the Mawla of those who believe, while the disbelievers have no Mawla" (47:11). They have entities that want to protect them, but no one actually protecting them. We have both Allah's intent to protect us (Wali) and His act of protecting us (Mawla). They have neither.

Notice how this ayah structures the relationship. Allah ﷻ is mentioned first: "Allah is the Wali of those who believe." In any relationship, there's typically a primary party and a secondary party—teacher and student, parent and child, master and slave, employer and employee. In this protective friendship, the One who is the Wali occupies the primary role. Those who are protected occupy the secondary role. Allah ﷻ takes the primary position in this sentence, and "those who believe" are the recipients.

Now observe what happens with the contrasting relationship later in the ayah: "And those who disbelieve—their allies are taghut." Who is the wali here? Taghut. So the primary entity in this opposing relationship is taghut. Yet grammatically, you would expect the ayah to say "Taghut is the wali of those who disbelieve," just as it said "Allah is the Wali of those who believe." But it doesn't. Taghut gets pushed to the end of the sentence.

Though the relationships are being compared, taghut doesn't deserve to be mentioned in the same grammatical position where Allah ﷻ was mentioned. Even structurally, the ayah refuses to give taghut that place. It's as if Allah ﷻ is saying: you don't belong in the beginning where I was mentioned. Go to the end. This is the Majesty of our Lord—the ayah's very structure belittles taghut. This is the kind of beauty you cannot capture in translation.

Allah ﷻ is the protective friend of those who believe—those who held onto that anchor and established a relationship of friendship with Him based on His protection. Now picture the scene: you're in the ocean on a dark night, holding onto this anchor during a storm. Sometimes you might dip under the water and come back up, go under again and emerge again. This is captured in the phrase: يُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ - "He brings them out of darkness into light."

The verb yukhriju is present tense, indicating continuous, repeated action. Allah ﷻ keeps pulling you out, again and again. There are different shades of darkness people come from—shirk, kufr, hypocrisy, atheism. Allah ﷻ brings people from these various darknesses into the light of Islam.

But even after entering Islam, this process continues. You can fall into darkness again, though these may be more subtle forms. Before Islam, you might be in the obvious darkness of polytheism or disbelief. After Islam, you might fall into the darkness of worldliness, arrogance, jealousy, greed, or hypocrisy. Muslims can have all of these. Those who hold onto the anchor will still feel the pull of the dunya—we're not beyond it. But so long as they've made the decision to hold on, Allah ﷻ keeps helping them out.

Notice the linguistic precision: الظُّلُمَاتِ - darknesses - is plural, while النُّورِ - light - is singular. The deeper you go into water, the darker it gets—less darkness, more darkness, complete darkness. Multiple degrees of darkness exist. But when you emerge into light, there aren't degrees—it's just light. Just as in the depths of the ocean, there are layers of darkness, but at the surface, there's simply light.

The contrasting picture for disbelievers is devastating: "And those who disbelieve—their allies are taghut. They take them out of the light into darknesses." We learn here that some people actually see the light of Islam, they see the light of the Quran, but taghut whispers: "Don't go crazy. Come back. It was more comfortable in the dark." They pull people back, then the person sees a little light again, and taghut pulls them back again. This tug-of-war continues.

This even happens to Muslims. A Muslim attends Jumu'ah, something clicks—"I need to change." But right after, a text arrives: "Want to catch a movie?" They get pulled back. Next week, they hear something else, it clicks again, it lasts until Asr. Then another text: "Want to hit up the party?" Pulled back again. The next Jumu'ah, they get a little light, then get pulled back. Taghut keeps pulling you down.

If you keep struggling against taghut and coming up for light, eventually you'll be saved—Allah ﷻ keeps pulling you up. But if you've accepted taghut as your protective friend, then you'll eventually drown. You'll get completely sucked in. It's not just one entity of taghut—أَوْلِيَآؤُهُمُ is plural. Multiple sources of rebellion exist, but they all amount to one thing: rebellion against Allah ﷻ. It may take different forms, appear in different media, come in different shapes and sizes, but it's all the same in the end. As the Prophet ﷺ said, kufr is one nation.

The example of this in Surah Al-Baqarah is the People of the Book who heard the Quran and said, "We have that too! That's awesome." Then they went back to taghut, who told them: "Stay in the darkness. Don't tell them anything." Taghut keeps people in the dark.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ - "Those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally." In this drowning, they ultimately drown inside the Fire, where they remain permanently.

translation: Have you not considered the one who argued with Abraham about his Lord [merely] because Allah had given him kingship? When Abraham said, "My Lord is the one who gives life and causes death," he said, "I give life and cause death." Abraham said, "Indeed, Allah brings up the sun from the east, so bring it up from the west." So the disbeliever was overwhelmed [by astonishment], and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِى حَآجَّ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ فِى رَبِّهِۦٓ أَنْ ءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ إِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّىَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ قَالَ أَنَا۠ أُحْىِۦ وَأُمِيتُ ۖ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْتِى بِٱلشَّمْسِ مِنَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ ٱلْمَغْرِبِ فَبُهِتَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَرَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

You should understand something about stories in the Quran: Allah ﷻ never mentions an entire story anywhere except for Yusuf (AS). Everywhere else, the narration seems scattered. But actually, only that part of any prophet's story is mentioned that directly relates to the subject at hand. Since we're learning about Ayat al-Kursi and the fundamental lessons of Tawheed, followed by the relationship that results from this Tawheed—the relationship captured in "Allah is the Wali of those who believe"—now the theory has been explained. Here's the case study.

This is someone who held onto the anchor and never let go. Someone Allah ﷻ protected no matter what. Even when he was speaking against a king without an army, standing alone—this is Ibrahim (AS) speaking to Nimrod. Notice that Nimrod isn't even dignified with a name in the Quran. The ayah simply says: "Have you not considered the one who argued with Ibrahim concerning his Master?"

We learn here about a fascinating psychology of power. People develop what's called the God complex. When people gain a little power, they start feeling they deserve it, and that others are inferior to them because of that power. This isn't limited to kings and dictators. Doctors can have it. Engineers can have it. Pioneering scientists and researchers can have it. Anyone given dominion over something can develop this arrogance—doctors who start thinking they save lives, they give life. Instead of recognizing that Allah ﷻ gave them the gift to help save lives, they start thinking life and death are in their hands.

Kings feel this way: "I provide for this entire nation. I sustain it. I do all this." Allah ﷻ says: أَنْ ءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ - "Because Allah had given him kingdom," he started arguing about Allah ﷻ, about his Master. Just because Allah ﷻ had given him dominion.

إِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّىَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ - "When Ibrahim said: 'My Master is the One who gives life and causes death.'" This connects to Ayat al-Kursi: الْحَىُّ - the Ever-Living. My Master gives life because He is the source of all life. He gives life and death.

The king responded: أَنَا۠ أُحْىِۦ وَأُمِيتُ - "I give life and death." He brought out two prisoners, both sentenced to death. He freed one and executed the other on the spot. "Look, I give life and death. I can do it too."

Ibrahim (AS) saw that this man was resorting to obnoxious argument, even though he knew perfectly well what Ibrahim meant. So Ibrahim (AS)—and this is from his wisdom—chose to expose the stupidity of shirk in an intelligent fashion. He said: "Okay, you win that round. فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْتِى بِٱلشَّمْسِ مِنَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ ٱلْمَغْرِبِ - My Master brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west. I'm waiting. I'll wait till evening. Let it set first, then you can bring it back up from the west."

فَبُهِتَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَرَ - The one who disbelieved was stunned, crushed, humiliated. He had nothing to say. Of course, at that point, his response would later be to throw Ibrahim into the fire. But that's the arrogance of taghut—when cornered intellectually, they resort to force.

وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people." Even though he was stumped in argument, he still didn't come to guidance, because he wasn't sincere. He was a wrongdoer. These arguments won't help people understand if they're not sincere in the first place.

The connection to life and death is profound. That first attribute of Allah ﷻ—Al-Hayy, the Ever-Living who gives life and death—that argument continues. How is this practically relevant? Ibrahim (AS) could stand before a king and speak about life and death, knowing that if he offended the king, he could be killed. But he knew that life and death belong to Allah ﷻ—not just for everything, but for him too. He didn't have to worry about that.

translation: Or [consider such an example] as the one who passed by a township which had fallen into ruin. He said, "How will Allah bring this to life after its death?" So Allah caused him to die for a hundred years; then He revived him. He said, "How long have you remained?" The man said, "I have remained a day or part of a day." He said, "Rather, you have remained one hundred years. Look at your food and your drink; it has not changed with time. And look at your donkey; and We will make you a sign for the people. And look at the bones [of this donkey] - how We raise them and then We cover them with flesh." And when it became clear to him, he said, "I know that Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
أَوْ كَٱلَّذِى مَرَّ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا قَالَ أَنَّىٰ يُحْىِۦ هَـٰذِهِ ٱللَّهُ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۖ فَأَمَاتَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ ثُمَّ بَعَثَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتَ ۖ قَالَ لَبِثْتُ يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ ۖ قَالَ بَل لَّبِثْتَ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِكَ وَشَرَابِكَ لَمْ يَتَسَنَّهْ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ حِمَارِكَ وَلِنَجْعَلَكَ ءَايَةً لِّلنَّاسِ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْعِظَامِ كَيْفَ نُنشِزُهَا ثُمَّ نَكْسُوهَا لَحْمًا ۚ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥ قَالَ أَعْلَمُ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Now comes the second case study of life and death: أَوْ كَٱلَّذِى مَرَّ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ - "Or like the one who passed by a town." This is Uzayr (AS). وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا - "And it was collapsed upon its roofs." Let me give you the historical background to understand this profound expression.

The Babylonians had attacked Jerusalem. Approximately 1.2 million people lived in the city at that time. They killed half the population—600,000 people—and took the other 600,000 as prisoners. The entire city was abandoned. Historical accounts tell us that not two bricks remained intact. The Quran's expression خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا literally means "collapsed upon its roofs."

When you think of "collapsed upon its roofs," you might imagine houses being picked up and flipped over. But that's not what it means. The expression indicates the town was abandoned. When there's no maintenance of structures—no protection from rain, winds, floods—the first thing to deteriorate is the roof. Even in modern construction, the roof requires constant attention, otherwise water builds up and the entire roof caves in.

This city remained abandoned so long that the roofs caved in first. Then, with the roofs gone, the walls—which were held in place by the pressure from the roof structure—began weakening. Wind, termites, weather—all took their toll. Eventually, the walls fell on top of the already-fallen roofs. This doesn't happen overnight. It happens over an extended period of abandonment.

When Allah ﷻ says the town was خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا, the expression captures that this town wasn't just destroyed—it was abandoned for such a long time that even the buildings collapsed on themselves. That entire scene of historical degradation and deterioration is captured in just one eloquent expression.

Uzayr (AS) passed by this scene and asked himself: أَنَّىٰ يُحْىِۦ هَـٰذِهِ ٱللَّهُ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا - "How will Allah bring this back to life after its death?" This is a logical question. There are towns destroyed today, war zones completely ravaged, where you would walk through and say: "No way. This place will never be back to normal. Life will never return here." Entire villages in Iraq, regions across the world bombed and destroyed, war-torn villages in Somalia, flood-ravaged areas in Pakistan—you look at such devastation and think: "There's no way this will ever come back."

Notice he didn't say "Won't Allah bring this back?" He said أَنَّىٰ - "How?" He's not doubting Allah's ability—that's important to understand. He's saying: "I don't understand how. How can He bring this back to life?"

فَأَمَاتَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ - So Allah ﷻ gave him death for one hundred years. ثُمَّ بَعَثَهُۥ - Then He raised him again. قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتَ - He said to him: "How long did you stay?" The question itself implies he was there a long time, but he has no idea. You know when you oversleep—you're waiting for a flight, you pass out, you wake up and the flight is gone. You wake up confused: "How long was I out? How long did I stay here?" This is what the People of the Cave said when they woke up: كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ - "How long were you here? I think we overslept a little."

قَالَ لَبِثْتُ يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ - He said: "I must have stayed a day, or part of a day." قَالَ بَل لَّبِثْتَ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ - Allah ﷻ said: "No, rather you stayed one hundred years."

At this point, he's shocked. فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِكَ وَشَرَابِكَ لَمْ يَتَسَنَّهْ - "Now look at your food and drink—it did not spoil." One hundred years, and the food is still fresh. Your milk goes bad overnight if you don't refrigerate it. But one hundred years, and this food remained fresh.

وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ حِمَارِكَ - "And look at your donkey." Before I tell you what happened to the donkey, let me tell you why this is happening: وَلِنَجْعَلَكَ ءَايَةً لِّلنَّاسِ - "And so We may make you a sign for people." I'm doing all of this so I can turn you into a miraculous sign for people. Your faith and conviction will become so strong that when people see you, it's like they're seeing a miracle from Allah ﷻ. Their Iman will strengthen because you will have witnessed life and death with your own eyes. You won't just tell people about miracles—you will be the miracle. You will be the ayah. You will become an inspiration for others.

وَٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْعِظَامِ كَيْفَ نُنشِزُهَا ثُمَّ نَكْسُوهَا لَحْمًا - "And look at the bones—how We raise them and then clothe them with flesh." The word نُنشِزُهَا means to raise something suddenly, spontaneously. The bones are half-buried in the dirt, and suddenly—pop!—they rise. A skeleton forms. No archaeologist gluing them together. Then flesh starts appearing on top of bone. He's watching flesh come onto bone, and eventually the animal comes together completely. All of this isn't happening in 3D or in a video with special effects—this is happening right before his eyes.

فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥ - "Then when it became absolutely clear to him." What became clear? He would never again ask "How can Allah ﷻ do that?" When Allah ﷻ wants something to live—like food that has the shortest lifespan—He can keep it alive for one hundred years. He caused him to die for one hundred years, then woke him up and brought him back to life. Then He showed him how He brings something else back to life.

This demonstrates that life and death are entirely in Allah's hands. But we learn something more profound here: it's not just about life after death. The concept of multiple lives and deaths, over and over. There was a life before, then He placed us in the wombs of our mothers. A new life started when we came out. This life will end and another will start. That death will come, then the life of Barzakh will begin. That life will end and the life on Judgment Day will start. That will end and the life in the Akhirah will start. Life and death and life and death and life and death—He controls all of them.

قَالَ أَعْلَمُ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ - When it became absolutely clear to him, he said: "I know for certain that Allah has complete control over everything." This conviction in Allah's control over life and death determines how courageous we are in carrying Islam, how fearless we become in speaking the truth. That courage depends entirely on our conviction that Allah ﷻ controls life and death.

Ibrahim (AS) spoke to Nimrod in the previous ayah, beginning with "My Master is the One who gives life and death"—he wasn't worried about the consequences. Now Uzayr (AS) is supposed to go back to Babylon, back to Iraq, to 600,000 prisoners of war, those enslaved Israelites. He's supposed to revive their Iman under that oppressive authority so they can rise again as a nation. He'll do that fearlessly, because now he knows life and death belong to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And [mention] when Abraham said, "My Lord, show me how You give life to the dead." [Allah] said, "Have you not believed?" He said, "Yes, but [I ask] only that my heart may be satisfied." [Allah] said, "Take four birds and commit them to yourself. Then [after slaughtering them] put on each hill a portion of them; then call them - they will come [flying] to you in haste. And know that Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّ أَرِنِى كَيْفَ تُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ قَالَ أَوَلَمْ تُؤْمِن ۖ قَالَ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَطْمَئِنَّ قَلْبِى ۖ قَالَ فَخُذْ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنَ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَصُرْهُنَّ إِلَيْكَ ثُمَّ ٱجْعَلْ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ جَبَلٍ مِّنْهُنَّ جُزْءًا ثُمَّ ٱدْعُهُنَّ يَأْتِينَكَ سَعْيًا ۚ وَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

Now comes Allah's final example of life and death, back to Ibrahim (AS). The first time, he was teaching someone else a lesson. Now he wants to learn a lesson himself.

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّ أَرِنِى كَيْفَ تُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ - "And when Ibrahim said: 'My Master, show me how You give life to the dead.'" Ibrahim (AS) has been through so much—there's no doubting his faith. Yet he asks.

قَالَ أَوَلَمْ تُؤْمِن - Allah ﷻ says: "Have you not believed?" The question itself is significant—أَوَلَمْ implies: "Don't you already believe?"

قَالَ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَطْمَئِنَّ قَلْبِى - "He said: 'Yes, but so my heart may be satisfied.'" We learn something profound about faith here. You can have faith in your mind, and there's faith in your heart. But then certain things happen in your life, and your heart becomes deeply satisfied with that faith. For different people, it's different things.

For the prophets, it's extraordinary experiences. For us, it could be surviving a car accident, hearing a powerful khutbah, a life-changing journey, even a dream. But once that happens, once you taste that deep conviction, you know beyond any shadow of doubt. There's no way it can't be true. When someone is really, deeply convinced in their heart of hearts that this is the truth, the way they call people to this truth transforms. The sincerity and passion with which they call people cannot be compared to someone who just memorizes something and repeats it.

What comes from the heart enters the heart. When there's deep conviction, happiness and joy from faith in someone, they can genuinely share it with others. The prophets were given this especially—they were shown incredible things so they could be role models of the highest form of passion in Islam. For them, it was belief in the unseen in some respects, but in other respects, they experienced belief in the seen.

Ibrahim (AS) will witness life come from death. He'll see it with his own eyes. It's different when you've seen it. The Prophet ﷺ saw Jibreel (AS) and the angel took over the entire sky. That's a different level of conviction. Nobody can tell him "This is all in your head." He saw the angel, was grabbed by the angel, was terrified by the experience. He knows what he saw. At that point, the conviction cannot be shaken.

This is why we emphasize the study of the Quran, especially appreciating its miraculous nature and beauty. We may already be Muslim, but the more you appreciate the Quran's beauty, your heart becomes satisfied. You feel a calm. Every time you appreciate something about the Quran, it's like you believe all over again, and again, and again. That satisfaction builds and deepens.

قَالَ فَخُذْ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنَ ٱلطَّيْرِ - Allah ﷻ said: "Then take four birds." فَصُرْهُنَّ إِلَيْكَ - "And incline them toward yourself." Train those birds. In those days, bird training was common for message delivery—you could send them to fly off and they'd return to your hand or cage.

ثُمَّ ٱجْعَلْ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ جَبَلٍ مِّنْهُنَّ جُزْءًا - "Then place on each mountain a portion of them." He's in a valley surrounded by multiple mountains. Put all the heads on one mountain, all the legs on another, all the wings on another, all the torsos on another, all the feet on yet another. Distribute their parts across different mountains.

ثُمَّ ٱدْعُهُنَّ يَأْتِينَكَ سَعْيًا - "Then call them; they will come to you in haste." Just call them like you always call them—whistle or say whatever you normally say when calling your birds. And watch as these parts come running and assembling together.

وَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ - "And know that Allah is the Almighty, the All-Wise." Allah ﷻ gives life—He has the ultimate authority to do so. And when He gives life and death, it's all part of His wisdom.

translation: The example of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is like a seed [of grain] which grows seven spikes; in each spike is a hundred grains. And Allah multiplies [His reward] for whom He wills. And Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing
commentary:
مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنۢبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ فِى كُلِّ سُنۢبُلَةٍ مِّا۟ئَةُ حَبَّةٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُضَـٰعِفُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ

After these profound lessons about life and death, there appears to be a shift in topic. We're about to enter what is probably the most comprehensive passage in the Quran about spending in the path of Allah ﷻ. After this will come a passage about riba (usury), then about loan transactions. So we have: spending in Allah's path, riba, and loan transactions. These three things have something in common—they're all financial in nature.

The first thing you'll learn is what's the best thing you can do with your money. The next thing you'll learn is the exact opposite—what's the worst thing you can do with your money. You learn the two polar extremes. What's the best? Spending in Allah's path. What's the worst? Riba. Then what's in the middle? What's legitimate, halal—loan transactions and business dealings.

But how does this connect to the previous conversation about life and death? When someone truly believes there are two lives, when they're absolutely convinced of life after death and that Allah ﷻ controls life and death, they become smart investors. They start investing in this life and the next life. The way they think about life fundamentally changes.

Someone who becomes a person who truly believes in the Akhirah and believes Allah ﷻ gives life and death with purpose—the way they think about investing their time, money and efforts transforms. Someone who doesn't believe in the afterlife also thinks about financial planning. So now there are two kinds of financial planners: one who believes in the Akhirah and one who doesn't.

The one who financially plans long-term while believing in this life and the next will make sure to spend and invest for the next life too. You need savings for when you move—you need property and assets already built there. He invests in his Akhirah. He sees both as savings accounts.

Consider people who engage in riba—banks, wealthy individuals who give loans with interest. Aren't they parting with their money? When somebody gives you a loan, aren't they letting go of their money? What drives a person to release their money? Because they say: "I'm going to get a return on this. Not today, not tomorrow, not next week—but long-term. Five years from now, ten years, twenty years—this is really going to pay off. This will make me rich." That's the concept of riba, isn't it? It requires long-term thinking, patience, expecting return on investment in the future.

There's something in common between the person who wants to make money from riba and the person who wants to invest فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - in Allah's path. They're both thinking long-term. But the one who believes in the Akhirah is thinking real long-term, because he knows there's another life. The one who engages in riba can only think within this life. So the longest-term investment they can make is riba-based transactions.

Riba itself, the very attitude of riba, is proof that someone is no longer concerned with the next life. That's how these concepts tie together—these attitudes. The one who believes in the Akhirah will invest for it, will save for it. He wants to arrive there with some really valuable assets.

Before we continue, the phrase فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "in the path of Allah" - requires clarification. The Quran discusses Sadaqat (general charity), Zakat (obligatory charity), and has a very particular term: fi sabil Allah. Sadaqah can go to your family, your cousin, your uncle. Zakat goes to those who are entitled to receive it, and one portion of Zakat goes fi sabil Allah—not all of it.

Fi sabil Allah doesn't just mean "for the sake of Allah"—that would be لِلَّهِ. This literally means "in the path of Allah." Was the Messenger ﷺ on a mission? Yes. That mission was to give Islam victory. That was the path of Allah ﷻ laid out for the Prophet ﷺ. Therefore, the Prophet ﷺ is told: قُلْ هَـٰذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ - "Say: This is my path" (12:108). Some interpret this to mean "This is Allah's path." Others say "This is my path"—meaning the Prophet's path. In the end, it's the same.

The Prophet ﷺ was on a journey, both literally and ideologically, to bring Islam to victory: لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ - "That He may make it prevail over all religion" (9:33). That mission required sacrifices, both physical and material. When someone spends toward that mission, that is considered إِنفَاق فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ.

This isn't the first time spending has been mentioned in this Surah. Earlier we learned: وَأَنفِقُوا۟ - "And spend" (2:195). That came right after discussion of battle, directly about spending in the mission for the Prophet ﷺ.

The manifestations of that today include work that spreads the Word of Allah ﷻ, institutions to spread His Word, masajid when they're fulfilling their purpose—fi sabil Allah. Education of the Deen that helps spread Allah's Word and continues the work of the prophets—that's fi sabil Allah.

Are there other aspects of spending—humanitarian causes, social causes? Yes, those are still Sadaqat, but you must distinguish between when Allah ﷻ is talking about fi sabil Allah in the particular context of the Prophet's mission, and when He's discussing general goodness in spending. The Quran says: وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍۢ - "And whatever you spend of good" (2:272). That's a different, broader term.

I mention this because often in fundraisers, these ayahs get quoted broadly. I respect the intent to motivate people to give more, but we need to be more precise in our study of the Quran.

مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "The example of those who spend their wealth in the path of Allah" - كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ - "is like a grain." Specifically a grain, because when grain is planted, you don't see results for a long time. When you spend in Allah's path, don't expect returns tomorrow. Be like you planted a grain—believe it and let it grow.

أَنۢبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ - "It produces seven ears of grain." These are like corn ears that you peel to reveal grains inside. Seven ears came from that one grain planted in the ground. فِى كُلِّ سُنۢبُلَةٍ مِّا۟ئَةُ حَبَّةٍ - "In each ear are one hundred grains."

Now what do you do with those hundred grains? Plant them again. So now you have 700 grains that came from one—actually 701 total including the original. But take those extra 700 and replant them. They rise and give seven ears each, and each of those has a hundred in it. Then replant those, and replant again, and again. All of that started with one grain. This is the multiplication of Allah ﷻ.

When somebody spends in this dunya for His cause, even when they wonder "How will Allah ﷻ bring Islam back? أَنَّىٰ يُحْىِۦ هَـٰذِهِ ٱللَّهُ - How will He revive this Ummah that seems dead? How will Islam, glorified only in the days of the Sahaba (RA), be revived? Why should I invest when I look at all the propaganda against it, all the hate, all the literature attacking it, all the powers that despise it, all the corruption within, all the infighting?" Despite all this, they still spend. They say: "No, it's Allah's cause. I'm going to spend."

If anyone had the right to say "This mission is impossible, it can't be done," it would be people like Muhammad ﷺ—knowing the struggle would continue for centuries. But they kept spending in Allah's path. Every little bit you spend, you're planting something. When you sponsor an orphan, at least you might get a picture of him holding a watermelon, waving thanks—you see some progress. When you spend in Allah's path, it's not necessary you'll see progress. It's sacrifice upon sacrifice without seeing immediate results.

You really have to believe you're planting something for your next life to be able to invest like this. It takes that kind of belief. When we become people obsessed with measurable progress—"If I don't see progress, I'm not spending. Only when I see expansion will I donate more"—we miss the point. You want to see physical progress in the work of Da'wah? You might not see it. You still have to spend. This is belief in the unseen kind of spending.

The imagery also hints at something else: 700 grains rising means when you spend in Allah's path, your rizq in this dunya increases, and Allah ﷻ wants you to increase even more so you can spend even more. It multiplies not only in the Akhirah—you'll get return on investment even here. He'll open more doors of opportunity. He'll help you get that raise, open that second business, secure that contract. You'll get opportunities and money, and when you spend more, He gives you even more opportunity to spend. Every time you're spending more, you're building your assets in Jannah.

Allah ﷻ says: "You've built some pretty good assets. I'd like you to expand your mansion in Jannah, so I'll give you some more money in dunya. Go ahead, spend for Me some more, and I'll build more for you in Jannah." But when Allah ﷻ gives you more and you start holding onto it, He says: "Oh, so you don't want to expand? Okay, I'll just take the money back and give it to someone else who will spend."

وَٱللَّهُ يُضَـٰعِفُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ - "And Allah multiplies for whom He wills." He expands for whomever He wants and withholds from whomever He wants. That's up to Allah ﷻ.

One linguistic note: The word سَنَابِلَ is the plural of سُنْبُلَة - an ear of grain. This word has two plural forms: سَنَابِلَ and سَنَابِلَات. Where else in the Quran are ears of grain mentioned? In Surah Yusuf, in the dream—also seven ears of grain. But there, Allah ﷻ uses سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ, the minimal plural form. Here, He uses سَنَابِلَ, the maximum plural form—a hyperbolized plural indicating abundance.

In Surah Yusuf, the context was limited rations, scarcity—so the minimal plural fit. Here, the context is increasing and multiplying, everything maximized—so the maximum plural form is used. Even in this subtle grammatical choice, Allah ﷻ emphasizes abundance.

The imagery continues: I've got one grain becoming 700, then 700 × 700, then 700 × 700 × 700—it keeps going exponentially, beyond imagination. Yet Allah ﷻ says: وَٱللَّهُ يُضَـٰعِفُ - "And Allah multiplies on top of that." My multiplication of 700 × 700 is just human calculation. Allah ﷻ is saying: "That's just your head. I know how to multiply way more than that, and I will multiply beyond that for whomever I want."

It's basically saying: this is beyond human comprehension. It's way more than our imagination can grasp.

وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ - "And Allah is All-Encompassing, All-Knowing." He encompasses everything and knows everything.

translation: Those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah and then do not follow up what they have spent with reminders [of it] or [other] injury will have their reward with their Lord, and there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ لَا يُتْبِعُونَ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مَنًّا وَلَآ أَذًى ۙ لَّهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "Those who spend their wealth in the path of Allah" - ثُمَّ لَا يُتْبِعُونَ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مَنًّا - "then do not follow up what they have spent with reminders of their generosity."

This is crucial. Mann means to impose a favor, to remind someone of what you've done for them. Some people donate to a cause and then want more say in how things are run because they're primary contributors. They say: "Well, I donated significantly. My opinions should be taken more seriously." Islamic causes sometimes go astray because they're dependent on donors, and those donors use their donations as leverage. "Who are you inviting as the main speaker this year? Because I really like this one..."  This happens because the donor imposes their will. This is mann—imposing your will and reminding people how you spent on them.

Imagine a brilliant young student of knowledge—not a loafer or lazy person, but genuinely intelligent with great potential and strong work ethic. They want to study Islam seriously, and you decide to sponsor them financially to study abroad or elsewhere. Don't impose that on them afterward. Allah ﷻ honored you by allowing you to invest in them. You built your Akhirah with that—not them. They're going to do what they're going to do. Allah ﷻ used them as an opportunity for you to earn your Akhirah.

The moment you impose it—"You know I spent so much on you. How are you doing? How's that $12,000 I gave you? It was $12,000, right? Just checking. I hope you appreciate it. You do appreciate it, right?"—don't bring it up. Don't impose it.

وَلَآ أَذًى - "Nor with injury" - they don't bring it up in hurtful ways. Don't say offensive things, especially after you've spent. Sometimes people say things that are deeply offensive. "You know that brother I sponsored? Are you going to mention that? Are you going to humiliate someone like that?" Don't say hurtful words.

Allah ﷻ says: If you do this, all that sadaqah you gave amounts to nothing. مَنًّا وَلَآ أَذًى - Imposing and using hurtful words.

لَّهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ - "For them is their reward with their Master." Those who don't follow up by imposing or using painful words—they alone will have their compensation with their Master. وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ - "They will have no fear, nor will they grieve."

In other words, those who do impose will be grieving: "Oh my God, I could have at least bought a big-screen TV with that money! I gave it to this cause and got nothing out of it." People who waste their spending fi sabil Allah by using filthy language afterward, by imposing or using hurtful words—they cancel out their sadaqah.

Then what will they have in the Akhirah? What was the point? When someone spends, it's like stepping into a minefield. Be extremely careful how you spend and how you act after you spend. Don't impose yourself. Don't feel entitled to more say because you've become a donor—whether to a masjid, organization or individual. Don't do that. It will cancel out your spending.

If you want to have an opinion, have it before you donate, and don't tie it to your donation. Make an anonymous donation, then give your opinion separately. Don't remind people: "By the way, I'm the guy who sent the $50,000 check, and I want to tell you, you should move your location..." No. Keep it separate. No strings attached.

translation: Kind speech and forgiveness are better than charity followed by injury. And Allah is Free of need and Forbearing
commentary:
۞ قَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن صَدَقَةٍ يَتْبَعُهَآ أَذًى ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَلِيمٌ

قَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ - "A kind word" - decent words, good words. وَمَغْفِرَةٌ - "and forgiveness."

Consider this interesting scenario: You spent money on somebody—maybe you helped someone learn and teach. Then afterward, you got into a conflict with them. There was some nasty conversation between you and them. At that moment, you might be tempted to say: "Did you forget what I did for you?" Don't do that.

Allah ﷻ says: قَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ - Decent words and just letting it go. خَيْرٌ مِّن صَدَقَةٍ يَتْبَعُهَآ أَذًى - "That is better than charity followed by injury." Don't say: "This is the gratitude you're showing? That's what I get for helping you?" Don't use that language. It's better for you not to.

وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَلِيمٌ - "And Allah is Self-Sufficient, Forbearing." Allah ﷻ didn't need you to provide. He is already Wealthy, free of all need. And He is Halim—Forbearing. He knows what both sides are going through. Even if you're offended by the one you helped, Allah ﷻ knows what you went through. He is forbearing with you, understanding the situation from all angles.

translation: O you who have believed, do not invalidate your charities with reminders or injury as does one who spends his wealth [only] to be seen by the people and does not believe in Allah and the Last Day. His example is like that of a [large] smooth stone upon which is dust and is hit by a downpour that leaves it bare. They are unable [to keep] anything of what they have earned. And Allah does not guide the disbelieving people
commentary:
PRIMARY_AYAH: 264

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُبْطِلُوا۟ صَدَقَـٰتِكُم بِٱلْمَنِّ وَٱلْأَذَىٰ كَٱلَّذِى يُنفِقُ مَالَهُۥ رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ صَفْوَانٍ عَلَيْهِ تُرَابٌ فَأَصَابَهُۥ وَابِلٌ فَتَرَكَهُۥ صَلْدًا ۖ لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ addresses the believers with a powerful warning: do not invalidate your charities by imposing obligations on others or causing them pain through hurtful words. The word تُبْطِلُوا (tubtilu) means to render something null and void, to make it worthless. This shows us that spending itself isn't enough—the manner in which we spend, and more importantly what happens after we spend, determines whether that charity holds any value.

Two specific behaviors are mentioned that corrupt our giving: المَنّ (al-mann) and الأذَىٰ (al-adha). Al-mann refers to reminding someone of the favor you did for them, imposing your charity on them, making them feel indebted. Al-adha refers to causing pain through your words or attitude after giving. Both of these behaviors are placed on equal footing, showing us they're equally destructive to the spiritual value of charity.

Allah ﷻ then compares such a person to one who spends his money رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ (ri'aa an-nas)—to show off to people. This person doesn't believe in Allah ﷻ or the Last Day. The comparison is striking: someone who shows off while giving and someone who gives but then ruins it through imposition are made equivalent. Why? Because both reveal a fundamental problem with their belief. When you impose on others after giving, you're demonstrating that you believe the wealth was truly yours, that you did someone a favor, that Allah ﷻ wasn't the One who gave you the ability to give in the first place. This attitude can only exist when someone has no real expectation that Allah ﷻ is watching and will hold them accountable.

The ayah then presents a powerful image: فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ صَفْوَانٍ (fa mathaluhu ka-mathali safwan)—his example is like a صَفْوَان (safwan), a smooth boulder or large rock. Picture a massive boulder with a layer of dirt covering it. Because of rainfall and natural conditions, vegetation begins to grow on this dirt, making the entire boulder appear lush and green. Someone walking by would never know there's solid, infertile rock underneath. They'd think this is rich, deep soil producing all this beautiful greenery.

Then comes أَصَابَهُۥ وَابِلٌ (asabahu wabil)—heavy rain falls upon it. The downpour washes away all that dirt and vegetation, فَتَرَكَهُۥ صَلْدًا (fa-tarakahu saldan)—leaving it completely bald and exposed. What's revealed? Just barren rock. Nothing of substance was ever there.

This is the image Allah ﷻ uses to depict the shallow nature of people who spend but then nullify their spending. On the outside, everyone sees the greenery. "MashaAllah, they spent so much money. What a charitable person. So generous." Everybody is impressed by its apparent richness. But when it's put to the test—and that real test will be on the Day of Judgment—there's nothing there. All of it washes away, and the hollow, infertile nature is exposed.

لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ (la yaqdiruna 'ala shay'in mimma kasabu)—they will have no control over what they earned. They thought they earned good deeds, they thought they were building something, but it disappears right in front of their eyes. Just like that vegetation washing away into the river, their supposed rewards vanish. They won't be able to say to Allah ﷻ, "But I spent this much money! I expect something in return." They can't expect anything, because Allah ﷻ is طَيِّب (tayyib)—good and pure. He does not accept anything except that which is good and pure. If you corrupt your actions with showing off or with painful words and imposing attitudes after you spend, everything is wasted.

وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (wallahu la yahdi al-qawma al-kafireen)—and Allah ﷻ does not guide the disbelieving nation. This is a powerful, shaking reminder. The behaviors described here are made tantamount to kufr. Why such strong language? Because these attitudes reveal something deep: a denial that Allah ﷻ gave you the wealth in the first place. When you feel entitled, when you feel you have a right to impose on others because "it's my money and I gave it," you've already denied that Allah ﷻ is the source of all provision. That attitude couldn't exist in someone who truly believed the wealth isn't theirs but a trust from Allah ﷻ, and that they've been honored to spend it in His path.

Kufr isn't just about denying Allah ﷻ verbally—it manifests in attitudes. And as we study the Quran, we're learning to identify these attitudes and trace them back to their roots. We're learning to avoid attitudes that don't align with iman, attitudes that reveal hidden forms of ingratitude or disbelief. This is why it's often said that ninety percent of our religion is about attitudes. The right perspective, the right heart, the right intentions—these are what we're learning from the example of Ibrahim (AS) and all the messengers.

translation: And the example of those who spend their wealth seeking means to the approval of Allah and assuring [reward for] themselves is like a garden on high ground which is hit by a downpour - so it yields its fruits in double. And [even] if it is not hit by a downpour, then a drizzle [is sufficient]. And Allah, of what you do, is Seeing
commentary:
وَمَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ وَتَثْبِيتًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ كَمَثَلِ جَنَّةٍۭ بِرَبْوَةٍ أَصَابَهَا وَابِلٌ فَـَٔاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا ضِعْفَيْنِ فَإِن لَّمْ يُصِبْهَا وَابِلٌ فَطَلٌّ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

In beautiful contrast to the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ now presents the example of those who spend with the right intention. These are people who give ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ (ibtighaa mardat Allah)—in pursuit of making Allah ﷻ extremely happy. The word مَرْضَات (mardat) is an emphatic form, indicating they don't just want to please Allah ﷻ a little—they want to make Him really, truly, deeply pleased. That's the pursuit that drives their spending.

This applies to all forms of spending in Allah's path, including spending on oneself to study the religion. But the key is the intention must remain pure throughout the entire journey—at the beginning, in the middle, and at the end. If someone studies Islam so people will recognize them, so they can say they graduated from a particular institute, so they'll get public speaking opportunities or positions of authority, then that wrong intention corrupts everything. The study becomes wasted.

But if the intention is purely to get closer to Allah ﷻ, to know Him better, to have a better relationship with His Book—if it's not about anyone else but about your own soul first and foremost—then you'll be safe. This doesn't mean you shouldn't teach others or help others. But those goals are so far secondary compared to your primary intention that they barely register. We're not performers who learn material to regurgitate it before crowds who will applaud. This is serious work meant to bring us closer to Allah ﷻ first, and then benefit others as a natural consequence.

The danger is real. When people start to appreciate you, when they say "MashaAllah, you speak so well, you're so knowledgeable," something shifts. Enough praise, and shaitan starts patting you on the back. Then you start patting yourself on the back, thinking of yourself as something special. That's spiritual death. The greatest celebrities of all time—the prophets—were the most humble. Ibrahim (AS) built the Kaaba, dedicating his entire life to pleasing Allah ﷻ, and at that very moment of construction he's making dua: رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا (Rabbana taqabbal minna)—"Our Lord, accept from us." He's worried it might not be good enough. That's the attitude we're learning.

وَتَثْبِيتًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ (wa-tathbeetan min anfusihim)—and to build firmness within themselves. What firmness? The firmness of belief in Allah's promises. That beautiful house in Jannah, that amazing garden, those rivers, that incredible company, and His company itself—all of it is real, absolutely true. And the confirmation that it's true? They keep putting more investment into that account. Whatever you believe in, you invest in. You put your time, effort and money into what you truly believe will yield results. That's how this world works, and it's the same with the Hereafter. Their continued spending is both an expression of their faith and a strengthening of it.

كَمَثَلِ جَنَّةٍۭ بِرَبْوَةٍ (ka-mathali jannatin bi-rabwah)—their example is like a garden built on elevated land, on top of a hill or mountain. This is a protected, ideal location. When أَصَابَهَا وَابِلٌ (asabaha wabil)—the same heavy rain falls upon it, something different happens. Unlike the boulder that was left bare, this elevated garden has moisture that stays, is protected from harsh elements, and when heavy rain falls, just the right amount of water is retained while the excess trickles down. The soil receives exactly the nutrients it needs. This is why hilltops and elevated lands often have the most beautiful, lush vegetation.

فَـَٔاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا ضِعْفَيْنِ (fa-atat ukulaha di'fayn)—it produces twice the yield. Some scholars interpret this to mean that those who spend during difficult times receive double the reward—rain falling represents difficulty, so spending despite hardship yields twice over. This aligns with what Allah ﷻ says in Surah Al-Hadid: those who spent before the victory and those who spent after are not the same; those who spent during hardship have a higher rank.

Others understand it to mean that the rewards in the Akhirah far exceed even what they expected. They get much more than they could have imagined.

فَإِن لَّمْ يُصِبْهَا وَابِلٌ فَطَلٌّ (fa-in lam yusibha wabilun fa-tall)—and even if no heavy rain comes, then light drizzle is enough. Even if no calamity strikes, it still produces. The garden is so well-positioned, so naturally blessed, that it continues to thrive. The meaning: these people are receiving rewards even here in this world, before the Akhirah comes. Their sincerity brings them blessings and contentment in this life, and when the Akhirah comes, they'll receive the full compensation.

وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (wallahu bima ta'maluna baseer)—and Allah ﷻ is in complete view of what you do. The word بَصِيرٌ (baseer) appears at the end when actions are being discussed, while in matters of the heart, intention and taqwa, Allah's attributes related to knowledge often come first. This is a subtle pattern in the Quran: when the focus is on internal states—sincerity, faith, taqwa—Allah ﷻ mentions His knowledge first. When the focus is on external actions—spending, fighting, performing rituals—the action is mentioned first, then Allah's attribute. It's a beautiful reminder that Allah ﷻ sees not just our deeds but the intentions behind them, and He knows which truly have substance.

translation: Would one of you like to have a garden of palm trees and grapevines underneath which rivers flow in which he has from every fruit? But he is afflicted with old age and has weak offspring, and it is hit by a whirlwind containing fire and is burned. Thus does Allah make clear to you [His] verses that you might give thought
commentary:
أَيَوَدُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن تَكُونَ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَابٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ لَهُۥ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ وَأَصَابَهُ ٱلْكِبَرُ وَلَهُۥ ذُرِّيَّةٌ ضُعَفَآءُ فَأَصَابَهَآ إِعْصَارٌ فِيهِ نَارٌ فَٱحْتَرَقَتْ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ

Allah ﷻ asks a rhetorical question: أَيَوَدُّ أَحَدُكُمْ (a-yawaddu ahadukum)—would any of you love? The question isn't really seeking an answer. Allah ﷻ is painting a picture He wants us to think deeply about.

Imagine: أَن تَكُونَ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَابٍ (an takuna lahu jannatun min nakhilin wa-a'nabin)—you own a garden, a beautiful orchard made of palm trees and grapevines. These are premium crops, valuable and beautiful. تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ (tajri min tahtiha al-anhar)—rivers flow from underneath it. Picture this: you have a garden on elevated ground, and when you reach the edge, there's a waterfall emerging from beneath. The implication is profound—the water supply is built into the land itself. You don't have to irrigate it. The water is already there, constantly nourishing the soil.

لَهُۥ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ (lahu fiha min kulli ath-thamarat)—in it you find all kinds of produce, all varieties of fruit. It's a completely self-sustaining paradise. This is the dream property. Beautiful, productive, valuable, and effortless to maintain.

But then the scene changes. وَأَصَابَهُ ٱلْكِبَرُ (wa-asabahu al-kibar)—old age strikes him like an arrow. The word أَصَابَ (asaba) means to hit or strike, like being targeted. The man who used to own and maintain this garden is now elderly, weak, unable to care for it as he once did.

وَلَهُۥ ذُرِّيَّةٌ ضُعَفَآءُ (wa-lahu dhurriyyatun du'afa)—and he has very weak children. They're still small, too young to take over the maintenance of the garden. So there's no one to continue his work. He's old and sick, his children can't help, and this valuable estate is vulnerable.

Then disaster strikes: فَأَصَابَهَآ إِعْصَارٌ فِيهِ نَارٌ (fa-asabaha i'sarun fihi nar)—a dry wind comes, the kind that has the potential to cause fires, إِعْصَار (i'sar) refers to a whirlwind or tornado-like wind. When it's dry, it becomes a perfect condition for wildfires. This wind carries fire with it. فَٱحْتَرَقَتْ (fahtaraqat)—the entire garden is set ablaze. Everything he worked for, all that investment, all that beauty and productivity—gone in moments.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (kadhalika yubayyinu Allahu lakumu al-ayati la'allakum tatafakkarun)—this is how Allah ﷻ clarifies His signs for you, so that you may think deeply.

What is Allah ﷻ teaching us? He started by asking, "Wouldn't you love to have this garden?" Of course we would. We all dream of security, comfort, beautiful property. Then He says: imagine you have it. But you grow old, your children are too young to help, and then a fire takes it all away. All that investment, burnt to nothing.

The parallel is clear: You will spend in this world. You will build that garden here. And it will be gone. You will grow old. You will die. Someone else will live in your house. Someone else will enjoy what you built. But if you spend for the Akhirah, you will get a garden there—and there will be no fire to take it away. No old age will make you unable to enjoy it. No inheritors will take it from you.

People have dreams in this world. They want beautiful homes, luxury, property, investments. This is all fine. But if your hopes and aspirations are placed entirely there, if that becomes your obsession, know that one day someone else will be living in that house whether you like it or not. The only real estate you and I will permanently occupy in this world is about six feet under the ground. And even that's temporary—eventually we'll be raised from there too.

Homes can become obsessions. The way you want them to look, the neighborhood, the yard, the interior design—it can consume your thoughts. Every time you go somewhere, you're thinking: "Those curtains would look nice in my living room. That rug would fit over there. I need to do that to my house. Where'd you get that art? Who did your paint job?" It's fine to care about your home, but when it becomes an obsession, when it's the only thing on your mind, Allah ﷻ is warning: it can all be taken away. The fire can come anytime.

Think deeply about this reality. Build for what lasts.

translation: O you who have believed, spend from the good things which you have earned and from that which We have produced for you from the earth. And do not aim toward the defective therefrom, spending [from that] while you would not take it [yourself] except with closed eyes. And know that Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ وَمِمَّآ أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَلَا تَيَمَّمُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنْهُ تُنفِقُونَ وَلَسْتُم بِـَٔاخِذِيهِ إِلَّآ أَن تُغْمِضُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ

Allah ﷻ continues the instruction on spending with a beautiful progression. First, He tells us to earn properly, then to spend from what we've earned properly.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ (ya ayyuha allatheena amanu anfiqu min tayyibati ma kasabtum)—O you who believe, spend from the good and pure things that you've earned. Notice the word طَيِّبَـٰت (tayyibat)—good, pure, wholesome, lawful. Allah ﷻ says first earn good and pure income, then spend from it.

Many people earn through haram means and then try to ease their guilty conscience through charity. They're involved in interest, selling alcohol, cheating in business, or other prohibited income, and they feel bad about it. So they think, "I'll just donate extra to the masjid, especially on the 27th night of Ramadan. I'll give a big donation and that'll make up for it. Then I can go back to the liquor store." This doesn't work. Allah ﷻ is طَيِّب (tayyib)—good and pure—and He only accepts what is good and pure.

So there are two separate rewards: one for earning halal income, and another on top of that for spending from that halal income. Both matter.

وَمِمَّآ أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ (wa-mimma akhrajna lakum min al-ard)—and spend from what We have brought out for you from the earth. This refers especially to farmers and those who work with agricultural produce. Spend from the crops, the fruits, the produce Allah ﷻ has blessed you with.

وَلَا تَيَمَّمُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنْهُ تُنفِقُونَ (wa-la tayammamu al-khabitha minhu tunfiqun)—and don't aim for the filthy from it to spend. The word تَيَمَّمُوا۟ (tayammamu) literally means to aim for something, to head toward something, to set your sights on something. So Allah ﷻ is saying: don't deliberately seek out the worst of what you have to give away.

Think about how this happens. You have many possessions—clothes, electronics, furniture, food. When it's time to give charity, what do you reach for? That sweater that's kind of old and smelly. Those shoes you don't wear anymore. The computer that's outdated. The food that's about to expire. You're aiming for الْخَبِيثَ (al-khabith)—the filthy, the inferior, the unwanted—to give away.

Allah ﷻ puts us in the recipient's shoes: وَلَسْتُم بِـَٔاخِذِيهِ إِلَّآ أَن تُغْمِضُوا۟ فِيهِ (wa-lastum bi-akhithihi illa an tughmidu fihi)—you yourselves would not take it except that you would close your eyes while taking it. Imagine you're on the receiving end of this charity. Someone gives you that smelly sweater, those worn-out shoes, that spoiled food. You wouldn't accept it willingly. You'd have to close your eyes, turn away in disgust, and reluctantly accept it just to be polite. "Uh, okay, thanks for the charity."

Don't be like that in your spending. This teaching is beautifully completed later in Surah Ali 'Imran where Allah ﷻ says: لَن تَنَالُوا۟ ٱلْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ (lan tanalu al-birra hatta tunfiqu mimma tuhibbun)—"You will never reach righteousness until you spend from what you love." Real spending for Allah's sake happens when you spend what you love.

You went shopping and stood in a long line. You carefully picked out the right size, the right style, the right items for your children. You bought eight new outfits. Then a wave of iman strikes your heart and you give all those brand new clothes away to someone else as sadaqah. That's real sacrifice, because you acquired them with love and you had to step on that love to give for Allah's sake.

What you were going to buy for yourself, you get for somebody else instead. What you were planning for your own children, you give to someone else's children who are in need. That's serious spending in Allah's path.

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (wa'lamu anna Allaha ghaniyyun hamid)—and know well that Allah ﷻ is غَنِىّ (ghani), completely free of need, wealthy beyond measure. He doesn't need your charity. He's giving you the opportunity to invest in your own Akhirah. And He is حَمِيدٌ (hamid), already Self-Praised, perfect in His praise. He doesn't need your praise either. When you give, you're not doing Allah ﷻ a favor—you're doing yourself a favor. He's opening a door for you out of His mercy.

translation: Satan threatens you with poverty and orders you to immorality, while Allah promises you forgiveness from Him and bounty. And Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing
commentary:
ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يَعِدُكُمُ ٱلْفَقْرَ وَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَعِدُكُم مَّغْفِرَةً مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلًا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ

Here Allah ﷻ exposes one of shaitan's primary tactics in preventing us from spending in His path.

ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يَعِدُكُمُ ٱلْفَقْرَ (ash-shaytanu ya'idukumu al-faqr)—shaitan promises you poverty. This is profound. When you're about to spend in Allah's path, in a good cause, what happens? Suddenly every expense comes rushing into your mind. You become a certified professional accountant for a few seconds. "Wait, I need money for the car repair. The electricity bill is due. I have to pay for groceries. I'm behind on rent. The computer needs upgrading. Gas prices are high."

You itemize every single expense with expert precision. This responsible, prudent financial planning somehow only activates when you're asked to give in charity. But after that fundraising moment passes and you go to the store, do you run through that same financial checklist? No. You put items in your cart without a second thought. "It's fine, I have money. I have a credit card. I got this."

This is shaitan's promise of poverty working on you. He makes you fear scarcity when it comes to Allah's path, but feel abundant when it comes to your own desires.

وَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ (wa-yamurukum bil-fahsha)—and he commands you to shamelessness. The connection is striking. What does commanding shamelessness have to do with promising poverty? Everything. When you listen to shaitan's whisper in one matter, you give him authority over yourself. He gains a foothold. So the next time around, he doesn't just promise you poverty again—he's already won that battle. Now he moves on to the next level. He commands you toward fahsha—shameful, indecent acts.

The word يَأْمُرُ (yamuru) starts with suggestion, but in Arabic grammar it can escalate to commanding. In other words, shaitan begins by suggesting shamelessness, but the more you listen to him, the more control he gains, until eventually he's commanding you and you feel powerless to resist. You reach a point where a person says, "I don't even know why I do this. I can't help myself." They've handed themselves over to shaitan completely.

وَٱللَّهُ يَعِدُكُم مَّغْفِرَةً مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلًا (wallahu ya'idukum maghfiratan minhu wa-fadlan)—but Allah ﷻ promises you forgiveness from Him specifically, and additional blessings on top of that. 

The promise from Allah ﷻ is dual: First, مَّغْفِرَةً مِّنْهُ (maghfiratan minhu)—forgiveness that comes especially from Him. This means that when we spend sincerely in Allah's path, it becomes a means of cleansing our sins. It compensates for our shortcomings. Sincere spending from halal income washes away mistakes we've made.

Second, وَفَضْلًا (wa-fadlan)—and surplus blessing beyond the forgiveness. It doesn't just erase bad; it adds good. Allah ﷻ doesn't merely balance the scales—He tips them overwhelmingly in our favor. He forgives our sins and then rewards us abundantly on top of that.

وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (wallahu wasi'un 'alim)—and Allah ﷻ is وَٰسِعٌ (wasi'), all-encompassing, vast beyond measure in His mercy and provision, and عَلِيمٌ ('alim), completely knowledgeable of everything—your situation, your intention, your struggle, your sincerity.

The choice is clear: shaitan's promise of poverty and path to shamelessness, or Allah's promise of forgiveness and abundant blessing. One path leads to fear and degradation; the other to security and elevation.

translation: He gives wisdom to whom He wills, and whoever has been given wisdom has certainly been given much good. And none will remember except those of understanding
commentary:
يُؤْتِى ٱلْحِكْمَةَ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُؤْتَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِىَ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا ۗ وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

يُؤْتِى ٱلْحِكْمَةَ مَن يَشَآءُ (yu'ti al-hikmata man yasha)—He gives wisdom to whomever He wills. This comes right after the discussion of spending, making the connection explicit: the wisdom being referred to here is the wisdom to spend your wealth where you should.

What is حِكْمَة (hikmah), wisdom? It's not simply having a lot of knowledge. It's not philosophical understanding or the ability to craft profound-sounding statements. The Arabs defined a حَكِيم (hakim), a wise person, as someone who has beneficial knowledge that is acted upon. Wisdom is when you understand the depth of things and that understanding impacts your life, changes your behavior.

A simple example: someone who knows that fire burns but touches it anyway isn't wise. Wisdom is knowing fire burns and staying away from it. That's the most basic, primitive form of wisdom, but it illustrates the principle. Knowledge alone doesn't make you wise—acting on that knowledge does.

Allah ﷻ says He gives this wisdom to whomever He wills. You can know all these teachings about spending. Anyone can read these ayahs and understand them intellectually. But who will actually spend in the right places? Who will control themselves from becoming mindless consumers? Who will prioritize the Akhirah over temporary worldly gain? That wisdom is a gift from Allah ﷻ to special people.

In the Quran, Allah ﷻ has given knowledge to all of humanity. Everyone can access it, read it, study it. Even a disbeliever can know a great deal about the Quran. But wisdom—that's selective. That's granted by Allah ﷻ to whom He chooses.

وَمَن يُؤْتَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِىَ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا (wa-man yu'ta al-hikmata faqad utiya khayran kathira)—and whoever has been given wisdom has been given an abundant good, an amazing treasure. The ability to align your knowledge with your actions, to spend wisely, to prioritize correctly, to see through shaitan's tricks and resist them—this is a massive blessing, a treasure beyond measure.

وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (wa-ma yadhdhakkaru illa ulu al-albab)—and none will make an effort to remember except the people of sound intellects, people of pure minds.

The term أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (ulu al-albab) is special in the Quran. It refers to people of deep understanding, people who can look at something and grasp its essence, get the hint without needing everything spelled out. The Arabs would say: اللَّبِيبُ فَمِنْ إِشَارَةٍ يَفْهَمُ (al-labib fa-min isharatin yafhamu)—the intelligent person understands from a hint. These are people Allah ﷻ ranks very highly.

Right now this might seem like a mystery. You hear "people of sound minds" and think, "Well, I guess that's not me. I'm not that smart." But here's the beautiful truth: anyone can become from among them. These ayahs are opening the door to that discussion. Later, particularly in Surah Ali 'Imran, Allah ﷻ will give us a comprehensive definition of who these people are, what qualities they have, and how they think.

More importantly, we'll learn the process they went through. How did they become people of sound intellect and wisdom? What steps did they take? How can we join their ranks? The door is open—Allah ﷻ is showing us these people exist and inviting us to become like them.

translation: And whatever you spend of expenditures or make of vows - indeed, Allah knows of it. And for the wrongdoers there are no helpers
commentary:
وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن نَّفَقَةٍ أَوْ نَذَرْتُم مِّن نَّذْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُهُۥ ۗ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ

وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن نَّفَقَةٍ (wa-ma anfaqtum min nafaqah)—and whatever spending you do, of any form. أَوْ نَذَرْتُم مِّن نَّذْرٍ (aw nathartum min nathr)—or any devotion you make to Allah ﷻ, any vow you take. This could be a commitment to sacrifice an animal, to devote time to study, to make a visit for Hajrah or Umrah, to donate money, or any other act of devotion.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُهُۥ (fa-inna Allaha ya'lamuhu)—then certainly, indeed, Allah ﷻ is the One Who knows about it. He knows fully and completely.

The implication is powerful: You don't have to tell anyone else about it. You don't have to announce your spending. You don't have to go around imposing your charity on others or reminding them what you did. "Brother, did you know I was the one who built this much of the masjid? I was the first one here. When I came, there was nothing but open desert. I made dua to Allah ﷻ and look at this place today. Alhamdulillah, I'm very grateful to Allah ﷻ. I'm very humble."

No, you're not humble if you're saying that. Allah ﷻ already knows what you did. You don't need to run around listing your credentials or announcing your biography. It's actually problematic to do so.

This is a reminder for everyone involved in serving the deen, whether as students, teachers, volunteers or activists. We've unfortunately fallen into a celebrity culture that's unhealthy. We're not supposed to be like speakers in other forums who get applause, photo opportunities and VIP treatment. That's not us. The culture of elaborate biographies, executive suites, first-class flights, security details and "I'm on break, don't talk to me" attitudes—this is wild. This isn't supposed to be us.

The example of true Islamic scholarship is humility. Teachers who came with only the clothes on their backs, who lived on the floor of the masjid, who taught 16-18 hours a day out of pure love for sharing knowledge, who would stay up until 4 AM giving individual lessons because "it's another opportunity to teach, let's do it." These are the people we should aspire to be like.

The entire culture of recognition-seeking is problematic. We need to return to seeking recognition from Allah ﷻ alone, not from people. When people praise you, take the advice of the Prophet ﷺ: just ask them to make dua for you, and that's it. The Prophet ﷺ even told people to throw dust in the mouths of those who excessively praise others—you're not helping anyone by praising them to their face. Praise Allah ﷻ instead; that will help you.

The kindest words anyone can say are: "I make dua that Allah ﷻ keeps you sincere." That's the best advice. Elders with wisdom will come and say, "People love you, so just watch out." That concern for your sincerity is the greatest gift they can give.

وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (wa-ma lil-zalimina min ansar)—and the wrongdoers will have no helpers whatsoever. Who are the wrongdoers in this context? Those who seek recognition from people instead of from Allah ﷻ. Those who spend to be seen, to be praised, to build their reputation. They are wronging themselves by misdirecting their efforts, and on the Day when they need help most, they'll find none. May Allah ﷻ protect us from being among them and make us people whose entire lives are lived seeking only His pleasure, undistracted by the fleeting appreciation of creation.

translation: If you disclose your charitable expenditures, they are good; but if you conceal them and give them to the poor, it is better for you, and He will remove from you some of your misdeeds [thereby]. And Allah, with what you do, is [fully] Acquainted
commentary:
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِىَ ۖ وَإِن تُخْفُوهَا وَتُؤْتُوهَا ٱلْفُقَرَآءَ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَيُكَفِّرُ عَنكُم مِّن سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

إِن تُبْدُوا۟ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِىَ (in tubdu as-sadaqati fa-ni'imma hiya)—if you expose your charity, make it public, then that is fine. The word نِعِمَّا (ni'imma) means "how good it is" or "it's fine, it's okay." This doesn't mean it's the best thing to do—it means it's acceptable, you're not in trouble for doing it.

There are situations when showing that you've given charity serves a purpose. The classic example is fundraising. They're trying to build the only masjid in a remote location—this actually happened in Alaska. Nobody is raising their hand to donate. There's awkward silence. Then someone stands up and says, "I'll give." That breaks the ice, and others start giving too. That person could have given quietly later, but by raising their hand publicly, they encouraged others. Their motivation was pure: help others feel comfortable to contribute as well.

We see this in the lives of the Companions (RA). When the Prophet ﷺ called for donations, Abu Bakr (RA) stood and announced he would give all his wealth. Umar (RA) announced he would give half his wealth. They did this openly to encourage others, not to boast. The latter intention makes it permissible and even commendable.

وَإِن تُخْفُوهَا وَتُؤْتُوهَا ٱلْفُقَرَآءَ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ (wa-in tukhfuha wa-tu'tuha al-fuqara fa-huwa khayrun lakum)—but if you can keep your charities hidden and you give them to the poor quietly, then that is better for you.

The contrast is clear. Public charity: نِعِمَّا (fine, acceptable). Hidden charity: خَيْرٌ (better, superior). Why is hidden charity better? Because it keeps your intentions intact. When you give publicly, even with good initial intentions, shaitan can creep in. You start noticing people's reactions. You feel their appreciation. That tiny seed of showing off can grow if you're not careful.

But when you give anonymously—maybe through a trusted intermediary, maybe as an "anonymous donor," maybe just quietly handing it to someone in need without announcement—your intention remains pure. There's no audience to perform for except Allah ﷻ.

وَيُكَفِّرُ عَنكُم مِّن سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ (wa-yukaffiru 'ankum min sayyi'atikum)—and it will bury away from you, cover over, erase some of your bad deeds. The word يُكَفِّرُ (yukaffiru) comes from the same root as كَفَرَ (kafara), which means to cover. When we say someone committed kufr, it means they covered the truth. Here, Allah ﷻ is saying your sincere charity will cover over your sins, will cause them to be erased and forgiven.

Notice it says مِّن سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ (min sayyi'atikum)—"some of your bad deeds." Not all of them, but some. This is actually encouraging because it means even small, regular charity has the power to wash away sins. You don't have to wait for major fundraisers or give thousands of dollars. Give a quarter. Give a dollar. Give half your lunch. Find opportunities to give sadaqah regularly, and don't miss those opportunities.

These small, consistent charities wash away sins. Since it says "some" sins rather than "all," we should seek every opportunity we can to give, because we need all the erasure we can get.

وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (wallahu bima ta'maluna khabir)—and Allah ﷻ has full, complete, detailed knowledge of whatever it is you're doing. He knows the amount you gave, the intention behind it, the struggle it took, whether you gave from what you love or from what you wanted to discard, whether you gave to be seen or purely for His sake. Everything is known to Him in perfect detail, and He will recompense accordingly.

translation: Not upon you, [O Muhammad], is [responsibility for] their guidance, but Allah guides whom He wills. And whatever good you [believers] spend is for yourselves, and you do not spend except seeking the countenance of Allah.
