And whatever you spend of good - it will be fully repaid to you, and you will not be wronged
commentary:
۞ لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكَ هُدَىٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ

لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكَ هُدَىٰهُمْ (laysa 'alayka hudahum)—it is not upon you to guide them. Allah ﷻ is addressing His Messenger ﷺ directly here. After all these instructions about spending, if people still aren't spending properly in the path of Allah ﷻ, the Prophet ﷺ shouldn't worry. Guiding them isn't his responsibility in the ultimate sense.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ (wa-lakinna Allaha yahdi man yasha)—rather, it is Allah ﷻ Who guides whomever He wills. The Messenger's job is to convey, to teach, to remind. But actual guidance—the opening of hearts, the granting of wisdom to act on knowledge—that's in Allah's hands alone.

وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنفُسِكُمْ (wa-ma tunfiqu min khayrin fa-li-anfusikum)—and whatever you spend from any good, any good cause, any good intent, then it is only for yourselves.

This statement is profound. Notice the grammatical shift: the nun that normally appears at the end of تُنفِقُونَ (tunfiquna) is missing here, making it مَجْزُوم (majzum), a grammatical form that strengthens the meaning. It emphasizes: you truly, genuinely, definitely did not spend for anyone else. It was only for yourselves.

You didn't spend for the person you helped—you spent for your own Akhirah. You didn't do Allah ﷻ a favor—He doesn't need your money. Every cent you gave, you were investing in your own future, building your own eternal home, securing your own success.

وَمَا تُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ ٱللَّهِ (wa-ma tunfiquna illa ibtighaa wajhi Allah)—and you do not spend anything, you haven't spent anything at all, except in pursuit of Allah's Face, seeking His pleasure.

This can be understood two ways. First, as a statement of fact that functions as a command: You must not spend for any reason except to seek Allah's pleasure. Don't you dare spend for showing off, for reputation, for people's praise—spend only pursuing Allah's Face.

Second, as a statement of reality: You think you've spent something? You haven't actually spent anything unless it was done sincerely for Allah ﷻ. Everything else is wasted, nullified, gone. The only spending that counts, that has lasting value, is what was done purely to seek His pleasure.

وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ (wa-ma tunfiqu min khayrin yuwaffa ilaykum)—and whatever you spend from good will be paid back to you in full. The word يُوَفَّ (yuwaffa) means to be given something completely, in full, with nothing missing. You will be reimbursed fully, completely, perfectly for every bit of good you spent.

Notice again the grammatical form—the alif is removed from يُوَفَّى, which is a stylistic choice emphasizing the certainty and completeness of this promise.

وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ (wa-antum la tuzlamun)—and you will not be wronged, you will not be cheated. Not a single atom's weight of your spending will be lost or diminished. Everything you did will be accounted for, valued properly, and rewarded fully. There is no injustice with Allah ﷻ.

translation: [Charity is] for the poor who have been restricted for the cause of Allah, unable to move about in the land. An ignorant [person] would think them self-sufficient because of their restraint, but you will know them by their [characteristic] sign. They do not ask people persistently [or at all]. And whatever you spend of good - indeed, Allah is Knowing of it
commentary:
لِلْفُقَرَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أُحْصِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ضَرْبًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَحْسَبُهُمُ ٱلْجَاهِلُ أَغْنِيَآءَ مِنَ ٱلتَّعَفُّفِ تَعْرِفُهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ لَا يَسْـَٔلُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ إِلْحَافًا ۗ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah provides a tangible, specific example within the general concept of "spending in Allah's path." Where should you spend? Who are the most deserving recipients?

لِلْفُقَرَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أُحْصِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (lil-fuqara allatheena uhsiru fi sabili Allah)—for those poor who have been restricted, confined, engulfed in the path of Allah ﷻ. The word أُحْصِرُوا۟ (uhsiru) means to be surrounded, completely taken in, confined to something. These are people who have dedicated themselves entirely to Allah's cause.

In the time of the Prophet ﷺ, this referred particularly to the people of As-Suffah, the Companions (RA) who lived in a covered area of the masjid, who had given up everything to be close to the Prophet ﷺ, to learn from him constantly, to serve the mission of Islam. They had no other income because they'd dedicated themselves full-time to this path.

In our time, this includes those who dedicate themselves to studying the religion seriously, to teaching it, to serving Islamic causes full-time. They've given up lucrative careers, comfortable lifestyles, worldly advancement—all for the sake of learning and preserving this deen.

لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ضَرْبًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (la yastatee'una darban fi al-ard)—they are not capable of traveling through the earth. The phrase ضَرْبًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ means to travel for business, to go make a living. They can't do this anymore because they've committed themselves fully to the path of knowledge and service. They're no longer in a position to pursue worldly income.

يَحْسَبُهُمُ ٱلْجَاهِلُ أَغْنِيَآءَ مِنَ ٱلتَّعَفُّفِ (yahsabuhumu al-jahilu aghniyaa min at-ta'affuf)—the ignorant person assumes they are wealthy because of their dignity and restraint. The word ٱلْجَاهِلُ (al-jahil) here is particularly striking. Allah ﷻ is calling the Muslim who sees someone dedicated to serving the deen but doesn't help them—simply because they didn't ask—ignorant. That's the divine verdict: you're ignorant if you don't recognize their situation and need.

These people have تَعَفُّف (ta'affuf)—dignity, restraint, self-respect. They're not going to go around begging. They didn't come to study Islam so they could become professional fundraisers for their own needs. They came to serve Allah ﷻ, and that service is too noble in their eyes to degrade it by begging.

This happens all the time. You see someone dedicating their life to teaching Quran, to serving the community, to preserving knowledge. They're going through financial difficulty, but they maintain their dignity. They don't complain, don't ask, don't impose. And because they don't ask, people assume they're fine and don't help them.

تَعْرِفُهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ (ta'rifuhum bi-simahum)—you will recognize them by their faces, by their mark. If you're a sensitive believer, you should be able to look at another believer, especially one serving the deen, and recognize when they're going through hardship. You can see it in their face, in their demeanor. You don't need them to spell it out for you.

لَا يَسْـَٔلُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ إِلْحَافًا (la yas'aluna an-nasa ilhafan)—they don't ask people persistently, wrapping around them. The word إِلْحَافًا (ilhafan) comes from لِحَاف (lihaf), which means a blanket. Professional beggars wrap themselves around people like a blanket—they grab onto your legs, they won't let go, they follow you, they persist until you give just to get rid of them.

These dignified people devoted to Allah's path are nothing like that. They're not professional beggars. They won't wrap themselves around you. They have too much self-respect, too much dignity, too much nobility in what they're doing to resort to that.

So how will you know they need help? بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ (bi-simahum)—by their faces, not by their words. You have to be observant, sensitive, caring. You have to look beyond the surface and recognize when someone is struggling, and then help them without being asked, without them having to humiliate themselves.

وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (wa-ma tunfiqu min khayrin fa-inna Allaha bihi 'alim)—and whatever good you spend, then certainly Allah ﷻ is fully knowledgeable of it.

Again, the refrain: you don't have to announce it. You don't have to impose. You don't have to remind them. Just give quietly, help them with dignity, and know that Allah ﷻ sees it all and will reward you fully. That's enough.

translation: Those who spend their wealth [in Allah 's way] by night and by day, secretly and publicly - they will have their reward with their Lord. And no fear will there be concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً (allatheena yunfiquna amwalahum bil-layli wan-nahari sirran wa-'alaniyah)—those who spend their wealth by night and by day, secretly and openly.

This describes a complete lifestyle of giving. These people don't just give once in a while. They don't wait for fundraising campaigns or special occasions. They're constantly in giving mode—nighttime, daytime, in secret when no one sees, and openly when there's benefit in it being known.

Whether it's giving away half their lunch, offering a small sadaqah, helping someone out, giving someone a ride, whatever form of good it takes—they're always giving. They've made giving a part of who they are. The Quran describes such people elsewhere: يُؤْثِرُونَ عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَوْ كَانَ بِهِمْ خَصَاصَةٌ (yu'thirouna 'ala anfusihim wa-law kana bihim khasasah)—"They give others preference over themselves even if they are in need themselves" (59:9). They just become these giving people. It's who they are.

فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ (fa-lahum ajruhum 'inda rabbihim)—then for them is their reward with their Master. Every single instance of spending is being recorded, compensated, rewarded. They're not losing wealth—they're constantly making deposits. They're getting richer and richer, just in a different account, an eternal account.

وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (wa-la khawfun 'alayhim wa-la hum yahzanun)—and there will be no fear upon them, nor will they grieve.

This is the ultimate promise of security. لَا خَوْفٌ (la khawf)—no fear means they won't be in any danger, won't face any threat they should worry about. وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (wa-la hum yahzanun)—nor will they grieve.

Who will grieve? Those who had opportunities to spend and didn't take them. Those who held onto their wealth tightly. Those who could have invested in the eternal but chose to hoard the temporary. On the Day of Judgment, when the true value of things is made clear, those people will grieve bitterly over their lost opportunities.

But these consistent givers? No fear of what's coming, and no grief over what's passed. Complete peace and security, forever.

translation: Those who consume interest cannot stand [on the Day of Resurrection] except as one stands who is being beaten by Satan into insanity. That is because they say, "Trade is [just] like interest." But Allah has permitted trade and has forbidden interest. So whoever has received an admonition from his Lord and desists may have what is past, and his affair rests with Allah. But whoever returns to [dealing in interest or usury] - those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide eternally therein
commentary:
PRIMARY_AYAH: 275

ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ لَا يَقُومُونَ إِلَّا كَمَا يَقُومُ ٱلَّذِى يَتَخَبَّطُهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مِنَ ٱلْمَسِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْبَيْعُ مِثْلُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ ۗ وَأَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْبَيْعَ وَحَرَّمَ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ ۚ فَمَن جَآءَهُۥ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ فَٱنتَهَىٰ فَلَهُۥ مَا سَلَفَ وَأَمْرُهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

After discussing the beauty of spending in Allah's path, the Quran now shifts dramatically to its opposite: riba (interest/usury), which is taking from others unjustly.

On one hand, there's the person who worries about their future in the Akhirah, who wants to be among those of whom Allah ﷻ says لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ. They spend in Allah's path constantly, building their eternal home.

On the other hand, there's someone who wants to make money whether they're sleeping or awake, day and night. How can you make money while sleeping? By getting your money to make money for you. That's what riba is—passive income, money making money, wealth generating from wealth itself without any actual work or value being added.

ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ لَا يَقُومُونَ (allatheena ya'kuluna ar-riba la yaqumun)—those who consume riba will not stand, will not be established, will have no basis. The phrase لَا يَقُومُونَ has multiple meanings. Literally, "they don't stand," which could mean you don't see them around—figuratively, they have no standing, no solid ground, no legitimate foundation.

إِلَّا كَمَا يَقُومُ ٱلَّذِى يَتَخَبَّطُهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مِنَ ٱلْمَسِّ (illa kama yaqumu alladhi yatakhabbatuhu ash-shaytanu min al-mass)—except like the standing of one who has been seized, grabbed, beaten by shaitan through possession. The image is of someone who's been touched by shaitan, gone crazy—hair disheveled, clothes in disarray, running wild in the streets. That was the expression the Arabs used: الشَّيْطَانُ مَسَّ

translation: Allah destroys interest and gives increase for charities. And Allah does not like every sinning disbeliever
Indeed, those who believe and do righteous deeds and establish prayer and give zakah will have their reward with their Lord, and there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve
O you who have believed, fear Allah and give up what remains [due to you] of interest, if you should be believers
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَذَرُوا۟ مَا بَقِىَ مِنَ ٱلرِّبَوٰٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

Before we examine this decisive command about riba (interest), we must understand what came immediately before it. The previous ayah described those who have truly internalized their faith: "Those who actually believed and acted on the righteous deeds they're expected to do, established prayer, and gave the Zakat—for them is their reward with their Master. There will be no fear upon them, nor will they grieve."

This ayah serves as a reset button. It addresses those who may have engaged in riba previously, telling them to return to the fundamentals of faith. Think of it as a fresh start: go back to genuine Iman, perform righteous deeds, and begin again. The command to abandon riba is connected to the very core of what it means to be a believer, because riba is considered among the most serious crimes in Islamic law.

The Prophet ﷺ gave us the most explicit and severe descriptions of riba to help us appreciate just how detestable this practice is to Allah ﷻ. Now Allah ﷻ addresses the believers directly: "O you who have Iman, have fear and awareness of Allah and abandon whatever remains of riba—if in fact you are truly believers."

Notice the structure here. The ayah begins by addressing those who already claim to have Iman, then commands them to have taqwa, and concludes with a conditional: "if you are truly believers." This powerful construction means that our entire faith and taqwa hinges on this single prohibition. If you engage in this crime, then you cannot claim to possess either Iman or taqwa. The implication is stark and unambiguous.

Before proceeding further, I should address something about modern financial instruments. When I've mentioned concerns about things like the stock market, that is not to declare categorically that the stock market is haram. What I can tell you is that I don't know enough about all its mechanisms, but I do know what financial greed does to people. I also know that historically, when stock trading first began, many accounts were structured as interest-bearing instruments. I cannot speak to what the current situation is.

What I can tell you is this: if you have any doubt in your conscience, if you fear that what you're getting into might be haram, you should consult scholars who specialize in Islamic finance. Seek out those who will not simply tell you what you want to hear, but who will share their authentic research-based conclusions. These can be complicated matters requiring scholarly expertise to navigate properly.

translation: And if you do not, then be informed of a war [against you] from Allah and His Messenger. But if you repent, you may have your principal - [thus] you do no wrong, nor are you wronged
commentary:
فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَأْذَنُوا۟ بِحَرْبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۖ وَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَلَكُمْ رُءُوسُ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ

The severity escalates dramatically in this ayah. Allah ﷻ declares: "And if you do not do so, then listen up—be informed of a declaration of war from Allah and His Messenger ﷺ."

The Arabic construction فَأْذَنُوا۟ is particularly striking. It comes from the same root as أذان, the call to prayer. But here it means to open your ears wide, to listen carefully to an announcement. This is no ordinary prohibition—this is an announcement of war. To continuously engage in riba is to place oneself in a state of war with Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. Let that sink in. Not just that it's haram. Not just that it's forbidden. But that it constitutes a declaration of war against the Divine.

Yet even here, the door of mercy remains open: "But if you repent, then you may have your capital assets back—the principal of your wealth." The Arabic uses the expression رُءُوسُ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ, literally "the heads of your wealth," meaning your original capital. If someone took an interest-bearing loan from you—say you lent them 1,000 dinars and expected 1,200 back—then upon repentance, you can reclaim your original 1,000.

"You will not wrong anyone, and you will not be wronged." This balanced statement protects both parties. The lender should not exploit the borrower, and the borrower should not take advantage of the lender's repentance by refusing to return even the principal amount.

translation: And if someone is in hardship, then [let there be] postponement until [a time of] ease. But if you give [from your right as] charity, then it is better for you, if you only knew
commentary:
وَإِن كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَىٰ مَيْسَرَةٍ ۚ وَأَن تَصَدَّقُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

But what if the one who owes you money cannot pay you back right away? Allah ﷻ addresses this beautifully: "And if the debtor is in difficulty, then grant him respite until ease comes."

Think about the psychology here. You're the one who engaged in the serious crime of charging interest. You're the one who committed riba, one of the worst transgressions in Islam. The least you can do now is show courtesy to the person who borrowed from you. Give them extra time. Extend their deadline without pressure or harassment.

Even better: "And if you give it as charity, it is better for you—if you truly understand." In other words, you committed a grave sin. If, as part of your sincere repentance and apology to Allah ﷻ, you say to your debtor, "Keep the money. I don't even want my principal back. Consider it sadaqah from me"—then this is far better for you. Many scholars consider this act of complete forgiveness a powerful means of compensation for the sin of riba.

translation: And fear a Day when you will be returned to Allah. Then every soul will be compensated for what it earned, and they will not be treated unjustly
commentary:
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ ثُمَّ تُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ

According to many scholars of Quranic sciences, this ayah holds a unique distinction: it is considered by numerous authorities to be the very last ayah revealed of the entire Quran.

"And be cautious and protect yourselves from a Day on which you will all be returned to Allah. Then every single soul will be given in full whatever it earned, and they will not be wronged in the least."

After all the discussion of riba, loans, financial ethics and the declaration of war against those who persist in interest, Allah ﷻ reminds us of the ultimate accountability. No matter how cleverly we structure our financial dealings, no matter what loopholes we think we've found, there is a Day coming when we will stand before Allah ﷻ. On that Day, every person will receive exactly what they earned—complete, precise compensation. And no one will be wronged.

This serves as both warning and comfort: warning to those who think they can escape the consequences of financial exploitation, and comfort to those who have been wronged, assuring them that perfect justice awaits.

translation: O you who have believed, when you contract a debt for a specified term, write it down. And let a scribe write [it] between you in justice. Let no scribe refuse to write as Allah has taught him. So let him write and let the one who has the obligation dictate. And let him fear Allah, his Lord, and not leave anything out of it. But if the one who has the obligation is of limited understanding or weak or unable to dictate himself, then let his guardian dictate in justice. And bring to witness two witnesses from among your men. And if there are not two men [available], then a man and two women from those whom you accept as witnesses - so that if one of the women errs, then the other can remind her. And let not the witnesses refuse when they are called upon. And do not be [too] weary to write it, whether it is small or large, for its [specified] term. That is more just in the sight of Allah and stronger as evidence and more likely to prevent doubt between you, except when it is an immediate transaction which you conduct among yourselves. For [then] there is no blame upon you if you do not write it. And take witnesses when you conclude a contract. Let no scribe be harmed or any witness. For if you do so, indeed, it is [grave] disobedience in you. And fear Allah. And Allah teaches you. And Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَدَايَنتُم بِدَيْنٍ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى فَٱكْتُبُوهُ ۚ وَلْيَكْتُب بَّيْنَكُمْ كَاتِبٌۢ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ كَاتِبٌ أَن يَكْتُبَ كَمَا عَلَّمَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ فَلْيَكْتُبْ وَلْيُمْلِلِ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ وَلَا يَبْخَسْ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ فَإِن كَانَ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ سَفِيهًا أَوْ ضَعِيفًا أَوْ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُ أَن يُمِلَّ هُوَ فَلْيُمْلِلْ وَلِيُّهُۥ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ شَهِيدَيْنِ مِن رِّجَالِكُمْ ۖ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُونَا رَجُلَيْنِ فَرَجُلٌ وَٱمْرَأَتَانِ مِمَّن تَرْضَوْنَ مِنَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءِ أَن تَضِلَّ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا فَتُذَكِّرَ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءُ إِذَا مَا دُعُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا تَسْـَٔمُوٓا۟ أَن تَكْتُبُوهُ صَغِيرًا أَوْ كَبِيرًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَأَقْوَمُ لِلشَّهَـٰدَةِ وَأَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَرْتَابُوٓا۟ ۖ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً حَاضِرَةً تُدِيرُونَهَا بَيْنَكُمْ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَلَّا تَكْتُبُوهَا ۗ وَأَشْهِدُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَبَايَعْتُمْ ۚ وَلَا يُضَآرَّ كَاتِبٌ وَلَا شَهِيدٌ ۚ وَإِن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَإِنَّهُۥ فُسُوقٌۢ بِكُمْ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

This is the longest single ayah in the entire Quran. After addressing the worst financial practice (riba), Allah ﷻ now outlines the fundamental ethics of permissible loans and how they should be properly conducted.

There's a hadith from the Prophet ﷺ telling us that Allah ﷻ does not even listen to the complaints of certain people—including someone who entered into a loan agreement but never put it in writing, then later cries about not being paid on time or disputes over terms. This ayah establishes the basic ethics for conducting loan transactions, keeping in mind that these guidelines were revealed at a time when most people could not read or write.

### Documentation is Essential

"O you who believe, when you engage in loan transactions for a specified term, write it down." The command is clear and direct. Any loan with a deadline—when the debt is to be paid in full—must be documented in writing.

"And let a scribe write it down between you with justice." In that historical context, literacy was rare. The person writing the loan agreement had to be trusted by both parties. They needed to be fair, explaining the terms clearly to both sides. If the scribe was partial to one party, they could insert unfair clauses that would disadvantage the other, who might not discover this until payment came due.

Today we face a similar challenge, though in different form. We don't have a scribe writing between us—instead, we have lawyers drafting contracts in incredibly tiny fine print, filled with complex clauses written in overwhelmingly technical language. Even if you read English fluently, legal language can be incomprehensible. These contracts contain all sorts of unfair provisions: "We reserve the right to do this or that" with terms that are never properly explained. In this sense, we've become like the ancient Arabs—functionally illiterate when it comes to financial language. We just want to know: where do I sign?

The principle remains: the one writing among you must write with justice.

### The Scribe's Responsibility

"And let not the scribe refuse to write as Allah has taught him." In that time, someone who could read and write was a rare commodity. Such a person should not refuse to provide this service to the community, saying "I'm too busy" or "I can't do this for you."

What we learn here is profound: when you have a talent or skill that your community needs, you should not refuse to help people with that gift. In the Medinan context, that talent was the ability to write. But the principle extends to other abilities. Perhaps you're an excellent writer, a skilled designer, or have professional expertise. When the Muslim community or people around you need your help, you shouldn't turn them down. Notice this isn't even a "religious" task—it's just a loan agreement—yet someone with the necessary skill shouldn't dismiss it as beneath them or claim they don't have time.

This is why, for example, physicians should volunteer at mosques and clinics, helping people with medical advice. Those with skills should help others. "So let him write, as Allah has taught him."

### The Borrower's Role

"And let the one upon whom is the obligation dictate." This is crucial. If I'm the one borrowing money—if I owe the debt—then I should be the one speaking to the scribe, saying what needs to be written while the lender listens. Why? Because the person taking the money should know exactly what he's getting himself into. The best way to ensure that understanding is to have him state the terms himself.

"And let him have taqwa of Allah, his Master, and not diminish anything from it." Even though the borrower is giving dictation, he shouldn't try to slip in ambiguous language or clauses that the lender doesn't realize—something that might help him escape the obligation, get a discount, or extend his deadline unfairly. The lender shouldn't engage in predatory practices, but neither should the borrower.

"But if the one upon whom is the obligation is limited in understanding, or weak, or unable to dictate himself, then let his guardian dictate with justice." If the borrower happens to be someone with limited intellect, or is very young, or is weak in understanding, or for any other reason cannot properly give dictation, then his wali (guardian)—a family member or protective friend who understands better—should dictate on his behalf. And this too must be done with justice.

### Witnesses: A Controversial Passage

"And bring two witnesses from among your men. But if there are not two men available, then one man and two women from those you accept as witnesses."

This is, of course, one of the passages that generates controversy in modern discussions of women's rights in Islam. How can the testimony of one man equal that of two women?

Let me be clear from the outset: whether we fully understand the sociological or legal wisdom of this ruling or not, this is the word of Allah ﷻ. Just as He has already established in this surah that He is Master and we are slaves (وَٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ), whether we comprehend it completely or not, this is the hukm (divine ruling), and that's what we believe and what we will follow.

Specifically regarding loan transactions: whether this principle of one man equating to two women extends to all other financial areas or all other forms of legal testimony is a matter for the fuqaha (jurists) to examine and discuss. There is nothing explicit in the Quran extending this beyond loan transactions. The only place the Quran explicitly addresses this ratio of testimony is in this specific context: loan agreements.

Having stated that we accept this with full confidence regardless of our understanding, I can now share some scholarly insights about potential wisdoms behind this ruling:

First, early Medinan society was overwhelmingly a man's business world. While there were rare exceptions—Khadijah (RA) being the most notable—most women were not involved in financial transactions as a norm. When women were brought into these matters, it was often out of necessity, not as standard practice. They were being asked to participate in affairs they typically had no experience with.

Second, some scholars suggest this was actually a matter of consideration, like jury duty. Those who weren't regularly engaged in business dealings should be less burdened by being called as witnesses, so requiring two women instead of one man meant each individual woman would be called less frequently.

Third, and significantly, because women generally were not versed in business terminology and lacked experience in these matters, they might not fully understand the agreement. This makes recalling details more difficult. It's a universal truth: you remember what you understand. If you attend a lecture and comprehend everything, you can recall it later. But if you sit through a lecture without understanding it, you can't remember it either. What you understand, you remember; what you don't understand, you cannot retain.

This helps us appreciate the next part of the ayah.

### Understanding "That One May Remind the Other"

"From those you accept as witnesses, so that if one of them errs, the other can remind her."

The common translation says "if one of them forgets" (تَضِلَّ), but this requires careful examination. The Arabic word for forgetting is نَسِيَ. The Arabic word for slipping up or erring is ضَلَّ. These are different words. In the Quran, there's an important principle: when two words with similar meanings are placed together, the purpose is to highlight what makes them different.

For example, "Muslim" and "Mu'min" can refer to the same person, but when Islam and Iman are mentioned together in the same ayah, Allah ﷻ is making a point about the difference between Islam and Iman. This is opposite to how we often speak today. We might say "I was angry and mad"—that's redundant. Or "happy, joyful, and content"—all meaning roughly the same thing. But in classical Arabic, especially in the Quran, when similar words appear together, it's to emphasize their distinction.

This matters because elsewhere Allah ﷻ says: لَا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلَا يَنسَى—"My Master neither errs nor forgets" (20:52). The fact that يَضِلُّ and يَنسَى are used together indicates they definitely mean different things.

Most classical mufassirun understood تَضِلَّ to mean "slip up" or "err," not "forget." In this sociological context, it makes perfect sense: she doesn't fully understand what's happening in the loan transaction, so she might slip up when remembering the terms. Because of that possibility, two women are called so one can remind and support the other.

Other scholars—and their position must also be respected even though I don't personally agree—argued that women have a greater tendency to forget. They cited various hadiths and made different arguments. Some even spoke about what they saw as benefits of this forgetfulness, with some recent scholars suggesting humorously that if women didn't forget, no marriage would survive—because a woman might be deeply hurt by something, then two days later move past it and say she doesn't remember what the fight was about.

But I don't believe that's forgetting—I think that's forgiveness. And in my experience, women remember remarkably well. I've been reminded by my mother, wife, and sisters of things like "on June 23, 1998, you said such-and-such while sitting on the couch eating my chocolate"—so their memory works quite effectively.

Some make arguments about emotional memory and traumatic experiences that might be blocked out. Perhaps there should be more research in Islamic psychology on these questions. But regardless, the word used is not نَسِيَ, so that entire discussion becomes secondary.

The word is ضَلَّ —to slip up or err. If one might slip up in understanding or recalling the technical terms, the other can remind her. That's the function being described.

### Witnesses Must Respond

"And let not the witnesses refuse when they are called." This addresses the inconvenience factor. Someone has gone to trial, and there's a dispute: "They owed me in January!" "No, February!" Now witnesses must be brought, which is an inconvenience—similar to being called for jury duty.

This is another reason some scholars suggested two women were called as witnesses: it was actually harder to find women available for this purpose (since they weren't typically in the marketplace), so having one man was simply more practical and efficient. Also, when a woman was called, she might feel uncomfortable going alone, so two women together provided mutual support.

### Don't Be Lazy in Documentation

"And do not be lazy in writing it down, whether small or large, until its deadline."

The word تَسْأَمُوا is particularly interesting. There are different Arabic words for weariness or boredom. سَأِمَ means you've been doing something for a long time and you've grown tired of it—you just can't continue. You close the book and walk away. This happens when reading through long, dense material.

Now imagine writing a loan transaction—all the fine print, writing and writing and writing: "Oh God, okay, you know what, just take the loan, here it is." Don't be lazy in documenting it properly. Whether the loan amount is small or large, whether the contract is simple or complex, don't cut corners in writing it all down thoroughly.

### The Wisdom of Documentation

"That is more just in the sight of Allah, and more upright for testimony, and closer to preventing you from doubt."

Allah ﷻ gives three reasons why thorough documentation matters:

First, it's more just as far as Allah ﷻ is concerned. It protects both parties fairly.

Second, it will be more upright (أَقْوَمُ) when the time comes to give testimony. If a dispute goes to trial, this thoroughly documented evidence will be far better than conflicting memories or verbal claims.

Third, it will be closer (أَدْنَىٰ) to preventing you from falling into doubt. This happens both consciously and subconsciously. I might lend a friend money without writing it down, then later wonder: "Did I give him $500 or $800? I can't remember." He says $500, but I thought it was $800. Now I have to take his word for it. The one who gave money might genuinely think he gave more, and the one who received might genuinely believe he received less. If it's written down, there's no doubt—just refer to the document, the printout, the PDF, and everyone can see the same clear record.

### Exception for Immediate Transactions

"Unless it is an immediate trade transaction that you conduct among yourselves—then there is no blame upon you if you do not write it down."

تِجَارَةً حَاضِرَةً تُدِيرُونَهَا —an immediate trade where goods and payment circle between you simultaneously. تُدِيرُونَ comes from دَارَ, to circle. One hand delivers the goods, the other receives the money. It's a circle of transaction: an arrow going this way, an arrow coming back that way.

You go to the grocery store and don't get a written receipt? That's fine. It's an immediate exchange where the delivery of services or goods happened right away. You can keep a receipt for your personal records, but it's not required in the same way as a loan agreement where payment and delivery are separated by time.

### Witnesses for Sales

"And take witnesses when you make a sale." This refers to sales where the transaction is not immediate—when you're paying money now but will receive goods or services later. In the early days, a farmer might be paid at the beginning of the season with the crop to be delivered at harvest time. That's not an immediate transaction.

Today this applies to services like tax preparation where you pay upfront, or seminar registration where you've paid but haven't yet attended. Your end is done, but their end hasn't been completed yet. In such cases, you need witnesses. Our modern equivalent of witnesses includes email receipts, transaction records, and bank statements—these serve that protective function.

### Protecting the Scribe and Witness

وَلَا يُضَارَّ كَاتِبٌ وَلَا شَهِيدٌ—"And let no harm come to scribe or witness."

The word يُضَارَّ is beautifully constructed. It's in a form that can be read both actively and passively—meaning both "should not harm" and "should not be harmed." It works both ways simultaneously.

The scribe and the witness should not harm others. The scribe shouldn't be a fraud artist writing deceptive contracts, and the witness shouldn't be seeking vengeance or trying to make money on the side through false testimony.

But they also should not be harmed. They should not be intimidated, bribed, or threatened because of what they wrote down or witnessed.

### Warning Against Corruption

"And if you do so, then it is corruption that comes from within yourselves." فَإِنَّهُ فُسُوقٌ بِكُمْ—this is corruption (fisq) that is because of you, that originates with you.

"And be mindful of Allah, and Allah teaches you." وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ—Allah ﷻ is teaching you through these guidelines. These aren't arbitrary rules; they're divine instruction in how to conduct your affairs justly.

"And Allah is Knowing of all things." وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ—He knows what was going on in financial corruption before anyone else did. He knows the irregularities. He knows what happens inside corporate executive offices. Financial criminals may hide their dealings behind technical terms, manipulate language, cook the books for governments, but Allah ﷻ knows exactly what transactions are occurring, how witnesses are being manipulated, how lawyers might be paid off, how judges could be corrupted. لِلَّهِ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ.

This, by the way, is business ethics directly from the Quran itself.

translation: And if you are on a journey and cannot find a scribe, then a security deposit [should be] taken. And if one of you entrusts another, then let him who is entrusted discharge his trust [faithfully] and let him fear Allah, his Lord. And do not conceal testimony, for whoever conceals it - his heart is indeed sinful, and Allah is Knowing of what you do
commentary:
۞ وَإِن كُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ وَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ كَاتِبًا فَرِهَـٰنٌ مَّقْبُوضَةٌ ۖ فَإِنْ أَمِنَ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا فَلْيُؤَدِّ ٱلَّذِى ٱؤْتُمِنَ أَمَـٰنَتَهُۥ وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ ۗ وَلَا تَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَـٰدَةَ ۚ وَمَن يَكْتُمْهَا فَإِنَّهُۥٓ ءَاثِمٌ قَلْبُهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah addresses special circumstances that might arise during travel.

### When You're Traveling

"And if you are on a journey and cannot find a scribe, then let there be a pledge taken in security." 

Imagine you're traveling and find yourself in need. You need to borrow money but you don't know how to write the agreement yourself, and you can't find anyone who can write it for you either. What should you do?

فَرِهَـٰنٌ مَّقْبُوضَةٌ—then there should be collateral (rihan) that is seized (maqbudah). Leave something of value with the lender: your saddle, a camel, jewelry, or even let them hold title to your house until you can pay them back. Some form of security must be taken.

### The Higher Standard of Amanah

But then something beautiful happens: فَإِنْ أَمِنَ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا—"But if you feel safe with one another," if the lender trusts the borrower enough that he doesn't feel the need to take collateral. The borrower seems reliable, trustworthy. So the lender gives the loan without anything in writing and without taking any collateral. He has nothing except the borrower's word.

Think about what this means. The person who took the money now has an amanah—a trust, a sacred responsibility. They have no written record proving they owe this money. They could literally run away with it, and the lender would have no recourse. This is why Allah ﷻ says:

فَلْيُؤَدِّ ٱلَّذِى ٱؤْتُمِنَ أَمَـٰنَتَهُۥ—"Then let the one who has been entrusted fulfill his trust." The loan isn't even called a loan anymore. It's called an amanah now. It has elevated to a sacred trust.

وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ—"And let him have taqwa of Allah, his Master."

You could escape from that person. He can't take you to court because there's no record. He can't seize collateral because you didn't leave any. He has no legal standing against you. But Allah ﷻ is still your Master, and you still need to have consciousness of Him. The one who takes loans under these circumstances should be extraordinarily cautious, because the only witness to this transaction is Allah ﷻ Himself.

### The Gravity of Concealing Testimony

وَلَا تَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَـٰدَةَ—"And do not conceal testimony."

وَمَن يَكْتُمْهَا فَإِنَّهُۥٓ ءَاثِمٌ قَلْبُهُۥ—"And whoever conceals it, truly his heart is sinful." 

The grammatical structure here is remarkable: ءَاثِمٌ قَلْبُهُۥ—"sinful is his heart." How incredibly sinful a person's heart must be to hide testimony! The sin doesn't just affect their actions; it corrupts their very heart. The one who witnessed a transaction but refuses to testify when called upon—or worse, actively conceals what they know—commits a sin that penetrates to the core of their being.

وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ—"And Allah is fully Knowing of all that you do."

You may hide your testimony from people, from courts, from lawyers, but you cannot hide it from Allah ﷻ. He knows every transaction, every witness, every concealment.

translation: To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. Whether you show what is within yourselves or conceal it, Allah will bring you to account for it. Then He will forgive whom He wills and punish whom He wills, and Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
لِّلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَإِن تُبْدُوا۟ مَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

This ayah serves as a powerful conclusion to all the guidance about financial dealings, and it summarizes themes that have run throughout the entire surah.

### Allah's Complete Ownership

لِّلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—"To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth."

Everything. Every transaction, every dirham, every dollar, every contract, every witness, every piece of collateral—all of it belongs to Allah ﷻ. We're merely stewards, temporarily managing what is ultimately His.

### The Accounting of All Things

وَإِن تُبْدُوا۟ مَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ ٱللَّهُ—"And whether you reveal what is within yourselves or conceal it, Allah will bring you to account for it."

يُحَاسِبْكُم means to audit, to call to account. Whether you expose what's in your heart or hide it, whether you show your intentions or conceal them, Allah ﷻ will audit you. He will examine everything—every hidden thought, every secret motive, every concealed intention regarding these financial matters.

Think about how this relates to everything we've just studied. People who thought they could engage in riba secretly. People who thought they could hide unjust contract terms. People who thought they could conceal testimony. People who thought they could manipulate witnesses or take advantage of unclear agreements. All of it—whether exposed or hidden—will be part of Allah's complete accounting.

### Divine Prerogative

فَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ—"Then He will forgive whom He wills and punish whom He wills."

Once that audit begins, Allah ﷻ will cover the sins of whomever He wishes, and He will hold accountable whomever He wishes. This isn't arbitrary—it's divine wisdom and justice operating according to His perfect knowledge.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ—"And Allah has complete power over all things."

This final statement seals everything. You know what this summarizes? Essentially every major passage we've encountered in this surah. Think about it:

We had people who thought they had some kind of guaranteed forgiveness with Allah ﷻ—no, only He decides whom to forgive. We had people who spoke against Allah ﷻ as if they had rights or shares in His dominion—no, everything belongs to Him alone. We had Shaytan displaying his sense of entitlement, demanding special treatment. We had people lacking trust in Allah ﷻ's judgment. We had those who thought they could escape punishment or that they were automatically entitled to forgiveness.

Now, at the conclusion of this extensive passage on financial ethics, Allah ﷻ reminds us: He owns everything in the heavens and earth. He will account for everything you reveal and everything you hide. He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills. And He has complete power over all things.

No one has any claim against Allah ﷻ. No one can demand anything from Him. No one can escape His accounting. This is the fundamental reality that should govern all our dealings—financial and otherwise.

translation: The Messenger has believed in what was revealed to him from his Lord, and [so have] the believers. All of them have believed in Allah and His angels and His books and His messengers, [saying], "We make no distinction between any of His messengers." And they say, "We hear and we obey. [We seek] Your forgiveness, our Lord, and to You is the [final] destination
commentary:
ءَامَنَ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۚ كُلٌّ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِۦ ۚ وَقَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

This ayah is deeply profound. Some scholars, including Ibn Kathir (RA), narrate hadiths indicating that these final ayahs of Surah Al-Baqarah—some say the last two, others say the last three—were revealed to the Prophet ﷺ during the Isra and Mi'raj. They were not revealed on earth. They were revealed when the Prophet ﷺ was in the seventh heaven. That alone should tell us something about their significance and honor.

### The Messenger's Own Faith

ءَامَنَ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ—"The Messenger has believed in what was revealed to him from his Master."

We believe in the Prophet ﷺ, but the Prophet ﷺ also had to believe. Think about what this means. He had to come to believe in himself as a prophet. Am I truly receiving revelation? Am I going insane?

Consider the psychological weight of this. Everyone around him—people who loved him and people who hated him—everyone was telling him he had lost his mind. If absolutely everyone you know told you that you were crazy, wouldn't the thought cross your mind? Even for a moment?

Then to make it worse: he stands before a crowd to deliver this message, and perhaps one young person stands up and says, "I believe you." And everyone else laughs at that kid: "Look, even the youth are going crazy!" That would further undermine your confidence, wouldn't it?

The first time he encountered revelation in the cave of Hira, he was in shock. He didn't know what to do. He ran home trembling, asking to be covered with a blanket. It was Khadijah (RA) who calmed him down. This was a leap of faith even for him. He had to come to believe.

ءَامَنَ ٱلرَّسُولُ—the Messenger himself came to believe.

### And the Believers With Him

وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ—"and so did the believers."

Now notice something beautiful about the structure. This surah is ending where it began. It began with Iman—guidance for those described as المُتَّقِين (the people of taqwa), those who "believe in what was revealed to you and what was revealed before you." That was risalah (the concept of messengership), wasn't it? The three components of Iman are: Iman in Allah ﷻ, Iman in His messengers, and Iman in the Last Day.

Now here at the conclusion, Allah ﷻ is expanding that concept fully:

كُلٌّ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ—"All of them believed in Allah"—that's the first pillar.

وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ—"and His angels"—these are the conveyors of the message.

وَكُتُبِهِۦ—"and His books"—these are the messages themselves.

وَرُسُلِهِۦ—"and His messengers"—these are the recipients and deliverers of those books.

They internalized complete belief in risalah. They understood and appreciated the entire concept of divine communication: Allah ﷻ is the originator, He sends His message through angels, who deliver His books to His chosen messengers. They believed in all of these components.

Remember earlier in the surah when discussing the People of the Book, Allah ﷻ used the singular "book" (الكِتَاب)? That was strategic. If He had used the plural "books" in that context, they could have said, "Oh, if goodness is in believing in books, we've got our book, you've got yours." So Allah ﷻ made it singular: there is now one book to believe in, which is the Quran. That specificity was crucial in that conversation with the Jews.

But now we're talking about Iman altogether. This isn't a debate with any specific group—it's a comprehensive description of belief. So here it must be كُتُبِهِۦ (books, plural) and رُسُلِهِۦ (messengers, plural).

### No Distinction Between Messengers

لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِۦ—"We make no distinction between any of His messengers."

We don't say some are true and others aren't. We don't pick and choose. We don't elevate some while rejecting others. All of Allah's messengers carried the same fundamental message, and we believe in all of them.

### The Response of True Believers

وَقَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا—"And they said: 'We hear and we obey.'"

Remember what this surah told us? The Children of Israel said سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا—"We hear and we disobey." They were given responsibility and honor, but they rejected it through their disobedience. They were eventually removed from that position of honor.

This Ummah was now chosen. These believers stood up and said the exact opposite: سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا—"We hear and we obey." This is the nation of hearing and obeying. This is the Ummah that fulfilled what the previous nation failed to fulfill.

### Seeking Forgiveness

غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا—"Your forgiveness, our Master."

Remember the Children of Israel were told to say حِطَّة —"forgiveness"—as they entered the city? But they changed the word and mocked the command. They didn't say it sincerely, and they didn't live it.

Now these believers are told: don't say حِطَّة anymore. Instead say غُفْرَانَكَ—"Your forgiveness, we seek Your forgiveness."

But notice the form: غُفْرَانَكَ is different from saying أَسْتَغْفِرُ اللهَ (I ask Allah's forgiveness). غُفْرَان means "forgiveness" itself, and when you add the ك (your), it becomes "Your forgiveness." The word ends with kasrah (غُفْرَانَكَ), which means it's the object of an implied verb. The full meaning would be "We ask Your forgiveness," but the verb is dropped.

In Arabic, when the verb is dropped and you mention only the object, it indicates overwhelming emotion. The speaker is so desperate, so overcome, they can't even complete the full sentence. They're at a loss for words.

Think about when you witness a terrible car accident—you don't say, "Look, there appears to be an accident over there by that window involving what seems to be two vehicles." You just yell, "Accident!" When someone is hanging off a cliff, they don't say, "Please, if anyone can hear me, I'm currently suspended at approximately 500 feet, and if I were to fall, I would likely sustain severe internal injuries. If you could perhaps move a bit north and west, you'll find me on the side of this cliff, and maybe you could assist me toward safety..." No. They just scream, "Help!"

That's desperation. That's being overwhelmed.

غُفْرَانَكَ—"Your forgiveness!" That's desperation. That's the believer saying: "I can't even articulate properly how much I need Your forgiveness. Just—Your forgiveness, Master! Your forgiveness!"

وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—"And to You is the final return."

There's nowhere else to turn. There's no one else to go back to. Only to You do we have to return, so only Your forgiveness matters in the end.

### The Honor of Being Mentioned Together

Here's something amazing: look at the grammar. Allah ﷻ didn't just talk about the Iman of the Prophet ﷺ in one sentence and then our Iman in another sentence. He placed us together in the same statement, as subjects of the same verb: ءَامَنَ ٱلرَّسُولُ... وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ—"The Messenger believed... and the believers."

We've been made partners with the Prophet ﷺ in this declaration of faith. We're mentioned in the same breath, in the same verb structure, as the Messenger of Allah himself. That is an unimaginable honor.

And if we accept the narration about these ayahs being revealed during the Mi'raj—and many scholars of great standing have accepted its authenticity—then this was given to the Prophet ﷺ at the highest point of honor, at the highest station a human being ever reached. And at that supreme moment, we were mentioned alongside him.

سُبْحَانَ اللهِ. What an honor Allah ﷻ has given this Ummah.

translation: Allah does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity. It will have [the consequence of] what [good] it has gained, and it will bear [the consequence of] what [evil] it has earned. "Our Lord, do not impose blame upon us if we have forgotten or erred. Our Lord, and lay not upon us a burden like that which You laid upon those before us. Our Lord, and burden us not with that which we have no ability to bear. And pardon us; and forgive us; and have mercy upon us. You are our protector, so give us victory over the disbelieving people
commentary:
لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا ٱكْتَسَبَتْ ۗ رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَآ إِن نَّسِينَآ أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَآ إِصْرًا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِۦ ۖ وَٱعْفُ عَنَّا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَآ ۚ أَنتَ مَوْلَىٰنَا فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

This final ayah of Surah Al-Baqarah is one of the most profound and most recited in the entire Quran. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that whoever recites these last two ayahs at night, they will be sufficient for him. Let's understand what Allah ﷻ is teaching us here.

### Allah Does Not Burden Beyond Capacity

لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا—"Allah does not burden any soul beyond its capacity."

This statement appears in various forms throughout the Quran, and it needs to be understood properly because it has several profound implications.

First implication: All of us have different capacities. Our abilities are products of many things—our heredity, our genes, our physical capabilities, our intellectual gifts, our language skills, our ability to process information, our various talents. Some people are remarkably capable in certain areas and struggle in others. Each person is different. Allah ﷻ knows that every soul (نَفْس) has its own capacity (وُسْع), its own unique potential.

Allah ﷻ is saying: I do not burden anyone except to the extent of their potential.

One direct implication of this is found elsewhere in the Quran: قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلَىٰ شَاكِلَتِهِۦ—"Say: Each works according to his own disposition" (17:84). You've been given certain strengths. You need to exhaust yourself serving Allah ﷻ within those strengths.

Let me put this in proportionate terms to make it clear. Imagine Allah ﷻ gave somebody 300 points of ability (think of it like a video game). They use 250 of those points in serving Allah ﷻ. They've succeeded—250 out of 300 is an excellent score. They've used most of their potential.

Now imagine someone else has been given 8,000 points. Much more wealth, much more intelligence, much more understanding, much more opportunity. Way more points. But they only score 500. Yes, 500 is numerically higher than 250. But they've actually failed. Why? Because they weren't being tested against the first person. They were being tested against what Allah ﷻ had given them.

So you and I have to ask ourselves: What has Allah ﷻ given me? How am I scoring? How am I using my potential? Am I exhausting it for what Allah ﷻ burdened me with?

لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا—this is a very deep statement. Every person is in a race against themselves, not against others.

Second implication: You cannot say "Allah made me a loser, so I just can't do it." You can't say "I just can't wake up for Fajr—it's beyond my ability." No, it's not. That's the point. Allah ﷻ does not burden anyone beyond what they can handle. If Allah ﷻ burdened us with this Book, this law, this responsibility—being the legacy of His Messenger ﷺ, being representatives of this Deen in whatever age we live in—then Allah ﷻ already knew we were perfectly capable of carrying it out. Otherwise He wouldn't have given it to us.

The very fact that you are Muslim is testimony that Allah ﷻ sees capability in you. He sees potential in you. He gave you the responsibility to stand by Islam, to represent this Deen, to be a witness to His Book—because He wouldn't have given it to you if you weren't capable.

He could have raised you in an easier time, a much more comfortable era when everyone around you was Muslim, when haram was difficult to access, when everyone was righteous and supportive. But He didn't. He raised you now. In this time. In this place. With these challenges.

There's a reason you have the education you have, the parents you have, the job you have, the opportunities you have, the life experiences you have. All of that comes together to form your capacity, your abilities. And whatever that capacity is, it needs to be utilized to its fullest for the sake of Allah ﷻ.

No one can argue "It's beyond my capacity." That's the silliest argument possible. "I know Islam is good and everything, but I just can't help myself. I know it's haram but what can I do? I'm in love." Or "I know I should pray five times but that's a lot. Five times! Every day! Even weekends! How do you do it? That's incredible!"

Some Muslims are amazed that you pray five times daily, as if it's some remarkable accomplishment. But nobody is amazed that you stop at every red light. Nobody says, "Wow, you stop at every single stop sign? I could never do that!" Why? Because everyone knows it's the law and you'll get in trouble if you don't. It's that simple.

If you truly understand that the Quran is the law—that staying away from haram is the law—then it's not an accomplishment to keep out of trouble. You're just being a decent citizen. You don't get an award for not running a red light. The mayor doesn't call you into his office to present you with "Citizen of the Year" for consistently stopping at stop signs.

You're not accomplishing anything extraordinary by following Allah's commands. But Allah ﷻ is telling us: if we have been given a burden, it is absolutely, definitively because we can bear it. You can never truthfully say "This is too much."

Who's right: you when you say "I can't," or Allah ﷻ when He says you can? Allah ﷻ says you can handle it. You cannot claim otherwise.

### Easy Good, Difficult Evil

لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا ٱكْتَسَبَتْ—"For it is what it earned, and against it is what it earned."

Notice the change in verb form. The first is كَسَبَتْ (kasabat) and the second is ٱكْتَسَبَتْ. Both mean "earned," but اكْتَسَبَ implies more difficulty, more effort in the earning.

Allah ﷻ is saying: I've made earning good easy for you, and I've made earning evil harder for you. I will open doors for good deeds. When you follow My path, فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ—"We will ease him toward ease" (92:7). وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مَخْرَجًا—"And whoever has taqwa of Allah, He will make a way out for him" (65:2).

But when you work toward evil, when you earn sin, it's اكْتِسَاب—it's harder, it comes with more difficulty and trouble.

How does this work practically? You commit a bad deed, and bad consequences follow. You yell at your mother, then immediately slip down the stairs. You drop something and break your ankle. You should realize: that's connected. That broken ankle? It's actually a gift from Allah ﷻ, a mercy. It hurts less than the punishment of the Hereafter. It's Allah ﷻ warning you, correcting you in this world.

You're home alone thinking nobody's watching, about to engage in something haram, about to go to that party—then you get into a car accident. Your car is totaled. Allah ﷻ helped you out. He stopped you. Yes, it's painful and inconvenient, but it's infinitely better than what could have awaited you in the akhirah.

That's how earning evil becomes harder—through the difficulties and consequences Allah ﷻ places in your path to wake you up.

### The Believers' Supplications

Now comes a series of beautiful du'as that Allah ﷻ is teaching us to make:

#### First Du'a: Mistakes and Forgetfulness

رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَآ إِن نَّسِينَآ أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا—"Our Master, do not seize us if we forget or we make a mistake."

There are two types of errors here. نَسِينَا means we genuinely forgot. "Oh my God, Maghrib already came in? I forgot!" You were busy, you forgot to pray. That's genuine forgetfulness.

أَخْطَأْنَا means we made a mistake. But this can include the kind of "mistake" where you wanted to forget. You were watching a movie, Maghrib came in: "Yeah, let me just finish this. It's almost the last scene." That's not really forgetting—that's choosing to delay, choosing to make an error.

"Our Master, don't grab us, don't seize us in punishment over these lapses—whether genuine forgetfulness or intentional mistakes we make."

#### Second Du'a: Not the Burden of Previous Nations

رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَآ إِصْرًا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا—"Our Master, and do not load upon us a burden as You loaded it upon those before us."

إِصْر means a heavy burden, like chains or shackles. We've learned extensively in this surah about the Children of Israel—Banu Isra'il—and how they were loaded with tremendous responsibility. And they failed catastrophically.

We're saying: "Ya Allah, don't give us those same kinds of unbearable burdens that You gave them. We've seen what happened to them. We know they couldn't handle it. We're afraid we couldn't either."

#### Third Du'a: Nothing Beyond Our Power

رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِۦ—"Our Master, and do not burden us with what we have no power to bear."

Don't pile burden after burden after burden upon us until we get crushed underneath them. Don't overwhelm us with tests and trials beyond what we can handle.

Remember what the previous nations said? They said مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا (we have no power whatsoever). The believers are now saying: "O Allah, don't give us responsibilities we have no power to fulfill."

But here's what's beautiful: when Allah ﷻ teaches us this du'a, He's already answering it. He's already told us لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا—He doesn't burden us beyond our capacity. So in terms of religious obligations, in terms of what He's revealed in this Quran, we've already acknowledged that Allah ﷻ will not burden us with more than we can handle.

But on top of religious obligations, He might test us with other trials—oppression, difficulty, challenges, hardships in life. And we're asking: "Allah, make those challenges something we can handle too. Don't make them so overwhelming that we break under the weight."

#### Fourth Du'a: Pardon, Cover, and Have Mercy

وَٱعْفُ عَنَّا—"And pardon us." عَفْو is to pardon someone out of love. Not because they deserve it, not because justice requires it, but purely out of Your sheer love for us. Just keep pardoning us. We'll keep messing up, and You just keep pardoning us.

وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا—"And forgive us." This is a step beyond pardoning. Pardon is "I won't punish you." But غُفْرَان is "I'll cover it." The word comes from the same root as مِغْفَر, a helmet that covers your head. "Cover our sins, Allah. Don't just pardon us internally—cover our mistakes so other people don't see them. Protect our reputation. Don't expose us."

وَٱرْحَمْنَآ—"And have mercy on us." At the end of the day, after all our efforts and all our failures, all we need is Your mercy. Everything comes back to Rahman, to Your mercy.

### The Final Appeal

أَنتَ مَوْلَىٰنَا—"You are our Protector, our Guardian, our Master."

Earlier in the surah, we saw Allah ﷻ described as وَلِيّ. Now we're calling Him مَوْلَىٰ. Why do we need Him to be our Mawla, our ultimate Protector?

فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ—"So grant us victory over the disbelieving people."

Notice: Allah ﷻ didn't say ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (those who disbelieve) like in the beginning of the surah. He says ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ—"the disbelieving nation," "the disbelieving people" as a collective.

This creates a beautiful pair, a powerful bookend for the surah. In the beginning, Allah ﷻ told us about those who disbelieved: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ—"Indeed those who disbelieve—it is the same for them whether you warn them or not, they will not believe" (2:6). The doors of guidance were closed for them. Warning them was pointless.

Now at the end, if the doors of da'wah are closed, if they refuse to listen to the message, then what remains? Only one option: we must defend ourselves against them. If they won't accept the truth peacefully, if they oppose and fight against it, then all we can ask is:

فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ—"Grant us victory over the disbelieving nation."

نَصْر (nasr) doesn't mean small help, like asking someone for a pen or help changing a tire. That's just مُسَاعَدَة (assistance) or مَعُونَة (aid). نَصْر means major support—military aid, massive reinforcement, overwhelming help in the face of powerful opposition.

"Help us in huge, decisive ways against the disbelieving nation."

This final du'a was echoed by believers throughout history. When the followers of Prophet Musa (AS) faced Pharaoh and his armies, they made essentially this same du'a. When the Companions (RA) faced overwhelming enemies at Badr, they were asking for this same divine support.

It's as if Allah ﷻ is telling us: "You've learned what you're supposed to learn in this surah. You've understood Iman, you've understood your obligations, you've learned about previous nations and their failures. Now, having learned all this, you should ask Me for the same thing they asked for at the moment of their greatest trial: نَصْر against the disbelieving nation."

---

## Conclusion

And with this, we complete—الحمد لله—the longest surah of the Quran.

The next surah, Surah Ali 'Imran, is its sister. They are called الزَّهْرَاوَانِ —"the two radiant ones," "the two glowing ones." The Prophet
